studies.title: Clarifying the neural basis for incentive salience of tobacco cues in smokers.
studies.abstract: In functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) studies, smoking cues have been found to elicit increases in brain activity in regions associated with processing rewarding and emotional stimuli. However, most smoking cue studies to date have reported effects relative to neutral control stimuli with no incentive properties, making it unclear whether the observed activation pertains to value in general or the value of cigarettes in particular. The current fMRI study sought to clarify the neural activity reflecting tobacco-specific incentive value versus domain-general incentive value by examining smoking cues, neutral cues, and a third set of cues, monetary cues, which served as an active control condition. Participants were 42 male daily smokers. Compared to neutral cues, significantly greater activation was found in the left ventral striatum in response to tobacco and money cues. Monetary cues also elicited significantly increased activation in the right inferior frontal gyrus and cuneus compared to the other two cue types. Overall, the results suggest that the salience of monetary cues was the highest and, as a result, might have reduced the incentive salience of tobacco cues.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Original human fMRI study (English) reporting cue-reactivity to smoking (drug) and monetary cues using visual stimuli in daily smokers, matching the review objective and inclusion criteria.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an empirical fMRI study using visual cue-reactivity stimuli (tobacco, money, neutral) in nicotine-dependent smokers. It includes within-subject contrasts comparing tobacco cues to neutral and to monetary cues, and reports group-level voxelwise whole-brain analyses (disjunction mask, voxelwise one-sample t-tests with cluster correction) with reported clusters/ROIs. Relevant participant demographics (age, all-male sample), cue types, scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (AFNI) are reported. Results are described with identified clusters (and supplementary materials noted), satisfying reporting requirements. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only, or non-empirical) apply. Therefore the study meets all specified inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

analyses.name: Omnibus effects and pairwise contrasts for discriminating empirical regions of interest (ROI) by cue type. Contrasts were only conducted on disjunction mask ROIs that surpassed a Bonferroni-corrected significance threshold of p<0.001.

studies.title: Enhanced cue-elicited brain activation in African American compared with Caucasian smokers: an fMRI study.
studies.abstract: Current evidence indicates that, although African Americans (AA) are more likely to attempt to quit smoking than Caucasians (CC) in any given year, success rates are lower for AA. However, factors contributing to these differences are not well known. In order to explore potential factors, this study assessed differences in attention to smoking cues between ethnic groups. Participants underwent morning functional magnetic resonance imaging scanning while viewing images of AA models and CC models who were either smoking (smoking cues) or engaging in everyday activities (neutral cues), interspersed with a fixation baseline period. The study was conducted at the Hoglund Brain Imaging Center of the University of Kansas Medical Center in Kansas City, KS. We studied 17 smokers (eight AA, nine CC) after 12-hour abstinence and 17 non-smokers (eight AA, nine CC) matched by age, gender, years of education, and handedness. The AA and CC smoking groups were also matched for number of cigarettes smoked per day. All results are P < 0.01, corrected for whole brain. There was a strong ethnicity by condition interaction among smokers in several a priori regions of interest. AA smokers showed a greater increase in response to smoking (versus neutral cues) than CC smokers in the medial prefrontal cortex, right lateral orbitofrontal cortex, and bilateral ventrolateral prefrontal cortex. In smoking versus baseline contrasts, additional areas of greater activation were found in AA, including the right amygdala and left caudate nucleus. No significant differences in cue-elicited brain activation were found between AA and CC non-smokers. These preliminary findings demonstrate variation in brain activation in response to smoking cues between AA and CC smokers in structures known to be associated with nicotine addiction. Differences in neural response may reflect fundamental differences in attention to smoking cues, which may in turn contribute to differences in effectiveness of nicotine dependence treatments among ethnic populations.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Empirical English-language fMRI study in human participants assessing smoking cue-reactivity using visual (image) stimuli. Abstract clearly reports original data and task fits the review objective; no exclusion criteria apply.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an fMRI study of nicotine (smoking) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. The paper reports within-subject contrasts of smoking versus neutral images, and group-level, whole-brain voxelwise statistical mapping (BrainVoyager GLM, voxelwise t-statistics, whole-brain correction) with results presented in tables and figures. Relevant participant demographics (age, sex), cue type (nicotine), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (BrainVoyager QX) are reported. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, or non-empirical) apply. Therefore all required inclusion criteria are met.

studies.title: Brain substrates of craving to alcohol cues in subjects with alcohol use disorder.
studies.abstract: This study's purpose was to identify the neural substrates and mechanisms responsible for craving among subjects with alcohol use disorders (AUDs) using functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI).
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Abstract describes an English-language empirical fMRI study of craving to alcohol cues in human participants with AUD. However, the abstract does not specify that the cues were visual, so modality is unclear. Include for full-text review to confirm use of visual stimuli.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is a task-based fMRI study of alcohol cue reactivity using visual stimuli (alcohol pictures) with within-subject contrasts of alcohol cues > beverage/control pictures. The authors performed group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses using SPM99, report cluster-corrected activations and provide Talairach coordinates/tables and figures. Relevant methodological details are reported: cue type (alcohol), participant demographics (male and female college students; demographics in Table 1), scanner field strength (3.0 T), and processing software (SPM99). Although the sample is small and all subjects are Asian (noted as limitation), no exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only, or non-empirical paper) apply. Therefore the study satisfies I3, I4, I5, I6, and I7 and is eligible for inclusion.

studies.title: Neural response to alcohol stimuli in adolescents with alcohol use disorder.
studies.abstract: Cue reactivity studies in alcohol-dependent adults have shown atypical physiological, cognitive, and neural responses to alcohol-related stimuli that differ from the responses of light drinkers. Cue reactivity and its neural substrates are unclear in youth. We hypothesized that teens with alcohol use disorder would show greater brain response than nonabusing teens to alcohol images relative to neutral beverage images in limbic and frontal brain regions.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Original empirical human fMRI study using visual alcohol cues (alcohol images vs neutral beverage images) in adolescents; meets inclusion criteria for English empirical data and fMRI visual cue-reactivity. Abstract provides sufficient detail to include for meta-analysis of visual cue-reactivity.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an fMRI study using pictorial alcohol cues (visual stimuli) in adolescents with AUD. It includes within-participant contrasts of alcoholic vs nonalcoholic pictures (drug cues > control), reports voxelwise, whole-brain group-level analyses (tables and figures), and provides participant age/sex and cue type. Processing software (AFNI) and detailed acquisition/processing parameters are reported. Therefore it meets the meta-analysis inclusion criteria for visual cue-reactivity fMRI studies.

studies.title: Prospective changes in neural alcohol cue reactivity in at-risk adolescents.
studies.abstract: Adolescence represents an ideal time for elucidating the etiology of cue reactivity profiles. This study examined the influence of three risk factors consistently associated with heavy adolescent drinking on alcohol cue reactivity. Youth were first assessed while still naïve to alcohol (12-14 years old) and followed after transitioning into alcohol use (17-21 years old). The effects of family history of substance use disorder, sex, and history of early of dating (i.e., before 14 years of age) on BOLD response contrast to alcohol picture cues were examined in a linear mixed model, controlling for age and alcohol use patterns at follow-up. Activation to alcohol picture cues differed as a function of risk factor and time. At baseline, family history positive youth showed greater activation to alcohol cues than family history negative peers in the right middle occipital and anterior cingulate gyri. Youth with a history of early-dating showed greater activation to alcohol cues, compared to non-early daters, in the left anterior cingulate/white matter region. Girls showed greater activation to alcohol than boys at baseline in left middle frontal gyrus. At follow-up, after drinking started, patterns reversed for each risk factor. These results indicate that even prior to initiating alcohol use, adolescents showed differences in activation to alcohol cues based on their family history, dating history, and sex.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Original empirical fMRI study in English assessing BOLD responses to alcohol picture cues (visual stimuli) in human adolescents, directly addressing reward/drug cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. Fits inclusion criteria I1 and I2.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is a task-based fMRI study of alcohol cue reactivity using visual alcohol and neutral beverage pictures, with an explicit within-subject contrast (alcohol>neutral). Group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses were conducted (AFNI 3dLME, cluster correction) and results reported in tables/figures. Participant age and sex, cue type (alcohol), scanner field strength (3.0T), and processing software (AFNI) are all reported. Thus the study satisfies all required inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis and does not violate exclusion criteria.

studies.title: 24-h smoking abstinence potentiates fMRI-BOLD activation to smoking cues in cerebral cortex and dorsal striatum.
studies.abstract: Exposure to smoking-related cues can trigger relapse in smokers attempting to maintain abstinence.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Abstract reports an English-language empirical fMRI study in human smokers examining BOLD responses to smoking-related cues (visual cue exposure) following abstinence, directly addressing cue-reactivity with visual stimuli relevant to the meta-analysis objective.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an fMRI study of nicotine (smoking) cue-reactivity using visual photographic smoking cues. It includes a within-subject contrast of smoking cues > control cues, reports group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses (random-effects, SPM5) with coordinates/tables and figures, and provides participant age/sex, cue type, scanner field strength (4.0 T) and processing software. Therefore it satisfies all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis and does not meet any exclusion criteria.

studies.title: Activation of prefrontal cortex and anterior thalamus in alcoholic subjects on exposure to alcohol-specific cues.
studies.abstract: Functional imaging studies have recently demonstrated that specific brain regions become active in cocaine addicts when they are exposed to cocaine stimuli. To test whether there are regional brain activity differences during alcohol cue exposure between alcoholic subjects and social drinkers, we designed a functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) protocol involving alcohol-specific cues.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Empirical fMRI study of alcoholic subjects exposed to alcohol-specific cues, addressing cue-reactivity. Abstract confirms original data and fMRI cue exposure but does not specify sensory modality (visual). Include for full-text review to confirm use of visual stimuli.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an fMRI study of alcohol cue-reactivity using visual stimuli with an explicit within-participant contrast (alcohol cues > nonalcohol beverage cues). The authors performed group-level voxelwise statistical mapping (SPM-based cluster analysis) and report activated clusters/coordinates. Participant age and sex, cue type (alcohol), scanner field strength (1.5T), and processing software (MEDx/SPM) are reported. Although imaging covered only the anterior brain slices, the analysis and reported results are voxelwise group-level maps and coordinate tables, satisfying the meta-analysis requirements.

studies.title: Neural substrates of attentional bias for smoking-related cues: an FMRI study.
studies.abstract: Attentional bias for drug-related stimuli, as measured by emotional Stroop (ES) tasks, is predictive of treatment outcomes for tobacco smoking and other abused drugs. Characterizing relationships between smoking-related attentional bias and brain reactivity to smoking images may help in identifying neural substrates critical to relapse vulnerability. To this end, we investigated putative relationships between interference effects in an offline smoking ES task and functional MRI (fMRI) measures of brain reactivity to smoking vs neutral images in women smokers. Positive correlations were found between attentional bias and reactivity to smoking images in brain areas involved in emotion, memory, interoception, and visual processing, including the amygdala, hippocampus, parahippocampal gyrus, insula, and occipital cortex. These findings suggest that smokers with elevated attentional biases to smoking-related stimuli may more readily shift attention away from other external stimuli and toward smoking stimuli-induced internal states and emotional memories. Such attentional shifts may contribute to increased interference by smoking cues, possibly increasing relapse vulnerability. Treatments capable of inhibiting shifts to drug cue-induced memories and internal states may lead to personalized tobacco dependence treatment for smokers with high attentional bias to smoking-related stimuli.
studies.journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology
studies.abstract_screening_reason: This is an English-language empirical fMRI study reporting original data examining brain reactivity to smoking vs. neutral images (visual cue-reactivity) in smokers. It directly matches the meta-analysis objective of fMRI studies using visual drug-related cues. No exclusion criteria apply from the abstract.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an fMRI study of smoking (nicotine) cue reactivity using visual stimuli (smoking vs neutral images), reporting within-subject contrasts (smoking > neutral) and correlating these whole-brain, group-level task activation maps with behavioral attentional-bias scores. The paper reports whole-brain random-effects ANCOVA results with cluster-level correction, tables/figures (coordinates and Figure 1) and includes participant age/sex, cue type, scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (BrainVoyager). No exclusion criteria (e.g., ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only, or non-empirical) apply. Therefore it meets all required inclusion criteria and is appropriate for inclusion in the meta-analysis.

studies.title: Hippocampal and Insular Response to Smoking-Related Environments: Neuroimaging Evidence for Drug-Context Effects in Nicotine Dependence.
studies.abstract: Environments associated with prior drug use provoke craving and drug taking, and set the stage for lapse/relapse. Although the neurobehavioral bases of environment-induced drug taking have been investigated with animal models, the influence of drug-environments on brain function and behavior in clinical populations of substance users is largely unexplored. Adult smokers (n=40) photographed locations personally associated with smoking (personal smoking environments; PSEs) or personal nonsmoking environment (PNEs). Following 24-h abstinence, participants underwent fMRI scanning while viewing PSEs, PNEs, standard smoking and nonsmoking environments, as well as proximal smoking (eg, lit cigarette) and nonsmoking (eg, pencil) cues. Finally, in two separate sessions following 6-h abstinence they viewed either PSEs or PNEs while cue-induced self-reported craving and smoking behavior were assessed. Viewing PSEs increased blood oxygen level-dependent signal in right posterior hippocampus (pHPC; F(2,685)=3.74, p<0.024) and bilateral insula (left: F(2,685)=6.87, p=0.0011; right: F(2,685)=5.34, p=0.005). In the laboratory, viewing PSEs, compared with PNEs, was associated with higher craving levels (F(2,180)=18.32, p<0.0001) and greater ad lib smoking (F(1,36)=5.01, p=0.032). The effect of PSEs (minus PNEs) on brain activation in right insula was positively correlated with the effect of PSEs (minus PNEs) on number of puffs taken from a cigarette (r=0.6, p=0.001). Our data, for the first time in humans, elucidates the neural mechanisms that mediate the effects of real-world drug-associated environments on drug taking behavior under conditions of drug abstinence. These findings establish targets for the development and evaluation of treatments seeking to reduce environment provoked relapse.

Meets inclusion criteria for original data and fMRI visual cue-reactivity. No exclusion criteria identified.

The paper includes group-level, whole-brain voxelwise task activation analyses (cluster-corrected results reported in text, Figure 2, and Supplementary Table 2). Whole-brain results are clearly described in figures/tables. Participant age and sex are reported, cue type is nicotine, scanner field strength (3T) and processing software (FSL v5.0.1) are provided. studies.title: Association between nicotine dependence severity, BOLD response to smoking cues, and functional connectivity.
studies.abstract: Enhanced motivational salience towards smoking cues is a consequence of chronic nicotine use, but the degree to which this value increases beyond that of other appetitive cues is unknown. In addition, it is unclear how connectivity between brain regions influences cue reactivity and how cue reactivity and functional connectivity are related to nicotine dependence severity. This study examined neural responses during the presentation of smoking cues and appetitive control cues, as well as functional connectivity in 116 smokers with a range of nicotine dependence severity. Smoking cues elicited greater response above baseline than food cues in orbitofrontal cortex (OFC) and supplementary motor area (SMA) and less deactivation below baseline in middle frontal gyrus, inferior parietal lobe, and middle temporal gyrus. Psychophysiological interaction (PPI) analysis using right OFC as a seed revealed increased connectivity with somatosensory cortex and lateral inferior parietal lobe during smoking cues compared with food cues. Similarly, a PPI analysis using left insula as a seed showed stronger connectivity with somatosensory cortex, right insula, OFC, and striatum. Finally, relationships with nicotine dependence scores showed enhanced response in insula and dorsal anterior cingulate cortex in the smoking vs food comparison, and increased connectivity between insula and circuits involved in motivated behavior. Combined, these results suggest that smokers engage attentional networks and default mode networks involved in self-referential processing to a greater degree during smoking cues. In addition, individuals with greater nicotine dependence severity show increased engagement of sensorimotor and motor preparation circuits, suggesting increased reliance on habitual behavior.
studies.journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Empirical human fMRI study (original data) that measured neural responses to smoking (drug) and food (natural reward) cues using visual stimuli. Abstract clearly reports task-based fMRI cue-reactivity and relates responses to dependence severity. No exclusion criteria apply.

Methods report participant age/sex, cue types (smoking, food), scanner field strength (Siemens 3T), and processing software (FSL). Whole-brain activation maps and coordinate tables are provided, and task activation (not only connectivity or ROI) is reported. Therefore all inclusion criteria are satisfied.

studies.title: Enhanced smoking cue salience associated with depression severity in nicotine-dependent individuals: a preliminary fMRI study.
studies.abstract: The association between cigarette smoking and depression has been well documented; however, little research has been done to elucidate the neurobiological substrates of this highly prevalent comorbidity. We used multiple linear regression analysis to evaluate the relationship between depression severity as assessed by the Hamilton Depression Rating Scale (HAMD) and blood oxygen level-dependent (BOLD) responses to visual smoking cues in drug-free nicotine-dependent smokers (n=18). Two functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) scans were completed over a single study day, following overnight smoking abstinence (pre-smoking scan) and after cigarette reinstatement (post-smoking scan). During the pre-smoking scan positive correlations between BOLD activity and HAMD scores were observed in areas of the mesocorticolimbic dopaminergic system [inferior frontal gyrus, middle frontal gyrus (MFG), hippocampus (HC), anterior cingulate gyrus] and areas of the visuospatial attention circuit (medial occipital lobe, middle cingulate cortex, superior frontal gyrus, angular gyrus). During the post-smoking scan positive correlations were observed in areas of the brain implicated in drug expectancy (MFG), memory (HC), attentional motivation (posterior cingulate cortex), and visual processing and attention (precuneus). These preliminary findings demonstrate that smokers with higher depression severity attribute greater incentive salience to smoking-related cues and this is especially pronounced during periods of acute abstinence. Such enhanced salience of smoking cues, even after smoking a cigarette, may play a critical role both in the maintenance of smoking in depression and in greater levels of nicotine dependence seen in this patient population.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Human fMRI study with original data assessing cue-reactivity to visual smoking stimuli in nicotine-dependent participants. Not a review/meta-analysis and does not use only non-visual cues. Fits the meta-analysis focus on fMRI visual drug-related cue reactivity.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an fMRI study of nicotine (drug) cue-reactivity using visual smoking-related images with a within-participant contrast (smoking cues > neutral cues). Analyses were conducted as group-level, whole-brain voxelwise random-effects SPM5 analyses (reports coordinates/tables and figures) on 18 participants (age and sex reported). Scanner field strength (3.0 T) and processing software (SPM5) are specified. Therefore it meets all required inclusion criteria for visual drug-cue fMRI whole-brain task activation reporting and violates none of the exclusion criteria.

studies.title: Effects of cue-exposure treatment on neural cue reactivity in alcohol dependence: a randomized trial.
studies.abstract: In alcohol-dependent patients, alcohol-associated cues elicit brain activation in mesocorticolimbic networks involved in relapse mechanisms. Cue-exposure based extinction training (CET) has been shown to be efficacious in the treatment of alcoholism; however, it has remained unexplored whether CET mediates its therapeutic effects via changes of activity in mesolimbic networks in response to alcohol cues. In this study, we assessed CET treatment effects on cue-induced responses using functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI).
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Abstract reports an original human fMRI study examining cue-induced brain responses to alcohol-related cues (treats cue-reactivity), but does not specify the sensory modality of cues. Therefore include for full-text review to confirm visual stimuli. Meets I1; I2 unclear from abstract.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an empirical fMRI study of alcohol cue reactivity using visual stimuli (pictures) with within-subject contrasts of alcohol > neutral cues. Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses were performed using SPM5 (reported with cluster thresholds and peak coordinates, and small-volume FWE correction for striatal ROIs), and results are reported with coordinates and cluster descriptions. Participant age and sex are reported, cue type is alcohol, scanner field strength is 3T, and processing software is specified. Therefore all inclusion criteria are satisfied and no exclusion criteria are met.

studies.title: Brain regions affected by impaired control modulate responses to alcohol and smoking cues.
studies.abstract: Despite the commonly observed comorbidity of alcohol and tobacco use disorders and years of research, the mechanism underlying concurrent use of alcohol and tobacco is not yet clear. In this study, we used functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) to investigate the relationship between brain responses to alcohol and smoking cues in 45 subjects with episodic drinking and regular smoking.

Although the alcohol cue used a taste paradigm, the presence of a visual smoking-cue contrast that meets the review’s visual-stimulus focus makes the study eligible. Whole-brain activation maps and clusterwise statistics are reported and illustrated, satisfying reporting requirements. Therefore all inclusion criteria (I3, I4, I5, I6, I7) are met and no exclusion criteria apply.

studies.title: Smoking cues impair monitoring but not stopping during response inhibition in abstinent male smokers.
studies.abstract: The attenuated inhibitory control of smokers is a stumbling block for treating nicotine dependence. Unfortunately, smokers are often exposed to cigarette-related salient cues, which may violate homeostasis, override self-control, and lead to relapse. To understand the mechanisms underlying these associations, we investigate the cognitive and neural processes of inhibitory control (including proactive and reactive inhibition) of smoking cues in abstinent smokers. Twenty-six smokers completed cue-reactivity and stop signal tasks during functional magnetic resonance imaging scans, on two separate sessions, 2-3 weeks apart: one involved a neutral cue reactivity task, and the other a smoking cue reactivity task. Findings pointed to no significant subjective craving changes, or behavioral influences of smoking cues on proactive and reactive inhibition. However, abstinent smokers exhibited hyperactive brain reactivity in response to smoking versus neutral stimuli, in regions including the insula, amygdala, anterior cingulate cortex (ACC), and putamen. They also had hypoactive bilateral rostral ACC (rACC) and hyperactive right pre-supplementary motor area during reactive inhibition in smoking versus neutral conditions. Support vector regression analysis showed that activation of these regions predicted and correlated with reactive inhibition index (i.e., SSRT), alluding to the possibility of their involvement in the reactive inhibition. Subjective craving scores were predicted by and correlated with activation of bilateral dorsal and rostral ACC, supporting the ideas of their possible involvement in subjective craving. These findings suggest that smoking cues evoke hyperactive brain reactivity; this may interfere with normal performance monitoring and rapid reactive inhibition. These findings have important implication for treating smoking dependence.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Study reports original fMRI data in humans examining smoking cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (smoking vs neutral) during tasks; meets both inclusion criteria and no exclusion criteria are applicable from abstract.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an fMRI study of nicotine (smoking) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (smoking vs neutral pictures), with a within-subject contrast of smoking>neutral. The paper reports whole-brain, group-level voxelwise analyses (paired t-tests; threshold z>2.3, cluster p<0.05) and lists significant clusters (Tables S2/S3). Processing software (FSL v5.0.9) is explicitly reported. Participant characteristics (26 male college students) are given and referenced in Table S1 (demographics/age). The study therefore provides the required behavioral/contrast, whole-brain activation results, and methodological details needed for inclusion. Relevant details (e.g., scanner acquisition parameters/field strength) are referenced in supplementary materials (supplementary tables/methods), consistent with reporting practices and I6 allowance for supplementary reporting. Based on these points, all inclusion criteria are met for the meta-analysis of visual drug-cue fMRI studies.

studies.abstract: To examine whether subscales of Food Craving Inventory (FCI) and Three-factor Eating Questionnaire (TFEQ) correlate with brain functional magnetic resonance imaging food-cue reactivity (fMRI-FCR) in the brain.

studies.title: Do brain responses to emotional images and cigarette cues differ? An fMRI study in smokers.
studies.abstract: Chronic smoking is thought to cause changes in brain reward systems that result in overvaluation of cigarette-related stimuli and undervaluation of natural rewards. We tested the hypotheses that, in smokers, brain circuits involved in emotional processing: (i) would be more active during exposure to cigarette-related than neutral pictures; and (ii) would be less active to pleasant compared with cigarette-related pictures, suggesting a devaluation of intrinsically pleasant stimuli. We obtained whole-brain blood oxygenation level-dependent (BOLD) functional magnetic resonance imaging data from 35 smokers during the presentation of pleasant (erotica and romance), unpleasant (mutilations and sad), neutral, and cigarette-related pictures. Whole-brain analyses showed significantly larger BOLD responses during presentation of cigarette-related pictures relative to neutral ones within the secondary visual areas, the cingulate gyrus, the frontal gyrus, the dorsal striatum, and the left insula. BOLD responses to erotic pictures exceeded responses to cigarette-related pictures in all clusters except the insula. Within the left insula we observed larger BOLD responses to cigarette-related pictures than to all other picture categories. By including intrinsically pleasant and unpleasant pictures in addition to neutral ones, we were able to conclude that the presentation of cigarette-related pictures activates brain areas supporting emotional processes, but we did not find evidence of overall reduced activation of the brain reward systems in the presence of intrinsically pleasant stimuli.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Original English-language empirical fMRI study in human smokers using visual stimuli (pleasant, unpleasant, neutral, cigarette-related images) to assess cue-reactivity; meets both inclusion criteria and no exclusion criteria are met.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is a task-evoked, whole-brain BOLD fMRI study of smokers viewing visual stimuli including cigarette-related (drug) and naturally rewarding (erotic, romantic) pictures. The paper reports within-subject contrasts (cigarette > neutral) using a random-effects whole-brain voxelwise analysis with thresholding and cluster coordinates (Table 2, figures). Participant age and sex, cue type (nicotine), MRI field strength (3.0 T), and processing software (BrainVoyager QX) are reported. Thus it meets all inclusion criteria for visual drug and natural reward cue-reactivity fMRI studies and violates none of the exclusion criteria.

studies.title: Predicting naltrexone response in alcohol-dependent patients: the contribution of functional magnetic resonance imaging.
studies.abstract: Effect sizes of pharmacotherapy in alcoholism are modest. They might improve if subjects could be divided into more homogeneous subgroups and would then be treated targeted to their neurobiological profile. In such an effort, we tested neural cue reactivity as a potential predictor of treatment response to naltrexone. Alcohol-associated cues cause brain activations in mesocorticolimbic networks due to the positive reinforcing properties of alcohol. These activations were reported to be associated with relapse behavior. Naltrexone, an antagonist at the mu-opioid receptor, improves drinking behavior in some but not all patients probably by blocking the positive reinforcement of alcohol. Conversely, acamprosate is proposed to modulate negative reinforcement (withdrawal and cue-induced withdrawal). Identifying subjects with elevated cue reactivity and testing their response to medical treatment could thus improve our understanding of some of the mechanisms underlying pharmacotherapy response.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Abstract describes an original human fMRI study assessing neural cue-reactivity to alcohol-associated cues in alcohol-dependent patients. While it does not explicitly state the sensory modality of cues, the study fits the review objective (drug cue-reactivity assessed with fMRI); insufficient detail about stimuli modality warrants inclusion for full-text review.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This study is an empirical fMRI investigation of alcohol cue reactivity using visual stimuli. It reports within-participant contrasts of alcohol versus control pictures, performs and reports group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses (one-sample t-test and task main effects) and presents results in tables/figures. Methods report participant demographics (age, sex), cue type (alcohol), MRI field strength (1.5 T), and processing software (SPM5). Although ROI analyses were also conducted, whole-brain task activation maps and results are clearly described, so no ROI-only exclusion applies. Therefore the study meets all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

studies.title: Blockade of cue-induced brain activation of abstinent alcoholics by a single administration of amisulpride as measured with fMRI.
studies.abstract: Once alcohol dependence is established, alcohol-associated cues may induce dopamine release in the reward system, which is accompanied by alcohol craving and may lead to relapse. In cocaine addicts, dopamine release in the thalamus was positively correlated with cocaine craving. We tested the effects of the atypical dopamine D(2/3) blocker amisulpride on cue-induced brain activation in a functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) paradigm.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Original human fMRI study examining cue-induced brain activation in abstinent alcoholics (addresses drug-related cue-reactivity). The abstract confirms fMRI cue-reactivity but does not specify that cues were visual; include for full-text review to confirm stimulus modality.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an fMRI study using visual alcohol-related cues and control stimuli, with within-subject contrasts of alcohol cues > control stimuli (first-level contrasts) and group-level voxelwise (whole-brain) analyses using SPM99 (thresholds reported). Participant age and sex (male-only), cue type (alcohol), scanner field strength (1.5T), and processing software are reported. Results are reported as whole-brain task-evoked maps (tables/figures and coordinates). Therefore all required inclusion criteria are met and no exclusion criteria apply.

studies.title: Influence of alcohol use and family history of alcoholism on neural response to alcohol cues in college drinkers.
studies.abstract: Heavy drinkers show altered functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) response to alcohol cues. Little is known about alcohol cue reactivity among college age drinkers, who show the greatest rates of alcohol use disorders. Family history of alcoholism (family history positive [FHP]) is a risk factor for problematic drinking, but the impact on alcohol cue reactivity is unclear. We investigated the influence of heavy drinking and family history of alcoholism on alcohol cue-related fMRI response among college students.

studies.title: Association of Prefrontal-Striatal Functional Pathology With Alcohol Abstinence Days at Treatment Initiation and Heavy Drinking After Treatment Initiation.
studies.abstract: Alcohol use disorder (AUD) is associated with neuroadaptations in brain stress and reward circuits. It is not known whether such neuroadaptations are affected by number of days of alcohol abstinence and whether they influence heavy drinking during the early treatment phase. The authors used a novel functional MRI (fMRI) approach to assess brain responses during sustained exposure to standardized visual stimuli of stressful, alcohol cue, and neutral control images combined with prospective assessment of drinking outcomes during early outpatient treatment, in two related studies.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Abstract reports an original human fMRI study assessing brain responses to standardized visual alcohol-related and neutral cues (cue-reactivity) in alcohol use disorder, matching the meta-analysis focus on visual reward-related cue-reactivity.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an fMRI study of alcohol (drug) cue-reactivity using standardized visual stimuli (alcohol, stress, neutral images). Within-subject contrasts (alcohol cue > neutral, stress > neutral, neutral > baseline) are reported and used in analyses. The paper reports whole-brain, voxel-wise group-level analyses with cluster-wise correction (p<0.001, alphasim/FWE), and presents voxel-based results in figures/tables. Necessary methodological details are reported: participant age ranges and sex composition, cue type (alcohol), MRI field strength (3T), and processing/software (BioImageSuite; AFNI used for cluster correction). No exclusion criteria are violated (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, includes original empirical data). Therefore it meets all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

studies.title: Dynamic neural responses to cue-reactivity paradigms in heroin-dependent users: an fMRI study.
studies.abstract: Neuroimaging methods have been employed to study cue-reactivity-induced neural correlates in the human brain. However, very few studies have focused on characterizing the dynamic neural responses to the factorial interactions between the cues and the subjects. Fifteen right-handed heroin-dependent subjects and 12 age-matched nondrug using subjects participated in this study. Cue-reactivity paradigms were employed, while changes in blood oxygenation level-dependent (BOLD) signals were acquired by functional MRI (fMRI). The fMRI datasets were analyzed with AFNI software and repeated two-way ANOVA was employed for factorial analyses. Neural correlates of factorial interactions between cue-factor and subject-factor were identified in the regions of the ventral tegmental area (VTA), the left and right amygdala, the left and right fusiform cortex, and the precuneus in the mesocorticolimbic system, and in the superior frontal, dorsal lateral prefrontal, and orbitofrontal cortices in the prefrontal cortex system. The neural response patterns in the prefrontal systems are dynamic: decreased response to neutral-cues and increased response to heroin-cues. Further, heroin-cue-induced neural responses within the subregions in the PFC system are significantly intercorrelated. In conclusion, the cue-reactivity paradigms significantly activated the dynamic neural activations in the prefrontal system. It is suggested that the dynamic response patterns in the PFC system characterize the impaired brain control functions in heroin-dependent subjects.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: English-language empirical fMRI study of heroin cue-reactivity with original data. Abstract confirms fMRI during cue-reactivity paradigms but does not specify that cues were visual, so inclusion on I2 cannot be confirmed from abstract alone. Include for full-text review to verify stimulus modality.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an empirical fMRI study using visual cue-reactivity (video) for heroin cues vs neutral cues with within-subject contrasts (heroin>neutral). Group-level voxelwise whole-brain analyses were performed (2×2 factorial, cluster-corrected maps) and results reported as activation coordinates and figures. Participant demographics (age, sex), cue type (heroin), scanner field strength (1.5T), and processing software (AFNI) are reported. Thus all required inclusion criteria are met and no exclusion criteria apply.

studies.title: Effects of high-dose baclofen on cue reactivity in alcohol dependence: A randomized, placebo-controlled pharmaco-fMRI study.
studies.abstract: Increased functional brain response towards alcohol-associated stimuli is a neural hallmark of alcohol dependence and a promising target for pharmacotherapy. For the first time, we assessed the effects of individually titrated high-dose baclofen on cue reactivity and functional connectivity in alcohol-dependent (AD) patients in a randomized controlled trial (RCT). We investigated 23 recently detoxified AD patients and 23 matched healthy controls (HC) with a cue reactivity functional magnetic resonance imaging task. Patients were further scanned at baseline without medication and during treatment with high-dose baclofen/placebo (30-270 mg/d). Analyses were conducted for alcohol cue-elicited brain response, alcohol cue-modulated and stimulus-independent functional connectivity with left ventral tegmental area (VTA) as seed region. At baseline, AD patients (N = 23) showed increased cue-elicited brain activation in the ventral striatum (VS) compared to HC (N = 23), which was decreased at the second scanning session compared to baseline. Patients receiving baclofen (N = 10) showed a significant stronger decrease in cue-elicited brain activation in left orbitofrontal cortex (OFC), bilateral amygdala and left VTA than patients receiving placebo (N = 13). Treatment with baclofen further led to a decrease in alcohol cue-modulated functional connectivity between left VTA and left anterior cingulate cortex (ACC) as well as left medial prefrontal cortex (MPFC). Regarding clinical outcome, significantly more patients of the baclofen group remained abstinent during the high-dose period. Baclofen specifically decreased cue-elicited brain responses in areas known to be involved in the processing of salient (appetitive and aversive) stimuli. Treatment with high-dose baclofen seems to provide a pharmacological relief of this neural "warning signal" evoked by alcohol-related cues, thereby possibly supporting patients in remaining abstinent. Trial Registration Identifier of the main trial [BACLAD study] at clinicaltrials.gov: NCT01266655.

Group-level voxelwise analyses were conducted (GLM, t-tests and ANOVAs) and whole-brain results are reported (uncorrected exploratory whole-brain results in supplementary tables), satisfying the requirement for a group-level, whole-brain task-evoked map. The paper reports participant age/sex, cue type (alcohol), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM12). Therefore it meets inclusion criteria I3, I4, I5, I6 and I7.

studies.abstract: Varenicline has been shown to reduce cigarette craving during a quit attempt.

studies.title: Modulation of Craving Related Brain Responses Using Real-Time fMRI in Patients with Alcohol Use Disorder.
studies.abstract: One prominent symptom in addiction disorders is the strong desire to consume a particular substance or to display a certain behaviour (craving). Especially the strong association between craving and the probability of relapse emphasises the importance of craving in the therapeutic process. Neuroimaging studies have shown that craving is associated with increased responses, predominantly in fronto-striatal areas.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Abstract indicates an original human fMRI study of craving in alcohol use disorder (meets I1). However, the abstract does not specify that cue-reactivity was assessed using visual stimuli; modality is unclear. Include for full-text review to confirm whether visual cues were used.

Methods report scanner field strength (3T), age range and subject numbers, cue type (alcohol), and processing software (TurboBrainVoyager, BrainVoyager, AFNI, FSL). Results include whole-brain task-evoked activation comparisons and tables/coordinates for significant clusters and seed-based connectivity as supplementary analyses. Therefore all required inclusion criteria for cue-reactivity visual fMRI studies are met and no exclusion criteria apply.

studies.title: Effects of cognitive bias modification training on neural alcohol cue reactivity in alcohol dependence.
studies.abstract: In alcohol-dependent patients, alcohol cues evoke increased activation in mesolimbic brain areas, such as the nucleus accumbens and the amygdala. Moreover, patients show an alcohol approach bias, a tendency to more quickly approach than avoid alcohol cues. Cognitive bias modification training, which aims to retrain approach biases, has been shown to reduce alcohol craving and relapse rates. The authors investigated effects of this training on cue reactivity in alcohol-dependent patients.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Abstract describes an empirical study in alcohol-dependent patients assessing neural alcohol cue reactivity and reports activation in mesolimbic areas, but it does not explicitly state that fMRI was used or that visual stimuli were presented. Insufficient detail to confirm neuroimaging modality and stimulus modality from abstract alone — include for full-text review.

Group-level voxelwise analyses were conducted and whole-brain activations are reported (with supplementary Table S1), alongside region-of-interest small-volume corrections. The manuscript provides participant sex (male), cue type (alcohol), MRI scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM8). Therefore all required inclusion criteria are met: it assesses visual drug cue reactivity with fMRI (I3), includes within-participant drug>control contrasts (I4), presents group-level whole-brain task activation results (I5) reported in the main text and supplement (I6), and reports participant and MRI details (I7). No exclusion criteria are violated.

studies.title: Nipping cue reactivity in the bud: baclofen prevents limbic activation elicited by subliminal drug cues.
studies.abstract: Relapse is a widely recognized and difficult to treat feature of the addictions. Substantial evidence implicates cue-triggered activation of the mesolimbic dopamine system as an important contributing factor. Even drug cues presented outside of conscious awareness (i.e., subliminally) produce robust activation within this circuitry, indicating the sensitivity and vulnerability of the brain to potentially problematic reward signals. Because pharmacological agents that prevent these early cue-induced responses could play an important role in relapse prevention, we examined whether baclofen-a GABAB receptor agonist that reduces mesolimbic dopamine release and conditioned drug responses in laboratory animals-could inhibit mesolimbic activation elicited by subliminal cocaine cues in cocaine-dependent individuals. Twenty cocaine-dependent participants were randomized to receive baclofen (60 mg/d; 20 mg t.i.d.) or placebo. Event-related BOLD fMRI and a backward-masking paradigm were used to examine the effects of baclofen on subliminal cocaine (vs neutral) cues. Sexual and aversive cues were included to examine specificity. We observed that baclofen-treated participants displayed significantly less activation in response to subliminal cocaine (vs neutral) cues, but not sexual or aversive (vs neutral) cues, than placebo-treated participants in a large interconnected bilateral cluster spanning the ventral striatum, ventral pallidum, amygdala, midbrain, and orbitofrontal cortex (voxel threshold p < 0.005; cluster corrected at p < 0.05). These results suggest that baclofen may inhibit the earliest type of drug cue-induced motivational processing-that which occurs outside of awareness-before it evolves into a less manageable state.
studies.journal: The Journal of neuroscience : the official journal of the Society for Neuroscience
studies.abstract_screening_reason: This is an English-language empirical fMRI study with original data testing human cue-reactivity to subliminal visual cocaine cues (backward-masking). It directly assesses drug-related visual cue reactivity using functional MRI, meeting the review objectives.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an empirical fMRI study using visual cues (subliminal cocaine, sexual, aversive, neutral) and reports within-subject contrasts (cocaine>neutral) and group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses (second-level t tests, cluster-corrected thresholds). Whole-brain results are reported with coordinates and figures. Relevant participant information (age range, all-male sample), cue types, scanner field strength (3T), and preprocessing/software (SPM8) are provided. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only without unmasked task maps, or non-empirical) apply. Therefore the study meets all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

studies.title: The effect of aripiprazole on cue-induced brain activation and drinking parameters in alcoholics.
studies.abstract: Because the effects of alcohol and its environmental cues on brain dopamine have been implicated in the maintenance of heavy drinking, drugs that modify dopamine might be useful in reducing drinking or promoting abstinence. The goal of the current study was to use an established brain imaging paradigm to explore the effect of aripiprazole (final dose 15 mg over a 14-day period), a dopamine stabilizer medication, on alcohol cue-induced brain activation and drinking in alcoholics. Non-treatment-seeking alcoholics were randomly assigned aripiprazole (n = 14) or identical placebo (n = 16) and reported their alcohol use while taking study medication for 14 days before an alcohol cue-induced brain functional magnetic resonance imaging study. In a Philips 3.0-T magnetic resonance imaging scanner, subjects were given a sip of alcohol before viewing a randomized presentation alcoholic- and nonalcoholic-beverage photographs while subjects rated their urge to drink. During photograph presentation, changes in regional brain activity were measured, and differences between viewing alcoholic beverage and nonalcoholic beverages were compared within and between groups. Brain activity analysis revealed increased activation for placebo-treated subjects in the right ventral striatum (P < 0.005; threshold 15 voxels), while there was a blunting of activation in this area in the aripiprazole-treated subjects. Aripiprazole-treated subjects, compared with placebo-treated subjects, also had significantly less heavy drinking during the 14-day medication period. The study provides both novel and valuable information regarding the effect of aripiprazole on cue-induced brain activation and voluntary drinking during treatment.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Empirical, English-language study reporting original fMRI data in human alcohol-dependent participants using visual alcohol vs. nonalcohol beverage cues (photographs) to assess cue-induced brain activation and drinking—meets both inclusion criteria.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an empirical fMRI study of alcohol cue reactivity using visual stimuli in alcohol-dependent participants. It includes within-subject contrasts of alcohol cues > non-alcohol beverage cues and reports group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses (SPM5; thresholded group t-maps reported). Scanner field strength (Philips 3.0T), cue type (alcohol), and processing software (SPM5) are explicitly reported; participant demographics are summarized (Table 1) including age/sex information. Whole-brain results are presented in tables/figures with coordinates. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only contrasts, or non-empirical) apply. Thus all inclusion criteria are met.

studies.title: Sex differences in associations between cannabis craving and neural responses to cannabis cues: Implications for treatment.
studies.abstract: Preclinical and clinical research indicates that there are sex differences in how men and women initiate, progress, respond to, and withdraw from cannabis use; however, neurophysiological differences, such as neural responses to cannabis cues, are not well understood. Using functional MRI and an event-related blood oxygen level-dependent backward-masking task, we compared neural responses to backward-masked cannabis cues to neutral cues in treatment-seeking, cannabis-dependent adults (N = 44; 27 males) and examined whether sex differences exist. In addition, functional MRI findings were correlated with cannabis craving. Backward-masked cannabis cues elicited greater neural responses than neutral cues in reward-related brain regions, including the striatum, hippocampus/amygdala, insula, anterior cingulate cortex, and lateral orbitofrontal cortex, p < .01, k > 121 voxels. Although no significant sex differences in neural responses to cannabis cues emerged, women showed a positive correlation between neural responses to cannabis cues in the bilateral insula and cannabis craving and an inverse correlation between neural responses to cannabis cues in the left lateral orbitofrontal cortex and cannabis craving. Men, however, showed a positive correlation between neural responses to cannabis cues in the striatum and cannabis craving. Given that cues and craving are important triggers and the focus on many behavioral treatment approaches, these findings suggest that treatment-seeking, cannabis-dependent men and women may benefit from sex-specific and tailored cannabis use disorder treatments.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: This is an English-language empirical fMRI study in human, treatment-seeking cannabis-dependent adults that measured neural responses to backward-masked cannabis cues versus neutral cues (visual cue-reactivity). It directly fits the meta-analysis objective and no exclusion criteria apply.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: The study is an fMRI investigation of cannabis cue-reactivity (drug-related visual cues) reporting neural responses to cannabis cues and their association with craving, and explicitly examines sex differences. The methods report within-subject contrasts of cannabis cues > control stimuli and present group-level, whole-brain (voxelwise) activation results (reported as coordinates/figures in the text/supplement), along with participant age/sex information, cue type (cannabis), MRI field strength, and processing software. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only contrasts, or non-empirical) appear to apply. Therefore it meets all listed inclusion criteria for visual drug cue fMRI whole-brain task-evoked activation.

studies.title: Brain activation in response to craving- and aversion-inducing cues related to alcohol in patients with alcohol dependence.
studies.abstract: Episodes of alcohol consumption produce use-limiting aversive effects as well as use-promoting euphoric effects. The brain regions associated with the reward circuit in patients with alcohol dependence (PAD) show signs of conditioning for alcohol craving. Alternatively, brain structures in the medial temporal region are known to be crucial for aversive conditioning. In this study, we compare differences in patterns of brain activation in response to cues that induce cravings versus aversion in PAD.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Abstract reports original empirical work on brain activation in patients with alcohol dependence in response to craving vs aversion cues but does not explicitly state fMRI and/or use of visual stimuli. Not enough detail to confirm I2 from abstract alone; include for full-text review to verify imaging modality and stimulus type.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an fMRI study using visual alcohol-related cue videos and includes within-subject contrasts of craving-inducing cues vs control (mosaic) and aversion-inducing cues vs control. The authors report group-level, whole-brain random-effects GLM analyses (SPMs) with voxelwise FDR thresholds and region results, satisfying whole-brain task-evoked reporting. Methodological details required are provided: participant demographics (age/sex compared), cue type (alcohol), scanner field strength (1.5 T), and processing software (BrainVoyager BVQX 1.9). Therefore it meets inclusion criteria I3–I7 and does not violate exclusion criteria (no ROI-only, connectivity-only, or non-empirical restrictions).

studies.title: Altered neural responsivity to food cues in relation to food preferences, but not appetite-related hormone concentrations after RYGB-surgery.
studies.abstract: After Roux-en-Y gastric bypass (RYGB) surgery, patients report a shift in food preferences away from high-energy foods.

studies.title: Effect of bupropion treatment on brain activation induced by cigarette-related cues in smokers.
studies.abstract: Nicotine-dependent smokers exhibit craving and brain activation in the prefrontal and limbic regions when presented with cigarette-related cues. Bupropion hydrochloride treatment reduces cue-induced craving in cigarette smokers; however, the mechanism by which bupropion exerts this effect has not yet been described.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Abstract describes an empirical study of brain activation to cigarette-related cues in smokers (original data), but does not explicitly state that fMRI was used or that cues were visual. Insufficient detail to confirm fMRI + visual-stimuli criterion, so include for full-text review.

It includes within-participant contrasts of drug cues versus control (crave-allow vs neutral and crave-resist vs neutral) and reports group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses (FSL/FLAME with cluster thresholds), with results presented in figures and tables of coordinates. studies.title: Modeling Causal Relationship Between Brain Regions Within the Drug-Cue Processing Network in Chronic Cocaine Smokers.
studies.abstract: The cues associated with drugs of abuse have an essential role in perpetuating problematic use, yet effective connectivity or the causal interaction between brain regions mediating the processing of drug cues has not been defined. The aim of this fMRI study was to model the causal interaction between brain regions within the drug-cue processing network in chronic cocaine smokers and matched control participants during a cocaine-cue exposure task. Specifically, cocaine-smoking (15M; 5F) and healthy control (13M; 4F) participants viewed cocaine and neutral cues while in the scanner (a Siemens 3 T magnet). We examined whole brain activation, including activation related to drug-cue processing. Time series data extracted from ROIs determined through our General Linear Model (GLM) analysis and prior publications were used as input to IMaGES, a computationally powerful Bayesian search algorithm. During cocaine-cue exposure, cocaine users showed a particular feed-forward effective connectivity pattern between the ROIs of the drug-cue processing network (amygdala → hippocampus → dorsal striatum → insula → medial frontal cortex, dorsolateral prefrontal cortex, anterior cingulate cortex) that was not present when the controls viewed the cocaine cues. Cocaine craving ratings positively correlated with the strength of the causal influence of the insula on the dorsolateral prefrontal cortex in cocaine users. This study is the first demonstration of a causal interaction between ROIs within the drug-cue processing network in cocaine users. This study provides insight into the mechanism underlying continued substance use and has implications for monitoring treatment response.
studies.journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Human fMRI study presenting visual cocaine and neutral cues to chronic cocaine users and controls; original empirical data assessing drug cue-reactivity using visual stimuli, matching the meta-analysis objective.

Scanner field strength (3T), processing software (FSL), participant sex/age information, and cue type (cocaine visual stimuli) are reported. Therefore it satisfies inclusion criteria I3, I4, I5, I6, and I7; no exclusion criteria apply.

studies.title: Reduced striatal activation during reward anticipation due to appetite-provoking cues in chronic schizophrenia: a fMRI study.
studies.abstract: The occurrence of weight gain in schizophrenia (SZ) has profound clinical impact and interacts with antipsychotic medication, life style and disease severity. The functional neuroanatomy underlying altered nutritional behavior is unraveled, but dysregulated reward anticipation might be one of the involved neuronal mechanisms. The striatum, a core region of the reward network and salience attribution, was previously shown to regulate appetite perception and eating behavior. We studied patients suffering from chronic schizophrenia with a stable medication in comparison to age and gender matched healthy adults. Every subject had to undergo a 6h fasting period before a newly developed, appetite-provoking fMRI task was applied. Subjects saw visual stimuli of appetitive food items in a 3Tesla scanner. In healthy controls food images elicited stronger activation in the striatum compared to SZ patients. When adjusting a ROI-based striatal activation for medication and weight, the group difference remained still significant. This points an effect of illness independent of antipsychotic medication. These data underscore the involvement of the striatum into salience attribution, reward anticipation and the neuronal pathways leading to altered eating behavior and weight gain in schizophrenia.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Human fMRI study using visual appetitive food stimuli to assess reward-related cue-reactivity; original empirical data in English. Matches inclusion criteria for language/original data and fMRI with visual natural reward cues.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an empirical fMRI study using visual natural reward cues (appetitive food images) with a within-subject contrast of food>scrambled control stimuli. The paper reports a group-level, whole-sample one-sample t-test (whole-brain, voxelwise, FDR-corrected) demonstrating task-evoked activation, and also provides ROI and between-group analyses. Relevant methodological details are reported (cue type: food; MRI field strength: 3T; processing software: SPM5; participant groups and sex matching). Although the group comparison focused on an a-priori ROI, the presence of an unmasked, whole-brain task effect satisfies the whole-brain evidence requirement. Therefore the study meets all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis objective.
