Cluster size in terms of number of voxels, Z values for peak voxels along with corresponding MNI coordinates, anatomical localization, and Brodmann areas are provided.

studies.title: The effect of N-acetylcysteine and working memory training on neural mechanisms of working memory and cue reactivity in regular cocaine users.
studies.abstract: The current study investigated the combined effects of N-acetylcysteine and working memory (WM) training on behavioral and neural mechanisms of cue reactivity and WM in cocaine users in a randomized, double-blind design. Twenty-four of 38 cocaine-using men completed a 25-day treatment with either 2400 mg/day NAC or placebo. Both groups performed WM-training. During pre- and post-test lab-visits, neural mechanisms of cue reactivity and WM, and cue-induced craving and WM performance were assessed. Additionally, exploratory whole brain analyses were performed. Overall, the hypotheses were not confirmed, possibly due to small sample size, low WM-training adherence and/or ongoing substance use.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Abstract reports an English-language empirical study assessing neural mechanisms of cue reactivity in cocaine users but does not explicitly state that functional MRI was used or that visual stimuli were presented. Insufficient detail to confirm fMRI with visual cues—include for full-text review to verify I2.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This study is an fMRI investigation of cue reactivity in regular cocaine users, reporting a cue reactivity task and cue-induced craving measures, and explicitly states that exploratory whole-brain analyses were performed. Participant age range (18–55) and sex (male only), cue type (cocaine), and MRI scanner field strength (Philips 3.0T) are reported; imaging preprocessing and additional analysis details are referenced in the supplementary materials. The paper therefore includes within-participant cue>control analyses (cue reactivity contrasts) and reports whole-brain task-evoked results (including in supplementary materials), meeting the meta-analysis requirements. Some specifics (explicit statement that stimuli were visual and full processing software details) are provided or implied in the supplementary materials rather than the main text, so confidence is moderate.

studies.title: Ventral striatal activation during reward anticipation correlates with impulsivity in alcoholics.
studies.abstract: Alcohol dependence is often associated with impulsivity, which may be correlated with dysfunction of the brain reward system. We explored whether functional brain activation during anticipation of incentive stimuli is associated with impulsiveness in detoxified alcoholics and healthy control subjects.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Empirical fMRI study of reward anticipation in alcohol-dependent humans (meets I1). Abstract does not specify cue modality or confirm use of visual stimuli for reward cue-reactivity, so inclusion cannot be ruled out from abstract alone. Include for full-text review to determine whether visual cue-reactivity (I2) was used.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an empirical fMRI study using visual cues (geometric figures) in a monetary incentive delay task to probe anticipation of monetary reward and loss (natural reward-related cue-reactivity). The paper reports within-participant contrasts (gain cues > neutral cues; loss cues > neutral cues) and group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses using SPM5, with reported coordinates and cluster statistics; small-volume correction for the ventral striatum was applied but whole-brain results and coordinates are provided. Participant sex and age-matching, cue type (monetary), scanner field strength (1.5T), and processing software (SPM5) are reported. Therefore all inclusion criteria I3, I4, I5, I6, and I7 are satisfied and no exclusion criteria apply.

studies.title: Citicoline affects appetite and cortico-limbic responses to images of high-calorie foods.
studies.abstract: Cytidine-5'-diphosphocholine (citicoline) has a variety of cognitive enhancing, neuroprotective, and neuroregenerative properties. In cocaine-addicted individuals, citicoline has been shown to increase brain dopamine levels and reduce cravings. The effects of this compound on appetite, food cravings, and brain responses to food are unknown.
studies.journal: The International journal of eating disorders
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Abstract reports an original human fMRI study assessing brain responses to images of high-calorie foods and measures appetite/food cravings. This meets inclusion for empirical English-language fMRI research using visual cue-reactivity; it is not a review and does not rely solely on non-visual cues.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an empirical fMRI study using visual natural reward cues (high-calorie food images). It includes within-subject contrasts of high-calorie food images > non-food control images and reports pre-post and group-level analyses. In addition to ROI analyses, the paper reports exploratory whole-brain, group-level results (and coordinates/tables) for task contrasts and correlations (p<.001 whole-brain analyses and Table 1), fulfilling the requirement for whole-brain task-evoked statistical maps. Participant age and sex, cue type (food), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM99) are all reported. Therefore all inclusion criteria are met and no exclusion criteria apply.

studies.title: Extended-release naltrexone (XR-NTX) attenuates brain responses to alcohol cues in alcohol-dependent volunteers: a bold FMRI study.
studies.abstract: Oral naltrexone reduces heavy drinking, but is less consistent as an abstinence promoter, whereas once-monthly extended-release naltrexone (XR-NTX) also maintains abstinence. The present study sought to determine if alcohol cue reactivity is attenuated by XR-NTX. Twenty-eight detoxified alcohol-dependent adult male and female volunteers received a single i.m. injection of either XR-NTX or placebo under double-blind conditions. An fMRI/cue reactivity procedure was conducted immediately before and two weeks after injection. At baseline, alcohol-related visual and olfactory cues elicited significant increases in orbital and cingulate gyri, inferior frontal and middle frontal gyri. Subsequently, brain activation was significantly altered in XR-NTX-treated individuals. These affected brain regions are associated with the integration of emotion, cognition, reward, punishment, and learning/memory, suggesting that XR-NTX attenuates the salience of alcohol-related cues. Such an effect on brain function may interrupt the processes associated with "slips" and relapse, which may account for XR-NTX's ability to maintain abstinence.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: English-language empirical fMRI study in humans assessing alcohol (drug) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (also olfactory). Abstract reports original data and fMRI cue-reactivity with visual cues, meeting inclusion criteria; no exclusion criterion clearly met.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an empirical BOLD fMRI study of alcohol cue-reactivity that presents visual (and olfactory) alcohol-related stimuli and contrasts alcohol cues vs control stimuli. The analysis is whole-brain, voxelwise (FSL; cluster-level FWE correction reported) and reports group-level task activation results. Participant demographics (age, sex), cue type (alcohol), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (FSL) are reported. Therefore it meets all required inclusion criteria for visual-cue fMRI whole-brain task activation analyses.

analyses.name: Standard space coordinates, cluster size and significance of contrast to each condition.

studies.title: Acute effects of sublingual buprenorphine on brain responses to heroin-related cues in early-abstinent heroin addicts: an uncontrolled trial.
studies.abstract: Replacement therapy with buprenorphine is clinically effective in reducing withdrawal and craving for heroin during detoxification but not in decreasing the probability of relapse after detoxification. This study examined the acute effects of buprenorphine on brain responses to heroin-related cues to reveal the neurobiological and therapeutic mechanisms of addiction and relapse. Fifteen heroin addicts at a very early period of abstinence, were studied in two separate periods 10-15 min apart: an early period (5-45 min) and a later period (60-105 min) after sublingual buprenorphine, roughly covering the onset and peak of buprenorphine plasma level. During both periods, fMRI scanning with heroin-related visual stimuli were performed followed by questionnaires. Under effect of buprenorphine, brain responses to heroin-related cues showed decrease in amygdala, hippocampus, ventral tegmental area (VTA) and thalamus but no changes in ventral striatum and orbital-prefrontal-parietal cortices. As an uncontrolled trial, these preliminary results suggest that buprenorphine has specific brain targets in reducing withdrawal and craving during early abstinence, and that ventral striatum and orbital-prefrontal-parietal cortices may be the key targets in developing therapy for drug addiction and relapse.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Human fMRI study reporting original data in English using visual heroin-related cues in early-abstinent heroin users; meets inclusion criteria for drug cue-reactivity with visual stimuli.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: The study reports a functional MRI experiment using visual heroin-related cues in early-abstinent heroin users (meets I3). It includes within-participant contrasts of heroin-related vs neutral stimuli and group-level analyses (heroin-related > neutral; meets I4). Analyses were whole-brain, voxelwise random-effects SPM2 tests with reported MNI coordinates (meets I5 and I6). The paper reports participant age and sex, cue type (heroin), scanner field strength (1.5T), and processing software (SPM2) (meets I7). It is empirical fMRI task activation data and does not violate exclusion criteria (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, includes whole-brain task maps).

analyses.name: Brain activation from voxel-based whole brain analysis in the two scan periods
Comparison of brain activations from voxel-based whole brain analysis between the two scan periods

studies.title: Volitional reduction of anterior cingulate cortex activity produces decreased cue craving in smoking cessation: a preliminary real-time fMRI study.
studies.abstract: Numerous research groups are now using analysis of blood oxygen level-dependent (BOLD) functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) results and relaying back information about regional activity in their brains to participants in the scanner in 'real time'. In this study, we explored the feasibility of self-regulation of frontal cortical activation using real-time fMRI (rtfMRI) neurofeedback in nicotine-dependent cigarette smokers during exposure to smoking cues. Ten cigarette smokers were shown smoking-related visual cues in a 3 Tesla MRI scanner to induce their nicotine craving. Participants were instructed to modify their craving using rtfMRI feedback with two different approaches. In a 'reduce craving' paradigm, participants were instructed to 'reduce' their craving, and decrease the anterior cingulate cortex (ACC) activity. In a separate 'increase resistance' paradigm, participants were asked to increase their resistance to craving and to increase middle prefrontal cortex (mPFC) activity. We found that participants were able to significantly reduce the BOLD signal in the ACC during the 'reduce craving' task (P=0.028). There was a significant correlation between decreased ACC activation and reduced craving ratings during the 'reduce craving' session (P=0.011). In contrast, there was no modulation of the BOLD signal in mPFC during the 'increase resistance' session. These preliminary results suggest that some smokers may be able to use neurofeedback via rtfMRI to voluntarily regulate ACC activation and temporarily reduce smoking cue-induced craving. Further research is needed to determine the optimal parameters of neurofeedback rtfMRI, and whether it might eventually become a therapeutic tool for nicotine dependence.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Original English-language fMRI study in human nicotine-dependent smokers using smoking-related visual cues to elicit craving and measure BOLD responses; directly addresses visual cue-reactivity for drug reward. Not a review.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an empirical fMRI study using visual smoking-related cues in nicotine-dependent participants. It reports within-participant contrasts (smoke vs rest) during cue exposure and a group-level, whole-brain random-effects analysis (thresholded maps reported and shown in figures). Participant demographics (age, sex), cue type (nicotine/smoking), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM8; TBV for real-time) are reported. Although some ROI analyses are described, whole-brain task activation maps are presented and discussed, satisfying the meta-analysis requirements.

studies.title: Neurophysiological effects of modafinil on cue-exposure in cocaine dependence: a randomized placebo-controlled cross-over study using pharmacological fMRI.
studies.abstract: Enhanced reactivity to substance related cues is a central characteristic of addiction and has been associated with increased activity in motivation, attention, and memory related brain circuits and with a higher probability of relapse. Modafinil was promising in the first clinical trials in cocaine dependence, and was able to reduce craving in addictive disorders. However, its mechanism of action remains to be elucidated. In this functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) study therefore, cue reactivity in cocaine dependent patients was compared to cue reactivity in healthy controls (HCs) under modafinil and placebo conditions.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Abstract reports an English-language empirical fMRI study assessing cue-reactivity in cocaine-dependent patients and healthy controls, satisfying I1. The abstract does not specify the sensory modality of the cues (visual vs. other), so inclusion for full-text review is warranted to confirm I2.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an fMRI cue-reactivity study using visual cocaine-related stimuli with within-subject contrasts (Cocaine > Neutral) and group-level random-effects voxelwise analyses. The paper reports whole-brain exploratory results (p<.001 uncorrected) and ROI small-volume corrected results, provides coordinates and statistical values, and reports participant demographics (age/sex), cue type (cocaine), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM8). Thus it meets all required inclusion criteria for visual drug cue-reactivity fMRI studies and does not violate listed exclusions.

analyses.name: Effects of watching cocaine pictures vs. neutral pictures under modafinil and placebo: within group and between group comparisons.

studies.title: Effects of a GABA-ergic medication combination and initial alcohol withdrawal severity on cue-elicited brain activation among treatment-seeking alcoholics.
studies.abstract: Many studies have reported medication effects on alcohol cue-elicited brain activation or associations between such activation and subsequent drinking. However, few have combined the methodological rigor of a randomized clinical trial (RCT) with follow-up assessments to determine whether cue-elicited activation predicts relapse during treatment, the crux of alcoholism.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Abstract reports an empirical human study reporting cue-elicited brain activation in treatment-seeking alcoholics (RCT with follow-up), but does not specify the sensory modality of cues. fMRI is implied but cue modality (visual) is not explicitly stated — include for full-text review to confirm visual stimuli.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This study is an empirical fMRI investigation of alcohol cue reactivity using visual stimuli (alcohol vs non-alcohol beverage images). It reports within-subject contrasts (ALC > BEV), conducts group-level, voxelwise whole-brain second-level GLMs with cluster-corrected results and reports peak coordinates and figures, and provides relevant methodological details including participant demographics (age, sex), cue type (alcohol), scanner field strength (3T Philips Interra), and processing software (SPM8). All required inclusion criteria are met and no exclusion criteria apply.

studies.title: The brain activations for both cue-induced gaming urge and smoking craving among subjects comorbid with Internet gaming addiction and nicotine dependence.
studies.abstract: Internet gaming addiction (IGA) has been classified as an addictive disorder in the proposed DSM 5 draft. However, whether its underlying addiction mechanism is similar to other substance use disorders has not been confirmed. The present functional magnetic resonance images study is aimed at evaluating the brain correlates of cue-induced gaming urge or smoking craving in subjects with both IGA and nicotine dependence to make a simultaneous comparison of cue induced brain reactivity for gaming and smoking. For this purpose, 16 subjects with both IGA and nicotine dependence (comorbid group) and 16 controls were recruited from the community. All subjects were made to undergo 3-T fMRIs scans while viewing images associated with online games, smoking, and neutral images, which were arranged according to an event-related design. The resultant image data was analyzed with full factorial and conjunction analysis of SPM5. The results demonstrate that anterior cingulate, and parahippocampus activates higher for both cue-induced gaming urge and smoking craving among the comorbid group in comparison to the control group. The conjunction analysis demonstrates that bilateral parahippocampal gyrus activates to a greater degree for both gaming urge and smoking craving among the comorbid group in comparison to the control group. Accordingly, the study demonstrates that both IGA and nicotine dependence share similar mechanisms of cue-induced reactivity over the fronto-limbic network, particularly for the parahippocampus. The results support that the context representation provided by the parahippocampus is a key mechanism for not only cue-induced smoking craving, but also for cue-induced gaming urge.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: The abstract describes an original English-language fMRI study (3T) with human participants viewing images (visual stimuli) of online games, smoking, and neutral images to assess cue-induced craving/urge. It is not a review and does not use non-visual cues, satisfying inclusion criteria.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an event-related fMRI study using visual cues (gaming screenshots and smoking images) that reports within-participant contrasts (gaming>neutral and smoking>neutral) and group-level, voxelwise whole-brain analyses using SPM5 (3T scanner) with corrected thresholds and conjunction analysis. Participant sex (males) and age criteria are reported. Results are reported as whole-brain activations and described in the text/tables/figures. Therefore all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis are met, and no exclusion criteria apply.

analyses.name: The brain activation for gaming and smoking cue reactivity in the comorbid group.
Brain activations for gaming urge and smoking cue reactivity showing higher activations in the comorbida group compared to the control group.
Results for conjunction analysis for brain correlates of gaming urgea and those of smoking craving.b

studies.title: Enhanced orbitofrontal cortex function and lack of attentional bias to cocaine cues in recreational stimulant users.
studies.abstract: Although cocaine is known to be a highly addictive drug, there appears to be a select subset of individuals who are able to use the substance recreationally without developing dependence. These individuals do not report experiencing feelings of craving for cocaine, an important distinction from dependent users. However, no prior studies have compared attentional bias with cocaine cues between these groups to confirm this difference. Additionally, previous investigations into cognitive abilities in these individuals have been conflicting, and no research has been conducted on the neurobiological processes underlying cognitive functioning in this group.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Abstract reports an original empirical study on attentional bias to cocaine cues but does not specify that a functional MRI scan was used or that visual stimuli were employed for cue-reactivity. Insufficient information to confirm I2, so include for full-text review.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an fMRI study of cocaine cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (cocaine-related words) with a within-participant cocaine>neutral contrast. The paper reports group-level whole-brain (voxelwise) task activation analyses (FWE-corrected), describes significant clusters and between-group contrasts, and refers to supplementary materials for results. Scanner field strength (3T) and processing software (CamBA/SPM) are reported, and participant age/sex and cue type (cocaine) are provided. Thus it satisfies inclusion criteria I3, I4, I5, I6, and I7.

studies.title: Individual variability in the locus of prefrontal craving for nicotine: implications for brain stimulation studies and treatments.
studies.abstract: Attenuation of cue-elicited craving with brain stimulation techniques is a growing area of attention in addiction research. This investigation aims to guide these studies by assessing individual variability in the location of peak cortical activity during cue-elicited craving.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Abstract describes an empirical investigation of cue-elicited craving (nicotine) and cortical activity but does not explicitly state fMRI or that visual stimuli were used. Insufficient detail to determine imaging modality/stimulus type from abstract alone—include for full-text review to verify I2.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an empirical 3T BOLD fMRI study of nicotine cue-reactivity using visual smoking vs neutral pictures, reporting within-participant contrasts (smoking cues > neutral). The paper presents voxelwise, whole-brain group-level analyses (cluster-corrected results with coordinates reported for the mPFC and individual peak coordinates) and details preprocessing and SPM5 software. Participant age, sex, cue type, scanner field strength, and processing software are reported. It therefore satisfies inclusion criteria for an fMRI visual cue-reactivity whole-brain task activation study and does not meet any exclusion criteria (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, includes unmasked whole-brain task effects).

studies.title: Thirsty heroin addicts show different fMRI activations when exposed to water-related and drug-related cues.
studies.abstract: Relapse to drug use is frequently preceded or caused by craving, an intense desire for drug. Advances in functional brain imaging techniques make it possible to directly investigate this special mental state in vivo and non-invasively. Extant imaging studies on craving have been mostly on cocaine which is the dominant drug abused in the U.S. Employing functional MRI, we examined substance specificity of the neural circuitry underlying craving for heroin. Heroin is the primary drug abused in south-east Asia and has, particularly, become a serious social problem for China in recent years. Following abstinence from water and drug, 14 active heroin addicts (all male, mean age 33.2 years, average drug use history 7.1 years) underwent scanning inside a 1.5T Philips MR scanner during exposure to water-related, drug-related, and neutral cues. Water-related cues elicited anterior cingulate activation (Brodmann's area BA 32/24). Drug-related cues activated bilateral inferior frontal cortex (BA 44/45), confirming the critical role of prefrontal cortex in drug craving. Results suggest heroin craving may involve different neural substrates than do desire from basic physiological drives, such as thirst. As the first fMRI study of heroin craving, our study adds to the scant but much-needed brain imaging literature on heroin addiction.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: English-language empirical fMRI study with original data that scanned human heroin addicts while exposing them to visual cues (drug-related and water-related) to assess cue-reactivity. Fits the meta-analysis focus on drug and natural reward (water) visual cue-reactivity. No exclusion criteria apply.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an empirical fMRI study using visual cues (drug-related heroin pictures and water-related pictures) and reports within-participant contrasts (Drug > Neutral; Thirst > Neutral). Analyses were conducted as group-level, whole-brain voxelwise SPM2 second-level one-sample t-tests with voxel-wise thresholds and cluster extent; results are reported with Talairach coordinates and cluster information. Participant age/sex, cue types, scanner field strength (1.5T), and processing software (SPM2) are all reported. No exclusion criteria apply (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, includes whole-brain task effects). Therefore it meets all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

analyses.name: Summary information for regions of activation revealed in the statistical contrasts

studies.title: Abdominal fat is associated with a greater brain reward response to high-calorie food cues in Hispanic women.
studies.abstract: Exposure to high-calorie foods may promote overeating by stimulating brain reward pathways and appetite. Abdominal fat has particularly adverse metabolic consequences and may alter brain pathways that regulate feeding behavior. Functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) was used to test the hypothesis that high-calorie food cues activate brain reward regions and increase appetite, and to examine the relationship between abdominal fat and brain reward responsiveness in Hispanic women.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Empirical fMRI study in English using visual high-calorie food cues (natural reward) in human participants, directly assessing cue-reactivity. No exclusion criteria met.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an empirical fMRI study using visual food cues (natural reward) with within-subject contrasts of food>non-food and high-calorie>non-food reported. The authors report group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses (Z>2.3, p<0.05 whole-brain corrected) and describe cluster/coordinate results; they also provide ROI analyses but do not rely exclusively on them. Relevant participant information (age, sex), cue type (food), MRI field strength (3T), and processing software (FSL) are reported. No exclusion criteria are violated (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, not only between-group contrasts, and is empirical). Therefore the study meets all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis of fMRI visual cue-reactivity to natural rewards.

activation_tables.table_caption: Brain regions with greater response to high-calorie foods compared to non-food images.

analyses.name: T1

studies.title: The neurokinin-1 receptor antagonist aprepitant in co-morbid alcohol dependence and posttraumatic stress disorder: a human experimental study.
studies.abstract: Posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD) and alcoholism are frequently comorbid, suggesting the possibility of overlapping neural substrates. The neurokinin 1 (NK1) receptor for substance P (SP) has been implicated in both stress- and alcohol-related behaviors. The NK1 antagonist aprepitant, clinically available as a treatment for chemotherapy-induced nausea, offers a tool to probe a potential role of the SP/NK1 system in comorbid PTSD and alcoholism.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Abstract reports a human experimental pharmacological study of aprepitant in comorbid PTSD and alcohol dependence but does not mention any functional MRI or visual cue-reactivity. Information is insufficient to confirm the fMRI visual cue-reactivity requirement (I2), so include for full-text review. Study is English empirical original research (I1).
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an empirical fMRI study of alcohol cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (alcoholic vs non-alcoholic beverage pictures). The paper reports within-subject contrasts (alcoholic>non-alcoholic and negative>neutral), group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses with family-wise error correction (Monte Carlo), and reports scanner (3T) and processing software (AFNI), and participant age/sex. Whole-brain results are described in the text and figures. Therefore it meets all required inclusion criteria for a whole-brain visual cue-reactivity fMRI study of a drug-related cue.

studies.title: Neural mechanisms of high-risk decisions-to-drink in alcohol-dependent women.
studies.abstract: A hallmark of alcohol dependence (AD) is continually drinking despite the risk of negative consequences. Currently, it is not known if the pattern of disordered activation in AD is more compatible with an over-sensitive reward system, a deficit in control systems or a combination of both to produce the high risk-taking behavior observed in alcohol dependents (ADs). Here, alcohol cues were used in an ecological decisions-to-drink task that involved high- and low-risk scenarios where the chance of serious negative imagined consequences was varied. Non-alcohol cues were included as control stimuli. Functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) was used to measure blood oxygen level-dependent (BOLD) signal change in 15 alcohol-dependent and 16 control women. This design allowed us to address two major questions concerning AD: first, is there a specific pattern of disordered activation that drives the heightened endorsement of high-risk decisions-to-drink in ADs? And, second, is that pattern specific to decisions-to-drink or does it generalize to other appetitive and/or neutral cues? The results showed that, during high-risk decisions-to-drink, alcohol-dependent women activated reward circuits, cognitive control circuits and regions of the default-mode network (DMN), while control women deactivated approach circuits and showed enhanced activation in regions of the DMN. Group differences were found only for decisions-to-drink, suggesting that they are specific to alcohol cues. Simultaneous activation of reward networks, cognitive control networks and the DMN in alcohol-dependent women suggests that over-endorsement of high-risk drinking decisions by alcohol-dependent women may be due to a problem with switching between different neural networks.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Human fMRI study with original data examining alcohol (drug) cue-reactivity during a decisions-to-drink task. Abstract does not explicitly state the cue modality is visual, so inclusion for full-text review is warranted to confirm visual stimuli were used.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an fMRI task study using visual cues (pictures) of alcohol, food (natural reward control), and neutral items, with within-participant contrasts comparing alcohol versus control stimuli and high-risk versus low-risk conditions. Analyses were conducted at the group level using whole-brain voxelwise GLM in FSL (mixed-effects FLAME, cluster-corrected), and results are reported as whole-brain statistical maps and figures; participant age/sex (female, 18–28), cue types (alcohol, food, items), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (FSL) are reported. Therefore it satisfies all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis (I3–I7) and does not meet any exclusion criteria.

studies.title: An initial fMRI study on neural correlates of prayer in members of Alcoholics Anonymous.
studies.abstract: Many individuals with alcohol-use disorders who had experienced alcohol craving before joining Alcoholics Anonymous (AA) report little or no craving after becoming long-term members. Their use of AA prayers may contribute to this. Neural mechanisms underlying this process have not been delineated.
studies.journal: The American journal of drug and alcohol abuse
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Abstract reports an original fMRI study in humans (meets I1) but does not state that fMRI assessed drug or natural reward cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. Insufficient information in abstract to determine I2, so include for full-text review to verify whether visual cue-reactivity was used.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an fMRI study using visual alcohol cues (90 alcohol-related images) with within-subject contrasts including condition vs baseline (alcohol images > baseline) and condition-modulation contrasts (prayer > passive/news). Group-level, whole-brain random-effects analyses are reported with tables/coordinates and cluster-corrected results. Participant age/sex information, cue type (alcohol), scanner field strength (Siemens Allegra 3T), and preprocessing software (SPM8) are provided. Although the primary reported contrast tests prayer modulation rather than a neutral-image control, within-participant alcohol-cue > baseline contrasts are reported and whole-brain maps/coordinates are available, satisfying the meta-analysis requirements.

studies.title: Nucleus accumbens response to food cues predicts subsequent snack consumption in women and increased body mass index in those with reduced self-control.
studies.abstract: Individuals have difficulty controlling their food consumption, which is due in part to the ubiquity of tempting food cues in the environment. Individual differences in the propensity to attribute incentive (motivational) salience to and act on these cues may explain why some individuals eat more than others. Using fMRI in healthy women, we found that food cue related activity in the nucleus accumbens, a key brain region for food motivation and reward, was related to subsequent snack food consumption. However, both nucleus accumbens activation and snack food consumption were unrelated to self-reported hunger, or explicit wanting and liking for the snack. In contrast, food cue reactivity in the ventromedial prefrontal cortex was associated with subjective hunger/appetite, but not with consumption. Whilst the food cue reactivity in the nucleus accumbens that predicted snack consumption was not directly related to body mass index (BMI), it was associated with increased BMI in individuals reporting low self-control. Our findings reveal a neural substrate underpinning automatic environmental influences on consumption in humans and demonstrate how self-control interacts with this response to predict BMI. Our data provide support for theoretical models that advocate a 'dual hit' of increased incentive salience attribution to food cues and poor self-control in determining vulnerability to overeating and overweight.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Original fMRI study in humans reporting food cue reactivity (natural reward) and linking neural responses to consumption and BMI. Language and empirical data present. Abstract does not explicitly state modality of cues, so include for full-text review to confirm visual stimuli.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an empirical fMRI study using visual natural reward cues (food images) with within-subject contrasts of food vs. non-food images. The paper reports group-level whole-brain (voxelwise) analyses (noted in Methods and Supplementary material) in addition to ROI analyses. Relevant details are provided: participant age and sex (25 healthy females, 18–29), cue type (food), scanner field strength (3 T), and processing software (FSL/FEAT). Although key hypotheses used ROI analyses, whole-brain results are described and available in the supplement, satisfying the meta-analysis requirements.

studies.title: Transcranial magnetic stimulation of the dorsal lateral prefrontal cortex inhibits medial orbitofrontal activity in smokers.
studies.abstract: Several studies have shown that repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS), applied to the dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (DLPFC), can reduce cue-elicited craving in smokers. Currently, the mechanism of this effect is unknown. We used functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) to explore the effect of a single treatment of rTMS on cortical and sub-cortical neural activity in non-treatment seeking nicotine-dependent participants.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Abstract reports fMRI in nicotine-dependent smokers and references cue-elicited craving, but does not specify that the fMRI assessed cue-reactivity with visual stimuli. Insufficient detail to confirm I2, so include for full-text review to determine whether visual cue-reactivity was used.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an empirical fMRI study of nicotine (drug) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli in nicotine-dependent smokers. It reports within-participant contrasts of smoking cues vs neutral/control stimuli and pre/post comparisons, and uses group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses (2×2 factorial; maps thresholded and cluster coordinates reported). Relevant methodological details are provided (participant age/sex, cue type: nicotine, 3T scanner field strength, SPM8 processing). Whole-brain results are reported with coordinates and cluster statistics. The study therefore meets all required inclusion criteria and does not meet any exclusion criteria (it is not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, not non-empirical).

studies.title: Adjunctive aripiprazole therapy with escitalopram in patients with co-morbid major depressive disorder and alcohol dependence: clinical and neuroimaging evidence.
studies.abstract: The effective treatment of depression has been reported to reduce the severity of alcohol use, potentially reflecting improvements in common brain reward circuits. We hypothesized that augmentation therapy of escitalopram with aripiprazole would improve depressive symptoms as well as reduce craving for alcohol and cue-induced brain activity in patients with co-morbid alcohol dependence and major depressive disorder, compared with treatment with escitalopram alone. Thirty-five subjects with major depressive disorder and alcohol dependence were recruited and randomly assigned into 17 aripiprazole + escitalopram and 18 escitalopram only groups. At baseline and following six weeks of treatment, symptoms of depression, craving for alcohol and brain activity were evaluated. During the six week treatment period, Beck Depression Inventory and clinical global index-severity (CGI-S) scores decreased in both the aripiprazole + escitalopram and escitalopram only groups. In addition, following the treatment period, the Korean alcohol urge questionnaire scores in the aripiprazole + escitalopram group were reduced from 23.3±8.4 to 14.3±4.9, compared with those of the escitalopram group of from 21.6±8.4 to 19.3±7.1 (F=13.1, p<0.01). The activity within the anterior cingulate was increased in response to the presentation of alcohol drinking scenes following treatment in the aripiprazole + escitalopram group. The change of brain activity within the left anterior cingulate gyrus in all patients with co-morbid alcohol dependence and major depressive disorder was negatively correlated with the change in craving for alcohol. These findings suggest that the effects of aripiprazole on anterior cingulate cortex might mediate the successful treatment of alcohol dependence in patients with major depressive disorder.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: English-language empirical study presenting original data; neuroimaging assessed cue-induced brain activity to visual alcohol cues (alcohol drinking scenes) in human participants, matching fMRI cue-reactivity with visual stimuli.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This study reports a task-based fMRI alcohol cue-reactivity paradigm using visual (video) alcohol cues with a within-participant alcohol>control contrast, analyzed with whole-brain voxelwise GLM and random-effects analysis (FDR-corrected clusters reported and tabulated). The paper provides participant demographics, cue type (alcohol), scanner field strength (1.5T), and processing software (Brain Voyager). Results include group-level whole-brain activation maps/clusters with coordinates and statistics. No exclusion criteria are met (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, includes within-subject task effect). Therefore it meets all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

studies.title: Neural correlates of adherence to extended-release naltrexone pharmacotherapy in heroin dependence.
studies.abstract: Injectable extended-release naltrexone (XRNTX) presents an effective therapeutic strategy for opioid addiction, however its utility could be hampered by poor adherence. To gain a better insight into this phenomenon, we utilized blood oxygenation level-dependent functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) in conjunction with a validated cue-induced craving procedure to examine neural correlates of XRNTX adherence. We operationalized treatment adherence as the number of monthly XRNTX injections (range: 0-3) administered to a group of fully detoxified heroin-dependent subjects (n=32). Additional outcomes included urine toxicology screening and self-reported tobacco use. The presented heroin-related visual cues reliably elicited heroin craving in all tested subjects. Nine, five, three and 15 of the participants, respectively, received zero, one, two and three XRNTX injections, predicted by the individual baseline fMRI signal change in response to the cues in the medial prefrontal cortex, a brain region involved in inhibitory self-control and emotional appraisal. The incidence of opioid-positive urines during the XRNTX therapy was low and remained about half the pre-treatment rate after the XRNTX ended. During the treatment, cigarette smoking behaviors followed patterns of opioid use, while cocaine consumption was increased with reductions in opioid use. The present data support the hypothesis that medial prefrontal cortex functions are involved in adherence to opioid antagonist therapy. A potential role of concurrent non-opioid addictive substances consumption during the XRNTX pharmacotherapy warrants further investigation. Our findings set the stage for further bio-behavioral investigations of the mechanisms of relapse prevention in opioid dependence.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: English-language empirical fMRI study in humans (n=32) using visual heroin-related cues to assess cue-induced craving (BOLD fMRI). Original data; uses visual stimuli for drug cue-reactivity, no exclusion criteria met.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an fMRI study of heroin-related cue reactivity using visual stimuli, with within-subject drug cue > neutral contrast and group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses. The manuscript reports whole-brain voxelwise paired t-tests and whole-brain correlations (thresholded and cluster-corrected) and provides peak coordinates for significant clusters. Relevant participant demographics (age, sex), cue type (heroin visual images), MRI field strength (3T Siemens Tim Trio) and processing software (FSL) are reported. Therefore all required inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis are met and no exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, or non-empirical) apply.

studies.title: (Still) longing for food: insulin reactivity modulates response to food pictures.
studies.abstract: Overweight and obesity pose serious challenges to public health and are promoted by our food-rich environment. We used functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) to investigate reactivity to food cues after overnight fasting and following a standardized caloric intake (i.e., a 75 g oral glucose tolerance test, OGTT) in 26 participants (body mass index, BMI between 18.5 and 24.9 kg m(-2)). They viewed pictures of palatable food and low-level control stimuli in a block design and rated their current appetite after each block. Compared to control pictures, food pictures activated a large bilateral network typically involved in homeostatically and hedonically motivated food processing. Glucose ingestion was followed by decreased activation in the basal ganglia and paralimbic regions and increased activation in parietal and occipital regions. Plasma level increases in insulin correlated with cue-induced appetite at the neural and behavioral level. High insulin increases were associated with reduced activation in various bilateral regions including the fusiform gyrus, the superior temporal gyrus, the medial frontal gyrus, and the limbic system in the right hemisphere. In addition, they were accompanied by lower subjective appetite ratings following food pictures and modulated the neural response associated with it (e.g., in the fusiform gyrus). We conclude that individual insulin reactivity is critical to reduce food-cue responsivity after an initial energy intake and thereby may help to counteract overeating.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Original empirical fMRI study in English with human participants assessing cue-reactivity to visual food pictures (natural reward). Fits the meta-analysis focus on visual cue-reactivity; no exclusion criteria apply.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is a task-based fMRI study using visual food (natural reward) pictures with a within-subject contrast of food>control and pre/post glucose administration. Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses were performed (SPM5) and results are reported as statistical maps and coordinate tables/figures. Participant age, sex, cue type (food), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software are reported. The paper reports whole-brain task activation (not ROI-only, connectivity-only, or between-group-only), matching the meta-analysis objective of visual cue-reactivity to natural rewards.

studies.title: Brain reward-system activation in response to anticipation and consumption of palatable food is altered by glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor activation in humans.
studies.abstract: To test the hypothesis that food intake reduction after glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor activation is mediated through brain areas regulating anticipatory and consummatory food reward.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Abstract reports an English-language empirical human study on brain reward responses to palatable food with GLP-1 manipulation, indicating original data (satisfies I1). The abstract does not specify that functional MRI was used during visual cue-reactivity or whether visual cues (rather than gustatory/consummatory stimuli) were employed, so I2 cannot be confirmed from the abstract. Include for full-text review to determine whether fMRI with visual cue-reactivity was performed.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an empirical fMRI study of natural reward (food) cue-reactivity using visual cues signaling impending delivery (orange triangle/blue star) and analyses contrasting chocolate milk vs tasteless solution during anticipation and receipt. It reports within-subject contrasts (chocolate > tasteless) entered into second-level random-effects (voxelwise) whole-brain analyses with thresholding and small-volume correction; results are reported in tables/figures (Tables S2–S4, figures). Participant age/sex, cue type (food), MRI field strength (3.0T GE Signa HDxt), and processing software (SPM8) are provided. Therefore all required inclusion criteria are met and no exclusion criteria apply.

studies.title: Increased ventro-medial prefrontal activations in schizophrenia smokers during cigarette cravings.
studies.abstract: Highly prevalent in schizophrenia, tobacco smoking substantially increases the risk of cardiac-related death. Compared to the general population, tobacco smoking cessation rates are lower in schizophrenia. Unfortunately, the reasons for these low cessation rates remain poorly understood. Recently, it has been shown that tobacco cravings are increased in schizophrenia smokers compared to smokers with no comorbid psychiatric disorder. In view of these results, we sought to examine - for the first time - the neurophysiologic responses elicited by cigarette cues in schizophrenia smokers. We hypothesized that cigarettes cues would elicit increased activations in brain regions involved in drug cravings in schizophrenia smokers relative to control smokers.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: The abstract describes an original empirical study of cigarette cue-reactivity in schizophrenia smokers reporting brain activations, meeting I1. However it does not explicitly state that a functional MRI scan was used or that cues were visual. Include for full-text review to confirm fMRI and visual-stimulus eligibility (I2).
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an empirical fMRI study using visual smoking-related (cigarette) cues and matched neutral images, addressing cue-reactivity (I3). Within-participant Craving>Neutral contrasts are reported for each group and used in analyses (I4). The paper reports group-level, whole-brain random-effects voxelwise analyses with cluster-correction, coordinates, and t-values for Craving>Neutral and between-group contrasts (I5, I6). Methods include participant demographic information, cue type (nicotine/cigarette), scanner field strength (3.0T), and processing software (BrainVoyager QX), fulfilling I7. No exclusion criteria (e.g., ROI-only, connectivity-only, non-empirical) apply. Therefore the study meets all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

studies.title: Association between CHRNA5 genetic variation at rs16969968 and brain reactivity to smoking images in nicotine dependent women.
studies.abstract: Tobacco smoking is the leading preventable cause of death in the developed world. Identifying risk factors for smoking may lead to more effective treatments. Genome wide association studies revealed a relationship between development of nicotine dependence and a single-nucleotide polymorphism (SNP, rs16969968) of the nicotine acetylcholine receptor (nAChR) alpha-5 subunit gene (CHRNA5). The relationship between this SNP and other factors contributing to smoking behavior such as smoking cue reactivity is unclear.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Abstract reports an original empirical human study examining brain reactivity to smoking images (visual cues), relevant to cue-reactivity. However the abstract does not explicitly state that functional MRI was used, so include for full-text review to confirm fMRI methods.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an fMRI study of visual smoking cue-reactivity (smoking images vs. neutral) using within-subject contrasts (smoking>neutral). The paper reports group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses (random-effects GLM and mixed-effects ANCOVA) with cluster correction and lists regional results. Participant characteristics include sex (only women), cue type (nicotine/smoking images), MRI scanner field strength (Siemens Trio 3T), and processing software (BrainVoyager QX). Thus it satisfies the review’s requirements for task-based whole-brain activation maps to visual drug cues and reports relevant methodological/participant details. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only without task effects, or non-empirical) apply.

studies.title: Treatment with a nicotine vaccine does not lead to changes in brain activity during smoking cue exposure or a working memory task.
studies.abstract: To assess whether immunization attenuates nicotinic stimulation of the brain and elucidate brain and behavioural responses during exposure to smoking cues and a working memory task.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Abstract reports human brain activity during smoking cue exposure, suggesting a cue-reactivity fMRI study, but does not explicitly state use of fMRI or that cues were visual. Insufficient detail to confirm I2 from abstract alone; include for full-text review to verify imaging modality and stimulus modality.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an empirical fMRI study of nicotine (smoking) cue reactivity using visual stimuli. The paper reports within-participant contrasts of smoking cues > neutral cues, and a whole-brain random-effects (group-level, voxelwise) GLM was performed and reported (maps/figures described in text). Whole-brain results are presented (although some did not survive multiple-comparisons correction), satisfying the requirement that a group-level task-evoked whole-brain map be available. Methods report relevant details: cue type (smoking/nicotine), MRI field strength (3T), processing software (BrainVoyager QX), and participant demographics are indicated in Table 1 (all-male sample). No exclusion criteria (e.g., ROI-only, connectivity-only, non-empirical) apply. Therefore the study meets all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

studies.title: Abnormal function of the posterior cingulate cortex in heroin addicted users during resting-state and drug-cue stimulation task.
studies.abstract: Previous animal and neuroimaging studies have demonstrated that brain function in heroin addicted users is impaired. However, the posterior cingulate cortex (PCC) has not received much attention. The purpose of this study was to investigate whether chronic heroin use is associated with craving-related changes in the functional connectivity of the PCC of heroin addicted users.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Abstract describes an English-language fMRI study of heroin users with a drug-cue stimulation task (original data), but does not specify the cue modality (visual vs. other). Include for full-text review to confirm whether visual stimuli were used.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This study is an fMRI cue-reactivity experiment using visual heroin-related vs neutral images and reports within-subject contrasts (heroin > neutral). Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise task activation results are presented (tables/figures, P<0.001 uncorrected) with coordinates and a ROI derived from task activation; resting-state analyses are also reported but do not preclude inclusion because the task-based whole-brain map is present. Relevant participant information (age, sex), cue type (heroin), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM5) are reported. No exclusion criteria (e.g., ROI-only, resting-state-only, non-empirical) are met.

studies.title: Neural correlates of alcohol-approach bias in alcohol addiction: the spirit is willing but the flesh is weak for spirits.
studies.abstract: Behavioral studies have shown an alcohol-approach bias in alcohol-dependent patients: the automatic tendency to faster approach than avoid alcohol compared with neutral cues, which has been associated with craving and relapse. Although this is a well-studied psychological phenomenon, little is known about the brain processes underlying automatic action tendencies in addiction. We examined 20 alcohol-dependent patients and 17 healthy controls with functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI), while performing an implicit approach-avoidance task. Participants pushed and pulled pictorial cues of alcohol and soft-drink beverages, according to a content-irrelevant feature of the cue (landscape/portrait). The critical fMRI contrast regarding the alcohol-approach bias was defined as (approach alcohol>avoid alcohol)>(approach soft drink>avoid soft drink). This was reversed for the avoid-alcohol contrast: (avoid alcohol>approach alcohol)>(avoid soft drink>approach soft drink). In comparison with healthy controls, alcohol-dependent patients had stronger behavioral approach tendencies for alcohol cues than for soft-drink cues. In the approach, alcohol fMRI contrast patients showed larger blood-oxygen-level-dependent responses in the nucleus accumbens and medial prefrontal cortex, regions involved in reward and motivational processing. In alcohol-dependent patients, alcohol-craving scores were positively correlated with activity in the amygdala for the approach-alcohol contrast. The dorsolateral prefrontal cortex was not activated in the avoid-alcohol contrast in patients vs controls. Our data suggest that brain regions that have a key role in reward and motivation are associated with the automatic alcohol-approach bias in alcohol-dependent patients.
studies.journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology
studies.abstract_screening_reason: This is an English-language empirical fMRI study in human participants using visual pictorial cues of alcohol and soft drinks to assess alcohol-approach bias (cue-reactivity). It directly examines neural responses to drug-related visual cues, meeting the review objective. No exclusion criteria are met.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This study is an fMRI investigation of alcohol (drug) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli in alcohol-dependent patients and controls. It includes within-subject contrasts isolating alcohol-related task effects (approach alcohol>avoid alcohol and the interaction vs soft-drink cues), reports group-level whole-brain analyses (Supplementary Table S1), and provides participant age/sex, cue type, 3T scanner field strength, and SPM8 processing details. Thus it satisfies inclusion criteria I3, I4, I5, I6, and I7. No exclusion criteria are met.

studies.title: Incentive-elicited striatal activation in adolescent children of alcoholics.
studies.abstract: Deficient recruitment of motivational circuitry by non-drug rewards has been postulated as a pre-morbid risk factor for substance dependence (SD). We tested whether parental alcoholism, which confers risk of SD, is correlated with altered recruitment of ventral striatum (VS) by non-drug rewards in adolescence.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Study reports fMRI in human adolescents assessing ventral striatum responses to non-drug (natural) rewards, relevant to the review. The abstract does not specify whether reward cues were visual, so full-text review is needed to confirm use of visual stimuli.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an empirical fMRI study using visual anticipation cues (monetary incentive delay task) to probe reward-related cue-reactivity. It includes within-participant contrasts of reward-cue versus non-incentive control, reports group-level whole-brain voxelwise statistical maps (with familywise correction and voxelwise t-tests), and provides necessary methodological details (participant ages/sex, cue type: monetary reward, 3T scanner, AFNI processing). The reported results include voxel coordinates and figures, satisfying reporting requirements. Although participants are adolescents and the reward is monetary (not drug images), the study assesses natural reward-related visual cue-reactivity and thus meets the meta-analysis inclusion criteria.

studies.title: Approach-bias predicts development of cannabis problem severity in heavy cannabis users: results from a prospective FMRI study.
studies.abstract: A potentially powerful predictor for the course of drug (ab)use is the approach-bias, that is, the pre-reflective tendency to approach rather than avoid drug-related stimuli. Here we investigated the neural underpinnings of cannabis approach and avoidance tendencies. By elucidating the predictive power of neural approach-bias activations for future cannabis use and problem severity, we aimed at identifying new intervention targets. Using functional Magnetic Resonance Imaging (fMRI), neural approach-bias activations were measured with a Stimulus Response Compatibility task (SRC) and compared between 33 heavy cannabis users and 36 matched controls. In addition, associations were examined between approach-bias activations and cannabis use and problem severity at baseline and at six-month follow-up. Approach-bias activations did not differ between heavy cannabis users and controls. However, within the group of heavy cannabis users, a positive relation was observed between total lifetime cannabis use and approach-bias activations in various fronto-limbic areas. Moreover, approach-bias activations in the dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (DLPFC) and anterior cingulate cortex (ACC) independently predicted cannabis problem severity after six months over and beyond session-induced subjective measures of craving. Higher DLPFC/ACC activity during cannabis approach trials, but lower activity during cannabis avoidance trials were associated with decreases in cannabis problem severity. These findings suggest that cannabis users with deficient control over cannabis action tendencies are more likely to develop cannabis related problems. Moreover, the balance between cannabis approach and avoidance responses in the DLPFC and ACC may help identify individuals at-risk for cannabis use disorders and may be new targets for prevention and treatment.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: This is an English-language empirical fMRI study in human participants measuring neural responses to cannabis-related approach/avoidance cues using a Stimulus Response Compatibility task (visual stimuli). It directly assesses drug cue-reactivity and predicts future cannabis problem severity, meeting inclusion criteria.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an empirical fMRI study using visual cannabis-related cues (vs matched control images) during a Stimulus Response Compatibility task. The primary within-subject contrast (approach-cannabis & avoid-control > avoid-cannabis & approach-control) isolates cannabis cue-related approach bias relative to control stimuli. Analyses were conducted at the group level using whole-brain, voxelwise methods (FLAME, cluster-corrected Z>2.3, p<.05) and reported as activation clusters/figures and discussed with localization atlases. Participant age/sex, cue type (cannabis), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (FSL/FEAT/FLAME) are all reported. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only, or non-empirical) apply. Therefore the study meets all required inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

studies.title: Selective reduction in neural responses to high calorie foods following gastric bypass surgery.
studies.abstract: To investigate changes in neural activation and desire to eat in response to appetitive cues from pre- to postbariatric surgery for obesity.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Abstract reports original human data on neural activation to appetitive (food) cues before and after bariatric surgery, but does not explicitly state use of functional MRI nor that cues were visual. Insufficient information to confirm I2; include for full-text review.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an empirical fMRI study of natural reward (food) cue-reactivity that used visual stimuli (in conjunction with auditory cues). It reports within-participant contrasts of food cues versus neutral nonfood stimuli (high-ED and low-ED foods > nonfood), and group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses conducted in SPM5 with reported coordinates, statistical maps, cluster thresholds, and figures. Participant age and sex, cue type (food), scanner field strength (1.5T), and processing software are all reported. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only contrasts, or non-empirical article) apply.

studies.title: Oral methylphenidate normalizes cingulate activity in cocaine addiction during a salient cognitive task.
studies.abstract: Anterior cingulate cortex (ACC) hypoactivations during cognitive demand are a hallmark deficit in drug addiction. Methylphenidate (MPH) normalizes cortical function, enhancing task salience and improving associated cognitive abilities, in other frontal lobe pathologies; however, in clinical trials, MPH did not improve treatment outcome in cocaine addiction. We hypothesized that oral MPH will attenuate ACC hypoactivations and improve associated performance during a salient cognitive task in individuals with cocaine-use disorders (CUD). In the current functional MRI study, we used a rewarded drug cue-reactivity task previously shown to be associated with hypoactivations in both major ACC subdivisions (implicated in default brain function) in CUD compared with healthy controls. The task was performed by 13 CUD and 14 matched healthy controls on 2 d: after ingesting a single dose of oral MPH (20 mg) or placebo (lactose) in a counterbalanced fashion. Results show that oral MPH increased responses to this salient cognitive task in both major ACC subdivisions (including the caudal-dorsal ACC and rostroventromedial ACC extending to the medial orbitofrontal cortex) in the CUD. These functional MRI results were associated with reduced errors of commission (a common impulsivity measure) and improved task accuracy, especially during the drug (vs. neutral) cue-reactivity condition in all subjects. The clinical application of such MPH-induced brain-behavior enhancements remains to be tested.
studies.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Empirical fMRI study in humans examining drug cue-reactivity (drug vs. neutral) during a rewarded cue-reactivity task with original data. Although the abstract does not explicitly state the cue sensory modality, the task assesses drug-related visual cue-reactivity commonly used; include for full-text review to confirm stimulus modality.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an fMRI study using visual drug-related stimuli (drug words) vs matched neutral words (a rewarded drug cue-reactivity task), with within-subject contrasts (drug>neutral) and placebo vs MPH conditions. The paper reports group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses (cluster-level corrected results and tables of coordinates and figures) and provides relevant participant information (age, sex/gender composition), cue type (cocaine words), MRI field strength (4T), and processing software (SPM2). Reporting includes whole-brain statistical maps summarized in tables/figures and ROI follow-ups; therefore all inclusion criteria are met and no exclusion criteria are triggered.

studies.title: fMRI BOLD response in high-risk college students (Part 1): during exposure to alcohol, marijuana, polydrug and emotional picture cues.
studies.abstract: This functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) study examined reactivity to alcohol, polydrug, marijuana and emotional picture cues in students who were referred to a college alcohol and drug assistance program.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Abstract reports an English-language empirical fMRI study in human college students exposed to alcohol, marijuana, polydrug and emotional picture cues (visual stimuli), directly addressing cue-reactivity. No exclusion criteria apply.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an empirical fMRI study using visual picture cues (alcohol, marijuana, polydrug, emotional) in a within-subject design comparing drug-related cues > neutral controls. Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses were performed using FSL's OLS mixed-effects model and results are reported in tables and figures (whole-brain activations described in text and shown in figures). Relevant details are reported: participant age and sex, cue types, scanner field strength (Siemens Allegra 3T), and processing software (FSL). Thus all inclusion criteria are met and no exclusion criteria apply.

studies.title: The effects of exercise on the neuronal response to food cues.
studies.abstract: Increased physical activity is associated with successful long-term weight loss maintenance due to mechanisms likely more complex than simply increased energy expenditure. The impact of physical activity on the central regulation of food intake may be an important mechanism of this effect. The objective of this study was to examine the effects of exercise training and acute exercise on the neuronal response to food cues as well as eating behaviors. fMRI was performed in the fasted state at baseline and again after a 6 month progressive exercise intervention (supervised, 5 days/wk) both with and without an acute exercise bout in 12 overweight/obese (5 women, 7 men; BMI 33 ± 4 kg/m(2)) healthy adults. fMRI data were acquired while subjects were presented with visual stimuli of foods of high hedonic value as compared to neutral control objects. Questionnaires on eating behaviors, ratings of appeal and desire for foods, and ratings of appetite (hunger, satiety, prospective intake) using visual analog scales were also performed at baseline and again after the 6-month exercise intervention. While only a trend was observed for a reduction in body weight (102 ± 5 to 99 ± 6 kg, p=0.09), a significant reduction in fat mass was observed (36.4 ± 2.8 to 33.7 ± 3.2 kg, p=0.04), although as expected changes in fat mass were variable (-10.0 to +3.7 kg). Chronic exercise was associated with a reduction in the neuronal response to food, primarily in the posterior attention network and insula. A significant positive correlation between the change in fat/body mass and the change in insula response to food cues with chronic exercise was observed. An acute exercise bout attenuated the effects of chronic exercise. The exercise intervention, however, did not impact any of the measures of appetitive behavior. In summary, despite no effects on behavioral measures of appetite, chronic exercise training was associated with attenuation in the response to visual food cues in brain regions known to be important in food intake regulation. The insula, in particular, appears to play an important role in the potential exercise-induced weight loss and weight loss maintenance.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Original human fMRI study presenting visual food cues (high-hedonic vs neutral) assessing neuronal response to natural reward (food). Fits the meta-analysis objective of visual cue-reactivity; no exclusion criteria met.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an empirical fMRI study using visual food cues (natural reward) with within-subject contrasts of hedonic food cues > nonfood objects. Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses were performed using SPM8 with a whole-brain cluster-corrected threshold and reported MNI coordinates/figures. Relevant participant demographics (age, sex), cue type (food), scanner field strength (3.0T), and processing software (SPM8) are reported. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only contrasts, or non-empirical paper) are met.

studies.title: Increased mesolimbic cue-reactivity in carriers of the mu-opioid-receptor gene OPRM1 A118G polymorphism predicts drinking outcome: a functional imaging study in alcohol dependent subjects.
studies.abstract: The endogenous opioid system is involved in the pathophysiology of alcohol-use disorders. Genetic variants of the opioid system alter neural and behavioral responses to alcohol. In particular, a single nucleotide polymorphism rs1799971 (A118G) in the mu-opioid receptor gene (OPRM1) is suggested to modulate alcohol-related phenotypes and neural response in the mesocorticolimbic dopaminergic system. Little is known about the clinical implications of these changes. The current study investigated the relationship of genotype effects on subjective and neural responses to alcohol cues and relapse in a sample of abstinent alcohol-dependent patients. Functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) was used to investigate alcohol cue-reactivity and drinking outcome of 81 abstinent alcohol-dependent patients. G-allele carriers displayed increased fMRI cue-reactivity in the left dorsal striatum and bilateral insulae. Neural responses to alcohol cues in these brain regions correlated positively with subjective craving for alcohol and positive expectations of alcohol׳s effects. Moreover, alcohol cue-reactivity in the left dorsal striatum predicted time to first severe relapse. Current results show that alcohol-dependent G-allele carriers׳ increased cue-reactivity is associated with an increased relapse risk. This suggests that genotype effects on cue-reactivity might link the OPRM1 A118G risk allele with an increased relapse risk that was reported in earlier studies. From a clinical perspective, risk-allele carriers might benefit from treatments, such as neuro-feedback or extinction-based therapy that are suggested to reduce mesolimbic reactivity.
studies.journal: European neuropsychopharmacology : the journal of the European College of Neuropsychopharmacology
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Human fMRI study reporting alcohol cue-reactivity in abstinent alcohol-dependent patients (original empirical data, English). The abstract does not specify whether cues were visual, so include for full-text review to confirm I2.

Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses are reported (cluster-corrected maps via Monte Carlo/AlphaSim) with results described in the text and figures/tables. Relevant participant information (age range, sex representation), cue type (alcohol), MRI field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM5/SPM8) are clearly reported. The study does not rely solely on ROI, connectivity-only, or between-group-only contrasts. Therefore it meets all review inclusion criteria.

studies.title: Neuronal correlates of appetite regulation in patients with schizophrenia: is there a basis for future appetite dysfunction?
studies.abstract: Given the undesired metabolic side effects of atypical antipsychotic medication it is important to understand the neuronal basis related to processing of appetite regulation in patients affected by schizophrenia.
studies.journal: European psychiatry : the journal of the Association of European Psychiatrists
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Abstract does not specify that an fMRI was conducted during visual drug/natural reward cue-reactivity. Appears to be an empirical English study on appetite in schizophrenia but insufficient detail to confirm I2; include for full-text review.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an empirical fMRI study using visual natural reward (food) cues with a within-subject contrast of appetitive > neutral stimuli. Group-level, whole-brain (voxelwise) random-effects GLM analyses and activation maps were reported (cluster thresholds and brain regions described). The paper reports participant sex and age matching, stimulus type (food images), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (Brain Voyager). Results are not limited to ROI, connectivity-only, or between-group-only contrasts. Therefore all inclusion criteria for cue-reactivity fMRI studies using visual natural rewards are met.

studies.title: Fasting levels of ghrelin covary with the brain response to food pictures.
studies.abstract: Ghrelin figures prominently in the regulation of appetite in normal-weighed individuals. The apparent failure of this mechanism in eating disorders and the connection to addictive behavior in general demand a deeper understanding of the endogenous central-nervous processes related to ghrelin. Thus, we investigated processing of pictures showing palatable food after overnight fasting and following a standardized caloric intake (i.e. a 75-g oral glucose tolerance test) using functional magnetic resonance imaging and correlated it with blood plasma levels of ghrelin. Twenty-six healthy female and male volunteers viewed food and control pictures in a block design and rated their appetite after each block. Fasting levels of ghrelin correlated positively with food-cue reactivity in a bilateral network of visual processing-, reward- and taste-related regions, including limbic and paralimbic regions. Notably, among those regions were the hypothalamus and the midbrain where ghrelin receptors are densely concentrated. In addition, high fasting ghrelin levels were associated with stronger increases of subjective appetite during the food-cue-reactivity task. In conclusion, brain activation and subjective appetite ratings suggest that ghrelin elevates the hedonic effects of food pictures. Thereby, fasting ghrelin levels may generally enhance subjective craving when confronted with reward cues.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Original empirical fMRI study in humans (healthy volunteers) assessing brain responses to visual food cues (palatable food pictures), directly addressing natural reward cue-reactivity; English language and not a review. Fits the meta-analysis objective of visual cue-reactivity for natural rewards.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This study is an fMRI investigation of natural reward (food) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (food pictures). It reports within-subject contrasts of food > control pictures, performs group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses (SPM5; second-level repeated-measures analysis) and provides coordinate tables and thresholded images. Participant age, sex, cue type (food), scanner field strength (3T) and processing software are reported. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only, or non-empirical) apply. Therefore it meets all required inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

studies.title: Obesity-associated melanocortin-4 receptor mutations are associated with changes in the brain response to food cues.
studies.abstract: Mutations in the melanocortin-4 receptor (MC4R) represent the commonest genetic form of obesity and are associated with hyperphagia.
authors: Elisabeth A H von dem Hagen
