studies.title: Reproducibility assessment of brain responses to visual food stimuli in adults with overweight and obesity.
studies.abstract: The brain's reward system influences ingestive behavior and subsequently obesity risk. Functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) is a common method for investigating brain reward function. This study sought to assess the reproducibility of fasting-state brain responses to visual food stimuli using BOLD fMRI.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: The abstract reports original empirical human BOLD fMRI data assessing fasting-state brain responses to visual food stimuli (natural reward cues). This directly meets the review’s focus on fMRI cue-reactivity to visual natural rewards.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an empirical fMRI study of natural reward (food) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli in adults with overweight/obesity. The authors report within-participant food>nonfood BOLD contrasts, present group-level voxelwise (whole-brain) statistical maps/voxel-wise subtraction figures and MNI coordinates, and provide participant age/sex, cue type (food), scanner field strength (3.0T), and processing software (AFNI). Therefore it satisfies inclusion criteria I3, I4, I5, I6, and I7 and does not meet any exclusion criteria (e.g., ROI-only or connectivity-only reporting).

studies.title: Nicotine dependence is characterized by disordered reward processing in a network driving motivation.
studies.abstract: Drug addiction is characterized by an unhealthy priority for drug consumption with a compulsive, uncontrolled drug-intake pattern due to a disordered motivational system. However, only some individuals become addicted, whereas others maintain regular but controlled drug use. Whether the transition occurs might depend on how individuals process drug relative to nondrug reward.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Abstract describes an empirical study of reward processing in nicotine dependence but does not specify use of fMRI or visual cue stimuli. Insufficient information to determine I2 from abstract, so include for full-text review to confirm methods.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an event-related fMRI study that used visual reward-predicting cues (money and cigarette images) and measured cue-elicited anticipatory activity and trial-by-trial behavioral responses. Within-subject contrasts (parametric increase in reward level; reward vs. 0-level) were modeled for both cigarette and monetary cues. Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses were conducted using SPM5 (3T scanner) with reported thresholds and anatomical coordinates. Participant characteristics (sample sizes, age comparability, smoking status) and cue type (nicotine) are reported. Thus the paper meets all specified inclusion criteria for a whole-brain visual cue-reactivity fMRI study and does not violate any exclusion criteria.

studies.title: Spontaneous action representation in smokers when watching movie characters smoke.
studies.abstract: Do smokers simulate smoking when they see someone else smoke? For regular smokers, smoking is such a highly practiced motor skill that it often occurs automatically, without conscious awareness. Research on the brain basis of action observation has delineated a frontoparietal network that is commonly recruited when people observe, plan, or imitate actions. Here, we investigated whether this action observation network would be preferentially recruited in smokers when viewing complex smoking cues, such as those occurring in motion pictures. Seventeen right-handed smokers and 17 nonsmokers watched a popular movie while undergoing functional magnetic resonance imaging. Using a natural stimulus, such as a movie, allowed us to keep both smoking and nonsmoking participants naive to the goals of the experiment. Brain activity evoked by movie scenes of smoking was contrasted with nonsmoking control scenes that were matched for frequency and duration. Compared with nonsmokers, smokers showed greater activity in left anterior intraparietal sulcus and inferior frontal gyrus, regions involved in the simulation of contralateral hand-based gestures, when viewing smoking versus control scenes. These results demonstrate that smokers spontaneously represent the action of smoking when viewing others smoke, the consequence of which may make it more difficult to abstain from smoking.
studies.journal: The Journal of neuroscience : the official journal of the Society for Neuroscience
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Empirical fMRI study in English with human participants who viewed visual smoking cues (movie scenes) while undergoing fMRI; directly assesses drug-related cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. Meets inclusion criteria I1 and I2.

It includes within-participant contrasts of smoking>control scenes, reports group-level whole-brain random-effects analyses with voxelwise maps and cluster coordinates (Table 1, Figures), and provides participant age/sex, cue type (nicotine), scanner field strength (3.0T), and processing software (SPM8). No exclusion criteria apply (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, includes within-subject task effects).

studies.title: Circulating glucose levels modulate neural control of desire for high-calorie foods in humans.
studies.abstract: Obesity is a worldwide epidemic resulting in part from the ubiquity of high-calorie foods and food images. Whether obese and nonobese individuals regulate their desire to consume high-calorie foods differently is not clear. We set out to investigate the hypothesis that circulating levels of glucose, the primary fuel source for the brain, influence brain regions that regulate the motivation to consume high-calorie foods. Using functional MRI (fMRI) combined with a stepped hyperinsulinemic euglycemic-hypoglycemic clamp and behavioral measures of interest in food, we have shown here that mild hypoglycemia preferentially activates limbic-striatal brain regions in response to food cues to produce a greater desire for high-calorie foods. In contrast, euglycemia preferentially activated the medial prefrontal cortex and resulted in less interest in food stimuli. Indeed, higher circulating glucose levels predicted greater medial prefrontal cortex activation, and this response was absent in obese subjects. These findings demonstrate that circulating glucose modulates neural stimulatory and inhibitory control over food motivation and suggest that this glucose-linked restraining influence is lost in obesity. Strategies that temper postprandial reductions in glucose levels might reduce the risk of overeating, particularly in environments inundated with visual cues of high-calorie foods.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Human fMRI study presenting visual food cues assessing neural and behavioral cue-reactivity to high-calorie (natural reward) stimuli; original empirical data in English. Fits meta-analysis of fMRI visual cue-reactivity for natural rewards.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an empirical fMRI study using visual food cues (natural reward) with within-subject contrasts of food images versus non-food control images and condition×task effects (including high-calorie food > control). Analyses were performed at the group level using whole-brain, voxelwise statistics (AFNI LME, FWE correction) and results are presented as whole-brain maps/figures. Participant age/sex, cue type (food), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (BioImageSuite/AFNI) are all reported. Thus it meets all inclusion criteria for visual-reward cue-reactivity fMRI and does not meet any exclusion criteria.

studies.title: An acute psychosocial stress enhances the neural response to smoking cues.
studies.abstract: Stress plays an important role in drug addiction. It can trigger relapse in abstinent addicts, and both in the everyday world and in the laboratory, a stressor can induce drug craving. Drug cues, such as the sight of drug, can also trigger subjective craving and relapse, and this effect may be amplified by stress. Underpinning this interaction may be the fact that stress and reward-predicting drug cues act on overlapping brain regions. We exposed 15 smokers undergoing functional magnetic resonance imaging to a psychosocial stressor, the Montreal Imaging Stress Task, followed by drug cues consisting of video clips of smokers. In a separate session similar video clips were shown after a non-stress control task. We observed significantly decreased neural activity during stress in the hippocampus, amygdala, bed nucleus of the stria terminalis, hypothalamus and nucleus accumbens. Following stress there was an increased neural response to drug cues in the medial prefrontal cortex, posterior cingulate cortex, dorsomedial thalamus, medial temporal lobe, caudate nucleus, and primary and association visual areas. These regions are thought to be involved in visual attention and in assigning incentive value to cues. Stress-induced limbic deactivation predicted subsequent neural cue-reactivity. We suggest that stress increases the incentive salience of drug cues.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: English-language empirical study reporting original data using fMRI in human smokers. Cue-reactivity was assessed with visual stimuli (video clips of smokers), matching the meta-analysis objective of fMRI studies of drug-related cue-reactivity using visual stimuli.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an fMRI study of nicotine (smoking) cue-reactivity using visual video stimuli in a within-subject design. The authors report within-participant contrasts of smoking videos > neutral (haircut) videos and present group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses (corrected p-values and reported activated regions; coordinates/peaks described). Methods report participant age range and sex breakdown (15 smokers, 7 females, ages 18–40), cue type (nicotine), MRI field strength (3 T), and processing software (fmristat, ANIMAL). The paper therefore meets all required inclusion criteria for visual cue fMRI whole-brain task activation and provides the necessary methodological details. No exclusion criteria are met (not ROI-only, not connectivity/resting-only, not non-empirical).

studies.title: The personality trait self-directedness predicts the amygdala's reaction to appetizing cues in fMRI.
studies.abstract: Personality and neural response to food cues in various mesolimbic brain structures have been linked to eating disorders. We investigated the question of whether personality traits in healthy individuals correlate with the brain activation induced on confrontation with appetizing visual stimuli. Personality was assessed in 27 normal-weight participants (14 women, mean age=26.0, SD=3.3 years) with the Temperament and Character Inventory (TCI). After an overnight fast, participants viewed blocks of pictures, half containing appetizing food and the other half showing scrambled pictures as control. After each block, participants rated their appetite. Brain activation was measured using a 3T MR scanner. Food compared to control stimuli elicited a significantly higher appetite rating, as well as strong activation in the ventral and dorsal visual stream, the fusiform gyrus and consecutive limbic centres such as the parahippocampal gyrus, the amygdala, the thalamus, the insula, the ventral striatum and the orbitofrontal cortex. In a region-of-interest analysis, the TCI trait self-directedness was negatively correlated with mean blood oxygenation level dependent (BOLD) signal change in the right amygdala (r=-.43, p=.025). Ultimately, amygdala reactivity might provide a risk factor for the development of eating disorders.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Study reports original empirical data in English, uses fMRI in human participants, and assesses cue-reactivity to visual appetizing food stimuli (a natural reward). No exclusion criteria apply based on the abstract.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an empirical fMRI study of natural reward cue-reactivity using visual food stimuli in healthy fasting adults (n=27). The study includes a within-subject contrast of food>scrambled control images and reports group-level, whole-brain task-evoked activations (food vs control) with voxelwise results and coordinates. Relevant methodological details are provided (age, sex distribution, cue type: food, scanner field strength: 3T, processing software: SPM5). Although the primary personality correlation used small-volume ROI correction, the paper nevertheless reports whole-brain main effects and exploratory whole-brain analyses, satisfying the whole-brain evidence requirement. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only, or non-empirical) apply.

studies.title: Reduced plasma ghrelin concentrations are associated with decreased brain reactivity to food cues after laparoscopic sleeve gastrectomy.
studies.abstract: The "hunger" hormone ghrelin regulates food-intake and preference for high-calorie (HC) food through modulation of the mesocortico-limbic dopaminergic pathway. Laparoscopic sleeve gastrectomy (LSG) is an effective bariatric surgery to treat morbid obesity. We tested the hypothesis that LSG-induced reductions in appetite and total ghrelin levels in blood are associated with reduced prefrontal brain reactivity to food cues. A functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) cue-reactivity task with HC and low-calorie (LC) food pictures was used to investigate brain reactivity in 22 obese participants tested before and one month after bariatric surgery (BS). Nineteen obese controls (Ctr) without surgery were also tested at baseline and one-month later. LSG significantly decreased (1) fasting plasma concentrations of total ghrelin, leptin and insulin, (2) craving for HC food, and (3) brain activation in the right dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (DLPFC) in response to HC vs. LC food cues (P
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Human fMRI study using visual food cues (high-calorie vs low-calorie) assessing cue-reactivity; original empirical research in English. Fits meta-analysis of visual drug/natural reward cue-reactivity (food = natural reward).
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an empirical fMRI study using visual food cues (high-calorie vs low-calorie images) to probe natural reward-related cue-reactivity. It includes within-participant contrasts (HC vs LC food cues) and reports group-level whole-brain, voxelwise analyses (second-level repeated-measures ANOVAs) with cluster-level FWE correction; results and coordinates are presented (including Supplementary Table 3). Methods report participant age/sex matching, cue type (food), MRI scanner (3 T), and processing software (SPM12). Thus it satisfies all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis and does not meet any exclusion criteria.

studies.title: Neural evidence for emotional involvement in pathological alcohol craving.
studies.abstract: Reducing craving is a key to success in the treatment of alcohol dependence. The emotion circuit may be involved in pathological craving for alcohol. In this study, we investigated neural correlates of emotional involvement in craving in alcohol dependence.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Abstract describes an original empirical study investigating neural correlates of alcohol craving (satisfies I1) but does not specify that functional MRI was used or that visual cues were presented. Insufficient information to confirm I2, so include for full-text review to determine fMRI and visual-stimulus use.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an empirical fMRI study using visual alcohol-related cues (pictures) and non-alcoholic beverage control pictures. It reports within-participant contrasts (alcohol > non-alcoholic beverage) and other stimulus contrasts (negative/positive > beverage) and includes group-level, voxelwise whole-brain analyses (AFNI; voxel-wise thresholds and tables of coordinates reported). Scanner field strength (1.5 T), processing software (AFNI), participant group characteristics (age- and gender-matched groups described), and cue type (alcohol) are all reported. Results include whole-brain contrast maps reported in tables/coordinates and group ANOVAs. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only without task maps, or non-empirical) apply. Therefore study meets all inclusion criteria for this meta-analysis.

studies.title: Genetic variation in the atrial natriuretic peptide transcription factor GATA4 modulates amygdala responsiveness in alcohol dependence.
studies.abstract: Two genome-wide association studies recently showed alcohol dependence to be associated with a single-nucleotide polymorphism (rs13273672) located on a gene (GATA4) that encodes a transcription factor of atrial natriuretic peptide (ANP). A growing body of evidence suggests that ANP might be involved in the symptomology of alcohol dependence. This study examined whether reactivity to alcohol cues in the ANP target region amygdala, a key area implicated in addictive behavior, differs depending on the GATA4 genotype of a patient. We also investigated potential associations between these differences in amygdala activation and relapse behavior.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: The abstract describes an original human study of alcohol cue reactivity and amygdala responsiveness (meets I1). However, it does not explicitly state that functional MRI was used or that visual stimuli were presented. Include for full-text review to confirm whether I2 is met.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an empirical fMRI cue-reactivity study in alcohol-dependent patients using visual alcohol images and neutral control images. It includes within-participant contrasts (alcohol > neutral), reports group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses with coordinate peaks and corrected thresholds, and provides participant age/sex, cue type (alcohol), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM5/SPM8). Therefore it meets all required inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

studies.title: fMRI of the brain's response to stimuli experimentally paired with alcohol intoxication.
studies.abstract: Individuals learn associations between alcohol's sensory properties and intoxication, with such conditioned stimuli (CS) becoming involved in craving and relapse. However, these CS also carry idiosyncratic associations.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Abstract reports an original fMRI study of conditioned stimuli paired with alcohol intoxication, meeting I1. The abstract does not specify that the cues were visual, so modality is unclear. Per guidelines, include for full-text review to determine whether I2 (visual stimuli) is met.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an empirical fMRI study of alcohol cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (novel geometric CS) paired with alcohol intoxication. The paper reports within-subject contrasts including CS+ > CS− (and the reverse) at the group level using whole-brain, random-effects SPM5 analyses with cluster correction. Coordinates, figures, and extracted ROI plots are reported. Participant age and sex (male, mean age reported), cue type (alcohol), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM5) are all provided. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only, or non-empirical) apply. Therefore it meets all listed inclusion criteria.

studies.title: Enhanced affective brain representations of chocolate in cravers vs. non-cravers.
studies.abstract: To examine the neural circuitry involved in food craving, in making food particularly appetitive and thus in driving wanting and eating, we used fMRI to measure the response to the flavour of chocolate, the sight of chocolate and their combination in cravers vs. non-cravers. Statistical parametric mapping (SPM) analyses showed that the sight of chocolate produced more activation in chocolate cravers than non-cravers in the medial orbitofrontal cortex and ventral striatum. For cravers vs. non-cravers, a combination of a picture of chocolate with chocolate in the mouth produced a greater effect than the sum of the components (i.e. supralinearity) in the medial orbitofrontal cortex and pregenual cingulate cortex. Furthermore, the pleasantness ratings of the chocolate and chocolate-related stimuli had higher positive correlations with the fMRI blood oxygenation level-dependent signals in the pregenual cingulate cortex and medial orbitofrontal cortex in the cravers than in the non-cravers. To our knowledge, this is the first study to show that there are differences between cravers and non-cravers in their responses to the sensory components of a craved food in the orbitofrontal cortex, ventral striatum and pregenual cingulate cortex, and that in some of these regions the differences are related to the subjective pleasantness of the craved foods. Understanding individual differences in brain responses to very pleasant foods helps in the understanding of the mechanisms that drive the liking for specific foods and thus intake of those foods.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Human fMRI study with original data examining neural responses to the sight of chocolate (visual cue) and its combination with flavor in cravers vs non-cravers. This addresses natural reward-related visual cue-reactivity and meets inclusion criteria I1 and I2. No exclusion criteria apply.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an empirical fMRI study of natural reward (food: chocolate) cue-reactivity that includes visual cues (pictures of chocolate) and contrasts versus a visual control (grey image). The study reports within-participant task contrasts (e.g., sight of chocolate > grey control; chocolate+picture > components), group-level, whole-brain random-effects analyses using SPM2 on data acquired at 3.0 T, with coordinates and corrected statistics reported. Participant demographics (age range 20–30, all female) and scanner/processing details are provided. Therefore all required inclusion criteria are met and none of the exclusion criteria apply.

studies.title: The corticotropin releasing hormone-1 (CRH1) receptor antagonist pexacerfont in alcohol dependence: a randomized controlled experimental medicine study.
studies.abstract: Extensive preclinical data implicate corticotropin-releasing hormone (CRH), acting through its CRH1 receptor, in stress- and dependence-induced alcohol seeking. We evaluated pexacerfont, an orally available, brain penetrant CRH1 antagonist for its ability to suppress stress-induced alcohol craving and brain responses in treatment seeking alcohol-dependent patients in early abstinence. Fifty-four anxious alcohol-dependent participants were admitted to an inpatient unit at the NIH Clinical Center, completed withdrawal treatment, and were enrolled in a double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled study with pexacerfont (300 mg/day for 7 days, followed by 100 mg/day for 23 days). After reaching steady state, participants were assessed for alcohol craving in response to stressful or alcohol-related cues, neuroendocrine responses to these stimuli, and functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) responses to alcohol-related stimuli or stimuli with positive or negative emotional valence. A separate group of 10 patients received open-label pexacerfont following the same dosing regimen and had cerebrospinal fluid sampled to estimate central nervous system exposure. Pexacerfont treatment had no effect on alcohol craving, emotional responses, or anxiety. There was no effect of pexacerfont on neural responses to alcohol-related or affective stimuli. These results were obtained despite drug levels in cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) that predict close to 90% central CRH1 receptor occupancy. CRH1 antagonists have been grouped based on their receptor dissociation kinetics, with pexacerfont falling in a category characterized by fast dissociation. Our results may indicate that antagonists with slow offset are required for therapeutic efficacy. Alternatively, the extensive preclinical data on CRH1 antagonism as a mechanism to suppress alcohol seeking may not translate to humans.
studies.journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology
studies.abstract_screening_reason: English-language original empirical study using fMRI to assess neural responses to alcohol-related cues and affective stimuli in humans, matching cue-reactivity objectives. Stimulus modality isn’t explicitly stated but alcohol-related cue-reactivity in fMRI studies is typically visual; include for full-text review to confirm stimulus modality and extract data.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This study reports task-based fMRI including visual alcohol vs neutral beverage images, with within-subject contrasts (alcoholic vs non-alcoholic and vs scrambled baseline) and group-level voxelwise analyses. The paper describes whole-brain voxel-wise statistical maps (placebo group activation to fearful vs neutral faces and comparisons between pexacerfont and placebo), reports scanner field strength (3T), processing software (AFNI), cue type (alcohol), and participant demographics. Although some results are reported uncorrected, whole-brain task-evoked maps and contrasts are presented in the text/figures, fulfilling the meta-analysis requirements. Therefore it meets inclusion criteria I3, I4, I5, I6, and I7.

studies.title: A comparison of region-of-interest measures for extracting whole brain data using survival analysis in alcoholism as an example.
studies.abstract: Aggregation of functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) data in regions-of-interest (ROIs) is required for complex statistical analyses not implemented in standard fMRI software. Different data-aggregation measures assess various aspects of neural activation, including spatial extent and intensity.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Abstract does not mention drug or natural reward cue-reactivity nor visual stimuli; it appears to be a methods paper using alcoholism data. Insufficient information to confirm cue-reactivity task, so include for full-text review to determine eligibility.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an fMRI study of alcohol cue-reactivity using visual stimuli with an explicit within-participant contrast (alcohol vs. neutral/abstract). Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses were performed and described (SPM5; voxelwise maps used to identify ROIs), and whole-brain results are reported in the text (coordinates and cluster information). Relevant participant information (age, sex), cue type (alcohol), MRI field strength (1.5T), and processing software (SPM5) are reported. Therefore the study meets all required inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis of visual cue-reactivity.

studies.title: Brain activation elicited by affectively positive stimuli is associated with a lower risk of relapse in detoxified alcoholic subjects.
studies.abstract: Stimuli that are regularly associated with alcohol intake (AI) may acquire incentive salience, while other reinforcers can be devalued. We assessed whether brain activation elicited by (1) alcohol associated, (2) affectively positive, and (3) negative versus neutral stimuli is associated with the subsequent risk of relapse.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Empirical human neuroimaging study assessing brain activation elicited by alcohol-associated and affective stimuli (visual cues implied). Fits inclusion criteria for original data in English and fMRI cue-reactivity with visual stimuli. Abstract does not explicitly state 'fMRI,' but context and authors indicate neuroimaging; include for full-text confirmation.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an empirical fMRI study using visual cues (alcohol-associated pictures and affective IAPS images) to assess drug and natural reward-related cue-reactivity. The paper reports within-participant contrasts (alcohol > neutral; positive > neutral; negative > neutral). Analyses were performed as group-level, whole-brain voxelwise tests using SPM5 (1.5 T scanner), with thresholds, tables/figures (glass brain) and coordinate reporting. Participant age and sex, cue types, scanner field strength, and processing software are all reported. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only without task effects, or non-empirical) apply. Therefore it meets all specified inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

studies.title: Where there's smoke, there's fire: the brain reactivity of chronic smokers when exposed to the negative value of smoking.
studies.abstract: The addictive nature of smoking is characterized by responses to cigarette stimuli that significantly impede smoking cessation efforts. Studies have shown that smokers are roused by appetitive smoking-related stimuli, and their consumption tends to be unaffected by the negative value of smoking.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Abstract reports an empirical study of smokers' brain reactivity to smoking cues but does not explicitly state that functional MRI was used or that visual stimuli were presented. Include for full-text review to confirm imaging modality and stimulus type.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an fMRI cue-reactivity study in chronic smokers using visual stimuli (aversive and appetitive smoking images and matched neutral images). The study reports within-participant contrasts of drug cues > control (aversive smoking-related vs neutral; appetitive smoking-related vs neutral) and group-level, whole-brain random-effects analyses (one-sample t-tests) with cluster correction; results are described in text and supplementary tables/figures. Required metadata are reported: participant age and sex, cue type (nicotine), scanner field strength (3.0 T), and processing software (SPM5). Therefore all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis are met.

studies.title: Dorsal anterior cingulate glutamate is associated with engagement of the default mode network during exposure to smoking cues.
studies.abstract: When exposed to smoking cues, nicotine dependent individuals activate brain regions overlapping with the default mode network (DMN), a network of regions involved in internally-focused cognition. The salience network (SN), which includes the dorsal anterior cingulate cortex (dACC), is thought to interact with the DMN and aids in directing attention toward salient internal or external stimuli. One possibility is that neurochemical variation in SN regions such as the dACC impact DMN reactivity to personally relevant stimuli such as smoking cues. This is consistent with emerging evidence suggesting an association between midline cortical glutamate (Glu) and activity in brain regions overlapping with the DMN.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Abstract reports original human imaging research on nicotine-dependent individuals exposed to smoking cues, indicating empirical data on cue-reactivity. However, it does not explicitly state that functional MRI was performed during cue exposure or that the cues were visual. Information is insufficient to confirm I2, so include for full-text review to assess fMRI and visual-stimulus methods.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an fMRI study of nicotine (drug) cue-reactivity using visual smoking and neutral images, reporting a within-participant contrast (smoking > neutral). The authors conducted a group-level, whole-brain mixed-effects (FLAME) analysis for the smoking vs. neutral contrast (cluster-corrected p<0.05) and describe the whole-brain activation and overlap with the DMN ROI. Participant age/sex, cue type (nicotine), scanner field strength (3T/Siemens Trio), and processing software (FSL) are reported. Therefore all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis are met and no exclusion criteria apply.

studies.title: The effects of single nucleotide polymorphisms in glutamatergic neurotransmission genes on neural response to alcohol cues and craving.
studies.abstract: The aim of the current study was to determine genotype effects of four single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) in the genes of the N-Methyl-d-aspartate receptor (GRIN1, GRIN2A, GRIN2C) and kainate receptor (GRIK1), which have been previously associated with alcoholism, on behavior, neural cue-reactivity and drinking outcome. Eighty-six abstinent alcohol dependent patients were recruited from an in-patient setting. Neuropsychological tests, genotyping and functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) were used to study genotype effects. GRIN2C risk allele carriers displayed increased alcohol cue-induced activation in the anterior cingulate cortex (ACC) and dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (dlPFC). Neural activation in the ACC positively correlated with craving for alcohol (r = 0.201, P = 0.032), whereas activation in the dlPFC showed a negative association (r = -0.215, P = 0.023). In addition, dlPFC activation predicted time to first relapse (HR = 2.701, 95%CI 1.244-5.864, P = 0.012). GRIK1 risk allele carriers showed increased cue-induced activation in the medial prefrontal (PFC) and orbitofrontal cortex (OFC) and in the lateral PFC and OFC. Activation in both clusters positively correlated with alcohol craving (rmedOFC, medPFC  = 0.403, P = 0.001, rlatOFC, latPFC  = 0.282, P = 0.008), and activation in the cluster that encompassed the medial OFC predicted time to first relapse (HR = 1.911, 95%CI 1.030-3.545, P = 0.040). Findings indicate that SNPs in the GRIN2C and GRIK1 genes are associated with altered cue-induced brain activation that is related to craving for alcohol and relapse risk.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Original human fMRI study of alcohol cue-reactivity in abstinent alcohol-dependent patients. Clearly meets I1 (English empirical) and reports fMRI cue-induced brain activation to alcohol cues. Cue modality not explicitly specified in abstract; include for full-text to confirm visual stimuli.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an empirical fMRI study of alcohol cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. It reports a within-participant contrast (alcohol > neutral) at the individual level and carries out group-level, voxelwise whole-brain second-level analyses with cluster-wise correction, providing tables/figures of coordinates and cluster results. Participant sex (n=86, 28 females), inclusion age range (18–65), cue type (alcohol), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM5/SPM8) are reported. The study therefore meets all required inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

studies.title: Insula-Dorsal Anterior Cingulate Cortex Coupling is Associated with Enhanced Brain Reactivity to Smoking Cues.
studies.abstract: The insula plays a critical role in maintaining nicotine dependence and reactivity to smoking cues. More broadly, the insula and the dorsal anterior cingulate cortex (dACC) are key nodes of the salience network (SN), which integrates internal and extrapersonal information to guide behavior. Thus, insula-dACC interactions may be integral in processing salient information such as smoking cues that facilitate continued nicotine use. We evaluated functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) data from nicotine-dependent participants during rest, and again when they viewed smoking-related images. Greater insula-dACC coupling at rest was significantly correlated with enhanced smoking cue-reactivity in brain areas associated with attention and motor preparation, including the visual cortex, right ventral lateral prefrontal cortex, and the dorsal striatum. In an independent cohort, we found that insula-dACC connectivity was stable over 1-h delay and was not influenced by changes in subjective craving or expired carbon monoxide, suggesting that connectivity strength between these regions may be a trait associated with heightened cue-reactivity. Finally, we also showed that insula reactivity to smoking cues correlates with a rise in cue-reactivity throughout the entire SN, indicating that the insula's role in smoking cue-reactivity is not functionally independent, and may actually represent the engagement of the entire SN. Collectively, these data provide a more network-level understanding of the insula's role in nicotine dependence and shows a relationship between inherent brain organization and smoking cue-reactivity.
studies.journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology
studies.abstract_screening_reason: The study reports original human fMRI data assessing smoking cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (smoking-related images). It is not a review and explicitly involves visual cue exposure during fMRI, satisfying inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an fMRI study of nicotine (drug) cue-reactivity using visual smoking images, with a within-participant smoking>neutral contrast and group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses (cluster-corrected maps, tables, and figures). Methods report participant age and sex, cue type (nicotine), MRI field strength (3T Siemens Trio), and processing software (FSL). The paper provides whole-brain results rather than ROI-only or connectivity-only findings, satisfying all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

studies.title: Influence of menstrual cycle phase on neural and craving responses to appetitive smoking cues in naturally cycling females.
studies.abstract: Functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) has been used extensively in an attempt to understand brain vulnerabilities that mediate maladaptive responses to drug cues. Using perfusion fMRI, we have consistently shown reward-related activation (medial orbitofrontal cortex/ventral striatum) to smoking cues (SCs). Because preclinical and clinical studies generally show that progesterone may reduce reward and craving, we hypothesized that females in the follicular phase of the cycle (FPs; when progesterone levels are low) would have greater reward-related neural responses to SCs compared with females in the luteal phase (LPs).
studies.journal: Nicotine & tobacco research : official journal of the Society for Research on Nicotine and Tobacco
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Empirical human perfusion fMRI study reporting neural responses to smoking cues (drug-related). Fits meta-analysis objective of fMRI cue-reactivity; likely uses visual smoking cues commonly used in such paradigms. Include for full-text review to confirm stimulus modality if needed.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an fMRI study of nicotine (smoking) cue reactivity using audio-visual (visual) stimuli with a within-subject contrast of smoking cues > non-smoking cues. Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses were performed using SPM8 with cluster correction and coordinates reported; scanner field strength (3.0T), participant age and sex (premenopausal females, ages 20–51), cue type (nicotine), and processing software are all reported. Therefore it meets all required inclusion criteria for whole-brain task-evoked activation maps and reports the necessary methodological details.

studies.title: Aversive aftertaste changes visual food cue reactivity: An fMRI study on cross-modal perception.
studies.abstract: In western cultures, we are surrounded by appealing visual food cues that stimulate our desire to eat, overeating and subsequent weight gain. Cognitive control of appetite (reappraisal) requires substantial attentional resources and effort in order to work. Therefore, we tested an alternative approach for appetite regulation via functional magnetic resonance imaging. Healthy, normal-weight women were presented with images depicting food (high-/low-caloric), once in combination with a bitter aftertaste (a gustatory stop signal) and once with a neutral taste (water), in a retest design. The aversive aftertaste elicited increased activation in the orbitofrontal/dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (OFC, DLPFC), striatum and frontal operculum during the viewing of high-caloric food (vs. low-caloric food). In addition, the increase in DLPFC activity to high-caloric food in the bitter condition was correlated with reported appetite reduction. The findings indicate that this aftertaste procedure was able to reduce the appetitive value of visual food cues.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Human fMRI study with original data (English) assessing visual food cue-reactivity (natural rewards). The task used images of high-/low-caloric food during fMRI, meeting the review objective. No exclusion criteria apply.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an empirical fMRI study using visual natural reward cues (food images) with healthy adult participants. The study reports within-subject contrasts comparing high-caloric > low-caloric food cues (and interactions with bitter vs. water conditions). Both exploratory whole-brain voxel-wise analyses and ROI analyses are reported, with group-level statistical maps summarized in text/tables/figures and coordinates (supplementary material referenced). Relevant metadata (participant age/sex, cue type: food, 3T scanner field strength, and processing software SPM12) are provided. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only contrasts, or non-empirical) apply.

studies.title: Mesolimbic recruitment by nondrug rewards in detoxified alcoholics: effort anticipation, reward anticipation, and reward delivery.
studies.abstract: Aberrant sensitivity of incentive neurocircuitry to nondrug rewards has been suggested as either a risk factor for or consequence of drug addiction. Using functional magnetic resonance imaging, we tested whether alcohol-dependent patients (ADP: n = 29) showed altered recruitment of ventral striatal (VS) incentive neurocircuitry compared to controls (n = 23) by: (1) cues to respond for monetary rewards, (2) post-response anticipation of rewards, or (3) delivery of rewards. Using an instrumental task with two-stage presentation of reward-predictive information, subjects saw cues signaling opportunities to win $0, $1, or $10 for responding to a target. Following this response, subjects were notified whether their success would be indicated by a lexical notification (“Hit?”) or by delivery of a monetary reward (“Win?”). After a variable interval, subjects then viewed the trial outcome. We found no significant group differences in voxelwise activation by task contrasts, or in signal change extracted from VS. Both ADP and controls showed significant VS and other limbic recruitment by pre-response reward anticipation. In addition, controls also showed VS recruitment by post-response reward-anticipation, and ADP had appreciable subthreshold VS activation. Both groups also showed similar mesolimbic responses to reward deliveries. Across all subjects, a questionnaire measure of “hot” impulsivity correlated with VS recruitment by post-response anticipation of low rewards and with VS recruitment by delivery of low rewards. These findings indicate that incentive-motivational processing of nondrug rewards is substantially maintained in recovering alcoholics, and that reward-elicited VS recruitment correlates more with individual differences in trait impulsivity irrespective of addiction.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Human fMRI study with original data assessing neural responses to monetary (nondrug) reward cues presented visually in alcohol-dependent patients and controls, matching the review focus on fMRI cue-reactivity to drug or natural rewards using visual stimuli. Not a review and does not use only nonvisual sensory cues.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This fMRI study uses visual monetary reward cues (cue shapes and WIN/HIT words) to probe neural responses to nondrug (natural) rewards in alcohol-dependent patients and controls. It includes within-subject contrasts of reward cues versus non-incentive cues (pre-response $1/$10 vs $0) and WIN vs HIT post-response cues, and reports group-level, whole-brain voxelwise statistical maps (with FDR correction) using AFNI/3dMEMA. Participant ages and sexes, cue type (monetary), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (AFNI) are reported. Therefore it meets inclusion criteria I3, I4, I5, I6, and I7 and does not violate exclusion criteria E3–E6.

studies.title: Neural processing of food cues in pre-pubertal children.
studies.abstract: Neuroimaging investigations of brain pathways involved in reward and motivation have primarily focused on adults. This study sought to identify brain responses to visual food cues and explore its relationships with adiposity and sex in pre-pubertal children.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Abstract reports an empirical human neuroimaging study (fMRI implied) that measured brain responses to visual food cues (natural rewards) in pre-pubertal children, matching the meta-analysis focus. It appears to be original data and uses visual stimuli for cue-reactivity.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an empirical fMRI study of visual natural reward (food) cue-reactivity in humans. It used visual food vs. non-food pictures with a within-subject contrast (food>non-food), conducted whole-brain, group-level voxelwise analyses (FLAME1) with cluster correction, and reports whole-brain results in figures/tables. Relevant methodological details are provided: participant age and sex (pre-pubertal children, 7–11 yrs), cue type (food), MRI field strength (3T), and processing software (FSL). Therefore it satisfies all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis of visual drug/natural reward cue-reactivity.

studies.title: Age-related changes in reappraisal of appetitive cravings during adolescence.
studies.abstract: The ability to regulate temptation and manage appetitive cravings is an important aspect of healthy adolescent development, but the neural systems underlying this process are understudied. In the present study, 60 healthy females evenly distributed from 10 to 23years of age used reappraisal to regulate the desire to consume personally-craved and not craved unhealthy foods. Reappraisal elicited activity in common self-regulation regions including the dorsal and ventral lateral prefrontal cortex (specifically superior and inferior frontal gyri), dorsal anterior cingulate cortex, and inferior parietal lobule. Viewing personally-craved foods (versus not craved foods) elicited activity in regions including the ventral striatum, as well as more rostral and ventral anterior cingulate cortex extending into the orbitofrontal cortex. Age positively correlated with regulation-related activity in the right inferior frontal gyrus, and negatively correlated with reactivity-related activity in the right superior and dorsolateral prefrontal cortices. Age-adjusted BMI negatively correlated with regulation-related activity in the predominantly left lateralized frontal and parietal regions. These results suggest that the age-related changes seen in the reappraisal of negative emotion may not be as pronounced in the reappraisal of food craving. Therefore, reappraisal of food craving in particular may be an effective way to teach teenagers to manage cravings for other temptations encountered in adolescence, including alcohol, drugs, and unhealthy food.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Original human fMRI study (N=60 females) using visual presentation of personally-craved vs non-craved foods (natural rewards). Abstract confirms cue-reactivity to visual food cues and reports neural responses, meeting inclusion criteria.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an fMRI study of visual natural reward (food) cue-reactivity using pictures of personally-craved and not-craved foods. It reports within-subject contrasts (e.g., Look Craved > Look Not Craved and Look Craved > Look Neutral) at the group level. Whole-brain, voxelwise task-evoked analyses are performed and reported (cluster thresholds, peak coordinates, cluster sizes) and small-volume relaxation for the ventral striatum is described. Participant demographics (ages 10–23, all female), cue type (food), scanner field strength (3.0 T), and processing software (SPM12b, FSL BET, AFNI AlphaSim referenced) are all reported. No exclusion criteria apply (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, not only between-group contrasts). Therefore the study meets all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis of drug/natural reward visual cue-reactivity.

studies.title: Resting state hypothalamic response to glucose predicts glucose-induced attenuation in the ventral striatal response to food cues.
studies.abstract: Feeding behavior is regulated by a complex interaction of central nervous system responses to metabolic signals that reflect nutrient availability and to food cues that trigger appetitive responses. Prior work has shown that the hypothalamus is a key brain area that senses and responds to changes in metabolic signals, and exposure to food cues induces the activation of brain areas involved in reward processing. However, it is not known how the hypothalamic responses to changes in metabolic state are related to reward responses to food cues. This study aimed to understand whether changes in hypothalamic activity in response to glucose-induced metabolic signals are linked to food-cue reactivity within brain areas involved in reward processing. We combined two neuroimaging modalities (Arterial Spin Labeling and Blood Oxygen Level Dependent) to measure glucose-induced changes in hypothalamic cerebral blood flow (CBF) and food-cue task induced changes in brain activity within reward-related regions. Twenty-five participants underwent a MRI session following glucose ingestion and a subset of twenty individuals underwent an additional water session on a separate day as a control condition (drink order randomized). Hunger was assessed before and after drink consumption. We observed that individuals who had a greater reduction in hypothalamic CBF exhibited a greater reduction in left ventral striatum food cue reactivity (Spearman's rho = 0.46, P = 0.048) following glucose vs. water ingestion. These results are the first to use multimodal imaging to demonstrate a link between hypothalamic metabolic signaling and ventral striatal food cue reactivity.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Human empirical fMRI study (ASL and BOLD) reporting food-cue task-induced brain activity (natural reward). Meets English original data criterion and involves fMRI assessment of food cue-reactivity; no exclusion criteria met. Visual modality is implied by 'food-cue task' but full text can confirm stimulus modality.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an empirical BOLD-fMRI study using visual food cues (pictures) to probe natural reward-related cue-reactivity, with within-subject food>non-food contrasts. The authors performed group-level voxelwise whole-brain analyses comparing food-cue reactivity after glucose vs. water (FDR cluster-corrected), in addition to ROI analyses. Participant age and sex are reported (19–25 years; 12M/13F), cue type is food, scanner details (MAGNETOM Prisma; parameters consistent with 3T) and processing software (FSL) are provided. Therefore the study meets inclusion criteria for (I3) task modality and cue type, (I4) within-participant food>control contrasts, (I5) group-level whole-brain voxelwise task analysis, (I6) whole-brain results reported in text/figures, and (I7) participant and methodological details reported. No exclusion criteria (e.g., ROI-only reporting, connectivity-only analyses, between-group-only contrasts, or non-empirical paper) apply.

studies.title: Modulation of brain response to emotional images by alcohol cues in alcohol-dependent patients.
studies.abstract: Alcohol is often used to modulate mood states. Alcohol drinkers report that they use alcohol both to enhance positive affect and to reduce dysphoria, and alcohol-dependent patients specifically state reduction of negative affect as a primary reason for drinking. The current study proposes that alcohol cues may reduce negative affect in alcoholics. We used functional magnetic resonance imaging to examine brain activation in response to combination images that juxtaposed negative or positive International Affective Picture System (IAPS) images with an alcohol or non-alcohol-containing beverage. We found that in the absence of the alcohol cue, alcoholics showed more activation to negative than to positive images and greater activation than controls to negative images. When the IAPS images were presented with the alcohol cue, there was a decreased difference in activation between the positive and negative images among the alcoholics, and a decreased difference in response to the negative images between controls and alcoholics. Additionally, in the neutral-beverage conditions, anxiety ratings significantly predicted activation in the right parahippocampal gyrus but did not predict activation when the alcohol cues were presented. In conclusion, the alcohol cues may have modulated cortical networks involved in the processing of emotional stimuli by eliciting a conditioned response in the alcoholics, but not in the controls, which may have decreased responsiveness to the negative images.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Abstract reports an original empirical human fMRI study that used visual alcohol-related cues (IAPS images paired with alcoholic or non-alcoholic beverages) to assess cue-reactivity; thus meets inclusion criteria and no exclusion criteria apply.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an empirical 3T fMRI study using visual alcohol cues (alcoholic vs non-alcoholic beverage images) and emotional IAPS images. The paper reports within-subject contrasts comparing alcohol versus non-alcohol cues (e.g., alcoholic vs non-alcoholic beverage cues paired with positive and negative images), and presents group-level, whole-brain voxelwise statistical maps with cluster-level correction (tables/figures described). Methods report scanner field strength (3T) and processing software (AFNI), participant sex (males) and refer to demographic details (Table 1). Therefore it meets inclusion criteria for visual drug-cue reactivity, within-participant cue>control contrasts, whole-brain reporting, and sufficient methodological details. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only, or non-empirical) apply.

studies.title: The effects of exercise on cigarette cravings and brain activation in response to smoking-related images.
studies.abstract: Smokers show heightened activation toward smoking-related stimuli and experience increased cravings which can precipitate smoking cessation relapse. Exercise can be effective for modulating cigarette cravings and attenuating reactivity to smoking cues, but the mechanism by which these effects occur remains uncertain.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Abstract reports brain activation to smoking-related images and exercise effects on cravings, indicating original human neuroimaging research in English. However, it does not explicitly state use of functional MRI in the abstract. Include for full-text review to confirm fMRI methods. I1 is satisfied; I2 cannot be confirmed from the abstract.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: Title explicitly reports brain activation in response to smoking-related images (visual nicotine cues) and the study appears to examine cue-reactivity in the context of exercise effects. Given the journal and DOI, this is consistent with an fMRI task-based cue-reactivity study likely reporting within-participant contrasts (smoking cues > control) and whole-brain voxelwise results. However, the full text could not be loaded for direct verification of whole-brain map reporting and detailed methodological reporting, so confidence is moderate.

studies.title: Opposing roles for amygdala and vmPFC in the return of appetitive conditioned responses in humans.
studies.abstract: Learning accounts of addiction and obesity emphasize the persistent power of Pavlovian reward cues to trigger craving and increase relapse risk. While extinction can reduce conditioned responding, Pavlovian relapse phenomena-the return of conditioned responding following successful extinction-challenge the long-term success of extinction-based treatments. Translational laboratory models of Pavlovian relapse could therefore represent a valuable tool to investigate the mechanisms mediating relapse, although so far human research has mostly focused on return of fear phenomena. To this end we developed an appetitive conditioning paradigm with liquid food rewards in combination with a 3-day design to investigate the return of appetitive Pavlovian responses and the involved neural structures in healthy subjects. Pavlovian conditioning (day 1) was assessed in 62 participants, and a subsample (n = 33) further completed extinction (day 2) and a reinstatement test (day 3). Conditioned responding was assessed on explicit (pleasantness ratings) and implicit measures (reaction time, skin conductance, heart rate, startle response) and reinstatement effects were further evaluated using fMRI. We observed a return of conditioned responding during the reinstatement test, evident by enhanced skin conductance responses, accompanied by enhanced BOLD responses in the amygdala. On an individual level, psychophysiological reinstatement intensity was significantly anticorrelated with ventromedial prefrontal cortex (vmPFC) activation, and marginally anticorrelated with enhanced amygdala-vmPFC connectivity during late reinstatement. Our results extend evidence from return of fear phenomena to the appetitive domain, and highlight the role of the vmPFC and its functional connection with the amygdala in regulating appetitive Pavlovian relapse.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Abstract reports original human fMRI research on appetitive (food) cue conditioning and reinstatement but does not specify that cue-reactivity used visual stimuli. Because modality of cues is unclear and exclusion E2 cannot be confidently applied, include for full-text review to determine if visual cues were used.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an empirical fMRI study of appetitive (natural reward: food) cue-reactivity using visual cues (pictures combined with tones) and liquid-food US. The main within-participant contrasts include CS+ > CS− (natural reward-related cues > control cues) at group level. Whole-brain group-level GLM analyses were performed (flexible factorial), and whole-brain exploratory results are reported (Supplementary) with coordinates; ROI SVC was applied for specific hypotheses but whole-brain task-evoked maps are available. Participant age/sex, cue type (food), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM12) are reported. No exclusion criteria apply (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, not non-empirical). Therefore the paper meets all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

studies.title: Effects of cognitive bias modification training on neural signatures of alcohol approach tendencies in male alcohol-dependent patients.
studies.abstract: Alcohol-dependent patients have been shown to faster approach than avoid alcohol stimuli on the Approach Avoidance Task (AAT). This so-called alcohol approach bias has been associated with increased brain activation in the medial prefrontal cortex and nucleus accumbens. Cognitive bias modification (CBM) has been used to retrain the approach bias with the clinically relevant effect of decreasing relapse rates one year later. The effects of CBM on neural signatures of approach/avoidance tendencies remain hitherto unknown. In a double-blind placebo-controlled design, 26 alcohol-dependent in-patients were assigned to a CBM or a placebo training group. Both groups performed the AAT for three weeks: in CBM training, patients pushed away 90 percent of alcohol cues; this rate was 50 percent in placebo training. Before and after training, patients performed the AAT offline, and in a 3 T magnetic resonance imaging scanner. The relevant neuroimaging contrast for the alcohol approach bias was the difference between approaching versus avoiding alcohol cues relative to soft drink cues: [(alcohol pull > alcohol push)  > (soft drink pull > soft drink push)]. Before training, both groups showed significant alcohol approach bias-related activation in the medial prefrontal cortex. After training, patients in the CBM group showed stronger reductions in medial prefrontal cortex activation compared with the placebo group. Moreover, these reductions correlated with reductions in approach bias scores in the CBM group only. This suggests that CBM affects neural mechanisms involved in the automatic alcohol approach bias, which may be important for the clinical effectiveness of CBM.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: English-language empirical human fMRI study assessing neural responses to alcohol versus soft-drink cues using the visual Approach–Avoidance Task in scanner; directly addresses drug-related cue-reactivity. Meets I1 and I2.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an empirical fMRI study of alcohol cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (AAT images). It includes within-subject contrasts comparing alcohol versus soft-drink cues (e.g., [alcohol pull > soft drink pull] and related approach-bias contrasts). The paper reports group-level whole-brain analyses (main effects and interaction effects reported in supplementary materials and a whole-brain result surviving FWE correction is described), not only ROI results. Relevant methodological details are provided (male participants, alcohol cue type, 3T MRI field strength, preprocessing and analysis with SPM8). Therefore all inclusion criteria are satisfied and no exclusion criteria apply.

studies.title: Prefrontal-striatal pathway underlies cognitive regulation of craving.
studies.abstract: The ability to control craving for substances that offer immediate rewards but whose long-term consumption may pose serious risks lies at the root of substance use disorders and is critical for mental and physical health. Despite its importance, the neural systems supporting this ability remain unclear. Here, we investigated this issue using functional imaging to examine neural activity in cigarette smokers, the most prevalent substance-dependent population in the United States, as they used cognitive strategies to regulate craving for cigarettes and food. We found that the cognitive down-regulation of craving was associated with (i) activity in regions previously associated with regulating emotion in particular and cognitive control in general, including dorsomedial, dorsolateral, and ventrolateral prefrontal cortices, and (ii) decreased activity in regions previously associated with craving, including the ventral striatum, subgenual cingulate, amygdala, and ventral tegmental area. Decreases in craving correlated with decreases in ventral striatum activity and increases in dorsolateral prefrontal cortex activity, with ventral striatal activity fully mediating the relationship between lateral prefrontal cortex and reported craving. These results provide insight into the mechanisms that enable cognitive strategies to effectively regulate craving, suggesting that it involves neural dynamics parallel to those involved in regulating other emotions. In so doing, this study provides a methodological tool and conceptual foundation for studying this ability across substance using populations and developing more effective treatments for substance use disorders.
studies.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Abstract reports an fMRI study with human cigarette smokers examining regulation of craving (drug and natural reward). It meets I1 (original empirical fMRI study). The abstract does not specify whether cue-reactivity was elicited using visual stimuli, so inclusion per I2 cannot be confirmed from the abstract alone; include for full-text review to determine use of visual cues.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is a task-based fMRI study in cigarette smokers using visual cues (photographs) of cigarettes and food to elicit craving, meeting the review’s focus on drug and natural reward cue-reactivity. The paper reports within-participant task contrasts isolating cue-evoked activity (e.g., NOW trials/picture period vs baseline and cue-type effects), whole-brain, group-level voxelwise analyses corrected for multiple comparisons (with additional a priori ROI thresholds), and results reported in figures/tables/SI. Participant age and sex, cue types (nicotine, food), scanner field strength, and processing software (SPM5, AFNI AlphaSim) are described. Therefore it satisfies I3, I4, I5, I6, and I7 of the inclusion criteria.

studies.title: Fighting food temptations: the modulating effects of short-term cognitive reappraisal, suppression and up-regulation on mesocorticolimbic activity related to appetitive motivation.
studies.abstract: The premise of cognitive therapy is that one can overcome the irresistible temptation of highly palatable foods by actively restructuring the way one thinks about food. Testing this idea, participants in the present study were instructed to passively view foods, up-regulate food palatability thoughts, apply cognitive reappraisal (e.g., thinking about health consequences), or suppress food palatability thoughts and cravings. We examined whether these strategies affect self-reported food craving and mesocorticolimbic activity as assessed by functional magnetic resonance imaging. It was hypothesized that cognitive reappraisal would most effectively inhibit the mesocorticolimbic activity and associated food craving as compared to suppression. In addition, it was hypothesized that suppression would lead to more prefrontal cortex activity, reflecting the use of more control resources, as compared to cognitive reappraisal. Self-report results indicated that up-regulation increased food craving compared to the other two conditions, but that there was no difference in craving between the suppression and cognitive reappraisal strategy. Corroborating self-report results, the neuroimaging results showed that up-regulation increased activity in important regions of the mesocorticolimbic circuitry, including the ventral tegmental area, ventral striatum, operculum, posterior insular gyrus, medial orbitofrontal cortex and ventromedial prefrontal cortex. Contrary to our hypothesis, suppression more effectively decreased activity in the core of the mesocorticolimbic circuitry (i.e., ventral tegmental area and ventral striatum) compared to cognitive reappraisal. Overall, the results support the contention that appetitive motivation can be modulated by the application of short-term cognitive control strategies.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Human fMRI study (original empirical data) where participants passively viewed foods and used cognitive strategies; assesses cue-reactivity to natural rewards (food) using visual stimuli. Fits inclusion criteria; no exclusion criteria met.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an fMRI study using visual natural reward cues (high-calorie food images) and reports within-subject contrasts between cue conditions and passive viewing/baseline. A group-level whole-brain RFX ANOVA was performed and corrected whole-brain maps/fROIs are reported. Participant demographics (age, sex), cue type (food), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (BrainVoyager) are all reported. Although neutral non-food control images are not used, the paper reports within-participant contrasts (e.g., up-regulation/suppression vs passive viewing and condition vs passive baseline) and whole-brain results, satisfying the review’s requirements for natural reward cue-reactivity with whole-brain task-evoked maps.

studies.title: The left dorsolateral prefrontal cortex and caudate pathway: New evidence for cue-induced craving of smokers.
studies.abstract: Although the activation of the prefrontal cortex (PFC) and the striatum had been found in smoking cue induced craving task, whether and how the functional interactions and white matter integrity between these brain regions contribute to craving processing during smoking cue exposure remains unknown. Twenty-five young male smokers and 26 age- and gender-matched nonsmokers participated in the smoking cue-reactivity task. Craving related brain activation was extracted and psychophysiological interactions (PPI) analysis was used to specify the PFC-efferent pathways contributed to smoking cue-induced craving. Diffusion tensor imaging (DTI) and probabilistic tractography was used to explore whether the fiber connectivity strength facilitated functional coupling of the circuit with the smoking cue-induced craving. The PPI analysis revealed the negative functional coupling of the left dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (DLPFC) and the caudate during smoking cue induced craving task, which positively correlated with the craving score. Neither significant activation nor functional connectivity in smoking cue exposure task was detected in nonsmokers. DTI analyses revealed that fiber tract integrity negatively correlated with functional coupling in the DLPFC-caudate pathway and activation of the caudate induced by smoking cue in smokers. Moreover, the relationship between the fiber connectivity integrity of the left DLPFC-caudate and smoking cue induced caudate activation can be fully mediated by functional coupling strength of this circuit in smokers. The present study highlighted the left DLPFC-caudate pathway in smoking cue-induced craving in smokers, which may reflect top-down prefrontal modulation of striatal reward processing in smoking cue induced craving processing. Hum Brain Mapp 38:4644-4656, 2017. © 2017 Wiley Periodicals, Inc.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Empirical fMRI study of smoking cue-reactivity in human participants; abstract does not specify cue sensory modality (visual) so insufficient info to confirm I2—include for full-text review to verify visual stimuli.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an fMRI study of nicotine (smoking) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli with an event-related within-subject contrast (smoking cue > neutral cue). The paper reports group-level, whole-brain voxelwise task-evoked analyses (FDR-corrected) with coordinates/figures and provides participant age and sex, cue type (nicotine), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM8/FSL). Thus it satisfies all required inclusion criteria and does not violate exclusion criteria.

studies.title: The top-down regulation from the prefrontal cortex to insula via hypnotic aversion suggestions reduces smoking craving.
studies.abstract: Hypnosis has been shown to have treatment effects on nicotine addiction. However, the neural basis of these effects is poorly understood. This preliminary study investigated the neural mechanisms of hypnosis-based treatment on cigarette smoking, specifically, whether the hypnosis involves a top-down or bottom-up mechanism. Two groups of 45 smokers underwent a smoking aversion suggestion and viewed smoking-related pictures and neutral pictures. One group underwent functional magnetic resonance imaging scanning twice (control and hypnotic states), whereas the other group underwent two electroencephalograph sessions. Our study found that self-reported smoking craving decreased in both groups following hypnosis. Smoking cue-elicited activations in the right dorsal lateral prefrontal cortex (rDLPFC) and left insula (lI) and the functional connectivity between the rDLPFC and lI were increased in the hypnotic state compared with the control state. The delta band source waveforms indicated the activation from 390 to 862 ms at the rDLPFC and from 490 to 900 ms at the lI was significantly different between the smoking and neutral conditions in the hypnotic state, suggesting the activation in the rDLPFC preceded that in the lI. These results suggest that the decreased smoking craving via hypnotic aversion suggestions may arise from the top-down regulation of the rDLPFC to the lI. Our findings provide novel neurobiological evidence for understanding the therapeutic effects of hypnosis on nicotine addiction, and the prefrontal-insula circuit may serve as an imaging biomarker to monitor the treatment efficacy noninvasively.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: English empirical study reporting original data; includes functional MRI scanning while participants viewed smoking-related and neutral pictures (visual stimuli) to assess cue-reactivity in human smokers, directly matching the review objective. No exclusion criteria met in the abstract.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This study reports task-based fMRI data using visual smoking cues (smoking-related pictures) with within-subject contrasts (smoking vs. neutral) and behavioral measures of craving. The manuscript presents group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses (2×2 ANOVA, FWE-corrected results reported) and tables/figures of activations, satisfying the whole-brain reporting requirement. Relevant participant characteristics (sex distribution, age reported in Table 1), cue type (nicotine/smoking), and analytic details are described in the main text with acquisition/preprocessing/analysis details referenced in the Supporting Information (acceptable per I6). The study therefore meets inclusion criteria I3, I4, I5, I6, and I7 and does not violate any exclusion criteria (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, includes within-subject task activation maps).

studies.title: Hedonic Hunger Is Related to Increased Neural and Perceptual Responses to Cues of Palatable Food and Motivation to Consume: Evidence from 3 Independent Investigations.
studies.abstract: The Power of Food Scale (PFS) seeks to identify individuals who experience high appetitive drive in response to food cues, which is a construct termed "hedonic hunger."
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Abstract indicates original empirical work (three investigations) on hedonic responses to food cues, but does not specify that neural measures were obtained with fMRI or that visual stimuli were used. Insufficient information to confirm I2, so include for full-text review to determine fMRI and visual-stimulus eligibility.

It reports a within-participant contrast (anticipatory milkshake cue > anticipatory tasteless-solution cue), and presents group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses with cluster-level q-FDR correction and reported activation coordinates (postcentral gyrus). Relevant participant demographics (age, sex), cue type (food), MRI field strength (Siemens Allegra 3T), and processing software (SPM12, FSL) are provided. Thus all required inclusion criteria are met.
