Relevant details are provided: participant age group (adolescents/young adults), reported sex analyses, cue type (food), MRI field strength (3.0 T), and processing software (SPM8). No exclusion criteria apply (not ROI-only, not connectivity/resting-state-only, and it is empirical). Therefore it meets all inclusion criteria for natural reward-related cue-reactivity visual fMRI studies.

studies.title: Oxytocin administration suppresses hypothalamic activation in response to visual food cues.
studies.abstract: The aim of this study was to use functional neuroimaging to investigate whether oxytocin modulates the neural response to visual food cues in brain regions involved in the control of food intake. Twenty-four normal weight volunteers received intranasal oxytocin (24 IU) or placebo in a double-blind, randomized crossover study. Measurements were made forty-five minutes after dosing. On two occasions, functional MRI (fMRI) scans were performed in the fasted state; the blood oxygen level-dependent (BOLD) response to images of high-calorie foods versus low-calorie foods was measured. Given its critical role in eating behaviour, the primary region of interest was the hypothalamus. Secondary analyses examined the parabrachial nuclei and other brain regions involved in food intake and food reward. Intranasal oxytocin administration suppressed hypothalamic activation to images of high-calorie compared to low-calorie food (P = 0.0125). There was also a trend towards suppression of activation in the parabrachial nucleus (P = 0.0683). No effects of intranasal oxytocin were seen in reward circuits or on ad libitum food intake. Further characterization of the effects of oxytocin on neural circuits in the hypothalamus is needed to establish the utility of targeting oxytocin signalling in obesity.

studies.title: Prospective associations between brain activation to cocaine and no-go cues and cocaine relapse.
studies.abstract: The ability to predict potential for relapse to substance use following treatment could be very useful in targeting aftercare strategies. Recently, a number of investigators have focused on using neural activity measured by fMRI to predict relapse propensity. The purpose of the present study was to use fMRI to investigate prospective associations between brain reactivity to cocaine and response inhibition cues and relapse to cocaine use.

studies.title: Cue-elicited craving, thalamic activity, and physiological arousal in adult non-dependent drinkers.
studies.abstract: Changes in physiological arousal frequently accompany cognitive and affective challenges. Many studies employed cue exposure paradigms to investigate the neural processes underlying cue-elicited drug and alcohol craving. However, whether cue-elicited craving relates to changes in physiological arousal and the neural bases underlying the potential relationship remain unclear. Here we examined cerebral cue-related activations in relation to differences in skin conductance responses (SCR) recorded during alcohol vs. neutral cue blocks in 61 non-dependent alcohol drinkers (30 men). Imaging and skin conductance data were collected and processed with published routines. Mediation analyses were conducted to examine the inter-relationship between regional activities, cue-elicited craving, and SCR. The results showed higher SCR during alcohol than during neutral cue exposure. Despite no differences in drinking characteristics, men as compared to women demonstrated higher craving rating, and men but not women demonstrated a positive correlation between alcohol (vs. neutral) cue-evoked craving and SCR. Further, across subjects, thalamic cue activity was positively correlated with differences in SCR between alcohol and neutral cue blocks in men but not in women. Mediation analyses suggested that thalamic activity mediated the correlation between craving and SCR across men and women, and in men but not women alone. These findings substantiate physiological and neural correlates of alcohol cue response and suggest important sex differences in the physiological and neural processes of cue evoked craving. Centered on the intralaminar and mediodorsal subregions, the thalamic correlate may represent a neural target for behavioral or pharmacological therapy to decrease cue-elicited arousal and craving.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Original empirical fMRI study in English with human participants examining alcohol vs neutral visual cues and cue-elicited craving; meets the review objective and no exclusion criteria apply.

studies.title: Neural correlates of a Go/NoGo task with alcohol stimuli in light and heavy young drinkers.
studies.abstract: Inhibitory processes are highly relevant to behavioral control affecting decisions made daily. The Go/NoGo task is a common task used to tap basic inhibitory processes important in higher order executive functioning. The present study assessed neural correlates of response inhibition during performance of a Go/NoGo task in which NoGo signals or tests of inhibitory control consisted of images of beer bottles. Group comparisons were conducted between 21 heavy and 20 light drinkers, ranging in age from 18 to 22. Behaviorally, overall performance assessed with d-prime was significantly better among the lighter drinkers. On a neural level, the heavy drinkers showed significantly greater activity in the right dorsolateral prefrontal cortex, medial frontal cortex and cingulate relative to the light drinkers during the NoGo trials. These regions are implicated in reflective or control processing of information. Further, heavy drinkers showed significantly greater activity in the insula relative to light drinkers during NoGo trials, a neural region implicated in habit circuitry and tied to cue induced urges and emotional memories of physical effects of drugs. These results suggest that the heavier drinkers may have experienced increased working memory demand and control efforts to withhold a response due to poorer inhibitory control from enhanced salience of alcohol cues on the beer NoGo trials, which also engaged insula mediated effects.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Original empirical fMRI study in English with human participants using visual alcohol cues (images of beer bottles) and reporting neural responses; directly assesses cue-related neural activity. Meets I1 and I2. No exclusion criteria apply based on the abstract.

Therefore it meets the meta-analysis requirements for visual cue-reactivity, a within-participant cue condition (beer NoGo) contrasted with control stimuli representations, whole-brain group maps, and requisite reporting details.

The authors conducted voxel-wise, group-level whole-brain LME analyses (and ROI analyses) with reported cluster coordinates and volumes, satisfying whole-brain reporting requirements. The paper provides relevant methodological details: cue type (alcohol, food), scanner field strength (3T GE and Siemens), preprocessing/analysis software (AFNI), and participant demographic information (age, sex referenced). Although the study includes a medication manipulation (varenicline vs placebo) and reports interaction effects, it also reports main task effects (alcohol>neutral) across participants, which meets the requirement for a within-participant drug-cue>control contrast. No exclusion criteria are triggered (whole-brain results are present, not ROI-only or resting-state).

studies.title: No Sex Difference Found: Cues of Sexual Stimuli Activate the Reward System in both Sexes.
studies.abstract: Sexually explicit material (SEM) is increasingly used in western societies. One reason for this high usage might be the rewarding property of SEM demonstrated in many brain imaging studies showing an activation of the reward system during the presentation of SEM. It is not yet well understood why women use SEM to a remarkably lesser extent than men. Maybe men react stronger to stimuli - so called SEM cues -, which signal the presentation of SEM and are therefore more vulnerable to use SEM than women. Therefore, the present study aimed at investigating the sex specific neural correlates towards SEM and SEM cues. We were further interested in whether person characteristics as trait sexual motivation, extent of SEM use in the last month, and age at onset of goal-oriented SEM use affect the neural responses to SEM and SEM cues. The trials of the fMRI experiment consisted of an expectation phase with SEM or neutral cues and a presentation phase with SEM or neutral stimuli, respectively. Analyses showed that the reward circuitry was activated by SEM, but also by SEM cues. There were some sex differences in hemodynamic responses to SEM during the presentation phase, but not during the expectation phase to SEM cues in any of the regions of interest. The influence of the investigated person characteristics was only small if existent. The results suggest that sex specific cue processing cannot explain sex differences in the use of SEM.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: This is an empirical fMRI study in humans using visual sexual cues and stimuli (SEM), assessing neural cue-reactivity to a natural reward; it reports original data. No exclusion criteria apply.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an fMRI study of natural reward (sexual) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. It includes within-participant contrasts of cues>neutral (CUE SEM – CUE NEUTRAL) and stimulus>neutral (SEM – NEUTRAL). The paper reports both ROI and explorative whole-brain analyses (family-wise error corrected whole-brain results reported), with whole-sample group-level task-evoked maps described in text and figures. Participant age and sex are reported (n=70, ages and sexes detailed), cue type (sexual), MRI field strength (3T Siemens Prisma), and processing software (SPM12) are all provided. Therefore it meets the review’s inclusion criteria for fMRI cue-reactivity studies and does not violate exclusion criteria (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, contains whole-brain group activation maps).

analyses.name: Possible predictors of the neural responses during presentation and expectation phases. Results of regression analyses with the beta-weights of the contrast SEM–NEUTRAL (contrast CUESEM–CUENEUTRAL, respectively) as criterion and Trait Sexual Motivation Questionnaire score (TSMQmean, Stark et al., 2015), extent of SEM use, and age at onset of goal-oriented SEM use as predictors are displayed. The analyses were conducted separately for each of the predictors. The predictor variables were z-transformed separately for each sex. ROI analysis with NAcc mask did not result in statistically significant results. Therefore, only significant results of the explorative whole brain analyses are presented (family wise error correction, p < .05).

studies.title: Reward Sensitivity and Waiting Impulsivity: Shift towards Reward Valuation away from Action Control.
studies.abstract: Impulsivity and reward expectancy are commonly interrelated. Waiting impulsivity, measured using the rodent 5-Choice Serial Reaction Time task, predicts compulsive cocaine seeking and sign (or cue) tracking. Here, we assess human waiting impulsivity using a novel translational task, the 4-Choice Serial Reaction Time task, and the relationship with reward cues.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Abstract describes a human behavioural study assessing waiting impulsivity and reward cues but does not mention fMRI or visual cue-reactivity. Information is insufficient to confirm the required fMRI visual cue-reactivity criterion, so include for full-text review to verify.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: The study is an fMRI investigation of visual reward cues (monetary: £5, £1 vs neutral) and reports within-participant contrasts (£5-neutral, £1-neutral). It provides group-level, whole-brain voxelwise results (FWE cluster-corrected maps and coordinate peaks) and accompanying ROI/PPIs, and reports participant age/sex, cue type, scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM8). These elements satisfy the meta-analysis requirements for cue-reactivity using visual stimuli and whole-brain reporting.

studies.title: Pain and reward circuits antagonistically modulate alcohol expectancy to regulate drinking.
studies.abstract: Expectancy of physical and social pleasure (PSP) promotes excessive drinking despite the potential aversive effects of misuse, suggesting an imbalance in the response to reward and pain in alcohol seeking. Here, we investigated the competing roles of the reward and pain circuits in PSP expectancy and problem drinking in humans. Using fMRI data during resting (n = 180) and during alcohol cue exposure (n = 71), we examined the antagonistic effects of the reward-related medial orbitofrontal cortex (mOFC) and pain-related periaqueductal gray (PAG) connectivities on PSP expectancy and drinking severity. The two regions' connectivity maps and strengths were characterized to assess their shared substrates and net relationship with PSP expectancy. We evaluated mediation and path models to further delineate how mOFC and PAG connectivities interacted through the shared substrates to differentially impact expectancy and alcohol use. During resting, whole-brain regressions showed mOFC connectivity in positive and PAG connectivity in negative association with PSP scores, with convergence in the precentral gyrus (PrCG). Notably, greater PAG-PrCG relative to mOFC-PrCG connectivity strength predicted lower PSP expectancy. During the alcohol cue exposure task, the net strength of the PAG vs. mOFC cue-elicited connectivity with the occipital cortex again negatively predicted PSP expectancy. Finally, mediation and path models revealed that the PAG and mOFC connectivities indirectly and antagonistically modulated problem drinking via their opposing influences on expectancy and craving. Thus, the pain and reward circuits exhibit functional antagonism such that the mOFC connectivity increases expectancy of drinking pleasure whereas the PAG serves to counter that effect.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: This is an original, English-language human fMRI study that includes an alcohol cue-exposure task assessing drug-related cue-reactivity. It meets inclusion criteria for empirical data (I1) and fMRI cue-reactivity with drug cues (I2).
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an fMRI study of alcohol cue reactivity using visual stimuli (alcohol vs neutral images), with a within-subject contrast (alcohol > neutral) reported. Group-level, whole-brain task-evoked results are presented (alcohol>neutral activations in mOFC, occipital cortex, PCC, SFG) and whole-brain analyses (voxelwise rsults and contrasts) are described. Participant age and sex are reported, cue type is alcohol, scanner field strength (3T) is specified, and preprocessing/analysis details are referenced in the Supplementary Methods (satisfying reporting of processing pipeline/software). Therefore the study meets inclusion criteria for visual drug-related cue-reactivity fMRI with whole-brain group results and requisite participant and methods information.

studies.title: Brain activity and desire for Internet video game play.
studies.abstract: Recent studies have suggested that the brain circuitry mediating cue-induced desire for video games is similar to that elicited by cues related to drugs and alcohol. We hypothesized that desire for Internet video games during cue presentation would activate similar brain regions to those that have been linked with craving for drugs or pathologic gambling.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Empirical fMRI study in humans examining cue-induced desire for Internet video game play (natural reward). Abstract indicates cue presentation during fMRI; while sensory modality isn’t explicitly stated, video-game cue studies typically use visual stimuli. Include for full-text review to confirm modality and extract data.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This study is an empirical fMRI investigation of cue-induced desire for an Internet video game using visual stimuli. It reports within-subject contrasts of video game cues > neutral control stimuli, conducted group-level, whole-brain random-effects voxelwise analyses with cluster-level FDR correction, and presents coordinates for significant clusters. Relevant participant information (age, sex), cue type (Internet video game), scanner field strength (3.0 T), and processing software (Brain Voyager BVQX) are provided. The design and reported results directly map onto cue-reactivity for a non-drug natural reward (video gaming) using visual cues, meeting the meta-analysis objective. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity/resting-state-only, between-group-only without task effect, or non-empirical) are met.

Abstract reports an empirical fMRI study of alcohol cue reactivity in alcohol-dependent young women (meets I1). The abstract does not specify the modality of alcohol stimuli (visual vs other); therefore include for full-text review to confirm visual stimulus use and fMRI task details.

Abstract reports an empirical human fMRI study of cue-induced cocaine craving (satisfies I1). Abstract reports an empirical human study examining neural responses to alcohol cues, but does not explicitly state that functional MRI was used or that visual stimuli were presented. Insufficient info to confirm I2, so include for full-text review.

Empirical human study (English) reporting original data using BOLD fMRI during presentation of visual smoking cues versus neutral stimuli; directly addresses cue-reactivity to a drug-related visual stimulus. No exclusion criteria met.

Abstract provides only a brief background sentence and does not state whether functional MRI was used or whether visual cues were employed. Insufficient information to determine I2; include for full-text review to assess fMRI and visual-stimulus criteria.

Abstract addresses cocaine cue-reactivity and neuroanatomy and appears to report original empirical data, but does not explicitly state that fMRI was used or that cues were visual. Include for full-text review to confirm fMRI and visual-stimulus methods.

Empirical, English-language human fMRI study reporting original data measuring brain reactivity to smoking-related versus neutral images (visual stimuli) in tobacco-dependent subjects, directly addressing cue-reactivity. No exclusion criteria met.

Original empirical human fMRI study using visual alcohol cues (pictures) to assess cue-reactivity; directly matches the review objective of visual drug-related cue-reactivity. Abstract provides sufficient information to include.
Human empirical fMRI study (N=41) in English assessing mesolimbic cue reactivity to alcohol-related pictures (visual stimuli) with original data; directly matches review objective.

Abstract does not report use of fMRI or visual cue-reactivity; it appears to be a pharmacologic/behavioral study of craving and withdrawal. Because the abstract lacks sufficient information to confirm fMRI during visual cue exposure, include for full-text review to verify eligibility.

Abstract reports original empirical data and a cue-reactivity fMRI experiment in alcohol-addicted humans, satisfying I1. However, the abstract does not specify that the cues were visual, so I2 cannot be confirmed from the abstract alone. Include for full-text review to determine stimulus modality.

Abstract reports an original human fMRI study examining cue-reactivity to drug cues (nicotine and alcohol), satisfying I1. The abstract does not specify that visual stimuli were used, so I2 cannot be confirmed from the abstract alone; include for full-text review to determine stimulus modality.

Human fMRI study with original data examining smoking cue-reactivity using visual smoking vs nonsmoking cues; matches meta-analysis objective and inclusion criteria. Not a review and not limited to non-visual cues.

Human fMRI study reporting cue-evoked and outcome responses during a monetary incentive delay task in substance-dependent patients and controls, so it addresses reward-related cue-reactivity with original data. This is an original human fMRI study examining cue-reactivity to cocaine cues (versus appetitive control stimuli) during an fMRI cue-reactivity task. Abstract reports structural MRI measures (striatal volume, surface area, shape) related to craving; it does not report a functional MRI scan conducted during cue-reactivity. Therefore it does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (fMRI during visual cue exposure).

Abstract explicitly refers to exposure to alcohol taste cues (gustatory) rather than visual stimuli required by the review. This indicates cue-reactivity used non-visual sensory modality, so it should be excluded.

Abstract describes an original human fMRI study of heroin cue-elicited craving (empirical data). However, it does not specify that cues were visual. Insufficient information on stimulus modality, so include for full-text review to confirm use of visual stimuli.

Empirical fMRI study in English with human participants; used visual smoking-related cues (2D and 3D/VR) to elicit cue-induced craving, which matches the review objective of visual cue-reactivity for drug-related rewards.

Abstract reports an English-language empirical fMRI study with original data (I1). English-language empirical fMRI study in human participants assessing neural responses to favorite-food visual cues (natural rewards). Matches meta-analysis objective and satisfies inclusion criteria I1 and I2.

This article is a systematic/meta-analytic review rather than an empirical fMRI study with original data assessing visual cue-reactivity. It does not report fMRI cue-reactivity using visual stimuli, so it meets exclusion criterion E1.

Human fMRI study reporting original data (chronic cocaine users and controls) that tested extinction of drug and pleasant cue associations using conditioned abstract cues (visual stimuli). This paper is a systematic review/meta-analysis rather than an original fMRI study of cue-reactivity; it does not report original fMRI data using visual drug or natural reward cues, so it meets exclusion criterion E1.

The study reports an original, single-blinded functional MRI study in human smokers (empirical data). Title suggests an original human study of alcohol cue-induced brain activation likely relevant to cue-reactivity, but the abstract text provided does not explicitly state that functional MRI was used or that cues were visual. Original English-language fMRI study in humans assessing cue-reactivity to visual natural reward stimuli (pictures of high-calorie food). Matches inclusion criteria; no exclusion criteria met.

Randomized trial reports original empirical data using real-time fMRI neurofeedback targeting cue-reactivity in alcohol use disorder (English). Empirical fMRI study with original data in English assessing drug cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (opioid-word Stroop). Human participants with OUD; task contrasted opioid vs neutral words, meeting review focus on visual drug-related cues.

Abstract describes an fMRI study of effort-related valuation and motivation in alcohol-dependent and control participants, but does not mention cue-reactivity to drug/natural rewards or use of visual stimuli. Empirical human fMRI study reporting original data on drug-cue reactivity across cocaine, alcohol, and nicotine users. However it does not state that cue-reactivity used visual stimuli or explicit visual reward cues, so inclusion for full-text review is warranted to determine I2.

This study reports resting-state fMRI before/after e-cigarette use and measures withdrawal/craving, not a cue-reactivity paradigm using visual stimuli. It does not assess drug/natural reward cue-reactivity with visual cues, so it does not meet the review objective.

Human fMRI study presenting visual cocaine and neutral cues to chronic cocaine users and controls; original empirical data assessing drug cue-reactivity using visual stimuli, matching the meta-analysis objective.

Human fMRI study (n=383) examining neural responses to alcohol cues and DRD2 methylation, meeting I1. The abstract describes cue-elicited activation but does not explicitly state the sensory modality (visual). Visual-stimuli criterion (I2) is therefore unclear; include for full-text review to confirm. No exclusion criteria met.

Human fMRI study using visual appetitive food stimuli to assess reward-related cue-reactivity; original empirical data in English. Matches inclusion criteria for language/original data and fMRI with visual natural reward cues.

Abstract appears to report original empirical data (I1) but does not explicitly state that a functional MRI scan was used. Because it is unclear whether the study conducted fMRI during visual cue exposure (I2), include for full-text review to determine eligibility.

Abstract focuses on stress-related impulsivity in alcoholism and does not specify fMRI cue-reactivity to drug or natural reward visual cues or the use of visual stimuli. Insufficient information to confirm I2; include for full-text review to determine eligibility.

Abstract examines human alcohol cue-processing but does not specify use of functional MRI or that cues were visual. Therefore insufficient information to confirm I2 from abstract alone; include for full-text review to determine imaging modality and stimulus type.

Abstract describes an original human fMRI study in English (I1). However, it reports a probabilistic delay-discounting task examining reward magnitude/delay in AUD, not clearly a visual cue-reactivity paradigm. Because the abstract does not explicitly state fMRI cue-reactivity to drug or natural visual cues (I2 unclear), I include it for full-text review to confirm relevance.

English-language empirical fMRI study with original data in human participants assessing cue-reactivity to visual (tobacco advertisement) stimuli related to a drug (nicotine). Not a review and does not use only non-visual cues.

Empirical human fMRI study of cue-reactivity to food and cocaine in active cocaine abusers (original data). Abstract does not explicitly state that the cues were visual, so modality is unclear; include for full-text review to confirm use of visual stimuli.

This is a structural MRI volumetric study (amygdala/hippocampus volumes) in cocaine addicts vs controls, not a functional MRI cue-reactivity study using visual stimuli. Therefore it does not meet inclusion criterion I2. It is an English empirical study (I1).

The study used PET (18FDG) imaging, not functional MRI, so it does not meet I2 (fMRI requirement). It is an English empirical study (I1 met) and uses visual cues, but imaging modality disqualifies it for this fMRI-focused meta-analysis.

The study used a gustatory (alcohol taste) cue during fMRI rather than visual stimuli. The review excludes cue-reactivity studies using only non-visual sensory cues. Although it is an original fMRI study of alcohol cue reactivity, the cue modality does not match the meta-analysis objective.

Original human neuroimaging study reporting fMRI responses to marijuana cues and craving in treatment-seeking, marijuana-dependent subjects (meets I1). The abstract does not specify that the cue modality was visual, so I2 cannot be confirmed from the abstract; include for full-text review to verify visual-stimulus criterion.

Abstract examines acute heroin effects on negative emotional processing and stress-related amygdala activity, not fMRI cue-reactivity to drug or natural reward-related visual stimuli. No evidence it assessed reward-related visual cues, so it does not meet I2.
English-language empirical study with original data; used functional MRI in human nicotine-dependent smokers while they viewed visual smoking cues and measured cue-induced craving, meeting I1 and I2.

Abstract reports an original English-language empirical fMRI study that compared brain activation during pictorial presentation of heroin-related cues (visual stimuli) in treatment groups. This meets inclusion criteria I1 and I2; no exclusion criteria apply.

Study used functional near-infrared spectroscopy (fNIRS), not functional MRI; thus it does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (fMRI during visual cue-reactivity). I1 (English original data) is satisfied.
Empirical human fMRI study measuring neural responses to visual smoking cues; original data in English. Fits review objective of visual drug cue-reactivity.

This is an English-language empirical study reporting fMRI in alcohol-dependent patients with cue exposure (neutral, alcohol, stress). The abstract does not specify cue modality (visual vs other), so inclusion criterion I2 cannot be confirmed; therefore include for full-text review to determine whether visual stimuli were used.

Abstract reports an empirical fMRI study in human nicotine-dependent subjects assessing smoking-related cue-reactivity but does not specify stimulus modality (visual vs. other). Insufficient information to confirm I2; include for full-text review to determine whether visual cues were used.

Abstract reports PET ([11C]-(+)-PHNO) study of dopaminergic binding in smokers related to nicotine metabolism, not an fMRI study and does not describe visual cue-reactivity. Therefore it does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (fMRI cue-reactivity). It is an English empirical study (I1 met).

Abstract reports an original human fMRI study in English assessing brain responses to opioid-related visual cues in patients with opioid use disorder, directly addressing visual cue-reactivity. Therefore it meets inclusion criteria for original data and visual fMRI cue-reactivity.

This is an empirical human BOLD-fMRI study examining neural reactivity to smoking cues (visual stimuli) in relation to nicotine status and genotype, matching the review objective and inclusion criteria.

Abstract reports an English-language empirical fMRI study in humans that used visual cues paired with a natural (juice) reward to assess reward-related cue processing; meets inclusion criteria and does not meet exclusion criteria.

This is a study protocol (no original empirical data reported) and does not describe an fMRI cue-reactivity task using visual drug/natural reward cues. It therefore fails inclusion criteria I1 and I2.

Empirical human study targeting drug cue reactivity with neurostimulation and reported frontal/striatal reactivity; abstract does not explicitly state fMRI during visual cue presentation, so inclusion criterion I2 cannot be confirmed from abstract alone. Include for full-text review to verify fMRI and visual stimuli details.

Abstract reports original empirical neuroimaging work on neural responses to smoking and monetary rewards but does not explicitly state that functional MRI was used or that visual cue-reactivity was the stimulus modality. Insufficient detail to confirm I2 from the abstract alone; include for full-text review to verify fMRI and visual cue procedures.
Human fMRI study (original data) assessing alcohol cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (beverage pictures); meets inclusion I1 and I2. No exclusion criteria apply.

English-language empirical study reporting fMRI in human methadone-maintained patients in response to visual heroin-related cues, directly matching the meta-analysis focus on visual drug cue-reactivity.

Original human fMRI study (15 cocaine users, 15 controls) using visual drug-related words versus neutral words to assess cue-reactivity; meets both inclusion criteria for empirical English fMRI research with visual drug-cue stimuli.

Original fMRI study in humans examining brain responses to smoking (drug) cues, meeting I1. Abstract confirms fMRI cue-reactivity but does not specify stimulus modality (visual). Include for full-text review to confirm I2.

Empirical human fMRI study assessing neural responses to visual smoking-related cues (cigarette) versus neutral stimuli in smokers, satisfying original data and visual cue-reactivity criteria. No exclusion criteria met.

This is an English-language empirical fMRI study that used an alcohol-related visual go/no-go task to examine cue reactivity and response inhibition in humans. It meets inclusion criteria I1 and I2; no exclusion criteria apply.

The abstract reports an English-language randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled human fMRI study examining neural reactivity to cocaine cues (drug cue-reactivity). It does not specify the sensory modality of cues (visual vs. other), so include for full-text review to confirm stimulus modality.

English-language empirical human study reporting neural cue reactivity to cannabis cues (original data). Abstract does not explicitly state that fMRI was used or that cues were visual, so insufficient information to confirm I2; include for full-text review.

Empirical fMRI study in nicotine-dependent humans using visual smoking-related cues vs similar neutral images; reports neural cue-reactivity (insula, midline cortex) during a visual DMS task. Meets inclusion criteria for English original data and fMRI with visual drug-related cues.

Randomized clinical trial with original empirical data reporting an fMRI alcohol cue-reactivity task in treatment-seeking individuals with alcohol dependence. This addresses drug-related cue-reactivity using fMRI and is in English. The abstract indicates cue-reactivity (visual alcohol cues are standard in such tasks), meeting inclusion criteria for the review.

This is an English-language empirical study with original data that used fMRI in human participants to assess alcohol-related cue-reactivity with alcohol-related visual stimuli presented during scanning. It directly meets the review objective and does not meet any exclusion criteria.

Abstract reports an original human study examining brain reactivity to smoking cues across sites (English language). However, it does not explicitly state use of functional MRI or that visual stimuli were used; include for full-text review to confirm I2.

Empirical human fMRI study (arterial spin-labeled perfusion) assessing neural responses to smoking vs nonsmoking cues (visual cue exposure) in treatment-seeking smokers; original data are reported, meeting inclusion criteria.

Human fMRI study reporting original data on neural responses during regulation and passive viewing of personally craved vs. not-craved energy-dense (food) cues, indicating visual cue-reactivity. Published in English in a peer-reviewed journal. Thus meets inclusion criteria for original English fMRI studies of natural reward (food) visual cue-reactivity.

Human fMRI study reporting original data on heroin cue-reactivity and prospective relapse prediction. Abstract does not specify the sensory modality of the cues (visual vs. others), so inclusion for full-text review is necessary to confirm use of visual stimuli.

Study reports original human fMRI data assessing neural responsivity to food cues (a natural reward), meeting I1. The abstract does not specify the stimulus modality (visual vs. gustatory/olfactory), so I2 cannot be confirmed from the abstract; include for full-text review to determine modality.

Abstract describes molecular imaging of beta2 nicotinic receptor availability during tobacco abstinence, not an fMRI study of drug or natural reward-related cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. It does not report an fMRI task or visual cue-reactivity, so it fails inclusion criterion I2.

Abstract describes an original human fMRI study (I1) but does not clearly report drug- or natural-reward visual cue-reactivity (no mention of visual cues or cue-reactivity). Unclear from abstract whether visual cue-reactivity was assessed; include for full-text review to determine I2.

This is an English-language empirical fMRI study in humans using a cocaine-word Stroop task (visual drug cues), directly addressing drug cue-reactivity with original data. It is not a review and does not use only non-visual cues, so it meets inclusion criteria for full-text review.

Abstract reports original empirical data and mentions functional/structural alterations but does not specify that fMRI was performed during visual cue‑reactivity tasks. Insufficient information to determine I2 from the abstract alone; include for full-text review.

Abstract reports a genome-wide association study of nicotine withdrawal (genetics), with no human fMRI or visual cue-reactivity paradigm described. It therefore does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (fMRI cue-reactivity).

Empirical English-language fMRI study measuring brain responses during a cannabis word Stroop (visual cues) in humans; directly assesses drug-related cue-reactivity and craving. Meets both inclusion criteria.

Abstract reports amygdala volumes (structural MRI) and craving in adolescent marijuana users during abstinence; it does not describe a functional MRI cue-reactivity task using visual stimuli. Does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (fMRI visual cue-reactivity).

Abstract reports original human data on brain responses to drug cues in heroin-dependent individuals (satisfies I1). It does not explicitly state that a functional MRI was used or that visual stimuli were employed, so inclusion cannot be confirmed from abstract alone; include for full-text review.
Original human fMRI study (2009) investigating cigarette cravings with smoking-related images (visual stimuli), matching the meta-analysis objective of drug cue-reactivity using visual cues.

Original, English fMRI study of human heroin cue-reactivity (meets I1). Abstract confirms cue-reactivity and fMRI but does not specify that cues were visual; modality unclear. Include for full-text review to confirm visual stimuli and fully satisfy I2.

This is a PET ([11C]raclopride) study, not an fMRI study, and it does not assess visual cue-reactivity to drug or natural rewards; therefore it fails inclusion criterion I2. It is an English empirical study (I1) but does not meet the required imaging/cue-reactivity criteria.

Human fMRI study reporting neural responses to desirable food cues (natural rewards) following walnut consumption; randomized crossover design indicates original empirical data reported in English. The study assesses cue-reactivity to food (visual) stimuli, meeting the review objective and inclusion criteria.

Abstract does not specify that fMRI was conducted during drug-related visual cue-reactivity (I2). It is an empirical human study in English addressing neural changes in smokers (I1). Insufficient information to confirm use of visual cue-reactivity, so include for full-text review to assess I2.

The study used resting-state fMRI to measure spontaneous increases in craving without presenting any visual drug- or reward-related cues. It is not a task-based cue-reactivity study using visual stimuli, so it does not meet inclusion criterion I2.

Human fMRI study (BOLD) using visual alcohol-related cues (sake/drinking images) with original data published in English. It directly assesses drug-related cue-reactivity using visual stimuli and meets inclusion criteria; no exclusion criteria apply.
The study reports resting-state functional connectivity after drug treatment, not an fMRI cue-reactivity task using visual stimuli; thus it does not meet inclusion criterion I2.

English-language original fMRI study with 38 human alcohol-dependent patients assessing cue-reactivity using visual alcohol-related pictures; directly addresses visual cue-reactivity for a drug reward. Clear match to inclusion criteria.

English-language empirical fMRI study in human participants that measured alcohol-related cue-reactivity using visual (picture) stimuli; reports original data on neural responses to alcohol cues. No exclusion criteria apply.

Abstract reports an empirical fMRI study in humans on stress-induced cocaine craving but does not specify whether drug cue-reactivity was assessed using visual stimuli. Insufficient information to confirm I2 from abstract; include for full-text review to determine whether visual cue-reactivity was used.
This is a PET study measuring mu-opioid receptor binding in abstinent cocaine users, not an fMRI study of drug or natural reward cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. It does not meet I2.

Empirical fMRI study of alcohol cue-reactivity (original data, English). Abstract does not specify the sensory modality of cues (visual vs other). Insufficient information about use of visual stimuli; include for full-text review to confirm eligibility.

Title/abstract report preliminary neuroimaging findings in an original empirical study of heavy-drinking smokers, but the abstract does not specify that functional MRI was conducted during a drug- or reward-related cue-reactivity task using visual stimuli. Include for full-text review to confirm imaging modality and cue paradigm. I1 confirmed; I2 unclear from abstract.

Title and abstract indicate an empirical study on drug cue reactivity and neural networks, but the abstract does not explicitly state that fMRI was used or that visual cues were presented. Study is English with original data. Insufficient information to confirm fMRI+visual cue criteria, so include for full-text review.

Abstract describes an fMRI study in heroin-addicted human volunteers assessing cue-induced brain responses and reports original data. However the cue modality (visual vs other) and full-text language are not specified, so include for full-text review.

Original empirical study reporting perfusion fMRI in human smokers during cigarette cue exposure, addressing drug-related cue reactivity. The abstract does not specify cue modality (visual vs other), so inclusion for full-text review is warranted to confirm use of visual stimuli.

Abstract describes an fMRI reward-based spatial learning task with monetary rewards in a virtual environment, not a visual cue-reactivity paradigm for drug or natural rewards. It therefore does not meet I2 (assessing drug or natural reward-related cue-reactivity using visual stimuli). Language and empirical data are present (I1).

This is an English-language empirical study reporting original data using human fMRI to assess alcohol (drug) cue-reactivity with an alcohol cue-reactivity task (visual cues) and measuring ventral striatal activation and drinking outcomes; it meets inclusion criteria I1 and I2.

Empirical human fMRI study reporting cue-induced alcohol craving, but abstract does not specify that cues were visual. Includes original data (single patient). Insufficient information about stimulus modality, so include for full-text review to assess visual cue use.
This is a PET study ([(11)C]raclopride displacement) not an fMRI study; therefore it does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (functional MRI during visual cue-reactivity).

The abstract describes cue-reactivity via mental re-enactment of personalized scripts (imagery/anticipation of shock), not visual cue presentation. Therefore it does not meet the review's requirement for visual-stimulus cue-reactivity and should be excluded.

Empirical English fMRI study with original data examining neural responses to cannabis-related visual cues in human participants (frequent, sporadic, and controls). Matches inclusion criteria I1 and I2. Not a review and does not use only non-visual cues.

Abstract reports original empirical fMRI data in human regular marijuana users during a cue-elicited craving paradigm with exposure to marijuana cues; meets inclusion for original data and fMRI cue-reactivity. Stimulus modality not explicitly stated but likely visual; include for full-text to confirm.

Human fMRI study in English reporting original data on brain responses to visual food cues after administration of a GLP-1 receptor agonist in T2DM participants — fits visual cue-reactivity for natural rewards. No exclusion criteria met in abstract.

Abstract describes an fMRI study of heroin cue-induced brain responses in human abstinent heroin users (original empirical data). However, it does not specify the sensory modality of the cues (visual vs. other). Include for full-text review to confirm use of visual stimuli.

Meets inclusion criteria: English empirical study with original data (PLOS One, 2013) and event-related fMRI in human heroin-dependent participants using visual heroin-related cue pictures versus neutral controls. No exclusion criteria apply based on the abstract.

Abstract reads as a narrative review/discussion rather than an empirical study: no methods, participants, or original fMRI results are reported. It summarizes evidence and discusses future directions, so it is not an original fMRI study of visual cue-reactivity and should be excluded as a review article.

The study evoked craving via highly individual verbal/event recall cues (individual verbal cues), not visual stimuli. Inclusion criterion I2 requires fMRI cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. This meets exclusion E2 (cue-reactivity using only non-visual/other sensory or internal cues).
This study used resting-state fMRI and did not assess drug or natural reward cue-reactivity with visual stimuli; it does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (task-based visual cue-reactivity).

English-language empirical fMRI study in human cocaine-dependent patients using a cocaine Stroop task (visual stimuli) to assess drug cue-related attentional bias. Fits the meta-analysis objective of fMRI studies of drug cue-reactivity using visual stimuli; no exclusion criteria apply.

Abstract reports an original empirical neuroimaging study of alcohol cue–induced ventral striatum activation, but does not specify that functional MRI was used during visual cue exposure. Insufficient information to confirm I2, so include for full-text review to determine modality and stimulus modality.
Empirical human fMRI study using visual smoking (drug) cues with original data; directly addresses cue-reactivity to drug-related visual stimuli and matches the meta-analysis objective.

English-language empirical fMRI study with original data in humans with cocaine use disorders using a visual drug Stroop task (drug vs matched neutral words) to assess cue-reactivity; meets I1 and I2. No exclusion criteria apply.

Abstract does not specify that the fMRI task used drug- or natural reward-related visual cues; it describes a multisensory Stroop in cocaine users. Because it’s unclear whether this is a visual cue-reactivity paradigm, include for full-text review to confirm.

Study uses resting-state fMRI and DTI and does not report any task-based cue-reactivity with visual stimuli; thus it does not meet inclusion criterion I2. It is an English empirical study with original data (I1).

Abstract reports original fMRI data examining cannabis cue-related neural activation in adolescent cannabis users, indicating an English empirical study of drug cue-reactivity. The abstract does not explicitly state that cues were visual, so include for full-text review to confirm stimulus modality.

This is an English-language empirical fMRI study of alcohol cue-reactivity in human participants using alcohol versus neutral cues (visual stimuli implied), matching both inclusion criteria. No exclusion criteria apply.
Original empirical human fMRI study explicitly investigating smoking-related cue-reactivity using pictorial (visual) stimuli, directly matching the meta-analysis objective and inclusion criteria.

Empirical human fMRI study (original data) that assessed cue-reactivity to drug (cannabis) and natural reward using visual stimuli. Although tactile cues were also used, visual cues were explicitly presented, satisfying the meta-analysis criteria.

Abstract reports an English-language empirical study assessing neural mechanisms of cue reactivity in cocaine users but does not explicitly state that functional MRI was used or that visual stimuli were presented. Insufficient detail to confirm fMRI with visual cues—include for full-text review to verify I2.

Abstract describes an original empirical human study on alcohol cue processing but does not specify use of functional MRI or visual stimuli. Because methods are insufficiently reported to determine I2, include for full-text review to confirm fMRI and visual cue use.

Human fMRI study assessing neural reactivity to visual alcohol cues in alcohol-dependent patients vs controls; original empirical data in English. Meets inclusion criteria for fMRI cue-reactivity with visual drug-related stimuli.

The study used individualized script-driven guided imagery (internal/auditory imagery), not visual stimuli for cue exposure. Therefore it does not meet the requirement that cue-reactivity be assessed using visual stimuli (exclusion E2).

The study used resting-state fMRI to examine DLPFC connectivity in smokers and IGD without any drug- or reward-related cue-reactivity task or visual stimulus exposure; therefore it does not meet inclusion criterion I2.

Empirical fMRI study of reward anticipation in alcohol-dependent humans (meets I1). Abstract does not specify cue modality or confirm use of visual stimuli for reward cue-reactivity, so inclusion cannot be ruled out from abstract alone. Include for full-text review to determine whether visual cue-reactivity (I2) was used.

Abstract reports an original fMRI study in humans (abstinent cocaine users and controls) examining responses to monetary reward magnitudes during a counting Stroop task. However, it does not clearly describe 'cue-reactivity' to drug or natural rewards using visual stimuli; it's unclear whether monetary incentives constitute visual cue-reactivity. Insufficient information to confirm I2, so include for full-text review.
Original human experimental study using fMRI to assess alcohol cue reactivity (visual alcohol cue reactivity task) in alcohol-dependent participants; not a review. Satisfies I1 and I2.

The abstract does not report any functional MRI during cue-reactivity; it reports diffusion tensor imaging (WM), telomere length, and eye-tracking. It therefore does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (fMRI cue-reactivity).

Human fMRI study in English reporting original data on neural responses to smoking-related visual cues (cue reactivity). This directly matches the meta-analysis objective of fMRI studies using visual drug-related cues.

Abstract reports an original human fMRI study assessing brain responses to images of high-calorie foods and measures appetite/food cravings. This meets inclusion for empirical English-language fMRI research using visual cue-reactivity; it is not a review and does not rely solely on non-visual cues.

English-language empirical fMRI study in humans assessing alcohol (drug) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (also olfactory). Abstract reports original data and fMRI cue-reactivity with visual cues, meeting inclusion criteria; no exclusion criterion clearly met.
Human fMRI study reporting original data in English using visual heroin-related cues in early-abstinent heroin users; meets inclusion criteria for drug cue-reactivity with visual stimuli.

Abstract describes an empirical study of craving in alcoholic patients but does not state that fMRI or visual cues were used. I1 (English empirical study) appears met; I2 is unclear. Include for full-text review to determine fMRI and visual cue use.

Empirical, English-language study reporting original data using event-related functional MRI in human dependent smokers with visual smoking cues; directly assesses drug-related cue-reactivity using visual stimuli, meeting inclusion criteria.
Empirical fMRI study in humans (smokers) with original data, but abstract does not state whether cue-reactivity was assessed using visual stimuli. Include for full-text review to confirm I2.

This is a clinical trial protocol of rTMS and does not report original empirical fMRI data assessing drug or natural reward cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. It therefore does not meet inclusion criteria I1 and I2.

Original English-language fMRI study in human nicotine-dependent smokers using smoking-related visual cues to elicit craving and measure BOLD responses; directly addresses visual cue-reactivity for drug reward. Not a review.

Abstract reports an English-language empirical fMRI study assessing cue-reactivity in cocaine-dependent patients and healthy controls, satisfying I1. The abstract does not specify the sensory modality of the cues (visual vs. other), so inclusion for full-text review is warranted to confirm I2.

Abstract reports fMRI of stress imagery in abstinent cocaine users, not fMRI cue-reactivity to drug or natural reward-related visual stimuli. It therefore does not meet the review’s cue-reactivity/visual-stimulus inclusion criterion.

The study used olfactory cues (alcohol, appetitive, and non-appetitive odors) during fMRI. The meta-analysis requires visual stimuli for cue-reactivity; this study used only non-visual (olfactory) cues, meeting exclusion criterion E2.

Abstract reports an empirical human study reporting cue-elicited brain activation in treatment-seeking alcoholics (RCT with follow-up), but does not specify the sensory modality of cues. fMRI is implied but cue modality (visual) is not explicitly stated — include for full-text review to confirm visual stimuli.
English-language randomized fMRI study in humans assessing cannabis-related cue-reactivity; fits meta-analysis of visual drug-related cue-reactivity and is an original empirical study.

Empirical fMRI study in humans assessing alcohol cue reactivity (original data) per abstract. Abstract does not specify cue sensory modality (visual vs. other), so inclusion for full-text review is warranted to confirm visual stimuli.

This study used resting-state fMRI after rTMS in smokers and did not assess cue-reactivity to drug or natural reward visual stimuli. It therefore does not meet the required cue-reactivity visual-stimuli fMRI criterion (I2).

Study used resting-state fMRI and examined intrinsic insula connectivity related to relapse, not a cue-reactivity task using visual stimuli. It therefore does not meet the required I2 criterion (visual cue-reactivity).

The study is an empirical human fMRI study addressing tobacco craving, so it meets I1. However the abstract does not specify that cue-reactivity was assessed using visual stimuli (or that the fMRI task involved visual drug-related cues). Because information on visual cue-reactivity is insufficient in the abstract, include for full-text review to determine I2.

The abstract describes an original English-language fMRI study (3T) with human participants viewing images (visual stimuli) of online games, smoking, and neutral images to assess cue-induced craving/urge. It is not a review and does not use non-visual cues, satisfying inclusion criteria.

Abstract reports an original empirical study on attentional bias to cocaine cues but does not specify that a functional MRI scan was used or that visual stimuli were employed for cue-reactivity. Insufficient information to confirm I2, so include for full-text review.

Abstract describes an empirical investigation of cue-elicited craving (nicotine) and cortical activity but does not explicitly state fMRI or that visual stimuli were used. Insufficient detail to determine imaging modality/stimulus type from abstract alone—include for full-text review to verify I2.

The study used resting-state fMRI rather than a cue-reactivity task with visual stimuli, so it does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (drug/natural reward cue-reactivity using visual stimuli). It is an English empirical study (I1 met) but clearly not a task-based visual cue-reactivity fMRI study.

English-language empirical fMRI study with original data that scanned human heroin addicts while exposing them to visual cues (drug-related and water-related) to assess cue-reactivity. Fits the meta-analysis focus on drug and natural reward (water) visual cue-reactivity. No exclusion criteria apply.
Empirical fMRI study in English using visual high-calorie food cues (natural reward) in human participants, directly assessing cue-reactivity. No exclusion criteria met.

Abstract reports a human experimental pharmacological study of aprepitant in comorbid PTSD and alcohol dependence but does not mention any functional MRI or visual cue-reactivity. Information is insufficient to confirm the fMRI visual cue-reactivity requirement (I2), so include for full-text review. Study is English empirical original research (I1).

The study uses resting-state functional connectivity and DTI, not a task-based fMRI cue-reactivity paradigm with visual (drug or natural reward) stimuli; therefore it does not meet inclusion criterion I2.

Human fMRI study with original data examining alcohol (drug) cue-reactivity during a decisions-to-drink task. Abstract does not explicitly state the cue modality is visual, so inclusion for full-text review is warranted to confirm visual stimuli were used.

Abstract reports an fMRI study in heroin users using psychological-inducing movie cues (visual stimuli) but does not specify whether cues were drug- or reward-related cue-reactivity. The study is in English and appears empirical with original data. Insufficient detail to confirm I2, so include for full-text review to determine eligibility.

Abstract reports an original fMRI study in humans (meets I1) but does not state that fMRI assessed drug or natural reward cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. Insufficient information in abstract to determine I2, so include for full-text review to verify whether visual cue-reactivity was used.

Human, English-language empirical fMRI study assessing cue-reactivity to smoking using visual stimuli (smoking-related pictures vs control). Fits the meta-analysis objective of visual drug cue-reactivity.

This is a study protocol (no original empirical fMRI data reported). It does not meet inclusion criterion I1 (empirical studies with original data). Abstract does not present fMRI results of visual cue-reactivity, so exclude based on lack of original data.

Abstract reports an English-language empirical human study and mentions fMRI responses to affective stimuli, but it does not specify that fMRI was conducted during visual drug or natural reward cue-reactivity tasks. Include for full-text review to confirm whether I2 (visual cue-reactivity fMRI) is met.

Study reports original human fMRI data using visual picture cues (alcohol, marijuana, polydrug) to probe cue-related responses, matching the review focus on drug-related visual cue-reactivity. Title/abstract indicate empirical data in college students and use of visual stimuli during fMRI.
Empirical fMRI study in humans (smokers and controls) using visual smoking cues during a task; directly examines neural cue-reactivity to drug-related visual stimuli. No exclusion criteria met.

Original fMRI study in humans reporting food cue reactivity (natural reward) and linking neural responses to consumption and BMI. Language and empirical data present. Abstract does not explicitly state modality of cues, so include for full-text review to confirm visual stimuli.

Abstract states the cue was an alcohol taste (gustatory) stimulus rather than visual stimuli. The review includes only fMRI cue-reactivity using visual stimuli, so this study meets exclusion criterion E2.

Empirical human fMRI study in English reporting original data. Used visual stimuli (gambling and smoking pictures) to assess cue-reactivity in problem gamblers and smokers, matching the meta-analysis objective on drug and natural reward–related visual cue-reactivity.

Human fMRI study with original data assessing alcohol cue-reactivity in recently detoxified patients. Abstract does not specify that cue stimuli were visual, so include for full-text review to confirm stimulus modality.

The fMRI component used personalized script-driven imagery (mental/aural scripts) for stress and drug cues, not visual stimuli. This fails the visual-stimuli cue-reactivity requirement (I2) and meets exclusion E2. Study is English with original data (I1 satisfied).

This is a neurosurgical case report of an anterior cingulate implant for alcohol dependence; abstract contains no evidence of functional MRI during visual cue-reactivity or use of visual drug/natural reward cues. Not an fMRI cue-reactivity study.

Abstract reports fMRI in nicotine-dependent smokers and references cue-elicited craving, but does not specify that the fMRI assessed cue-reactivity with visual stimuli. Insufficient detail to confirm I2, so include for full-text review to determine whether visual cue-reactivity was used.

English-language empirical study presenting original data; neuroimaging assessed cue-induced brain activity to visual alcohol cues (alcohol drinking scenes) in human participants, matching fMRI cue-reactivity with visual stimuli.

This is a resting-state fMRI study of ventral striatal connectivity in cocaine dependence, not a task-based cue-reactivity study using visual stimuli. Therefore it does not meet the key inclusion criterion I2.

English-language empirical fMRI study in humans (n=32) using visual heroin-related cues to assess cue-induced craving (BOLD fMRI). Original data; uses visual stimuli for drug cue-reactivity, no exclusion criteria met.
English-language empirical fMRI study with original data in human smokers assessing nicotine cue-reactivity using visual cues in a virtual environment. Meets I1 and I2; no exclusion criteria met.

Human fMRI study investigating neural responses to food cues (a natural reward) in adolescents; original empirical data. While the abstract does not explicitly state the sensory modality of cues, ‘food cues’ in fMRI cue-reactivity studies are typically visual and the study aims match the review objective. Full text can confirm stimulus modality.

This study used structural MRI to compare hippocampal and amygdala volumes and examined genetic variation; it did not report a functional MRI cue-reactivity task or visual cue-reactivity. Therefore it does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (fMRI cue-reactivity using visual stimuli).

Abstract describes a resting-state fMRI ReHo comparison between heroin relapsers and non-relapsers under MMT with no task-based cue-reactivity or visual stimulus paradigm mentioned; therefore it does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (cue-reactivity with visual stimuli).

The study uses resting-state fMRI to assess frontostriatal connectivity and tests medication effects and abstinence; it does not report an fMRI cue-reactivity task using visual drug or natural reward cues (fails I2).

Study is an English-language empirical fMRI study in humans (I1). Abstract does not specify that fMRI was conducted during visual drug-cue exposure or a visual cue-reactivity task, so I2 cannot be confirmed from abstract alone. Include for full-text review to determine eligibility.

Original empirical fMRI study in English with human participants assessing cue-reactivity to visual food pictures (natural reward). Fits the meta-analysis focus on visual cue-reactivity; no exclusion criteria apply.

Abstract reports an English-language empirical human study on brain reward responses to palatable food with GLP-1 manipulation, indicating original data (satisfies I1). The abstract does not specify that functional MRI was used during visual cue-reactivity or whether visual cues (rather than gustatory/consummatory stimuli) were employed, so I2 cannot be confirmed from the abstract. Include for full-text review to determine whether fMRI with visual cue-reactivity was performed.

The abstract describes an original empirical study of cigarette cue-reactivity in schizophrenia smokers reporting brain activations, meeting I1. However it does not explicitly state that a functional MRI scan was used or that cues were visual. Include for full-text review to confirm fMRI and visual-stimulus eligibility (I2).

Abstract reports an original empirical human study examining brain reactivity to smoking images (visual cues), relevant to cue-reactivity. However the abstract does not explicitly state that functional MRI was used, so include for full-text review to confirm fMRI methods.

Abstract describes a narrative review discussing results from neuroimaging studies of alcohol dependence, not an original empirical fMRI study of cue-reactivity with visual stimuli. No evidence of fMRI cue-reactivity methods or original data in humans.

Abstract reports original human fMRI data examining cue-induced cocaine craving, meeting I1. However the abstract does not specify the sensory modality of cues (visual vs other). Because modality is unclear, include for full-text review to confirm I2.

Abstract reports human brain activity during smoking cue exposure, suggesting a cue-reactivity fMRI study, but does not explicitly state use of fMRI or that cues were visual. Insufficient detail to confirm I2 from abstract alone; include for full-text review to verify imaging modality and stimulus modality.

Abstract reports an rtfMRI neurofeedback intervention in nicotine-dependent smokers but does not specify that the fMRI assessed drug cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. I1 (English empirical study) is met; I2 is unclear. Include for full-text review to determine presence of visual cue-reactivity tasks.

This is a resting-state fMRI study of long-term abstinent heroin users, not a task-based cue-reactivity study using visual stimuli. Therefore it does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (cue-reactivity with visual stimuli), though it is an English empirical study (I1).

This is a PET receptor-occupancy study using 2-FA radioligand, not an fMRI study of cue-reactivity with visual stimuli. It does not assess drug or natural reward cue-reactivity using visual cues, failing inclusion criterion I2.

Abstract describes an English-language fMRI study of heroin users with a drug-cue stimulation task (original data), but does not specify the cue modality (visual vs. other). Include for full-text review to confirm whether visual stimuli were used.

This is an English-language empirical fMRI study in human participants using visual pictorial cues of alcohol and soft drinks to assess alcohol-approach bias (cue-reactivity). It directly examines neural responses to drug-related visual cues, meeting the review objective. No exclusion criteria are met.

Original empirical human fMRI study (English) examining brain responses to smoking versus control videotapes (visual stimuli) in cigarette smokers; directly assesses drug cue-reactivity using visual cues.

This study used resting-state fMRI (no task-based cue exposure) rather than a visual cue-reactivity paradigm, so it does not meet inclusion criterion I2. It is an original empirical study (I1 met) but not relevant to fMRI visual cue-reactivity.

Study reports fMRI in human adolescents assessing ventral striatum responses to non-drug (natural) rewards, relevant to the review. The abstract does not specify whether reward cues were visual, so full-text review is needed to confirm use of visual stimuli.

The abstract describes behavioral testing and self-reported substance use; it does not report a functional MRI scan conducted during drug or natural reward-related cue-reactivity with visual stimuli (I2 not met).

Abstract reports original empirical fMRI data in human, nicotine-deprived smokers exposed to visual smoking cues, directly addressing visual cue-reactivity for a drug reward. Satisfies inclusion criteria and does not meet any exclusion criteria based on abstract information.
This is a Medical Hypotheses article proposing a theory; it does not report original empirical fMRI data or methods assessing cue-reactivity with visual stimuli. Not eligible for inclusion.

Abstract describes an rTMS intervention study targeting the superior frontal gyrus to modulate cigarette craving; it does not report conducting functional MRI during visual cue-reactivity. Does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (fMRI cue-reactivity with visual stimuli).

Abstract reports original English-language human data including fMRI measures of drug-cue reactivity in cocaine-addicted participants. Although it does not explicitly state that visual stimuli were used for the cues, fMRI cue-reactivity is central; include for full-text review to confirm stimulus modality.

The study uses fMRI but investigates acute effects of intravenous cocaine on cognitive control (motor inhibition), not cue-reactivity to drug or natural rewards using visual stimuli. It therefore does not meet inclusion criterion I2, so excluded. The paper is an original empirical human fMRI study (satisfies I1).

This is an English-language empirical fMRI study in human participants measuring neural responses to cannabis-related approach/avoidance cues using a Stimulus Response Compatibility task (visual stimuli). It directly assesses drug cue-reactivity and predicts future cannabis problem severity, meeting inclusion criteria.

Abstract reports original human data on neural activation to appetitive (food) cues before and after bariatric surgery, but does not explicitly state use of functional MRI nor that cues were visual. Insufficient information to confirm I2; include for full-text review.

Abstract focuses on nicotine effects on working memory; it does not mention drug- or reward-related cue-reactivity with visual stimuli. Information is insufficient to determine whether fMRI cue-reactivity paradigms were used, so include for full-text review to confirm.

Empirical fMRI study in humans examining drug cue-reactivity (drug vs. neutral) during a rewarded cue-reactivity task with original data. Although the abstract does not explicitly state the cue sensory modality, the task assesses drug-related visual cue-reactivity commonly used; include for full-text review to confirm stimulus modality.

The study examines fMRI responses to pleasant interoceptive (internal bodily) stimuli related to stimulant addiction, not cue-reactivity to drug or natural rewards using visual stimuli. The abstract does not mention visual cues; therefore it meets exclusion criterion E2 (non-visual sensory/interoceptive cues).

Abstract describes PET (O15) neuroimaging and imagery-based craving induction, not fMRI. It did not assess cue-reactivity using external visual stimuli, so it does not meet the review's fMRI-with-visual-cues requirement.

Abstract reports an English-language empirical fMRI study in human college students exposed to alcohol, marijuana, polydrug and emotional picture cues (visual stimuli), directly addressing cue-reactivity. No exclusion criteria apply.

Abstract investigates impulse control/stop-signal performance and neural correlates, not drug or natural reward cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. Does not meet inclusion criterion I2. Study appears to be an original empirical English-language study (I1).

The study used PET (rCMRglc) rather than functional MRI and did not report task-based visual cue-reactivity to drug/natural rewards; therefore it does not meet inclusion criterion I2. It is an English-language empirical study (I1 satisfied).

This article is a study protocol, not an empirical study reporting original fMRI data. It therefore does not satisfy inclusion criterion I1 (original empirical data). The abstract does not present fMRI results of cue-reactivity, so exclusion is appropriate.

Abstract reports an original human fMRI study assessing BOLD response during presentation of alcohol cues (empirical fMRI data). However, it does not specify the sensory modality (visual vs other) of the cues. Include for full-text review to confirm use of visual stimuli required by the review.

Original human fMRI study presenting visual food cues (high-hedonic vs neutral) assessing neuronal response to natural reward (food). Fits the meta-analysis objective of visual cue-reactivity; no exclusion criteria met.
The abstract reports an empirical human fMRI study examining brain responses to visual food cues (natural reward), satisfying I1 and I2. No exclusion criteria apply.

Abstract appears to report original human fMRI research in opioid users but does not state whether the fMRI involved drug or natural reward cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. Insufficient information to confirm I2, so include for full-text review to assess cue-reactivity and visual stimulus use.

This is a PET imaging study measuring β2*-nAChR occupancy after e-cigarette use, not an fMRI study of visual cue-reactivity. It is an English empirical study (I1) but does not meet I2 (fMRI cue-reactivity), so exclude.

Abstract describes an original human study of smoking and monetary reward anticipation (natural reward) but does not explicitly state that fMRI was used or that visual cue-reactivity stimuli were employed. Insufficient detail to confirm I2 from the abstract alone, so include for full-text review to determine eligibility.

Human fMRI study reporting alcohol cue-reactivity in abstinent alcohol-dependent patients (original empirical data, English). The abstract does not specify whether cues were visual, so include for full-text review to confirm I2.

Empirical human fMRI study (original data, English) assessing smoking (drug) cue-reactivity during a cue-reactivity task with smoking vs neutral cues. Although stimulus modality isn’t explicitly stated, cue-reactivity tasks typically use visual cues; include for full-text review.

Abstract describes an fMRI GO–NOGO response inhibition task examining executive dysfunction in cocaine users, not a cue-reactivity paradigm using visual drug or natural reward cues. Therefore it does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (drug/natural reward cue-reactivity with visual stimuli).

Abstract reports an fMRI study with an incentive sustained-attention task in cocaine abusers but does not explicitly state that drug or natural reward-related visual cues were presented or that a cue-reactivity paradigm was used. Modality and cue-reactivity status are unclear; include for full-text review.

Human fMRI study of cocaine-dependent and control participants viewing cocaine-related visual stimuli; original empirical research in English. Meets both inclusion criteria: original data (I1) and fMRI with visual drug-related cue-reactivity (I2).

Abstract does not specify that an fMRI was conducted during visual drug/natural reward cue-reactivity. Appears to be an empirical English study on appetite in schizophrenia but insufficient detail to confirm I2; include for full-text review.

The study uses fMRI to assess working memory effects of tolcapone in abstinent smokers rather than drug- or natural reward-related cue-reactivity to visual stimuli. It does not address cue-reactivity, so it does not meet the review objective (I2).

Empirical human fMRI study examining nicotine cue anticipation, satisfying I1. Abstract does not specify whether cue stimuli were visual, so I2 cannot be confirmed from abstract. Include for full-text review to determine visual stimulus use.

English-language empirical fMRI study reporting original data using a reward-cue antisaccade task (reward vs neutral trials). Reward cues are visual and the study assesses neural responses to reward-related visual cues in human participants, matching the review objective.

Abstract reports an fMRI study in human alcohol-dependent patients with original data (I1). However, it describes a reinforcement-learning task and does not specify drug- or natural reward-related cue-reactivity using visual stimuli, so inclusion based on I2 is unclear. Insufficient information to determine whether visual cue-reactivity was used; include for full-text review.

Abstract describes an English-language randomized trial with original data on alcohol cue-reactivity but does not specify that fMRI was used or that visual stimuli were presented. Insufficient information to determine eligibility for fMRI/visual-cue meta-analysis; include for full-text review to confirm I2.

English empirical fMRI study with human participants using visual stimuli (alcoholic and nonalcoholic pictures) to assess reward-related cue-reactivity in abstinent alcoholics. Not a review and does not use only non-visual cues; meets the review objective.

This is a resting-state fMRI study of heroin-dependent individuals on opioid maintenance; it does not involve task-based cue-reactivity with visual stimuli. Therefore it does not meet the review objective of visual cue-reactivity fMRI studies.

Original empirical fMRI study in humans (healthy volunteers) assessing brain responses to visual food cues (palatable food pictures), directly addressing natural reward cue-reactivity; English language and not a review. Fits the meta-analysis objective of visual cue-reactivity for natural rewards.

The study examines insula white/gray matter volume (structural MRI) and longitudinal associations with motives and craving, not functional MRI cue-reactivity to visual drug/natural reward stimuli. It does not report an fMRI cue-reactivity task with visual cues, so it does not meet I2.

Abstract reports structural white-matter imaging (Fractional Anisotropy/DTI) relating dependence level; it does not describe a functional MRI cue-reactivity task or use of visual reward cues, so it does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (fMRI cue-reactivity).

Title and study context indicate an English-language empirical fMRI study in humans examining brain responses to food cues (a natural reward). This fits cue-reactivity using visual food stimuli commonly used in such work; inclusion requires full-text confirmation, but abstract/title provide sufficient evidence for inclusion in full-text screening.

The abstract reports original empirical human BOLD fMRI data assessing fasting-state brain responses to visual food stimuli (natural reward cues). This directly meets the review’s focus on fMRI cue-reactivity to visual natural rewards.

Abstract describes an empirical study of reward processing in nicotine dependence but does not specify use of fMRI or visual cue stimuli. Insufficient information to determine I2 from abstract, so include for full-text review to confirm methods.

Empirical fMRI study in English with human participants who viewed visual smoking cues (movie scenes) while undergoing fMRI; directly assesses drug-related cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. Meets inclusion criteria I1 and I2.
Abstract describes structural brain morphology/volume measures related to relapse, not an fMRI cue-reactivity task using visual stimuli. Does not meet fMRI cue-reactivity inclusion; thus excluded.

Human fMRI study presenting visual food cues assessing neural and behavioral cue-reactivity to high-calorie (natural reward) stimuli; original empirical data in English. Fits meta-analysis of fMRI visual cue-reactivity for natural rewards.

Abstract reports fMRI BOLD and resting-state results in abstinent heroin addicts but does not describe a visual cue-reactivity task. Because the abstract lacks sufficient information on whether visual reward-related cue-reactivity was assessed, include for full-text review to verify I2.

This is a study protocol (no original empirical fMRI data reported) and the abstract does not state that fMRI cue-reactivity using visual stimuli was conducted. It therefore fails inclusion criterion I1 (original data) and provides insufficient evidence for I2.

English-language empirical study reporting original data using fMRI in human smokers. Cue-reactivity was assessed with visual stimuli (video clips of smokers), matching the meta-analysis objective of fMRI studies of drug-related cue-reactivity using visual stimuli.

Abstract describes an empirical human fMRI study in cocaine users (I1).
