Study reports original empirical data in English, uses fMRI in human participants, and assesses cue-reactivity to visual appetizing food stimuli (a natural reward). No exclusion criteria apply based on the abstract.

Human fMRI study using visual food cues (high-calorie vs low-calorie) assessing cue-reactivity; original empirical research in English. Fits meta-analysis of visual drug/natural reward cue-reactivity (food = natural reward).

Abstract describes an original empirical study investigating neural correlates of alcohol craving (satisfies I1) but does not specify that functional MRI was used or that visual cues were presented. Abstract describes a Phase II RCT and mentions neuroimaging before and after treatment but does not specify functional MRI nor cue-reactivity tasks using visual (drug or natural reward) stimuli. Abstract reports an original fMRI study of conditioned stimuli paired with alcohol intoxication, meeting I1. The abstract does not specify that the cues were visual, so modality is unclear. Per guidelines, include for full-text review to determine whether I2 (visual stimuli) is met.

Study is a post-mortem biochemical enzyme activity study, not an fMRI cue-reactivity study using visual stimuli. It does not assess human participants with fMRI during visual drug or natural reward cue exposure, so it fails inclusion criterion I2.

Human fMRI study with original data examining neural responses to the sight of chocolate (visual cue) and its combination with flavor in cravers vs non-cravers. This addresses natural reward-related visual cue-reactivity and meets inclusion criteria I1 and I2. No exclusion criteria apply.

Abstract does not state that functional MRI was used or that visual cue-reactivity paradigms were employed. It is English and reports original data, but imaging modality and visual cue-reactivity are unclear. Include for full-text review to determine if I2 (fMRI with visual cues) is met.

This study reports resting-state pharmacological MRI (ASL) measuring cerebral perfusion after heroin injection, not a functional MRI task assessing drug- or natural reward-related cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. Abstract describes an English-language randomized clinical trial with original data (I1). However, it does not mention functional MRI or visual cue-reactivity (I2 unclear). Abstract reports an English-language empirical fMRI study but does not specify that stimuli were drug or natural reward-related visual cues; it focuses on negative affective stimuli. Insufficient information to confirm I2, so include for full-text review to determine relevance.

English-language original empirical study using fMRI to assess neural responses to alcohol-related cues and affective stimuli in humans, matching cue-reactivity objectives. Abstract does not mention drug or natural reward cue-reactivity or use of visual stimuli. It appears to report fMRI/perfusion in alcoholism and network dynamics, but provides insufficient information about cue-reactivity tasks. Include for full-text review to determine whether I2 is met.

Abstract does not mention drug or natural reward cue-reactivity nor visual stimuli; it appears to be a methods paper using alcoholism data. Insufficient information to confirm cue-reactivity task, so include for full-text review to determine eligibility.

Empirical human neuroimaging study assessing brain activation elicited by alcohol-associated and affective stimuli (visual cues implied). Abstract reports an empirical study of smokers' brain reactivity to smoking cues but does not explicitly state that functional MRI was used or that visual stimuli were presented. Include for full-text review to confirm imaging modality and stimulus type.

Abstract reports original human imaging research on nicotine-dependent individuals exposed to smoking cues, indicating empirical data on cue-reactivity. fMRI cue-reactivity was elicited using olfactory (alcohol drink odors) rather than visual stimuli. This meets exclusion E2 (non-visual sensory cues).

This is a single-case surgical report (anterior cingulotomy) with neuropsychological testing, not an fMRI cue-reactivity study using visual stimuli. It does not meet I2 (fMRI cue-reactivity with visual stimuli).

Title and abstract indicate an English empirical study examining neural responses to methamphetamine (drug) and sexual (natural reward) cues in humans—consistent with cue-reactivity. Abstract indicates a neuroimaging study of alcohol and TBI but does not specify that fMRI cue-reactivity using visual stimuli was used. Insufficient methodological detail to determine I2, so include for full-text review to verify.

Original human fMRI study of alcohol cue-reactivity in abstinent alcohol-dependent patients. Clearly meets I1 (English empirical) and reports fMRI cue-induced brain activation to alcohol cues. Cue modality not explicitly specified in abstract; include for full-text to confirm visual stimuli.

Original empirical human fMRI study presenting alcohol-associated visual stimuli to assess cue-reactivity; reports brain activation related to alcohol cues and relapse prediction, matching the review objective.

The study reports original human fMRI data assessing smoking cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (smoking-related images). It is not a review and explicitly involves visual cue exposure during fMRI, satisfying inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

Abstract reports original human data using functional neuroimaging with heroin-related visual cues (meets I1 and addresses visual cue-reactivity). However the modality is described as 'functional neuroimaging' without explicit mention of fMRI. Include for full-text review to confirm fMRI methods.
This paper is a meta-analysis combining results of existing fMRI studies rather than reporting original empirical fMRI data, so it meets exclusion criterion E1 (systematic reviews/meta-analyses).

This fMRI study examines nicotine effects on attention/default-network deactivation, not cue-reactivity to drug or natural reward visual stimuli, so it does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (reward-related cue-reactivity). The study used resting-state fMRI (no task-based cue-reactivity) and did not present visual drug or natural reward cues. It therefore does not meet I2 (fMRI assessing cue-reactivity using visual stimuli), so it does not address the meta-analysis objective.

Abstract reports an English-language empirical study with original fMRI data on cigarette (drug) cue reactivity and treatment outcome. This is an empirical fMRI study of smoking-related cue reactivity in human smokers (original data). The abstract does not specify the sensory modality of cues (visual vs. other), so include for full-text review to confirm visual stimuli.

Empirical human perfusion fMRI study reporting neural responses to smoking cues (drug-related). Fits meta-analysis objective of fMRI cue-reactivity; likely uses visual smoking cues commonly used in such paradigms. Include for full-text review to confirm stimulus modality if needed.

Human fMRI study with original data (English) assessing visual food cue-reactivity (natural rewards). The task used images of high-/low-caloric food during fMRI, meeting the review objective. No exclusion criteria apply.

The paper is a meta-analysis/systematic review of antipsychotics for stimulant dependence, not an original fMRI study of visual cue-reactivity. It clearly meets exclusion criterion E1. No fMRI or visual cue-reactivity methods are reported in the abstract.

This study reports molecular imaging of GABAA receptor availability during alcohol withdrawal, not an fMRI study of cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. It is an English-language empirical study but does not assess drug-related cue reactivity with visual cues, so it does not meet I2.

Abstract does not report original fMRI data or a cue-reactivity visual task; it reads like a review/narrative summary of cannabinoids for treating cannabis use disorder rather than an empirical fMRI study. Human fMRI study with original data assessing neural responses to monetary (nondrug) reward cues presented visually in alcohol-dependent patients and controls, matching the review focus on fMRI cue-reactivity to drug or natural rewards using visual stimuli. Human fMRI study with original data assessing cigarette cue-reactivity in nicotine-deprived smokers using visual cigarette cues during tasks; directly addresses drug-related cue-reactivity. Abstract provides sufficient information to meet inclusion criteria.

This study is a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials (not an original empirical fMRI study). It does not report fMRI cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. Therefore it meets exclusion criterion E1.

Abstract reports an empirical human neuroimaging study (fMRI implied) that measured brain responses to visual food cues (natural rewards) in pre-pubertal children, matching the meta-analysis focus. It appears to be original data and uses visual stimuli for cue-reactivity.

Original human fMRI study (N=60 females) using visual presentation of personally-craved vs non-craved foods (natural rewards). Abstract confirms cue-reactivity to visual food cues and reports neural responses, meeting inclusion criteria.

Human empirical fMRI study (ASL and BOLD) reporting food-cue task-induced brain activity (natural reward). Original empirical English-language fMRI study measuring BOLD responses in human smokers and nonsmokers to smoking-related and emotional images (visual stimuli), directly addressing drug and natural reward-related cue-reactivity.

Abstract provides insufficient information to confirm that a functional MRI cue-reactivity paradigm with visual stimuli was used. Title suggests structural damage in detoxified alcoholics rather than visual cue-reactivity. Include for full-text review to determine whether I2 is satisfied.

Abstract reports an original empirical human fMRI study that used visual alcohol-related cues (IAPS images paired with alcoholic or non-alcoholic beverages) to assess cue-reactivity; thus meets inclusion criteria and no exclusion criteria apply.

The article appears to be a narrative/review article (Alcohol Research: Current Reviews) summarizing human laboratory and brain-imaging findings rather than reporting original empirical fMRI data on visual cue-reactivity. This record is a Cochrane systematic review (secondary research) rather than an empirical fMRI study with original data; thus it meets exclusion criterion E1 (systematic reviews/meta-analyses).

Abstract reports brain activation to smoking-related images and exercise effects on cravings, indicating original human neuroimaging research in English. The title/abstract indicate an overview/review of neuromodulation for addiction rather than an empirical fMRI study with original data assessing cue-reactivity to visual stimuli. This is not an eligible primary fMRI study.

English-language empirical human fMRI study assessing neural responses to alcohol versus soft-drink cues using the visual Approach–Avoidance Task in scanner; directly addresses drug-related cue-reactivity. Meets I1 and I2.

Original empirical human fMRI study (N=32) investigating visual food-cue reactivity and influence of self-reported hunger — assesses natural reward (food) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli and is in English, meeting inclusion criteria.

Abstract reports an empirical study on neural mechanisms of alcohol cue-related craving and relapse but does not explicitly state fMRI during visual cue-reactivity. Insufficient detail to confirm I2 from abstract alone, so include for full-text review to determine eligibility.

The abstract is a meta-analysis/systematic review of genetic association studies of DRD4 and alcohol-related phenotypes, not an fMRI study assessing visual cue-reactivity. It meets exclusion criterion E1 (systematic review/meta-analysis).

Abstract describes structural volumetric (in vivo) measurements of striatal and basal forebrain in alcoholism; no mention of functional MRI during cue-reactivity or use of visual reward cues, so it does not meet inclusion criterion I2.

Abstract describes an empirical pilot study in English but does not explicitly state that functional MRI was conducted. Because the review requires fMRI cue-reactivity, the abstract provides insufficient information to confirm I2, so include for full-text review to verify imaging methods.

Abstract is a symposium overview that mentions neuroimaging and presentations of data but does not specify that fMRI was used during visual cue-reactivity tasks. Abstract is very brief and does not specify whether this is an original human fMRI study of cue-reactivity using visual stimuli or a review. Insufficient information to determine eligibility; include for full-text review.

Abstract indicates an empirical fMRI study comparing alcohol dependence and internet gaming disorder, but does not specify that cue-reactivity to drug or natural reward cues was assessed using visual stimuli. Insufficient information to confirm I2, so include for full-text review.

Human fMRI study (original empirical data) where participants passively viewed foods and used cognitive strategies; assesses cue-reactivity to natural rewards (food) using visual stimuli. Fits inclusion criteria; no exclusion criteria met.

Abstract describes an original English-language human fMRI study examining neuronal responses to alcohol (drug) cues during cue-exposure (visual stimuli). It is empirical, not a review, and does not rely solely on non-visual cues. Therefore it meets inclusion criteria I1 and I2.

English empirical study reporting original data; includes functional MRI scanning while participants viewed smoking-related and neutral pictures (visual stimuli) to assess cue-reactivity in human smokers, directly matching the review objective. No exclusion criteria met in the abstract.
Abstract describes a behavioral cocaine Stroop reaction-time study with no mention of fMRI scanning; thus it does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (fMRI cue-reactivity).
This paper is a meta-analysis (not an original fMRI study) and does not report primary fMRI data of visual cue-reactivity; excluded per E1.

This article is a systematic review and meta-analysis of cytokine levels in alcohol use disorder, not an original task-based fMRI study using visual drug or natural reward cues. It therefore meets exclusion criterion E1 and does not address the review objective.

The study used fMRI during a cognitive-control (Stroop) task and a stress-precipitated smoking paradigm rather than a visual drug- or natural reward-related cue-reactivity task. It therefore does not meet inclusion criterion I2.

Abstract reports original neuroimaging research on smoking decisions with nicotine delivery, but does not specify fMRI during visual cue-reactivity or use of visual drug-related/natural reward cues. Insufficient information to confirm I2; include for full-text review to determine eligibility.

Abstract reports empirical human research on neural responses to food cues (a natural reward). However, it does not explicitly state that fMRI was used nor that visual stimuli were the cue modality. Insufficient detail to confirm I2 from the abstract alone, so include for full-text review to determine eligibility.

Abstract indicates original empirical work (three investigations) on hedonic responses to food cues, but does not specify that neural measures were obtained with fMRI or that visual stimuli were used. Insufficient information to confirm I2, so include for full-text review to determine fMRI and visual-stimulus eligibility.

The study is explicitly a quantitative meta-analysis (not original empirical data), matching the exclusion criterion for reviews/meta-analyses (E1). Abstract indicates it's a meta-analysis of neuroimaging studies of visually induced sexual arousal rather than an original fMRI study.

Human fMRI study presenting visual food pictures (natural reward cues) and original empirical data. Fits review objective of visual cue-reactivity to natural rewards. Not a review and no exclusion criteria met.

This record is a Cochrane systematic review of pharmacotherapies for cannabis dependence, not an original fMRI study assessing visual cue-reactivity in humans. It meets the exclusion criterion for systematic reviews/meta-analyses and does not report original fMRI visual cue-reactivity data.

This is an English-language empirical study reporting original fMRI data in human participants using a visual food-cue paradigm (high- and low-calorie images), which constitutes natural reward-related cue-reactivity. No exclusion criteria apply from the abstract.

Abstract describes pooled clinical trial data for treatment of cocaine dependence; it does not mention functional MRI, cue-reactivity paradigms, or use of visual reward cues. Therefore it does not meet the review’s fMRI cue-reactivity inclusion requirements.

Original English-language empirical fMRI study in human smokers and controls measuring BOLD responses during an attentional bias paradigm with smoking cues as distractors (visual). This directly matches the meta-analysis objective of fMRI studies of drug-related cue-reactivity using visual stimuli.

Abstract describes an original randomized, controlled fMRI study of cue-reactivity in amphetamine-dependent humans, satisfying I1. The abstract does not state the sensory modality of the cues (visual vs. other), so inclusion for full-text review is needed to confirm I2 (visual stimuli).

Abstract reports neural outcomes and food reward but does not state that functional MRI was used during visual cue-reactivity. Insufficient information to confirm I2 from abstract alone — include for full-text review.

This record is a Cochrane systematic review (update) on pharmacotherapies for cannabis dependence, not an original empirical fMRI study assessing cue-reactivity with visual stimuli. It therefore meets exclusion criterion E1 (systematic reviews/meta-analyses).

The study used fMRI to investigate attention in smokers versus non-smokers (location-cueing and sustained attention), not drug- or natural reward-related visual cue-reactivity. Therefore it does not meet inclusion criterion I2. It is an empirical fMRI study (I1 satisfied) but not relevant to the review objective.

English-language empirical fMRI study with original human data using visual smoking-cue videos versus neutral videos to assess cue-reactivity in smokers/ex-smokers/non-smokers. Matches review objective and satisfies I1 and I2; no exclusion criteria met in abstract.

This is an English-language empirical fMRI study in humans examining cue influences on responding to natural rewards (food). The abstract does not specify the sensory modality of the cues (visual vs. other), so inclusion for full-text review is warranted to determine whether visual stimuli were used.
Abstract indicates an original human fMRI study examining neural responses to smoking cues (visual) in adolescents, matching the meta-analysis focus on visual drug-related cue-reactivity.

Abstract indicates food cue–induced activation in human reward pathways but does not specify use of fMRI or that visual stimuli were used. Likely an original empirical study, but insufficient methodological detail to confirm I2; include for full-text review to determine eligibility.

This is an English-language empirical study reporting original data that used fMRI to measure brain reactivity to smoking-related cues (visual smoking cues) in human participants, matching the review objective focusing on visual cue-reactivity.

Abstract reports original empirical human neuroimaging findings on nicotine-related brain activation but does not specify that fMRI cue-reactivity to visual drug or natural reward cues was used. Insufficient information to determine I2, so include for full-text review.

Study used PET (2-[18F]-2-deoxy-D-glucose) rather than fMRI and did not assess cue-reactivity to visual drug or natural reward stimuli; thus it fails the key inclusion criterion of fMRI-based visual cue-reactivity.

The abstract reports an ENDS nicotine delivery/smoking-reduction trial with no mention of functional MRI, cue-reactivity, or visual stimuli. It is an English-language empirical study (I1 met) but clearly does not perform fMRI cue-reactivity (I2 not met).

Human, English-language empirical study reporting an fMRI session examining brain responses to alcohol cues. Abstract does not specify that the cue presentations were visual, so include for full-text review to confirm stimulus modality and extract data.

This is an English-language empirical study reporting original fMRI data in humans using visual food cues (pictures with vs without chili) to assess craving for a natural reward (spicy food), meeting the review objective and inclusion criteria.

English-language empirical fMRI study with original data (N=57) on treatment-seeking cigarette smokers assessing brain responses to cigarette cues presented visually (holding and viewing a cigarette). Thus it addresses visual cue-reactivity and matches the review objective.

The abstract describes a narrative/theoretical review proposing a biobehavioural model and synthesizing prior genetic and neuroimaging studies; it does not report original empirical fMRI data or methods assessing visual cue-reactivity. Therefore it does not meet the review’s inclusion requirements for primary empirical fMRI studies.
Case report of Wernicke's encephalopathy in a pregnant woman; no fMRI, no drug or natural reward cue-reactivity, and no visual cue-reactivity reported. Does not meet the review objective (I2).

Abstract describes an original empirical human fMRI study examining limbic responses to drug and sexual cues presented visually (though ‘unseen’), directly addressing visual cue-reactivity for drug and natural rewards. Not a review.
Abstract describes a narrative/review article about the PFC and appetite (Current Obesity Reports) with no indication of original fMRI data or visual cue-reactivity; meets exclusion E1.

Abstract describes an empirical human fMRI study measuring neuronal responses to visual food cues (natural rewards) after overfeeding. This matches inclusion criteria: original data, fMRI, and visual cue-reactivity. No exclusion criteria apply.
The study is a quantitative meta-analysis rather than an original empirical fMRI study. It meets exclusion criterion E1 (systematic review or meta-analysis) so it should be excluded.

Abstract does not report that a task-based fMRI cue-reactivity paradigm using visual stimuli was used; it describes neuromodulation and appetite in obesity and the abstract is truncated. Insufficient information to confirm I2, but it is an English empirical study with original data (I1). Include for full-text review to determine whether fMRI visual cue-reactivity was performed.

The study reports original human fMRI data assessing neural responses to food images (visual stimuli), a natural reward-related cue, and is in English. This directly matches the review objective of visual cue-reactivity for natural rewards.

Abstract/title indicate an English-language empirical human lab study on smoking-related stimuli but do not mention use of functional MRI or visual cue paradigms. Information is insufficient to confirm fMRI cue-reactivity, so include for full-text review to determine I2.
This study is a systematic review and meta-analysis (not original empirical fMRI data) and does not report fMRI cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. It therefore meets exclusion criterion E1.

Abstract reports a transcranial direct current stimulation (tDCS) intervention to reduce alcohol craving during cue exposure; it does not report any functional MRI scanning or fMRI cue-reactivity using visual stimuli, so it fails inclusion criterion I2.

English-language empirical fMRI study of a natural reward (chocolate) measuring neural responses predicting intake. The abstract does not specify that cue-reactivity used visual stimuli; exposure to real chocolate suggests possible gustatory/olfactory cues. Insufficient information to confirm I2, so include for full-text review.

The abstract describes a survey of appetite sensations in pregnancy with no mention of fMRI or cue-reactivity to visual stimuli, so it does not meet I2. It is an English empirical study (I1 satisfied) but lacks neuroimaging and visual cue-reactivity required for inclusion.

Human empirical fMRI study (English) assessing cue-reactivity to natural reward (food) using Pavlovian cues presented during the task (visual cues implied). Matches inclusion criteria for original data and fMRI assessment of natural reward-related cue-reactivity with visual stimuli.

Abstract explicitly states this is a review of neuroimaging studies and does not report original empirical fMRI data. It meets exclusion criterion E1 (systematic review/meta-analysis), so exclude from the meta-analysis.

Although an English-language empirical fMRI study (I1), the task uses fearful faces (emotional/stress processing), not drug- or natural reward-related visual cues. It therefore does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (reward or drug cue-reactivity).

Human fMRI study presenting visual food cues (food images) assessing neural cue-reactivity to a natural reward; original empirical data in English. Fits meta-analysis focus on fMRI visual cue-reactivity to natural rewards.

Abstract reports a pilot open-label study of heroin craving in methadone patients but does not state whether functional MRI or visual cue-reactivity were used. Insufficient information to determine inclusion for an fMRI visual cue-reactivity meta-analysis; include for full-text review to confirm imaging modality and stimuli.

Abstract describes a narrative review of appetitive traits and child obesity and discusses prior studies and future research rather than reporting original fMRI data. It does not involve human fMRI cue-reactivity with visual stimuli and fits exclusion as a review article.

Empirical fMRI study in humans examining food-related neural activation in response to food images (visual stimuli), which is a natural reward cue. Abstract indicates original data and fMRI cue-reactivity using images, satisfying inclusion criteria.

Abstract identifies the article as a review integrating fMRI and ERP findings rather than reporting original empirical fMRI data; therefore it meets exclusion criterion E1 (systematic reviews or meta-analyses).

Abstract describes an evaluation of frontal cortical involvement in drug addiction rather than reporting original empirical fMRI data. It reads like a review article; no mention of human fMRI experiments or visual cue-reactivity. Therefore it meets exclusion for being a review (E1).

This article is a meta-analysis (not an original empirical fMRI study) and therefore meets exclusion criterion E1. It does not report fMRI cue-reactivity using visual stimuli in original data, so it is not eligible for inclusion.
Human fMRI study with original data assessing cue-reactivity to visual drug-related stimuli (cigarette pictures); meets language and task criteria. No exclusion criteria apply.

Abstract does not clearly report an fMRI scan during visual cue-reactivity. It reports ERP/LORETA and DTI (structural) measures related to drug cues and tDCS effects. Because the presence of task-based fMRI with visual cue stimuli is unclear from the abstract, include for full-text review to confirm. English-language empirical study is satisfied based on abstract.
This record is a Cochrane systematic review (not an original empirical fMRI study). It does not report original fMRI data on visual cue-reactivity, so it meets exclusion criterion E1.

Original human fMRI study examining neural responses to palatable food images (visual cues) across lean to obese participants, directly addressing natural reward-related cue-reactivity with visual stimuli.

Abstract reports factor analysis of a deeply phenotyped clinical sample (original empirical data) but does not mention fMRI or visual cue-reactivity. Insufficient information to confirm I2; include for full-text review to determine whether fMRI visual cue-reactivity was performed.

Empirical human study assessing brain reactivity to food cues (natural rewards). Abstract does not explicitly state use of functional MRI or specify visual stimuli; insufficient information to confirm I2. Include for full-text review to determine fMRI modality and stimulus modality.

Abstract reports an empirical study of methamphetamine-induced craving and neural cue-reactivity but does not explicitly state that fMRI was used or that visual stimuli were used. Include for full-text review to confirm fMRI and visual cue methodology. Meets I1 based on being an English empirical study with original data.

Human empirical fMRI study in English assessing cue-reactivity to e-cigarettes using sight (visual) cues (and smell). Visual stimuli were explicitly used during fMRI, meeting the meta-analysis criterion for visual cue-reactivity. Olfactory cues were present but not the sole modality.

Human fMRI study presenting original data using visual food cues (a natural reward). The abstract reports cue-reactivity to food images in fed and fasted states. Fits inclusion I1 and I2; no exclusion criteria met.

Original empirical fMRI study assessing smoking cue-reactivity in humans. Abstract confirms fMRI cue-reactivity but does not specify the sensory modality of the cues (visual vs. other). Because modality is unclear, include for full-text review to confirm whether visual stimuli were used.

This study reports psychometric validation of a 6-item self-report craving questionnaire with factor analyses; no fMRI scanning or visual cue-reactivity paradigm is mentioned. It therefore does not meet the required inclusion criterion I2 (fMRI cue-reactivity).

The study used fMRI but assessed cue-reactivity to food aromas (olfactory cues) rather than visual stimuli. The review requires visual cue-reactivity; this meets exclusion criterion E2 (only non-visual sensory cues).

Empirical human fMRI study reporting a drug-cue exposure task in adolescents (original data, English) so I1 is met. The abstract does not specify the cue modality (visual vs other), so I2 cannot be confirmed from the abstract; include for full-text review to determine visual stimulus use.

Original empirical fMRI study in English testing human participants (nonsmokers and smokers) using visual smoking-related cues (BEGIN and END stimuli) to assess cue-reactivity; directly matches meta-analysis objective.

Original empirical study in English using fMRI to measure human participants’ cue-reactivity to cigarette (drug) cues presented as visual stimuli (videos). Abstract clearly reports task-based fMRI during viewing of smoking vs. non-smoking videos, satisfying inclusion criteria I1 and I2.
Abstract describes a survey/review of published fMRI and PET studies rather than an empirical study with original data, so it meets exclusion criterion E1 (systematic reviews or meta-analyses).

Empirical fMRI study in humans examining cue-reactivity to cigarettes; participants viewed (and held) a cigarette during scanning, meeting visual stimulus requirement. This is original data in English relevant to drug-related reward cue-reactivity.

Human fMRI study with original data assessing brain responses to visual food cues (high-calorie food images vs nonfood). Directly examines natural reward (food) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli, matching the review objective.

The study uses a Go/No-Go and oddball fMRI task to assess inhibitory control and attention during acute heroin administration, not drug or natural reward cue-reactivity using visual stimuli; thus it does not meet inclusion criterion I2.

Abstract reports empirical human brain activation differences to smoking-related and secondary (money) cues, indicating original data (I1). However the abstract does not explicitly state use of functional MRI or that visual stimuli were used, so inclusion regarding I2 is uncertain. Include for full-text review to confirm imaging modality and stimulus type.
Abstract describes affective (emotional) cue-reactivity and affective Stroop task, not drug- or natural reward-related visual cue-reactivity. Therefore it does not meet inclusion criterion I2.

English-language empirical human study reporting cue reactivity and smoking cue–induced brain activation, but abstract does not explicitly state that functional MRI was used nor that cues were visual. Include for full-text review to confirm fMRI modality and visual stimuli.

This is a Cochrane systematic review (meta-analysis) of baclofen for AUD, not an original empirical fMRI study using visual cue-reactivity; therefore it meets exclusion criterion E1. No fMRI original data are reported in the abstract.

Empirical human fMRI study assessing brain responses to food cues (appetizing vs plain foods) using visual stimuli; directly examines natural reward (food) cue-reactivity and reports original data. Meets inclusion criteria I1 and I2.

Human fMRI study reporting original empirical data in English using visual appetitive cues (happy faces) versus neutral cues to assess cue-reactivity; directly matches meta-analysis focus on reward-related visual cue-reactivity.

This paper is a systematic review and meta-analysis of noninvasive brain stimulation (tDCS, rTMS) for alcohol dependence, not an original fMRI study of cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. It therefore meets the exclusion criterion for reviews/meta-analyses (E1).

Human BOLD fMRI study presenting visual romantic cues (natural rewards) to participants; original empirical data in English. It directly assesses visual cue-reactivity suitable for inclusion in the meta-analysis of drug and natural reward cue-reactivity using visual stimuli.

Human empirical study reporting original data using functional MRI to assess neural responses to visual food cues (a natural reward). Abstract clearly meets inclusion criteria and does not meet any exclusion criteria.

This study used resting-state fMRI to examine trait food cravings (no task exposure to visual cues). It does not assess cue-reactivity during visual stimulus presentation, so it fails the required inclusion criterion I2.

Empirical human fMRI study that measured brain responses to visual food cues (natural reward) in children with obesity and related cue-reactivity to treatment outcomes. This meets I1 (original English-language data) and I2 (fMRI during visual reward-related cue exposure). No exclusion criteria apply from the abstract.

This is a resting-state fMRI study of abstinence-induced craving without presentation of drug-related visual cues. It does not assess cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (fails I2), so it does not address the meta-analysis objective.

Empirical fMRI study in English examining cue-reactivity to smoking cues in human participants, indicating original data. Abstract does not specify cue modality (visual), so include for full-text review to confirm visual-stimulus criterion (I2).

The title and abstract indicate this is a systematic review (not original empirical fMRI research), so it meets exclusion criterion E1 and does not provide original cue-reactivity fMRI data using visual stimuli.
fMRI study with original data assessing food cue-reactivity (natural reward). Abstract does not explicitly state that cues were visual, so include for full-text review to confirm stimulus modality.

Human fMRI study with original data examining responses to visual smoking-related cues (images) in daily smokers; directly addresses cue-reactivity to drug-related visual stimuli relevant to the meta-analysis.

Study is a single-case SPECT resting-state pre/post treatment report, not an fMRI study and it does not assess drug or natural reward-related cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. Therefore it fails the required imaging modality and task (I2).
Human fMRI study in English with original data assessing cue-reactivity to visual food images (drug manipulation: nicotine). Meets both inclusion criteria.

Human study examining neural responses to food (a natural reward) and appears to present original data. However the abstract does not state that fMRI was used or that cues were visual. Insufficient information to confirm I2, so include for full-text review to verify imaging modality and stimulus sensory modality.
The study used olfactory (food aroma) cues during fMRI rather than visual stimuli. This meets exclusion criterion E2 (cue-reactivity using only other sensory cues).

Title/abstract indicate original human research reporting neural responses to food stimuli after leptin therapy, consistent with fMRI cue-reactivity for a natural reward (food). Language and empirical data implied. Include for full-text confirmation of visual-stimulus modality and fMRI details.
This is a systematic review and meta-analysis (not original empirical fMRI data) and does not report fMRI cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. Excluded under E1.
This article is a systematic review and meta-analysis rather than an original empirical fMRI study assessing cue-reactivity with visual stimuli; it meets exclusion criterion E1.

Abstract indicates this is a review article (comprehensive MEDLINE search, describes recent advances) rather than an original empirical fMRI study. Therefore it meets the exclusion criterion for systematic reviews/meta-analyses.

The fMRI task was a go/no-go response-inhibition paradigm, not a visual cue-reactivity paradigm for drug or natural rewards. Therefore it does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (fMRI assessing cue-reactivity using visual stimuli).

Human fMRI study presenting visual images of appetizing vs bland foods to assess external food sensitivity (cue-reactivity to natural reward). It's an original empirical study in English reporting fMRI responses to visual food cues, matching inclusion criteria.

Human fMRI study reporting original data on responses to visual food cues (images of high-calorie foods) — a natural reward — meeting both inclusion criteria for language and task. Abstract clearly describes fMRI with visual cue-reactivity to food; no exclusion criteria apply.
Empirical fMRI study with original human data in English that measured cue-reactivity using visual drug-related pictures (methamphetamine cues). No exclusion criteria apparent.

The abstract describes a review/meta-analysis of buprenorphine replacement therapy, not an original fMRI study. It does not report human functional MRI cue-reactivity using visual stimuli and therefore does not meet the review’s inclusion criteria. Exclude as a review/meta-analysis.

Human fMRI study in English with original data assessing cue-reactivity to visual natural-reward stimuli (sexually explicit videos). Fits the meta-analysis objective of fMRI studies of drug and natural reward-related cue-reactivity using visual stimuli, comparing CSB and controls.

Human empirical fMRI study presenting visual food cues (hedonic foods vs neutral) and measuring neuronal responses and intake. This is an original English-language study of natural reward (food) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli, meeting the meta-analysis criteria.

Empirical fMRI study in English with human participants viewing smoking-related visual cues (pictures) during scanning; original data on cue-reactivity. Meets inclusion criteria for language/original data and fMRI with visual drug-related cues.

Human fMRI study reporting original data in English that measured neural responses to visual food cues (high- vs low-caloric) — a natural reward cue-reactivity paradigm. Matches the meta-analysis objective; no exclusion criteria met.

Abstract does not specify that an fMRI scan was conducted nor that visual cue-reactivity paradigms were used; it describes hormonal and CNS responses to a meal (gustatory). Because the abstract lacks sufficient detail to determine whether I2 is met, include for full-text review to confirm imaging modality and cue type.
Human fMRI study presenting visual food cues (natural rewards) with original data in English. Directly assesses neuronal cue-reactivity to visual stimuli, meeting both inclusion criteria.

English empirical study reporting original data; used 1.5T fMRI to assess brain responses to visually presented video-game (StarCraft) cues in human participants with Internet video game addiction—this fits fMRI cue-reactivity to a natural reward using visual stimuli.

This is a nutrition/intervention study about snacking, appetite, mood and cognition, not an fMRI cue-reactivity study using visual drug or natural reward cues. It does not meet the fMRI/visual cue-reactivity inclusion criterion.

Empirical human fMRI study measuring food (natural reward) cue-reactivity in chronic dieters with original data. The abstract reports an fMRI food cue-reactivity task and links brain activity to real-world eating, meeting inclusion criteria; modality of cues (visual) is not explicitly stated in the abstract, so full-text review is needed to confirm stimulus modality.

Human fMRI study examining neural responses to food-related visual cues (rewarding and aversive) in fed vs fasted states; original empirical data in English. Fits meta-analysis focus on natural reward cue-reactivity using visual stimuli.

Abstract is a study protocol about neural changes related to delaying smoking but does not specify fMRI cue-reactivity with visual stimuli or present original data. Insufficient information to determine whether it meets I2 and I1, so include for full-text review to confirm imaging modality and stimuli used.

Abstract reports original human fMRI data probing responses to favorite-food (natural reward) cues but does not specify the stimulus modality (visual). Because modality is unclear, include for full-text review to determine whether I2 (visual stimuli) is met.

This article is a meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials on baclofen for alcohol dependence, not an original fMRI study of cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. The abstract reports behavioral and endocrine measures during exposure to visual food cues but does not mention any functional MRI scanning. Therefore it does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (fMRI cue-reactivity). The study is empirical and in English (I1 met).

This is a meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials of baclofen for alcohol use disorders, not an original fMRI study of visual cue-reactivity. It is itself a meta-analysis, meeting exclusion criterion E1.

Abstract reports an English-language empirical study linking FTO genotype to corticolimbic activation and food intake, implying neuroimaging, but does not specify that an fMRI scan was conducted during visual cue-reactivity. Insufficient methodological detail to confirm I2; include for full-text review to determine whether visual reward cue-reactivity was assessed.

English-language empirical fMRI study in humans examining cue-reactivity to visual erotic (natural) rewards using predictive cues and BOLD responses; directly fits meta-analysis of visual drug/natural reward cue-reactivity.

Empirical, English-language human fMRI study where participants passively viewed food cues (visual stimuli) assessing natural reward-related cue-reactivity, directly matching the meta-analysis inclusion criteria.

This fMRI study measured brain responses to intravenous cocaine infusion and subjective ratings, not cue-reactivity to visual stimuli. This is a narrative review article (not an empirical study with original fMRI data assessing visual cue-reactivity). It does not meet the inclusion criterion requiring original human fMRI data (I1/I2). Therefore exclude.

Abstract indicates a human study of brain reward responses to food cues but does not specify that fMRI with visual cue-reactivity was used. Insufficient information to confirm I2, so include for full-text review to determine whether functional MRI with visual stimuli was employed.

The study uses an fMRI probabilistic reversal learning task measuring reward prediction errors (and PET dopamine synthesis), not a cue-reactivity paradigm with visual drug or natural reward cues; it therefore does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (visual cue-reactivity).

Human fMRI study in English with original data examining neural responses to food cues (natural rewards). Visual food stimuli were used (alongside auditory), meeting cue-reactivity visual-stimulus requirement. No exclusion criteria met.

English empirical study with original data that used fMRI while participants viewed and rated smoking (drug-related) and neutral images (visual stimuli) to assess cue-induced craving. No exclusion criteria met; abstract provides sufficient detail to determine eligibility.

Human fMRI study examining brain responses to palatable food cues (a natural reward). Abstract indicates cue-reactivity to food; while it does not explicitly state visual modality, ‘food cues’ in cue-reactivity neuroimaging studies are typically visual. Study meets inclusion for original human fMRI cue-reactivity research; include for full-text to confirm stimulus modality.
This article is a systematic review of mHealth interventions for SUD, not an original empirical fMRI study. It does not report fMRI cue-reactivity using visual stimuli.
This is a resting-state fMRI study of insula connectivity in young smokers, not a task-based cue-reactivity fMRI using visual stimuli; therefore it does not meet inclusion criterion I2.

Abstract describes a methodological/ conceptual paper providing suggestions for measuring and manipulating alcohol craving; it does not report original empirical data or any fMRI study using visual cues. Therefore it is a review/commentary and not eligible for inclusion.

Abstract reports brain and behavioral impact of graphic cigarette warning labels in smokers using visual stimuli but does not explicitly state that fMRI was used. The article is a meta-analysis/systematic review of topiramate for alcohol use disorders, not an original empirical fMRI study of cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. Therefore it meets exclusion criterion E1 and does not meet the fMRI cue-reactivity inclusion criterion.

Abstract reports original fMRI data in humans (English) but does not clearly indicate that the task was visual drug/natural reward cue-reactivity: it examines responses to smoking-cessation messages. Insufficient information to confirm I2, so include for full-text review.

This publication is a narrative review/meta-discussion of individualized treatments and imaging findings, not an empirical fMRI study reporting original data on cue-reactivity with visual stimuli. It therefore meets exclusion criterion E1 (systematic reviews or meta-analyses).

This study is a meta-analysis of medications (naltrexone and acamprosate) for alcohol use disorders rather than an original fMRI study assessing visual cue-reactivity. It meets exclusion criterion E1 (systematic review/meta-analysis) and does not report fMRI cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (I2).

The article is a Cochrane systematic review (not an empirical fMRI study with original data). It therefore meets exclusion criterion E1 (systematic reviews/meta-analyses) and is not eligible for inclusion.

The abstract describes an rTMS intervention assessing craving and attentional blink to alcohol-related pictures but makes no mention of functional MRI scanning. It therefore does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (fMRI scan during visual cue-reactivity).

Abstract reports an English-language empirical fMRI study in human participants using visual food cues (high- vs low-calorie images) assessing neural cue-reactivity to a natural reward. Human fMRI study with original data assessing cue-reactivity to visual food (natural reward) stimuli (high-calorie food vs. nonfood images); meets inclusion I1 and I2, no exclusion criteria met.

Abstract does not specify that functional MRI was conducted; title and content focus on attentional bias modification in methamphetamine users (behavioral). Empirical human fMRI study reporting original data using arterial spin-labeled perfusion fMRI during exposure to smoking pictures (visual stimuli) in cigarette-dependent smokers; directly assesses cue-reactivity. Meets I1 and I2.

Empirical English fMRI study of heroin cue-reactivity in human participants with original data. The abstract confirms an fMRI cue-reactivity task but does not specify that cues were visual, so stimulus modality is unclear; include for full-text review to confirm I2.

The study used PET imaging to measure dopamine after varenicline, not functional MRI and did not assess cue-reactivity to visual stimuli. Therefore it does not meet the fMRI visual cue-reactivity inclusion criteria.

Abstract discusses neural systems linked to inability to resist calorie-rich foods and appears to report original research, but it does not specify that functional MRI was used during visual cue-reactivity. Include for full-text review to confirm fMRI methods and use of visual reward cues.

The article is explicitly a review of evidence (systematic review), not an original empirical fMRI study. It does not report original human fMRI data or visual cue-reactivity procedures. Therefore it meets exclusion criterion E1.

Abstract describes a review/meta-analysis of disulfiram mechanisms rather than an original empirical fMRI study of human cue-reactivity with visual stimuli. It therefore meets exclusion criterion E1 (systematic reviews or meta-analyses).

This study reports resting-state fMRI comparing obese and normal-weight females and does not describe task-based cue-reactivity to visual stimuli. It therefore does not meet I2 (fMRI during visual cue-reactivity).

This study used diffusion tensor imaging (DTI) and probabilistic tractography (structural/WM imaging), not functional MRI, and did not report a visual cue-reactivity fMRI task. It therefore does not meet I2 (fMRI cue-reactivity with visual stimuli).
This publication is a Cochrane systematic review (not original empirical fMRI research) and does not report fMRI cue-reactivity data using visual stimuli. Excluded per E1.

This article is itself a systematic review and meta-analysis of clinical trials, not an original fMRI study assessing cue-reactivity with visual stimuli. It therefore meets exclusion criterion E1 (systematic reviews or meta-analyses).
This study is a coordinate-based meta-analysis (not original empirical fMRI data), so it meets exclusion criterion E1 (systematic reviews or meta-analyses).

This fMRI study examines nicotine withdrawal effects on verbal working memory (2-Back), not cue-reactivity to drug or natural reward visual stimuli. It does not assess cue-induced craving using visual cues, so it does not meet the review’s fMRI cue-reactivity inclusion (I2).

Human fMRI study (original data, English) assessing neural responses to visual natural reward cues (subliminal sexual images) after stress. Meets inclusion criteria for fMRI cue-reactivity to visual natural rewards. No exclusion criteria apply.

The study reports fMRI of error monitoring during a stop-signal task and compares early vs late abstinence; it does not assess drug or natural reward cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. It therefore does not meet the cue-reactivity inclusion criterion.

Original human fMRI study using visual cues (food and sexual images) measuring nucleus accumbens activity and relating cue-reactivity to later behavior; meets inclusion criteria for language, empirical data, and fMRI visual cue-reactivity to natural rewards.

Abstract describes genetic factors and general overview of alcohol dependence; it does not report original fMRI data or cue-reactivity experiments using visual stimuli.
