This is a resting-state fMRI study comparing young smokers and nonsmokers; it does not involve task-based cue-reactivity or presentation of visual drug/natural reward cues, so it fails inclusion criterion I2.

Human fMRI study with original data in English assessing cigarette cue-reactivity (desire to smoke) using visual stimuli (graphic vs non-graphic FDA warning labels). Matches meta-analysis focus on fMRI visual cue-reactivity for drug rewards; no exclusion criteria apply.

Abstract states explicitly 'In this review...', indicating a review article rather than an empirical study with original fMRI data. This meets exclusion criterion E1 (systematic reviews or meta-analyses).

Abstract describes an English-language empirical fMRI study of reward expectancy/receipt in methamphetamine-dependent and control adults, but does not specify cue-reactivity to drug or natural rewards using visual stimuli. Because I2 cannot be confirmed from the abstract, include for full-text review to determine whether visual cue-reactivity was assessed.

Human fMRI study presenting visual food cues (high/low-energy images) assessing cue-reactivity in restrained vs. unrestrained eaters; original empirical data in English. Fits meta-analysis of visual cue-reactivity to natural rewards (food).

Abstract describes a resting-state fMRI study (regional homogeneity) in chronic smokers with no mention of task-based cue-reactivity or visual cue stimuli. It is an English empirical fMRI study (I1) but does not assess drug/natural reward cue-reactivity with visual stimuli (fails I2).

Empirical English-language fMRI study with human participants viewing visual food cues (videos) assessing neural reward/taste responses to natural reward stimuli (healthy/unhealthy foods). Matches meta-analysis focus on fMRI visual cue-reactivity to natural rewards.
Abstract describes a review of buprenorphine treatment in primary care, not an empirical fMRI study of cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. It is a review article, meeting exclusion E1.

Abstract reports original human empirical data and explicitly states that a conditioning and extinction task was performed using functional magnetic resonance imaging while assessing responses to sexual (visual) cues. This matches the meta-analysis focus on fMRI studies of visual drug or natural reward-related cue-reactivity.

Empirical human fMRI study using visual e-cigarette advertisements (sweet/fruit vs tobacco) to assess brain responses (cue-reactivity/product preference), matching the review objective. Not a review and not limited to non-visual cues.
This is a Cochrane systematic review (not an original empirical fMRI study) and does not report fMRI cue-reactivity data using visual stimuli. It meets exclusion criterion E1.

Abstract reads like a narrative review/theoretical paper and does not report original fMRI data or specify visual cue-reactivity methods. Insufficient information to confirm inclusion criteria I1/I2. Include for full-text review to verify whether original fMRI visual cue-reactivity data are present.

The abstract describes a narrative review of neuroimaging in addiction rather than reporting original empirical fMRI data or visual cue-reactivity experiments. It is a review article, meeting the exclusion criterion for systematic reviews/meta-analyses (E1).

Human fMRI study reporting responses to monetary and drug-related incentives during the MID task; original empirical data in English. Although the abstract doesn’t explicitly state stimulus modality, the MID and drug-related cues are task-based (likely visual); include for full-text review to confirm visual cue modality and extract data.

Human fMRI study using visual sexual images (natural reward cues) vs neutral images to assess attentional interference/cue-reactivity; empirical original data in English. Fits meta-analysis of fMRI visual cue-reactivity for natural rewards. No exclusion criteria met.

Abstract suggests original empirical work on reward circuitry and compulsive overeating but does not specify that fMRI cue-reactivity using visual stimuli was performed. Insufficient methodological detail to confirm I2; include for full-text review to determine fMRI and visual cue use.
Study used visual food pictures but did not perform functional MRI; outcomes were plasma ghrelin levels. Does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (fMRI cue-reactivity).

Abstract describes personality assessment (NEO inventories) in people with alcohol addiction; it makes no mention of fMRI or visual cue-reactivity. Because the abstract lacks information about neuroimaging cue-reactivity methods, include for full-text review to determine if I2 is met.

This paper is a meta-analysis/systematic review of randomized controlled trials of naltrexone for alcoholism, not an original fMRI study of cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. It meets exclusion criterion E1 (systematic reviews/meta-analyses).

Abstract describes a review of neuroimaging studies rather than reporting original empirical fMRI data; it is a review article, thus meeting exclusion criterion E1 (systematic reviews or meta-analyses).

This is a 2000 meta-analysis of transtheoretical model studies (not original fMRI research) and does not involve fMRI assessment of drug or natural reward visual cue-reactivity. It meets exclusion criterion E1 (meta-analysis).

Empirical fMRI study in humans using pornographic (visual) cues to examine neural and behavioral cue-reactivity in compulsive sexual behavior vs controls. Presents original data and directly assesses visual reward-related cue-reactivity, matching the meta-analysis objective.

The abstract describes a meta-analysis of genetic association studies of serotonin-related genes in alcohol dependence. It is a systematic review/meta-analysis rather than an original fMRI study of cue-reactivity using visual stimuli, so it meets exclusion criterion E1.
Empirical fMRI study (BOLD) in humans using visual food cues (natural rewards), original data and in English; directly addresses visual cue-reactivity, matching meta-analysis focus.

Abstract reports an original human study investigating neural responses to food cues (natural reward), meeting I1. However the abstract does not explicitly state that fMRI was used or that visual stimuli were presented, so I2 cannot be confirmed from the abstract alone. Include for full-text review to verify fMRI and visual cue use.

The study reports original human fMRI work on neural responses to food cues (a natural reward), meeting I1. The abstract does not specify that cues were visual, so I2 cannot be confirmed from the abstract; include for full-text review.

This is a protocol for a systematic review (not an original empirical study) and does not report human fMRI data assessing drug or natural reward-related cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. It therefore does not meet the inclusion requirements for original fMRI cue-reactivity studies.

Title/abstract report neural responses to drug cues and relapse but do not explicitly state an fMRI scan or that visual stimuli were used. The study is an English empirical article. Insufficient information to confirm I2, so include for full-text review to determine whether fMRI with visual cue-reactivity was used.

This article is a Cochrane systematic review/meta-analysis rather than an original empirical fMRI study assessing cue-reactivity; it does not present original fMRI data. Exclusion criterion E1 applies.

Abstract reads like a narrative review/commentary summarizing animal and human studies and neuroimaging concepts; it does not report original human fMRI data or an fMRI cue-reactivity experiment using visual stimuli. Therefore it is not an empirical fMRI study relevant to the meta-analysis.
This study is an ALE meta-analysis (not original fMRI data) of visual food cues and therefore is a meta-analysis/systematic review, meeting exclusion criterion E1.

Abstract reports original empirical human fMRI data (resting-state and task-based) in smokers and non-smokers but does not specify whether the task involved drug or natural reward cue-reactivity or visual stimuli. Insufficient information to determine I2 from the abstract alone—include for full-text review.

Human fMRI study reporting original empirical data in English that measured neural responses to smoking-related visual cues (attentional bias paradigm and go/no-go with visual smoking cues). Meets the review objective of fMRI cue-reactivity to visual drug-related stimuli; no exclusion criteria apparent.

This article is a meta-analytic review (not original empirical fMRI data). It explicitly synthesizes 22 imaging studies rather than reporting new participant-level fMRI data, meeting exclusion criterion E1 (systematic reviews/meta-analyses).

Abstract appears to report an original empirical study in English on craving regulation in smokers, but does not explicitly state that fMRI was used during visual cue-reactivity. Insufficient methodological detail to confirm I2, so include for full-text review.

This is an English-language empirical fMRI study in human participants examining brain responses to monetary (non-drug) reward cues presented during a rewarded antisaccade task. It directly assesses visual reward-related cue-reactivity, matching the meta-analysis objective.

This is a narrative/review article discussing noninvasive brain stimulation and neuroimaging findings rather than an original empirical fMRI study of drug or natural reward cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. It does not report primary fMRI data, so it meets exclusion criterion E1.

Human fMRI study reporting original data on food (natural reward) cue-reactivity in craniopharyngioma patients. Abstract does not specify modality of cues (visual vs other). Insufficient info to confirm visual stimuli, so include for full-text review.

This article is a meta-analysis (review) of self-report and psychophysiological cue-reactivity studies rather than an original empirical fMRI study in humans; it therefore meets exclusion criterion E1.

Empirical human fMRI study presenting visual cues for natural rewards (monetary and erotic), original data, and measures cue-reactivity; not a review. Meets language and imaging criteria and uses visual stimuli predicting rewards, so satisfies I1 and I2.

Abstract explicitly describes the paper as a review (“This review aims to show...”) rather than an original empirical study. It does not report original human fMRI data of visual cue-reactivity. Exclusion criterion E1 applies.

This is an expert review/narrative paper summarizing pharmacotherapy studies, not an original empirical fMRI study of drug or natural reward cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. It does not report fMRI data or visual cue-reactivity experiments.

The abstract reports original data (300 human participants) in English and describes fMRI measures of brain responses to an alcohol cue versus a juice cue, meeting the review objective of visual cue-reactivity to drug/natural rewards. No exclusion criteria apply.
This article is a meta-analytic review (not an original empirical fMRI study), meeting exclusion criterion E1. The abstract does not report primary fMRI data collection with visual cue stimuli.

The study acquired resting-state fMRI (pre/post rTMS) rather than a task-based fMRI during visual cue exposure. Although participants received drug-related cue exposure and rated craving, the MRI was not conducted during visual cue-reactivity, so it does not meet I2.

Abstract reports an original empirical fMRI study in human smokers using visual smoking-related cues (BEGIN/END stimuli) to assess cue-reactivity. It meets English original-data (I1) and fMRI with visual cue-reactivity (I2) criteria; no exclusion criteria apply.

Original human fMRI study (n=32) using visual food cues (food picture viewing) to probe reward-system cue-reactivity for a natural reward (food). Matches meta-analysis objective (fMRI cue-reactivity with visual stimuli). No exclusion criteria met.

Abstract describes an English-language empirical fMRI study in humans on delayed orgasm but does not specify that it assessed drug or natural reward cue-reactivity or used visual stimuli. Insufficient information to determine I2 from abstract alone; include for full-text review to assess cue type and stimuli modality.
Original English-language fMRI study in humans using visual food images (high- vs low-calorie) to assess cue-reactivity to a natural reward (food), directly matching the review objective.

Original empirical fMRI study in humans assessing neural responses to visual package-colour cues (with taste) for food (natural reward). It reports cue-related brain activation to visual stimuli, meeting inclusion criteria for language and fMRI visual cue-reactivity.

Empirical English-language study reporting original fMRI data in human participants assessing visual sexual cue-reactivity (a natural reward). The paradigm used visual sexual stimuli and measured BOLD responses, directly matching the review objective.

The study used an fMRI chocolate-specific delayed discounting task following actual chocolate consumption (gustatory cue) and reports decision-related activation; the abstract does not indicate use of visual cue-reactivity stimuli. Excluded per E2 (only gustatory/other non-visual cues described).

Empirical human fMRI study using visual food cues (natural rewards) to assess cue-reactivity before and after eating; not a review and uses visual stimuli only. Meets inclusion criteria for original data and fMRI visual cue-reactivity.

This is a narrative/review article about acamprosate and other pharmacotherapies, not an empirical fMRI study. The abstract reports no original human fMRI data or visual cue-reactivity paradigms. Exclude as a review (E1).

English-language empirical fMRI study using visual cues predicting monetary gains/losses (original data). Unclear if monetary (secondary) rewards are considered 'natural rewards' for the review—requires full-text review to confirm eligibility.

Abstract reports an original human imaging study of alcohol cue-elicited craving (eligible topic) but does not specify that fMRI was used or that visual stimuli were presented. Insufficient modality detail in abstract — include for full-text review to confirm I2.

Qualitative hermeneutic-phenomenological study using in-depth interviews; no fMRI or any neuroimaging or visual cue-reactivity task mentioned. Does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (fMRI cue-reactivity).
Empirical human fMRI study using visual images of appetizing (natural reward) foods to assess cue-reactivity; meets language and original data requirements. No exclusion criteria apply.
Human fMRI study presenting visual food cues and measuring cue-reactivity (desire to eat) with original data. Matches natural reward visual cue-reactivity and is not a review or non-visual cue study.

This is an English-language empirical double-blind placebo-controlled fMRI study in humans that measures neural responses to visual natural reward-related cues (erotic/attachment scenes). It matches the meta-analysis focus on fMRI cue-reactivity to natural rewards using visual stimuli.

Human fMRI study with original data assessing cue-reactivity to food (a natural reward) using visual stimuli (written food labels). Not a review and not limited to non-visual cues. Fits meta-analysis objective of fMRI cue-reactivity to natural rewards.

This is a resting-state fMRI study of ketamine dependence and does not assess cue-reactivity to visual drug or natural reward cues (does not meet inclusion criterion I2). It reports spontaneous brain activity (ReHo), not responses to visual cues.

Study uses maternal rats (non-human) and tests cue-reactivity to a cocaine-associated odor (olfactory cue) rather than visual stimuli. This meets exclusion criterion E2 (other sensory cue modality), so it does not match the review’s requirement for visual cue-reactivity in humans.

Abstract describes fMRI functional connectivity related to food craving and obesity but does not specify that data were acquired during cue-reactivity tasks using visual stimuli. Insufficient information to confirm criterion I2, so include for full-text review.

Original empirical fMRI study in English assessing brain reward reactivity to visual food cues (natural rewards) in human participants. Fits the meta-analysis focus on fMRI cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. No exclusion criteria met.

fMRI study in humans with original data using visual sexual stimuli (possible natural reward cues). However the abstract does not explicitly state assessment of reward-related cue-reactivity, so full-text review is needed to confirm I2.

Abstract reports an original empirical study examining neural responses to sexual (visual) stimuli in humans with problematic hypersexual behavior, but does not explicitly state that functional MRI was used. Include for full-text review to confirm fMRI cue-reactivity methods.

Abstract describes cue-reactivity to beer flavor (gustatory stimulus) during fMRI/PET. The review requires visual stimuli; this study uses a taste cue (beer flavor) and thus meets exclusion criterion for non-visual sensory cues.

The study reports resting-state fMRI connectivity, not task-based cue-reactivity using visual stimuli, so it does not meet inclusion criterion I2. It is an English empirical human fMRI study (I1 satisfied) but does not assess visual cue-reactivity.

Abstract describes attentional/inhibition of return effects using pharmacologic psychosis models, not reward-related cue-reactivity. It does not report fMRI of drug/natural reward visual cue reactivity. Although an empirical human study (I1), it fails the review’s focus (I2).

Abstract is truncated and does not state whether fMRI was conducted during visual reward cue-reactivity. Title implies neural imaging in humans, but insufficient detail to confirm I2. The paper appears to be an English empirical study with original data (I1). Include for full-text review to determine if fMRI with visual cue-reactivity was used.

This is a symposium summary/report, not an original empirical study reporting primary fMRI data. The abstract describes conference presentations and reviews animal and human neuroimaging findings but does not report original human fMRI experiments using visual cue-reactivity. Therefore it meets exclusion criterion E1.

Abstract describes neural responsivity to soft drink intake, anticipated intake, and advertisement exposure (visual stimuli). However, it does not explicitly state fMRI was used; therefore inclusion is uncertain and full-text review is needed to confirm I2. Presumed original empirical study in English.

This is a resting-state fMRI study of young smokers using fALFF and RSFC; it does not assess cue-reactivity to drug or natural reward-related visual stimuli (no task-based cue exposure), so it fails I2.

Study reports original human fMRI data using visual food images to probe responses to natural rewards (high-fat foods), fitting cue-reactivity with visual stimuli. Meets inclusion criteria I1 and I2; no exclusion criteria apply.

Abstract indicates investigation of neural correlates of food addiction (appears to be original empirical research) but does not specify use of functional MRI or whether visual cue-reactivity was used. Insufficient information to determine eligibility based on imaging modality and stimulus type; include for full-text review.

This publication is a Cochrane systematic review (not an original empirical fMRI study), so it meets exclusion criterion E1 (systematic reviews/meta-analyses) and does not provide original fMRI cue-reactivity data.

Human fMRI study presenting visual food cues (high- and low-calorie pictures) with original empirical data in English. It examines neural cue-reactivity to a natural reward (food), matching the meta-analysis objective. No exclusion criteria met.

Abstract reports an original empirical study on neural responsivity to food cues (a natural reward) in unmedicated first-episode psychosis, meeting relevance. However, the abstract does not explicitly state that functional MRI was used or that visual stimuli were presented; full-text review needed to confirm I2.

Abstract describes fMRI during decision-making tasks (two-choice prediction/response) in methamphetamine-dependent subjects, not cue-reactivity to drug or natural reward visual stimuli. Therefore it does not meet inclusion criterion I2. Study is English with original data (I1). No exclusion criteria E1 or E2 apply.

This is an expert-opinion/review article summarizing pharmacological treatments, not an original empirical fMRI study. The abstract does not report any fMRI cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. It meets exclusion criterion E1 (review/meta-analysis/expert opinion).

The study uses fMRI and is an English empirical study (I1) but investigates nicotine effects on visuospatial attention, not cue-reactivity to drug or natural reward visual stimuli. It does not assess visual reward-related cue-reactivity (fails I2), so it is not relevant to the review objective.
This paper is a systematic review of fMRI studies rather than an original empirical study with primary data, so it meets exclusion criterion E1.

Abstract references neuroimaging and infant-face cue-reactivity related to maternal substance use but does not explicitly state that an fMRI scan with visual stimuli was used. Information is insufficient to confirm I2, so include for full-text review to determine eligibility.

Abstract reports resting-state functional connectivity after weight loss and does not describe any task-based fMRI assessing cue-reactivity to visual drug or natural reward cues. Therefore it does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (fMRI during visual cue-reactivity).

Original empirical fMRI study in humans using visual erotic images (natural reward) to assess reward-related cue-reactivity and delay discounting; fits the meta-analysis focus on visual drug/natural cue-reactivity.

Empirical human fMRI study examining brain responses to food cues (natural rewards), satisfying I1. Abstract does not specify the sensory modality of the cues (visual vs gustatory/olfactory), so I2 cannot be confirmed; include for full-text review.

This fMRI study examines cough suppression in response to inhaled capsaicin (a chemosensory irritant) in smokers, not drug or natural reward-related cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. It does not meet I2; stimulus modality is non-visual/irritant. Exclusion per E2.

This is an English-language empirical fMRI study measuring neural responses to visual food cues (natural rewards) in human children; it reports original data on cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. No exclusion criteria apply.

English-language empirical fMRI study with human participants using visual smoking-related cues to assess cue-induced craving; directly matches the review objective and inclusion criteria. Not a review and does not use non-visual cues.

Abstract reports original empirical fMRI data in humans (three leptin-deficient adults) using visual food cues; this assesses natural reward (food) cue-reactivity with visual stimuli, matching review objective.

Abstract reports measurement of brain activation to high- vs low-calorie food cues in human participants (natural rewards), indicating an fMRI cue-reactivity study. While the modality (visual) is not explicitly stated, the task contrasts food stimuli and reports regional brain activation typical of fMRI studies, so include for full-text confirmation.

Abstract reports an English-language empirical study examining brain responses to anticipation and receipt of a beverage with manipulated perceived caloric content (natural reward). However the abstract does not explicitly state that functional MRI was used nor whether cue-reactivity was elicited via visual stimuli (health labels) rather than only gustatory receipt. Insufficient detail to confirm I2, so include for full-text review.
Abstract explicitly states this article is a review of fMRI studies rather than reporting original empirical data. This meets exclusion criterion E1 (systematic reviews or meta-analyses).

Original empirical human fMRI study measuring brain responses to visual food (natural reward) cues (photographs), not a review and not limited to non-visual cues; therefore meets inclusion criteria for visual cue-reactivity meta-analysis.

Study reports original human fMRI data (English) and uses visual reward cues (monetary incentive) during scanning, i.e., natural reward-related cue presentation; thus meets inclusion criteria for fMRI studies of reward-related cue-reactivity using visual stimuli.

Abstract does not state whether a functional MRI cue-reactivity paradigm with visual stimuli was used (I2 unclear). Insufficient information to determine inclusion based on imaging/cue modality; include for full-text review to confirm.

Original human fMRI study examining smoking cue-induced craving and brain responses; abstract indicates cue-reactivity but does not specify stimulus modality (visual). Include for full-text review to confirm visual stimuli.

The article is a systematic review of biofeedback/neurofeedback in eating disorders rather than an original empirical fMRI study. It does not report fMRI cue-reactivity using visual stimuli on human participants. Therefore it meets exclusion criterion E1 (systematic review).

Abstract reports original empirical fMRI data in English using visual food cues (food vs non-food images) in human participants, assessing natural reward–related cue-reactivity (evening hyperphagia vs controls). Meets inclusion criteria I1 and I2; no exclusion criteria apply.

This is a literature review summarizing neuromodulation studies rather than reporting original empirical fMRI data. It does not present new fMRI cue-reactivity experiments with visual stimuli, so it meets exclusion criterion E1 (systematic reviews or meta-analyses).

Human fMRI study using natural (food) rewards and cue–outcome associations, so meets I1. Abstract does not explicitly state that cues were visual or that it specifically assessed visual cue-reactivity (I2 uncertain). Include for full-text review to confirm stimulus modality and cue-reactivity.

This 1999 paper is a meta-analysis/systematic review of cue-reactivity rather than an original empirical fMRI study of human participants using visual cues. It therefore meets exclusion criterion E1 (systematic reviews/meta-analyses).

Abstract examines responses to visual food stimuli and discusses reward-related brain regions, indicating relevance to natural reward cue-reactivity. However, the abstract does not explicitly state that a functional MRI scan was performed. Include for full-text review to confirm fMRI methods.

Title and abstract indicate this is an integrative review (not an original empirical fMRI study). It summarizes neuroimaging findings rather than reporting original human fMRI data on cue-reactivity, meeting exclusion criterion E1 (systematic reviews/meta-analyses).

Human fMRI study (3T) presenting visual stimuli (pictures) of high-calorie foods to compare obese and control women — original empirical data in English examining natural reward (food) cue-reactivity using visual cues, directly meeting review objective.

Abstract describes tDCS effects on resting-state networks and craving in abstinent methamphetamine users; it uses resting-state fMRI and neuromodulation rather than an fMRI cue-reactivity task using visual drug/natural reward cues. Does not meet inclusion criterion I2.

The abstract describes a review article summarizing brain-imaging studies rather than reporting original empirical fMRI data. It therefore meets exclusion criterion E1 (systematic reviews or meta-analyses). The abstract does not present new fMRI cue-reactivity data using visual stimuli.

This is a case series of three patients reporting medication effects on eating behaviors in frontotemporal lobar degeneration; it does not report an fMRI study assessing drug or natural reward cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. Therefore it is not relevant to the fMRI cue-reactivity meta-analysis.

Study reports original human fMRI data in English assessing brain responses to food (a natural reward) during observation of food stimuli (visual). This directly matches meta-analysis focus on visual cue-reactivity to natural rewards.

This is an English-language empirical fMRI study in human adolescents where participants viewed images of food (visual stimuli) during scanning, assessing neural responses to natural reward cues (food). The abstract clearly reports original data and meets the meta-analysis focus on visual cue-reactivity to natural rewards. No exclusion criteria apply.

The title and abstract indicate this is a systematic review (not an empirical fMRI study with original data). It therefore meets exclusion criterion E1 and does not provide evidence of fMRI visual cue-reactivity data.

Empirical English-language study reporting original data using fMRI BOLD signal during an alcohol cue-reactivity task in human adolescent heavy drinkers (visual cue-reactivity implied). Meets objective; no exclusion criteria apparent from abstract.

English-language empirical fMRI study using visual food cues to assess brain responses to natural reward (food) images before and after a weight-loss intervention and during maintenance. This directly addresses visual cue-reactivity to a natural reward (food).

Human fMRI study presenting visual cues that predict viewing opposite- or same-sex faces (smiling) and measuring neural cue-reactivity; this is original empirical data in English and examines natural (social) reward-related cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. No exclusion criteria apply from the abstract.

Although this is an English-language empirical fMRI study (I1), the task assessed decision-making (Iowa Gambling Task), not drug- or natural reward-related cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. It therefore does not meet inclusion criterion I2.

The study probes brain responses to mechano-receptive C-fiber (tactile) stimulation (slow stroking), not visual reward-related cues. The review requires visual cue-reactivity fMRI; this uses non-visual (tactile) stimuli, so E2 applies.

Abstract describes an English-language empirical fMRI study of human cigarette smokers' neural responses to smoking cues, satisfying I1. However the abstract does not specify that the cues were visual, so I2 is unclear. Include for full-text review to confirm cue modality and other details.

The abstract reports an original English-language empirical fMRI study in human participants assessing cue-reactivity to visual natural reward stimuli (high- vs low-calorie food images), meeting inclusion criteria I1 and I2.

Empirical fMRI study in English with human participants presenting visual (and auditory) stimuli previously paired with methamphetamine; assesses neural cue-reactivity to drug-paired visual stimuli during fMRI on a drug-free test day. Meets review objective and inclusion criteria.

Abstract examines anxiety, caloric intake, food cue perception, and ‘satiety-related brain activation’, indicating natural reward cue-reactivity (food). However, the abstract does not explicitly state that functional MRI was conducted or that visual stimuli were used. Information is insufficient to confirm I2, so include for full-text review to determine imaging modality and stimulus type.

Abstract describes an English-language empirical study developing/validating visual cue stimuli for inhalant craving (meets I1). However, the abstract does not state that functional MRI was conducted, so I2 cannot be confirmed from the abstract. Include for full-text review to verify whether fMRI was used.

Abstract does not specify that functional MRI was used for visual cue-reactivity. It is an English empirical study with original data, but I2 (fMRI with visual cue-reactivity) cannot be confirmed from the abstract. Include for full-text review to determine whether fMRI visual cue-reactivity was performed.

Abstract reports original human data on brain responses to sucrose and specifically mentions responses during visual food-related cues, but does not explicitly state that functional MRI was used. Because visual cue-reactivity is central to the review and the abstract lacks sufficient methodological detail, include for full-text review to confirm fMRI methods.

Human fMRI study of food (a natural reward) cue-reactivity with original data; abstract indicates an fMRI food-cue reactivity (fMRI-FCR) paradigm. Modality of cues not explicitly stated but highly likely visual; include for full-text verification.

Original English fMRI study in human participants assessing neural responses to visual food cues (high- and low-calorie images), which qualifies as natural reward cue-reactivity. Abstract reports BOLD contrasts and brain activation results, meeting inclusion criteria.

Abstract appears to report original empirical data on neural responses to reward/aversion during bupropion treatment, but does not specify that fMRI was used or that visual cue-reactivity paradigms were employed. Insufficient information to confirm I2; include for full-text review.

The manuscript is a theoretical article (part 1) outlining hypotheses and drug development, not reporting original empirical data and it does not describe an fMRI study or visual cue-reactivity. Therefore it fails inclusion criteria I1 and I2.
The abstract explicitly states this is a review ('This review includes the data from 44...'), i.e., a systematic review/meta-analysis rather than an original empirical fMRI study. Excluded per E1.

This is an English-language empirical human neuroimaging study reporting neural cue reactivity and cue-induced craving. However the abstract does not explicitly state that cue-reactivity was measured with fMRI during visual stimuli (I2). Insufficient information to determine I2 from the abstract alone — include for full-text review to confirm.

Abstract reports altered hemodynamic responses during appetitive conditioning in an original empirical study, but does not explicitly state fMRI scanning, use of visual stimuli, or that cues were drug or natural rewards. Insufficient information to determine I2 from the abstract, so include for full-text review.

Abstract describes physiological (blood pressure) responses after exercise and behavioral stressors; no fMRI was conducted. The smoking cue was handling a lit cigarette (non-visual/tactile/olfactory cue), so it does not meet the fMRI + visual cue inclusion (I2) and meets exclusion E2.

Human fMRI study presenting visual food cues (high/low calorie) assessing reward-related neural responses under hunger/satiety; original empirical data in English. This matches natural reward cue-reactivity with visual stimuli.

The fMRI task assessed emotional (sad) induction combined with intragastric fatty acid infusion, not drug or natural reward-related cue-reactivity to visual stimuli. Therefore it does not meet the review’s I2 criterion.

Abstract does not specify that functional MRI was conducted or that visual cue-reactivity to drug/natural rewards was assessed. Title mentions 'brain' but methods are not described. Insufficient information to determine inclusion, so include for full-text review.
The article is a meta-analysis (uses activation likelihood estimation) rather than an original empirical fMRI study of cue-reactivity, so it meets exclusion criterion E1.

Abstract is a narrative overview summarizing animal and human literature and discussing imaging techniques; it does not report original empirical fMRI data from human participants responding to visual reward cues, so it does not meet inclusion criteria (I1/I2).

Abstract describes a narrative/review paper discussing neurobiological research and future directions, and does not report original empirical fMRI data assessing cue-reactivity with visual stimuli. Therefore it does not meet the inclusion criterion for original data.

Abstract reports an English-language empirical fMRI study of reward and control circuitry in humans after sleeve gastrectomy but does not specify a visual cue-reactivity task or visual stimuli. Insufficient information to determine whether I2 is met, so include for full-text review to confirm whether a visual cue-reactivity paradigm was used.

Human fMRI study administering ghrelin and measuring neural responses to food pictures (visual stimuli). Original empirical data in English; addresses natural reward (food) cue-reactivity using visual cues, meeting the review objective.
The article is a narrative review of recent research on food addiction neurobiology and does not present original fMRI data; it is therefore a review/meta-analysis and meets exclusion criterion E1.

Human fMRI study with original data (English) using visual infant-face cues (natural reward) to assess reward-related brain activation in mothers with substance addiction; meets both inclusion criteria and no exclusion criteria are met based on the abstract.

Abstract describes neural response to receipt of an ice-cream milkshake (gustatory/consummatory response) and does not indicate cue-reactivity to visual stimuli or that visual cues were used. This matches exclusion E2 (only non-visual sensory/gustatory cue).

Abstract describes an English-language empirical human study on food reward-related brain signaling comparing responses to food vs nonfood stimuli, suggesting original data. However the abstract does not explicitly state that functional MRI was used or that visual stimuli were the cue modality. Insufficient detail to confirm I2, so include for full-text review.

Human fMRI empirical study involving a natural reward (pleasant juice) and aversive gustatory outcome during Pavlovian conditioning. Abstract does not specify the modality of conditioned cues (visual vs gustatory), so it is unclear whether it meets the visual-stimuli requirement (I2). Include for full-text review to confirm cue modality.

Empirical human fMRI study examining food-cue-related neural activity (natural reward). Abstract confirms original data and cue-reactivity in the brain, but does not explicitly state the modality of cues (visual). Included for full-text review to confirm use of visual stimuli.

The study used an fMRI-compatible device for participants to smoke cigarettes but does not report any visual cue-reactivity paradigm. The cue is smoking itself (non-visual/tactile/olfactory), so it does not meet the review’s requirement for visual cue-reactivity. Exclude under E2.

Empirical fMRI study in English (I1 satisfied). However, the abstract describes a decision-making Cigarette Purchase Task rather than a visual cue-reactivity paradigm; it does not clearly state use of drug-related visual cues (I2 unclear). Include for full-text review to verify whether visual cue-reactivity was used.

Abstract reports an original human study of neural responses to stress- and food cues and food craving in obesity. It implies neuroimaging but does not explicitly state that fMRI was used or that cues were visual. No exclusion criteria are met; insufficient detail to confirm I2, so include for full-text review to verify fMRI and visual-stimulus use.

Study is not a functional MRI cue-reactivity study: it reports diffusion tensor imaging (DTI) and behavioral measures, with no fMRI during visual reward cue exposure, thus it fails inclusion criterion I2.

The article is a voxel-based meta-analysis of structural (gray matter) MRI in alcohol-use disorders, not an original fMRI study assessing visual cue-reactivity. It is a meta-analysis, so it meets exclusion criterion E1.

This paper is a review article ('In this review...') summarizing neuroimaging/neuropharmacology literature rather than reporting original empirical human fMRI data on visual cue-reactivity. Excluded under E1.
Abstract describes a review of neuroimaging research (a review article), not an original empirical fMRI study with data. Therefore it meets exclusion E1 (systematic review or meta-analysis).

Abstract does not specify that an fMRI was conducted during drug or natural reward visual cue-reactivity, nor clearly state original empirical data; information is insufficient to determine eligibility for the cue-reactivity fMRI meta-analysis. Include for full-text review.

Study reports original empirical human data in English, investigates neural attentional bias to food (a natural reward) using a food Stroop task with visual word cues, and implicates lateral PFC (indicating fMRI neural measures). Therefore it meets inclusion criteria for fMRI visual cue-reactivity to natural rewards.

This article is a systematic review/meta-analysis of opioid antagonists for smoking cessation, not an original fMRI study of visual cue-reactivity. It meets exclusion criterion E1 (systematic reviews/meta-analyses). No evidence of task-based fMRI cue-reactivity using visual stimuli is reported.

This article is a systematic review of qualitative evidence, not an original empirical fMRI study. It does not report functional MRI cue-reactivity data using visual stimuli. Therefore it meets exclusion criterion E1.

Abstract describes synthesis/commentary on existing fMRI craving studies and recommendations, with no mention of original data or participants. This fits exclusion criterion E1 (systematic review/review/meta-analysis), not an empirical fMRI study.

Abstract describes a systematic review of the literature (not an original empirical fMRI study). It does not report primary fMRI data assessing visual cue-reactivity in humans. Therefore it meets exclusion criterion E1.

Although this is an fMRI study with original human data, the cue-reactivity paradigm used taste (gustatory) alcohol vs water cues rather than visual stimuli. This meets exclusion E2 (non-visual sensory cues only).

Abstract describes a behavioral study of sensory-specific satiety in amnesic patients with no mention of fMRI or visual cue-reactivity. This examines consummatory/gustatory satiety rather than visual cue-reactivity, so it meets exclusion criterion E2.

The study used magnetoencephalography (MEG) rather than functional MRI, so it does not meet inclusion criterion I2. It is an English-language empirical study (I1 satisfied) and uses visual food cues, but the imaging modality mismatch excludes it from an fMRI cue-reactivity meta-analysis.

Abstract reports neural correlates of alcohol cue reactivity and appears to be an original empirical human study, but it does not explicitly state that functional MRI was used or that visual cues were presented. Abstract describes an original empirical neuroimaging study of neural alcohol cue reactivity and drinking outcomes, satisfying I1. Abstract describes assessment of perceived orosensory pleasure from tasting a milkshake (gustatory cue) and does not report fMRI visual cue-reactivity. This fits exclusion criterion E2 (cue-reactivity using only other sensory cues).

The study used structural MRI and PET to assess gray-matter volume and dopamine receptors and did not perform functional MRI during visual cue-reactivity tasks. It therefore does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (fMRI cue-reactivity with visual stimuli).

Abstract describes a conceptual/methods discussion proposing neuroimaging paradigms and moderators/mediators for relapse prediction rather than reporting original empirical fMRI data using visual cue-reactivity. Empirical human fMRI study reporting brain responses to food cues (a natural reward). Original data in English. Abstract does not specify cue modality (visual vs. other), so include for full-text review to confirm that visual stimuli were used.

Abstract focuses on brain insulin resistance in obesity and does not mention fMRI cue-reactivity to drug or natural rewards using visual stimuli. Abstract indicates an empirical human study on neural responsivity to food cues after RYGB, but it does not explicitly state that fMRI was used during visual cue exposure. Insufficient detail to confirm I2, so include for full-text review.

This is an English-language empirical fMRI study of neural responses to food (a natural reward) and explicitly reports brain response to a visual food cue during scanning, satisfying the review’s visual cue-reactivity requirement. Abstract describes an experimental behavioral study of e-cigarette adverts and perceptions with no mention of fMRI or neuroimaging; does not assess fMRI cue-reactivity to visual drug/natural rewards. Title also includes a meta-analysis component, meeting exclusion criterion E1.
This study is an ALE meta-analysis of fMRI studies (a meta-analysis), not an empirical fMRI study with original participant data; it meets exclusion criterion E1 (systematic reviews/meta-analyses).

The abstract reports an original human fMRI study using visual stimuli (palatable food vs neutral/non-food images) to assess cue-reactivity to a natural reward (food). It meets English original data and fMRI visual cue-reactivity criteria.

Human empirical fMRI study using visual food images (high- vs low-calorie) to measure neural cue-reactivity in regions controlling food intake. Fits meta-analysis on visual cue-reactivity to natural rewards (food).

Study reports original human fMRI data assessing chocolate cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (chocolate and neutral pictures) during scanning. Although olfactory exposure occurred before scanning, cue-reactivity was measured with visual stimuli, meeting inclusion criteria I1 and I2.

Human fMRI study reporting brain responses to food images (visual cues) in healthy women; original empirical data on natural reward-related cue-reactivity (food). Meets language and modality criteria and does not meet any exclusion criteria.
The article is explicitly a systematic review (title and abstract indicate a systematic review), so it is not an original empirical fMRI study and meets exclusion criterion E1.

Abstract describes structural MRI/DTI and VBM in healthy participants and behavioral scales, not an fMRI cue-reactivity experiment using visual drug or natural reward cues. Does not meet the required I2 criterion (task-based fMRI cue-reactivity with visual stimuli).

The abstract describes a broad review of cannabinoid physiology and pharmacology rather than an empirical human fMRI study of visual cue-reactivity. It does not report original fMRI data or visual cue-reactivity experiments, so it meets exclusion criterion E1 (review/meta-analysis).

This paper is a narrative review of neuroimaging and neuromodulation approaches, not an empirical fMRI study reporting original data on visual cue-reactivity. It does not meet the requirement for original human fMRI data, so it should be excluded.

The abstract states this is a review article ('In this review...') rather than an original empirical fMRI study. It does not report primary fMRI data assessing cue-reactivity to visual drug or natural reward cues, so it meets exclusion criterion E1.

Original English empirical study used PET (MOR-specific radioligand) to measure neural responses to food pictures. The review requires functional MRI during visual cue-reactivity; this study does not use fMRI, so it is excluded. It does satisfy I1 but not I2.

This is a meta-analysis (not original fMRI data) examining negative affect manipulations on smoking in laboratory paradigms. It does not report original fMRI studies of visual cue-reactivity, so it meets exclusion criterion E1.

Abstract does not specify whether fMRI was used or whether visual cue-reactivity to food cues was assessed. Title mentions ‘neural signals’ but provides insufficient detail to confirm I2. Include for full-text review to determine if fMRI visual cue-reactivity was conducted.

Abstract describes an original empirical study on neural responses to food cues (a natural reward), but the provided abstract is truncated and does not explicitly confirm that fMRI was conducted during visual cue exposure. Insufficient information to confirm I2, so include for full-text review.

This article is a systematic review (not original empirical fMRI data). Inclusion I1 requires original data; exclusion E1 applies. Although it discusses fMRI among methods, the paper itself is a review, so it should be excluded.

Abstract describes a review/narrative summary of multiple fMRI studies and discusses future research; it does not report original empirical data or methods assessing participants, so it is not an original fMRI study. Does not meet inclusion criterion I1. Therefore excluded as a review article.
Human fMRI study of nicotine effects on attentional reorienting, not a cue-reactivity paradigm to drug or natural reward-related visual stimuli; does not address the review objective.

Original empirical fMRI study in English with human participants (n=39) exposed to visual sexual stimuli assessing neural cue-reactivity (natural reward). Not a review and does not use only non-visual cues. Meets I1 and I2.

Abstract addresses reward processing and risk-taking but does not specify that fMRI was conducted or that visual cue-reactivity was used. Insufficient information to confirm I2, so include for full-text review to determine eligibility.

Abstract reports an fMRI study in methamphetamine-dependent humans but does not specify that cues were drug- or natural reward-related visual stimuli. Insufficient information in abstract to determine whether I2 is met; include for full-text review to verify stimuli type.

Abstract explicitly states this is a review ('we review the scant literature') rather than an empirical fMRI study with original data; thus excluded under E1. No evidence of fMRI cue-reactivity experiments using visual stimuli.

Abstract describes an integrative/modeling review of imaging findings (narrative synthesis), not an original empirical fMRI study of cue-reactivity with visual stimuli. Therefore it meets exclusion criterion E1 (review/meta-analysis).

Abstract provides insufficient information to confirm that fMRI cue-reactivity was conducted using visual stimuli. Title suggests brain reward/craving measures and likely original empirical work, but no explicit mention of fMRI during visual cue exposure. Include for full-text review.

Abstract does not state it reports original empirical fMRI data or that visual cue-reactivity was used; it reads like a review or commentary. Because abstract is insufficient to determine whether I1 and I2 are met, include for full-text review to verify methods and stimuli.

Abstract indicates an original empirical study on bupropion effects on reward-related brain activation but does not explicitly state fMRI methods or use of visual cue-reactivity. Insufficient detail to confirm I2; include for full-text review to verify imaging modality and stimulus type.

This article is a systematic review and meta-analysis of laboratory-based interventions targeting food craving rather than an original empirical fMRI study assessing cue-reactivity with visual stimuli; it meets exclusion criterion E1.

Empirical fMRI study examining brain reactivity to cocaine cues and relapse, so meets I1. Abstract does not specify that cue modality was visual, so cannot confirm I2 from abstract alone. Include for full-text review to verify use of visual stimuli.

This paper is a review article (non-empirical) summarizing evidence rather than reporting original fMRI data; it meets exclusion criterion E1 (systematic review/meta-analysis). No original cue-reactivity fMRI with visual stimuli is reported in abstract.

Original English fMRI study in humans using a monetary incentive delay task with visual incentive cues assessing neural responses to gain/loss anticipation (reward-related cue-reactivity). Meets inclusion criteria for empirical fMRI reward cue-reactivity with visual stimuli.

Abstract reports original empirical work in English examining responses to visual (and olfactory) food cues but does not mention any fMRI scan. Insufficient information to confirm the required fMRI cue-reactivity procedure, so include for full-text review.

Abstract reports a PET study measuring dopamine transporter density, not an fMRI study of cue-reactivity with visual stimuli; it does not meet inclusion criterion I2. Language and original data likely met, but modality and task do not address visual cue-reactivity.

This is a systematic review and meta-analysis of experimental trials on physical activity effects on cigarette cravings, not an original fMRI study assessing drug or natural reward-related cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. Therefore it meets exclusion criterion E1.

This article is an integrative review/introductory piece for a special issue describing review and empirical studies; it is not an original empirical fMRI study reporting primary data. Excluded as a review (E1).

Abstract explicitly states this paper is a review (‘This paper will review...’), indicating it is not an original empirical fMRI study. Therefore it meets exclusion criterion E1 (systematic reviews/meta-analyses).

Study used PET and structural MRI to assess receptor availability and cortical thickness; no functional MRI task or visual cue-reactivity was reported. It meets I1 (empirical, English) but does not meet I2, so excluded from fMRI cue-reactivity meta-analysis.

Original empirical fMRI study in English with human participants using visual alcohol cues (images of beer bottles) and reporting neural responses; directly assesses cue-related neural activity. Meets I1 and I2. No exclusion criteria apply based on the abstract.

This is an English-language empirical report presenting original data (preliminary single-blind, sham-controlled crossover study), but the abstract does not specify that an fMRI was conducted during visual cue-reactivity tasks. Human fMRI study examining brain responses to high-calorie food cues (visual) with original empirical data; aligns with reward-related visual cue-reactivity. Meets language and imaging criteria.

The abstract describes a qualitative review and quantitative meta-analyses of cue-reactivity fMRI studies (i.e., a meta-analysis rather than original empirical data), meeting exclusion criterion E1. Exclude based on being a meta-analysis/systematic review.

The paper is explicitly a systematic review (title states 'a systematic review'), not an original fMRI study with human participants, so it meets exclusion criterion E1 and does not satisfy the fMRI inclusion criterion.

Abstract reports original empirical work on neural regulation of cue-induced food craving (a natural reward) in anorexia nervosa, but does not explicitly state that fMRI was conducted during visual cue exposure. Insufficient info to confirm I2, so include for full-text review.

Abstract describes laterality and commentary on risk factors of addiction (impulsivity, craving) without indicating original empirical fMRI data; appears to be a review/commentary. Does not meet inclusion requirement for original empirical fMRI studies, so excluded as a review (E1).

Abstract reports an original human fMRI study of tramadol effects on reward-related brain activity (meets I1). However, it does not specify whether cue-reactivity was assessed using visual stimuli (I2 unclear). Insufficient detail to determine inclusion, so include for full-text review.
Article is a systematic review and meta-analysis of rTMS for substance dependence, not an original empirical fMRI study of visual cue-reactivity in humans. It therefore meets exclusion criterion E1.

The article is a review/theoretical synthesis across multiple literatures rather than an empirical study reporting original fMRI data. It therefore meets exclusion criterion E1 (systematic review or meta-analysis).

Abstract reports a behavioral study of smoking withdrawal trajectories and abstinence; no mention of fMRI, cue-reactivity, or visual stimuli. Does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (fMRI cue-reactivity using visual stimuli).

Title and abstract language indicate a synthesis of neuroimaging studies (discussion of inconsistent results across studies) rather than an original fMRI experiment presenting new participant data. This matches exclusion criterion E1 (systematic review/meta-analysis).
Qualitative study of alcohol use among Nigerian students with no mention of functional MRI or assessment of visual cue-reactivity; does not meet inclusion criterion I2.
The fMRI paradigm used gustatory (taste) alcohol cues rather than visual stimuli. The review requires visual cue-reactivity studies; exclusion criterion E2 (only other sensory cues) applies.

fMRI study in humans reporting neural responses to visual alcohol cues and appetitive food/goal-related pictures, indicating original empirical data in English; thus meets criteria for fMRI cue-reactivity using visual drug and natural reward stimuli.

The study examines neural correlates of alcohol salience in heavy drinkers, suggesting an empirical neuroimaging investigation, but the abstract does not explicitly state that fMRI was used or that cue-reactivity employed visual stimuli.
