This is an empirical fMRI study of nicotine cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (videos), reporting within-participant contrasts (Smoking>Food, Smoking>Baseline), and group-level whole-brain voxelwise results (FLAME, thresholding, coordinate tables/figures). Methods report participant age/sex, cue types (smoking, food), scanner field strength (Siemens 3T), and processing software (FSL). Whole-brain activation maps and coordinate tables are provided, and task activation (not only connectivity or ROI) is reported. Therefore all inclusion criteria are satisfied.

This is an empirical fMRI study of alcohol cue reactivity using visual stimuli (pictures) with within-subject contrasts of alcohol > neutral cues. Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses were performed using SPM5 (reported with cluster thresholds and peak coordinates, and small-volume FWE correction for striatal ROIs), and results are reported with coordinates and cluster descriptions. Participant age and sex are reported, cue type is alcohol, scanner field strength is 3T, and processing software is specified. Therefore all inclusion criteria are satisfied and no exclusion criteria are met.

This study used an alcohol taste-cue (gustatory) paradigm rather than visual cue stimuli, so it does not meet the meta-analysis requirement for studies employing visual stimuli (I3). Although the paper includes a within-participant Alcohol>Water contrast (I4), reports whole-brain, group-level voxelwise results and coordinates (I5,I6), and provides participant/cue/scanner/processing details (I7), the modality mismatch (taste vs. visual) disqualifies it per the stated inclusion criteria. No exclusion criteria (E3–E6) were directly violated.

This is an fMRI study of nicotine (smoking) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (smoking vs neutral pictures), with a within-subject contrast of smoking>neutral. The paper reports whole-brain, group-level voxelwise analyses (paired t-tests; threshold z>2.3, cluster p<0.05) and lists significant clusters (Tables S2/S3). Processing software (FSL v5.0.9) is explicitly reported. Participant characteristics (26 male college students) are given and referenced in Table S1 (demographics/age). The study therefore provides the required behavioral/contrast, whole-brain activation results, and methodological details needed for inclusion. Relevant details (e.g., scanner acquisition parameters/field strength) are referenced in supplementary materials (supplementary tables/methods), consistent with reporting practices and I6 allowance for supplementary reporting. Based on these points, all inclusion criteria are met for the meta-analysis of visual drug-cue fMRI studies.

This is an empirical fMRI study of food (natural reward) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (food images vs matched objects). It reports within-participant task contrasts (food>object) and conducts group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses using FSL (FLAME/randomise) with cluster correction. Participant age and sex are reported, scanner field strength (3T Siemens Skyra) and processing software (FSL, Freesurfer) are described. Results include whole-brain statistical maps and cluster-based findings. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only, or non-empirical) apply. Therefore it meets all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

This study is an empirical fMRI investigation of alcohol cue reactivity using visual stimuli. It reports within-participant contrasts of alcohol versus control pictures, performs and reports group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses (one-sample t-test and task main effects) and presents results in tables/figures. Methods report participant demographics (age, sex), cue type (alcohol), MRI field strength (1.5 T), and processing software (SPM5). Although ROI analyses were also conducted, whole-brain task activation maps and results are clearly described, so no ROI-only exclusion applies. Therefore the study meets all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

This is an empirical fMRI study of alcohol cue reactivity using visual stimuli (alcohol and non-alcohol beverage images) in young adults. The paper reports within-subject contrasts of alcohol vs. non-alcohol images and also repetition contrasts. Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses were conducted (SPM5) with clusterwise correction and results reported in tables/figures. Participant age and sex, cue type (alcohol), scanner field strength (3T Siemens Allegra), and processing software (SPM5) are all reported. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, or non-empirical) apply.

The study used individualized script-driven imagery (auditory/imaginal) cues rather than visual stimuli. Therefore it does not satisfy inclusion criterion I3, which requires cue-reactivity assessed using visual stimuli. The paper does meet other inclusion criteria (within-participant drug cue > control contrasts [I4]; whole-brain, group-level voxelwise analyses reported [I5/I6]; and reported participant age/sex, cue type (cocaine/alcohol), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software AFNI [I7]). Because the visual-stimulus requirement (I3) is unmet, the study must be excluded from this meta-analysis.

While this is an fMRI cue-reactivity study of nicotine using visual cigarette cues and within-subject cigarette>neutral contrasts, the manuscript does not report any group-level, whole-brain (voxelwise) task-evoked statistical maps. Analyses focus on ROI-based activation comparisons and directed functional/effective connectivity among a priori ROIs (GIMME/uSEM). Therefore it fails the requirement for whole-brain task activation reporting and instead reports only ROI/effective-connectivity results. This matches exclusion criteria E3 (ROI-only analyses with no whole-brain results) and E4 (only effective/seed-based connectivity without an accompanying whole-brain task map).

This is an fMRI study of alcohol (drug) cue-reactivity using standardized visual stimuli (alcohol, stress, neutral images). Within-subject contrasts (alcohol cue > neutral, stress > neutral, neutral > baseline) are reported and used in analyses. The paper reports whole-brain, voxel-wise group-level analyses with cluster-wise correction (p<0.001, alphasim/FWE), and presents voxel-based results in figures/tables. Necessary methodological details are reported: participant age ranges and sex composition, cue type (alcohol), MRI field strength (3T), and processing/software (BioImageSuite; AFNI used for cluster correction). No exclusion criteria are violated (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, includes original empirical data). Therefore it meets all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

This is a resting-state fMRI seed-based connectivity study examining raphe nucleus–hippocampal connectivity and links to tobacco withdrawal. It does not involve any cue-reactivity task using visual stimuli, contains no within-participant drug-cue > control-stimulus contrasts, and reports no task-evoked, whole-brain activation maps. Therefore it does not meet the review’s inclusion criteria for task-based visual cue-reactivity. It specifically falls under exclusion criterion E4 (only seed-based connectivity/resting-state analyses without a whole-brain task activation map).

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual cue-reactivity (video) for heroin cues vs neutral cues with within-subject contrasts (heroin>neutral). Group-level voxelwise whole-brain analyses were performed (2×2 factorial, cluster-corrected maps) and results reported as activation coordinates and figures. Participant demographics (age, sex), cue type (heroin), scanner field strength (1.5T), and processing software (AFNI) are reported. Thus all required inclusion criteria are met and no exclusion criteria apply.

This is an fMRI pharmaco-study of alcohol cue-reactivity using visual stimuli with within-participant contrast (alcohol cues > neutral cues). Group-level voxelwise analyses were conducted (GLM, t-tests and ANOVAs) and whole-brain results are reported (uncorrected exploratory whole-brain results in supplementary tables), satisfying the requirement for a group-level, whole-brain task-evoked map. The paper reports participant age/sex, cue type (alcohol), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM12). Therefore it meets inclusion criteria I3, I4, I5, I6 and I7.

This is an fMRI study of visual food cues (natural reward) with within-participant contrasts (e.g., fattening food > object), and reports participant demographics, scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (FSL). However, the group-level analyses were conducted using a region-of-interest (ROI) approach and the authors explicitly state they “limited our investigation to regions of interest.” No unmasked, whole-brain (voxelwise) group activation map is reported. Therefore the study fails the requirement for whole-brain task-evoked results and meets exclusion criterion E3 (ROI-only analyses).

This is an fMRI study of smoking (nicotine) visual cue reactivity with within-subject smoking>neutral contrasts, but the manuscript reports analyses based on ROI-extracted beta weights (left/right NAc and caudate) rather than presenting a group-level, whole-brain (voxelwise) task activation map or whole-brain coordinates/figures/tables. While first-level and subject-level contrasts were generated, no unmasked group-level whole-brain results are reported in the text, tables, or figures to meet the meta-analysis requirement for whole-brain evidence. Therefore it fails the whole-brain reporting criterion and must be excluded.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual drug-related cues (alcohol pictures) with within-participant contrasts (alcohol-related > neutral) and voxelwise, group-level whole-brain analyses reported (including coordinates and corrected statistical maps). Methods report scanner field strength (3T), age range and subject numbers, cue type (alcohol), and processing software (TurboBrainVoyager, BrainVoyager, AFNI, FSL). Results include whole-brain task-evoked activation comparisons and tables/coordinates for significant clusters and seed-based connectivity as supplementary analyses. Therefore all required inclusion criteria for cue-reactivity visual fMRI studies are met and no exclusion criteria apply.

Although this study is an fMRI investigation of natural reward (juice) using a visual conditioned cue and reports within-subject event contrasts (CS vs other event types) and relevant participant/scanner/method details, the reported analyses are focused on a priori ROI-based statistics. The full text describes voxel-wise preprocessing but presents group results and statistical testing primarily as ROI mean values rather than unmasked, whole-brain (voxelwise) group-level activation maps or coordinate tables/figures of whole-brain task effects. Therefore it does not meet the requirement for whole-brain task-evoked results (I5) and falls under exclusion criterion E3 (ROI-only reporting).

This study is an fMRI investigation using visual alcohol cues in alcohol-dependent patients and reports within-subject contrasts of alcohol > control (soft drink) before and after training. Group-level voxelwise analyses were conducted and whole-brain activations are reported (with supplementary Table S1), alongside region-of-interest small-volume corrections. The manuscript provides participant sex (male), cue type (alcohol), MRI scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM8). Therefore all required inclusion criteria are met: it assesses visual drug cue reactivity with fMRI (I3), includes within-participant drug>control contrasts (I4), presents group-level whole-brain task activation results (I5) reported in the main text and supplement (I6), and reports participant and MRI details (I7). No exclusion criteria are violated.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual cues (subliminal cocaine, sexual, aversive, neutral) and reports within-subject contrasts (cocaine>neutral) and group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses (second-level t tests, cluster-corrected thresholds). Whole-brain results are reported with coordinates and figures. Relevant participant information (age range, all-male sample), cue types, scanner field strength (3T), and preprocessing/software (SPM8) are provided. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only without unmasked task maps, or non-empirical) apply. Therefore the study meets all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

This is an fMRI study using visual alcohol-related cue videos and includes within-subject contrasts of craving-inducing cues vs control (mosaic) and aversion-inducing cues vs control. The authors report group-level, whole-brain random-effects GLM analyses (SPMs) with voxelwise FDR thresholds and region results, satisfying whole-brain task-evoked reporting. Methodological details required are provided: participant demographics (age/sex compared), cue type (alcohol), scanner field strength (1.5 T), and processing software (BrainVoyager BVQX 1.9). Therefore it meets inclusion criteria I3–I7 and does not violate exclusion criteria (no ROI-only, connectivity-only, or non-empirical restrictions).

This is an empirical within-subject fMRI study of natural reward (food) cue-reactivity that includes visual (picture) stimuli. Subject-level contrasts include HFHS and LFLS and NF versus rest and HFHS>NF and LFLS>NF comparisons; group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses were performed (SPM12) reporting significant clusters (p=.001 uncorrected, cluster extent). Participant age/sex, cue type (food), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software are all reported. Therefore the study satisfies all required inclusion criteria for visual natural reward cue-reactivity fMRI and does not meet any exclusion criteria.

This is an fMRI study of emotion processing (emotional faces task and dot-probe) in AUD participants and does not use drug-related or natural reward-related visual cues (e.g., alcohol images) nor report any within-participant contrast of drug-cues > control stimuli. Although the paper reports group-level, whole-brain task activation maps and provides coordinate tables and methods (satisfying I5 and I6), it fails to meet the core inclusion criteria for this meta-analysis: I3 (task must assess drug or natural reward-related cue-reactivity using visual stimuli) and I4 (must include a within-participant drug-cue > control contrast). Therefore the study is ineligible for inclusion in this meta-analysis.

This is an fMRI study with visual drug-related words and whole-brain analyses, but it does not report any within-participant contrast of drug cues > control stimuli (analyses were collapsed across word type because no word-type effects were observed). The meta-analysis requires at least one within-participant drug-cue>control or natural-reward>control contrast (I4), which is not present. The study does meet other requirements (visual stimuli, whole-brain task maps, and reporting of participant demographics, cue type, scanner and processing software), but absence of the critical drug-cue vs. control within-subject contrast precludes inclusion.

The study is an fMRI investigation using visual cocaine cues and neutral control stimuli, reports within-participant contrasts (cocaine cues > neutral cues), and presents group-level whole-brain voxelwise task-evoked results (FLAME mixed-effects, cluster-thresholded maps) with tables/figures describing activations. Scanner field strength (3T), processing software (FSL), participant sex/age information, and cue type (cocaine visual stimuli) are reported. Therefore it satisfies inclusion criteria I3, I4, I5, I6, and I7; no exclusion criteria apply.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual natural reward cues (appetitive food images) with a within-subject contrast of food>scrambled control stimuli. The paper reports a group-level, whole-sample one-sample t-test (whole-brain, voxelwise, FDR-corrected) demonstrating task-evoked activation, and also provides ROI and between-group analyses. Relevant methodological details are reported (cue type: food; MRI field strength: 3T; processing software: SPM5; participant groups and sex matching). Although the group comparison focused on an a-priori ROI, the presence of an unmasked, whole-brain task effect satisfies the whole-brain evidence requirement. Therefore the study meets all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis objective.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual alcohol cues (alcohol-related pictures) and reports within-participant contrasts (‘favourite drinks > neutral’) at the individual level and group-level analyses. The paper reports whole-brain voxelwise results (P<0.001 uncorrected; cluster tables/coordinates and figures) as well as ROI analyses, and provides participant age/sex, cue type (alcohol), MRI field strength (3 T) and processing software (SPM5). No exclusion criteria apply (not ROI-only, not connectivity/resting-state-only, and includes whole-brain task activation maps).

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual tobacco advertisements as drug-related cues. It reports within-subject contrasts (tobacco > control advertisement) and conducts group-level, whole-brain voxelwise second-level analyses using SPM5 with FDR-corrected statistical maps and tables/figures. Participant demographics (age, all male), cue type (nicotine/tobacco advertisement), MRI scanner field strength (1.5 T) and processing software (SPM5) are all reported. Thus it meets all required inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis and does not violate any exclusion criteria.

This is an fMRI study of nicotine (smoking) cue-reactivity using visual video stimuli, reporting within-participant contrasts of smoking versus control videos. The paper describes group-level, mixed-effects whole-brain voxelwise analyses (t-maps) with correction and lists significant peaks (Tables S1–S2) and figures, fulfilling whole-brain evidence requirements. Participant age and sex are reported, cue type is nicotine, scanner field strength (3T) and processing software (NIAK, fmristat) are specified. Results are not limited to ROI-only analyses and a within-subject drug>control contrast is explicitly tested and reported. Therefore it meets all inclusion criteria and does not violate exclusion criteria.

This study is an fMRI investigation of drug/alcohol cue reactivity using visual stimuli and within-subject drug>neutral contrasts, but its primary reported analyses are seed-based task-modulated functional connectivity (gPPI) across selected ROIs rather than group-level, whole-brain (voxelwise) task-evoked activation maps. The manuscript does not present an unmasked whole-brain task activation map or whole-brain voxelwise results (or coordinate tables/figures) for the drug>neutral task contrast; instead results focus on ROI-based connectivity changes. Therefore it meets inclusion criteria I3 and I4 but fails I5 and violates exclusion criteria E3 and E4.

This is an empirical fMRI study in daily smokers that uses visual cues (picture representations of money, cigarette puffs, or neutral) during a rewarded guessing task and reports within-subject contrasts comparing smoking cue anticipation (puff) > neutral and money > neutral. Group-level, whole-brain (exploratory) analyses are reported (uncorrected p<.001, cluster thresholds) in addition to ROI analyses. Key methodological details required by the meta-analysis are present: participant age/sex, cue type (nicotine), scanner field strength (3.0T), and processing software (SPM8). Thus the study meets inclusion criteria I3, I4, I5, I6, and I7 and does not violate listed exclusion criteria (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, includes task activation maps).

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual drug-related cues (drug words) versus neutral words in 15 cocaine-dependent and 15 control participants. It reports within-subject drug>neutral contrasts and voxelwise whole-brain group-level analyses (SPM2) with cluster-corrected coordinates (reported in Table 2 and text). Participant demographics (age, sex) and cue type (cocaine), scanner field strength (4T), and processing software (SPM2) are described. Therefore it meets all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis of visual cue-reactivity.

Full text could not be accessed (page failed to load). Title indicates an alcohol-related fMRI go/no-go task, so I3 (fMRI study with alcohol-related visual stimuli) appears likely, but critical inclusion criteria cannot be verified: there is no way to confirm a within-participant contrast of alcohol cues > control stimuli (I4), the presence of group-level whole-brain (voxelwise) task-evoked statistical maps or coordinates (I5/I6), or reporting of scanner details and processing software (I7). Because the required unmasked, whole-brain task effects cannot be confirmed from the available information, the study cannot be included in the meta-analysis.

This is an fMRI study of cocaine cue reactivity using visual stimuli (paraphernalia and cocaine images) with within-subject cocaine>neutral contrasts reported. Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses were performed (SPM8) with cluster-level inference and peak coordinates/clusters reported. The manuscript provides participant age range and sex (men and women, ages 18–65), specifies the cue type (cocaine), scanner field strength (Siemens 3.0T Trio), and processing software (SPM8). Results include whole-brain statistical maps and tables/coordinates of significant clusters (e.g., left angular gyrus, lateral occipital cortex, nucleus accumbens). No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, non-empirical) apply. Therefore the study meets inclusion criteria I3–I7 for the meta-analysis of visual cue-reactivity fMRI studies.

This is an empirical fMRI study of cannabis cue reactivity using visual stimuli. It includes within-subject contrasts of cannabis cues > neutral control stimuli, reports whole-brain voxelwise task activation results (ANOVA and post hoc voxelwise t tests) with coordinates and region-of-interest analyses, and provides participant demographics (male-only sample) and notes that fMRI acquisition and processing details are provided in the Supplement. Whole-brain results are reported in the text and coordinate-based results are described. The study is not limited to ROI-only or connectivity-only analyses. Therefore it meets all listed inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

This is an fMRI alcohol cue-reactivity study using visual stimuli and within-subject ALC>BEV contrasts (meets I3 and I4). Scanner field strength (3T), processing software (FSL), cue type (alcohol), and participant demographics are reported (meets I7). However, the full text reports ROI-based analyses (percent signal change extracted from anatomically defined ventral striatum ROIs) and focuses on ROI results; there is no description or presentation of group-level, whole-brain (voxelwise) statistical maps, voxel coordinates, whole-brain tables/figures, or supplementary whole-brain results. Because no whole-brain, voxelwise task-evoked activation map is reported, the study does not meet the whole-brain evidence requirement (I5) and therefore must be excluded per exclusion criterion E3 (ROI-only reporting).

Although this fMRI study examines nicotine (smoking) cue reactivity using visual stimuli and within-subject SC>non-SC contrasts and reports participant age/sex and scanner/software details, all task analyses were conducted within an a priori ROI mask and results are reported as ROI-restricted voxelwise clusters and ROI beta extractions. The paper does not present an unmasked, group-level whole-brain (voxelwise) task activation map. Therefore it reports only ROI-restricted task results and meets exclusion criterion E3 (ROI-only results).

This is an event-related fMRI study using visual heroin-related versus neutral cues and includes a within-subject heroin-related > neutral contrast. Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses are reported (random-effects two-sample t-tests) with multiple-comparison correction (AFNI Alphasim) and results presented in tables/figures. Methods report participant demographics (male subjects; age referenced), cue type (heroin), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM8). Thus all required inclusion criteria for visual drug cue-reactivity whole-brain fMRI studies are satisfied.

This is an fMRI study of natural reward (food) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli, reporting within-subject contrasts of high-calorie vs. low-calorie cues. Whole-brain, group-level voxelwise analyses were performed with SPM5 (cluster-corrected maps reported), and relevant participant demographics (age, sex), cue type (food), scanner field strength (1.5 T), and processing software are provided. All required inclusion criteria are met and no exclusion criteria apply.

This is an fMRI study of performance feedback and pharmacologic effects in smokers (task: motion prediction with emoji feedback), not a cue-reactivity task using visual drug or natural reward cues. It does not present or contrast drug-related (or natural reward-related) visual stimuli versus control stimuli, so it fails key inclusion criteria I3 and I4. The study does report whole-brain, voxelwise task-evoked maps and sufficient methodological details (scanner field strength, processing software, participant age/sex), but because it does not assess cue-reactivity contrasts (drug cues > control), it is not eligible for the meta-analysis.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual drug cues (cocaine words) in a within-subject cocaine-word Stroop task (CW vs NW). The paper reports a voxelwise, group-level SPM12 second-level analysis of the CW minus NW contrast (whole-brain), and provides cluster information and DCM results. Relevant participant details (age range, sex distribution), cue type (cocaine), MRI field strength (3.0T), and processing software (AFNI for despiking, SPM12 for preprocessing/analysis) are reported. The study therefore meets inclusion criteria I3, I4, I5, I6, and I7 and does not violate exclusion criteria (e.g., it is not ROI-only or connectivity-only).

This study used resting-state fMRI (ICA and ACC-seeded rsFC) and DTI to examine neural correlates of withdrawal-induced craving and structural connectivity. It did not use a task-based visual cue-reactivity paradigm or present drug-related visual cues, and no within-participant contrast of drug cues > control stimuli is reported. There are no whole-brain, task-evoked activation maps from a cue-reactivity task. Therefore it meets exclusion criterion E4 (reporting only resting-state/seed-based connectivity without a whole-brain task activation map).

This study is a task-based fMRI investigation of heroin cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (heroin-related images) in heroin-dependent participants under methadone maintenance. The primary contrast reported is heroin-related > neutral cues (within-participant contrast), and group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses (ANOVA and cluster correction via Monte Carlo simulation) are presented. Results are reported in whole-brain tables/figures and described in text (with coordinate-based cluster results), and the manuscript reports participant sex (all male), cue type (heroin), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM8, DPABI). The study is not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, and provides the required whole-brain task activation map. Therefore it meets all included criteria for the meta-analysis.

This is an event-related fMRI study using visual heroin-related cues (images) and neutral controls. It reports within-participant contrasts (Heroin > Neutral) and group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses (two-sample t-tests) with FDR correction and cluster thresholds, and results are described in the text (with coordinates/regions). Participant demographics (male, ages 18–50), cue type (heroin), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM8) are reported. No exclusion criteria apply (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, includes unmasked whole-brain task effects). Therefore the study meets all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

This study is a resting-state fMRI investigation of insula functional connectivity after 12 h smoking abstinence, not a task-based cue-reactivity experiment. It does not employ visual drug or natural reward cues, nor report any within-participant contrast of drug-cues > control stimuli or any task-evoked, whole-brain group-level activation maps. Instead, it reports seed-based resting-state connectivity analyses. Therefore it does not meet the meta-analysis inclusion criteria for visual cue-reactivity fMRI studies and violates exclusion criterion E4 (resting-state/seed-based connectivity without task activation).

This is an fMRI study of cue reactivity using visual stimuli (cocaine and food videos). The paper reports within-subject contrasts comparing cocaine vs food cues, with group-level, whole-brain voxel-wise analyses (cluster results, corrected thresholds, cluster volumes) using AFNI on a 3T scanner. Participant age/sex, cue types, scanner strength, and processing software are reported. Results are not limited to ROI or connectivity-only analyses. Therefore all inclusion criteria for visual cue-reactivity fMRI whole-brain task activation are met and no exclusion criteria apply.

Although this study uses visual alcohol cues and reports within-subject cue>control fMRI contrasts (I3, I4), it is a single-case report (one patient) and does not provide a group-level, whole-brain (voxelwise) task-evoked statistical map as required by I5. Key methodological details required by I7 (e.g., MRI scanner field strength and processing software) are not clearly reported in the provided text. Because it lacks the required group-level whole-brain activation maps, it does not meet the meta-analysis inclusion criteria and must be excluded.

This is a task-based fMRI study using visual cannabis cues versus matched neutral images (meets visual-stimuli requirement). It includes within-participant contrasts of cannabis cues > control stimuli and reports group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses (FSL mixed-effects, cluster-corrected Z>2.3, P<0.05) with clusters/localizations described in text/tables/figures. Relevant participant demographics (age range, sex distribution), cue type (cannabis), MRI field strength (3T) and processing software (FSL) are reported. Therefore all required inclusion criteria are met and no exclusion criteria (e.g., ROI-only, connectivity-only, or non-empirical) apply.

This is a task-based fMRI study using visual food cues (a natural reward) with within-participant contrasts (high-calorie food vs low-calorie food; food vs nonfood). Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses were performed using SPM12 (cluster-level thresholding reported), and results are described in the text. Relevant methodological details (participant age, cue type: food, scanner field strength: 3T, processing software: SPM12) are provided. Therefore the study meets the meta-analysis inclusion criteria for visual natural reward cue-reactivity and reports whole-brain task activation maps.

This is an event-related fMRI study using visual heroin-related pictures and neutral pictures, reporting within-participant contrasts (heroin vs neutral). Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses were performed with SPM8 and results reported as cluster activations and MNI coordinates (Monte Carlo-corrected). The manuscript reports participant age/sex info, cue type (heroin), scanner field strength (3.0T) and processing software (SPM8). Therefore it satisfies all specified inclusion requirements for whole-brain visual cue-reactivity studies.

The title and bibliographic information indicate this is an fMRI study of smoking (nicotine) cue-reactivity using smoking cues (visual stimuli) and reports transient BOLD responses to smoking cues. This implies within-participant contrasts of smoking cues > control stimuli and task-evoked fMRI activation. Published in Neuropsychopharmacology (2008), it likely reports group-level, whole-brain voxelwise results (typical for the journal/period) and would include coordinates/figures as described. Participant demographics and scanner/processing details are typically reported. Because the full text could not be loaded, I cannot directly view the maps/tables, so confidence is moderate.

This is an fMRI study of cue-reactivity using visual drug-related stimuli (drug words) in cocaine-addicted participants. The study includes within-participant contrasts comparing drug versus matched neutral words (drug>neutral and drug<neutral contrasts were created), and reports group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses using random-effects SPM2 (voxelwise t-tests and coordinates reported). Reporting includes voxel coordinates and descriptions in the text and figures. Participant demographics (age, sex), cue type (cocaine), MRI scanner field strength (4T), and processing software (SPM2) are all provided. Although the drug>neutral difference did not survive cluster-level correction, the inclusion criteria do not require significance, only that the within-subject contrast and whole-brain group analysis were performed and reported. Therefore all required inclusion criteria are met.

This study is an fMRI investigation of cognitive control (a multisensory numeric Stroop task) in individuals with cocaine use disorder, not a drug or natural reward cue-reactivity task using visual cues. It does not include any within-participant drug-cue > control-stimulus contrasts or report cue-reactivity to visual drug/natural reward stimuli. Although whole-brain task-evoked results and methodological details (scanner field strength, AFNI processing, participant age/sex) are reported (I5, I6), it fails to meet key inclusion criteria I3 and I4 (no drug/natural reward visual cue-reactivity and no drug-cue > control contrast). Therefore it is not eligible for inclusion in the meta-analysis.

This is an empirical fMRI study examining visual cannabis cue-reactivity in adolescent cannabis users. The study reports within-subject contrasts of cannabis vs. non-cannabis images, performs group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses (with cluster-thresholding described), and provides required sample details (age, sex), cue type (cannabis), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (FSL/FEAT). Thus it satisfies all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis and violates none of the exclusion criteria.

This is an fMRI study using visual smoking-related pictures (nicotine cues) with healthy adult participants and reports within-subject contrasts of smoking-related > neutral cues. The paper provides group-level, whole-brain voxelwise task-evoked analyses (mixed-effects whole-brain results reported with cluster correction in ACC/OFC, SFG, occipital cortex) as well as ROI results; thus whole-brain task maps are available. Participant age/sex, cue type (nicotine), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (FSL/FEAT) are explicitly reported. All required methodological details for inclusion in a meta-analysis of visual drug cue-reactivity fMRI studies are present.

This is an empirical fMRI study of cannabis cue-reactivity that explicitly compares drug cues to natural reward (fruit) and neutral cues using visual (and tactile) stimulus presentations. First-level within-subject contrasts reported include cannabis>neutral, cannabis>fruit, and fruit>neutral. Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses were performed (FSL FEAT/FLAME) with cluster/FWE correction and results described in text/figures/tables. The manuscript reports scanner field strength (3T), processing software (SPM8 and FSL/FILM/FEAT), cue types (cannabis, fruit/food), and participant demographics are referenced (demographics table). The study therefore meets all specified inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis and does not meet any exclusion criteria (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, includes within-subject and group whole-brain maps, and is original empirical work).

This study is an fMRI investigation of cue reactivity in regular cocaine users, reporting a cue reactivity task and cue-induced craving measures, and explicitly states that exploratory whole-brain analyses were performed. Participant age range (18–55) and sex (male only), cue type (cocaine), and MRI scanner field strength (Philips 3.0T) are reported; imaging preprocessing and additional analysis details are referenced in the supplementary materials. The paper therefore includes within-participant cue>control analyses (cue reactivity contrasts) and reports whole-brain task-evoked results (including in supplementary materials), meeting the meta-analysis requirements. Some specifics (explicit statement that stimuli were visual and full processing software details) are provided or implied in the supplementary materials rather than the main text, so confidence is moderate.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual cues (geometric figures) in a monetary incentive delay task to probe anticipation of monetary reward and loss (natural reward-related cue-reactivity). The paper reports within-participant contrasts (gain cues > neutral cues; loss cues > neutral cues) and group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses using SPM5, with reported coordinates and cluster statistics; small-volume correction for the ventral striatum was applied but whole-brain results and coordinates are provided. Participant sex and age-matching, cue type (monetary), scanner field strength (1.5T), and processing software (SPM5) are reported. Therefore all inclusion criteria I3, I4, I5, I6, and I7 are satisfied and no exclusion criteria apply.

This is not a cue-reactivity fMRI study using visual drug or natural-reward cues. The paradigm is a counting Stroop with parametric monetary reward contingencies (money cues), not drug-related or natural reward visual cue exposure. It therefore does not meet inclusion criteria I3 (cue-reactivity using visual drug/natural reward stimuli) or I4 (within-participant contrast of drug- or natural-reward cues > control). Additionally, the report focuses on ROI-based analyses (parametric and PPI within predefined ROIs) rather than providing an unmasked, whole-brain task activation map relevant to cue-reactivity. Thus it is not eligible for the meta-analysis objective.

This is an fMRI alcohol cue-reactivity study using visual stimuli and reports within-participant alcohol>beverage (ALC–BEV) contrasts in predefined ROIs (VS, mPFC, OFC). However, the paper reports only region-of-interest analyses (extraction of ROI timecourses and GLMs/HLMs on ROI averages) and does not present or describe a group-level, whole-brain (voxelwise) task-evoked statistical map or unmasked whole-brain task effect. Therefore it violates the requirement for whole-brain results (I5) and meets exclusion criterion E3. The study does meet other criteria (fMRI cue-reactivity with visual alcohol cues and reporting participant/scanner/processing details), but the absence of whole-brain voxelwise activation maps necessitates exclusion from the meta-analysis.

This is an empirical BOLD fMRI study of alcohol cue-reactivity that presents visual (and olfactory) alcohol-related stimuli and contrasts alcohol cues vs control stimuli. The analysis is whole-brain, voxelwise (FSL; cluster-level FWE correction reported) and reports group-level task activation results. Participant demographics (age, sex), cue type (alcohol), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (FSL) are reported. Therefore it meets all required inclusion criteria for visual-cue fMRI whole-brain task activation analyses.

The study reports a functional MRI experiment using visual heroin-related cues in early-abstinent heroin users (meets I3). It includes within-participant contrasts of heroin-related vs neutral stimuli and group-level analyses (heroin-related > neutral; meets I4). Analyses were whole-brain, voxelwise random-effects SPM2 tests with reported MNI coordinates (meets I5 and I6). The paper reports participant age and sex, cue type (heroin), scanner field strength (1.5T), and processing software (SPM2) (meets I7). It is empirical fMRI task activation data and does not violate exclusion criteria (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, includes whole-brain task maps).

This is an fMRI cue-reactivity study using visual cocaine-related stimuli with within-subject contrasts (Cocaine > Neutral) and group-level random-effects voxelwise analyses. The paper reports whole-brain exploratory results (p<.001 uncorrected) and ROI small-volume corrected results, provides coordinates and statistical values, and reports participant demographics (age/sex), cue type (cocaine), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM8). Thus it meets all required inclusion criteria for visual drug cue-reactivity fMRI studies and does not violate listed exclusions.

This is an fMRI study of cannabis cue-reactivity using visual stimuli with within-subject cannabis>neutral contrasts and reports participant demographics, scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM8). However, all reported group-level results are restricted to a priori ROIs with small-volume FWE correction; the paper does not present an unmasked, whole-brain (voxelwise) task-activation map or whole-brain group results. Therefore it fails the requirement for whole-brain evidence and must be excluded (E3).

This is an event-related fMRI study using visual cues (gaming screenshots and smoking images) that reports within-participant contrasts (gaming>neutral and smoking>neutral) and group-level, voxelwise whole-brain analyses using SPM5 (3T scanner) with corrected thresholds and conjunction analysis. Participant sex (males) and age criteria are reported. Results are reported as whole-brain activations and described in the text/tables/figures. Therefore all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis are met, and no exclusion criteria apply.

This is an fMRI study of cocaine cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (cocaine-related words) with a within-participant cocaine>neutral contrast. The paper reports group-level whole-brain (voxelwise) task activation analyses (FWE-corrected), describes significant clusters and between-group contrasts, and refers to supplementary materials for results. Scanner field strength (3T) and processing software (CamBA/SPM) are reported, and participant age/sex and cue type (cocaine) are provided. Thus it satisfies inclusion criteria I3, I4, I5, I6, and I7.

This is an empirical 3T BOLD fMRI study of nicotine cue-reactivity using visual smoking vs neutral pictures, reporting within-participant contrasts (smoking cues > neutral). The paper presents voxelwise, whole-brain group-level analyses (cluster-corrected results with coordinates reported for the mPFC and individual peak coordinates) and details preprocessing and SPM5 software. Participant age, sex, cue type, scanner field strength, and processing software are reported. It therefore satisfies inclusion criteria for an fMRI visual cue-reactivity whole-brain task activation study and does not meet any exclusion criteria (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, includes unmasked whole-brain task effects).

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual cues (drug-related heroin pictures and water-related pictures) and reports within-participant contrasts (Drug > Neutral; Thirst > Neutral). Analyses were conducted as group-level, whole-brain voxelwise SPM2 second-level one-sample t-tests with voxel-wise thresholds and cluster extent; results are reported with Talairach coordinates and cluster information. Participant age/sex, cue types, scanner field strength (1.5T), and processing software (SPM2) are all reported. No exclusion criteria apply (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, includes whole-brain task effects). Therefore it meets all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual food cues (natural reward) with within-subject contrasts of food>non-food and high-calorie>non-food reported. The authors report group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses (Z>2.3, p<0.05 whole-brain corrected) and describe cluster/coordinate results; they also provide ROI analyses but do not rely exclusively on them. Relevant participant information (age, sex), cue type (food), MRI field strength (3T), and processing software (FSL) are reported. No exclusion criteria are violated (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, not only between-group contrasts, and is empirical). Therefore the study meets all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis of fMRI visual cue-reactivity to natural rewards.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual natural reward cues (food images) with within-subject contrasts of food vs. non-food images. The paper reports group-level whole-brain (voxelwise) analyses (noted in Methods and Supplementary material) in addition to ROI analyses. Relevant details are provided: participant age and sex (25 healthy females, 18–29), cue type (food), scanner field strength (3 T), and processing software (FSL/FEAT). Although key hypotheses used ROI analyses, whole-brain results are described and available in the supplement, satisfying the meta-analysis requirements.

This is an fMRI study of heroin-related cue reactivity using visual stimuli, with within-subject drug cue > neutral contrast and group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses. The manuscript reports whole-brain voxelwise paired t-tests and whole-brain correlations (thresholded and cluster-corrected) and provides peak coordinates for significant clusters. Relevant participant demographics (age, sex), cue type (heroin visual images), MRI field strength (3T Siemens Tim Trio) and processing software (FSL) are reported. Therefore all required inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis are met and no exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, or non-empirical) apply.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual smoking-related (cigarette) cues and matched neutral images, addressing cue-reactivity (I3). Within-participant Craving>Neutral contrasts are reported for each group and used in analyses (I4). The paper reports group-level, whole-brain random-effects voxelwise analyses with cluster-correction, coordinates, and t-values for Craving>Neutral and between-group contrasts (I5, I6). Methods include participant demographic information, cue type (nicotine/cigarette), scanner field strength (3.0T), and processing software (BrainVoyager QX), fulfilling I7. No exclusion criteria (e.g., ROI-only, connectivity-only, non-empirical) apply. Therefore the study meets all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

This is an fMRI study of visual smoking cue-reactivity (smoking images vs. neutral) using within-subject contrasts (smoking>neutral). The paper reports group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses (random-effects GLM and mixed-effects ANCOVA) with cluster correction and lists regional results. Participant characteristics include sex (only women), cue type (nicotine/smoking images), MRI scanner field strength (Siemens Trio 3T), and processing software (BrainVoyager QX). Thus it satisfies the review’s requirements for task-based whole-brain activation maps to visual drug cues and reports relevant methodological/participant details. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only without task effects, or non-empirical) apply.

Although the study presents fMRI CBF responses to cocaine-related videos (visual drug cues) and thus meets I3, it does not report a within-participant task contrast of drug cues > control stimuli nor a group-level, whole-brain voxelwise task-evoked activation map. The analyses are primarily seed-based connectivity (amygdala–dorsal cingulate) using wavelet transform coherence and individual-level ASL connectivity measures. These are connectivity-focused results rather than unmasked whole-brain task activation maps required for inclusion. Therefore it meets exclusion criterion E4 (only seed-based/connectivity analyses without a whole-brain task activation map).

This study includes an fMRI drug-cue task (drug word > fixation/neutral contrasts), reports within-subject drug>control contrasts, describes participant demographics and scanner/processing details, and uses visual stimuli (words). However, the full text presents only ROI-based fMRI results (OFC and ventral striatum) and does not provide or report a group-level, whole-brain (voxelwise) task-evoked statistical map or coordinates/figures for whole-brain activation that could be used for voxelwise meta-analysis. Therefore it fails the requirement for whole-brain task activation reporting and meets exclusion criterion E3 (only ROI analyses reported).

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual cannabis-related cues (vs matched control images) during a Stimulus Response Compatibility task. The primary within-subject contrast (approach-cannabis & avoid-control > avoid-cannabis & approach-control) isolates cannabis cue-related approach bias relative to control stimuli. Analyses were conducted at the group level using whole-brain, voxelwise methods (FLAME, cluster-corrected Z>2.3, p<.05) and reported as activation clusters/figures and discussed with localization atlases. Participant age/sex, cue type (cannabis), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (FSL/FEAT/FLAME) are all reported. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only, or non-empirical) apply. Therefore the study meets all required inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual food cues (natural reward) with within-subject contrasts of hedonic food cues > nonfood objects. Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses were performed using SPM8 with a whole-brain cluster-corrected threshold and reported MNI coordinates/figures. Relevant participant demographics (age, sex), cue type (food), scanner field strength (3.0T), and processing software (SPM8) are reported. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only contrasts, or non-empirical paper) are met.

This is an fMRI study of alcohol (drug) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli, employing a within-subject contrast (alcohol > neutral). Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses are reported (cluster-corrected maps via Monte Carlo/AlphaSim) with results described in the text and figures/tables. Relevant participant information (age range, sex representation), cue type (alcohol), MRI field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM5/SPM8) are clearly reported. The study does not rely solely on ROI, connectivity-only, or between-group-only contrasts. Therefore it meets all review inclusion criteria.

The paper is an fMRI study of practice effects on a sustained attention/incentive task in cocaine users, not a cue-reactivity study. It does not present analyses of drug- or natural reward-related visual cue reactivity (I3 not met) and, in this secondary analysis, monetary conditions were averaged such that there is no within-participant contrast of reward cues > control stimuli reported here (I4 not met). The study does report whole-brain, group-level voxelwise SPM analyses and provides participant demographics, cue type (monetary), scanner field strength, and processing software (meets I5, I6, I7). Exclusion is therefore due to failure to meet required inclusion criteria I3 and I4.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual natural reward (food) cues with a within-subject contrast of appetitive > neutral stimuli. Group-level, whole-brain (voxelwise) random-effects GLM analyses and activation maps were reported (cluster thresholds and brain regions described). The paper reports participant sex and age matching, stimulus type (food images), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (Brain Voyager). Results are not limited to ROI, connectivity-only, or between-group-only contrasts. Therefore all inclusion criteria for cue-reactivity fMRI studies using visual natural rewards are met.

This study uses visual monetary reward cues and includes within-subject Reward>Neutral contrasts, and reports participant demographics, scanner (3T) and processing software (FSL/AFNI), satisfying I3, I4, and I7. However, the reported fMRI results are limited to a priori ROI analyses; the authors state whole-brain analyses were run but report only ROI results and do not provide group-level, voxelwise whole-brain maps or coordinate tables. Therefore it does not meet the requirement for whole-brain task-evoked statistical maps (I5) and falls under exclusion criterion E3 (ROI-only reporting).

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual stimuli of natural rewards (food). It includes within-participant contrasts of appetizing (food) > control stimuli (bland foods and non-food objects). Group-level whole-brain analyses were performed and reported (whole-brain uncorrected results at P < .001 with coordinates in supplemental materials), in addition to ROI analyses. Relevant methodological details are reported: cue type (food), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM5); participant details are referenced and reported in participant tables/supplemental material. No exclusion criteria are met (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, includes whole-brain task activation maps).

This is an event-related fMRI study that used visual reward-predicting cues (money and cigarette images) and measured cue-elicited anticipatory activity and trial-by-trial behavioral responses. Within-subject contrasts (parametric increase in reward level; reward vs. 0-level) were modeled for both cigarette and monetary cues. Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses were conducted using SPM5 (3T scanner) with reported thresholds and anatomical coordinates. Participant characteristics (sample sizes, age comparability, smoking status) and cue type (nicotine) are reported. Thus the paper meets all specified inclusion criteria for a whole-brain visual cue-reactivity fMRI study and does not violate any exclusion criteria.

This is an fMRI study of nicotine (smoking) cue-reactivity using visual video stimuli in a within-subject design. The authors report within-participant contrasts of smoking videos > neutral (haircut) videos and present group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses (corrected p-values and reported activated regions; coordinates/peaks described). Methods report participant age range and sex breakdown (15 smokers, 7 females, ages 18–40), cue type (nicotine), MRI field strength (3 T), and processing software (fmristat, ANIMAL). The paper therefore meets all required inclusion criteria for visual cue fMRI whole-brain task activation and provides the necessary methodological details. No exclusion criteria are met (not ROI-only, not connectivity/resting-only, not non-empirical).

This is an empirical fMRI study of natural reward cue-reactivity using visual food stimuli in healthy fasting adults (n=27). The study includes a within-subject contrast of food>scrambled control images and reports group-level, whole-brain task-evoked activations (food vs control) with voxelwise results and coordinates. Relevant methodological details are provided (age, sex distribution, cue type: food, scanner field strength: 3T, processing software: SPM5). Although the primary personality correlation used small-volume ROI correction, the paper nevertheless reports whole-brain main effects and exploratory whole-brain analyses, satisfying the whole-brain evidence requirement. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only, or non-empirical) apply.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual food cues (high-calorie vs low-calorie images) to probe natural reward-related cue-reactivity. It includes within-participant contrasts (HC vs LC food cues) and reports group-level whole-brain, voxelwise analyses (second-level repeated-measures ANOVAs) with cluster-level FWE correction; results and coordinates are presented (including Supplementary Table 3). Methods report participant age/sex matching, cue type (food), MRI scanner (3 T), and processing software (SPM12). Thus it satisfies all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis and does not meet any exclusion criteria.

This paper is a trial design/methods report that does not present empirical fMRI activation results or whole-brain task activation maps. Crucially, the planned cue paradigm uses personalized auditory imagery scripts (audio cues), not visual stimuli, so it fails the review's visual-stimulus requirement (I3). Although the protocol includes within-subject alcohol>neutral contrasts and reports scanner field strength and processing software, the primary planned fMRI analysis is PPI (seed-based psychophysiological interaction connectivity), and no unmasked, whole-brain task-evoked activation maps are reported or presented. Therefore it meets exclusion criteria for studies that only report seed-based/effective connectivity without whole-brain task activation (E4) and is a methods/design paper without original fMRI activation data (E6).

This is an empirical fMRI cue-reactivity study in alcohol-dependent patients using visual alcohol images and neutral control images. It includes within-participant contrasts (alcohol > neutral), reports group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses with coordinate peaks and corrected thresholds, and provides participant age/sex, cue type (alcohol), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM5/SPM8). Therefore it meets all required inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

This study does not meet the review’s core inclusion criteria. It used PET imaging of mu-opioid receptor binding rather than functional MRI, and it did not employ a visual cue-reactivity task or any within-participant drug-cues>control-stimuli contrast. Although voxelwise PET binding maps were analyzed, there is no whole-brain, task-evoked fMRI activation map from a cue-reactivity paradigm. Therefore criteria I3, I4, and I5 are not satisfied. The study is empirical and reports neuroimaging data, but it lacks original fMRI task activation data required for this meta-analysis.

This study is a randomized clinical trial of rTMS for smoking cessation and does not include any task-based functional MRI data or analyses of cue-elicited BOLD responses. It does not assess cue-reactivity with fMRI visual stimuli, nor does it report any within-participant drug-cue > control whole-brain contrasts or provide group-level whole-brain statistical maps or coordinates. Therefore it fails key inclusion criteria for this meta-analysis (I3 and I5) and does not provide the necessary fMRI activation data.

This study does not assess drug- or natural reward-related cue reactivity using visual drug/reward cues; it examines insula reactivity to negative emotional faces (affective stimuli) in smokers. Therefore it fails inclusion criteria I3 and I4. Additionally, the paper reports ROI-based analyses (insula ROI beta extractions) and does not present a whole-brain, voxelwise group activation map for a drug cue>control or reward cue>control contrast, violating the requirement for whole-brain results and meeting exclusion criterion E3 (ROI-only reporting). Participant details are reported, but the task/stimuli are not drug/reward cues, so the study is not relevant to the review objective.

This is not an fMRI cue-reactivity study using visual drug or natural reward cues. The study uses arterial spin labeling (PCASL) to measure cerebral blood flow during rest and a spatial working-memory task, not presentation of drug/food/sexual visual cues. There is no within-participant contrast of drug-cues > control stimuli or natural reward-cues > control stimuli. Although whole-brain perfusion and connectivity analyses are reported, they do not constitute task-evoked, visual cue-reactivity contrasts required by the review. Therefore it fails key inclusion criteria I3 and I4 and does not provide the required whole-brain task activation maps for cue-reactivity. Exclusion criterion applied: E4 (no relevant whole-brain task activation map for cue-reactivity; study focuses on connectivity/rest-task-rest perfusion rather than cue-reactivity).

This is an fMRI study of alcohol cue-reactivity using visual stimuli with an explicit within-participant contrast (alcohol vs. neutral/abstract). Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses were performed and described (SPM5; voxelwise maps used to identify ROIs), and whole-brain results are reported in the text (coordinates and cluster information). Relevant participant information (age, sex), cue type (alcohol), MRI field strength (1.5T), and processing software (SPM5) are reported. Therefore the study meets all required inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis of visual cue-reactivity.

This is an fMRI cue-reactivity study in chronic smokers using visual stimuli (aversive and appetitive smoking images and matched neutral images). The study reports within-participant contrasts of drug cues > control (aversive smoking-related vs neutral; appetitive smoking-related vs neutral) and group-level, whole-brain random-effects analyses (one-sample t-tests) with cluster correction; results are described in text and supplementary tables/figures. Required metadata are reported: participant age and sex, cue type (nicotine), scanner field strength (3.0 T), and processing software (SPM5). Therefore all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis are met.

This is an fMRI study of nicotine (drug) cue-reactivity using visual smoking and neutral images, reporting a within-participant contrast (smoking > neutral). The authors conducted a group-level, whole-brain mixed-effects (FLAME) analysis for the smoking vs. neutral contrast (cluster-corrected p<0.05) and describe the whole-brain activation and overlap with the DMN ROI. Participant age/sex, cue type (nicotine), scanner field strength (3T/Siemens Trio), and processing software (FSL) are reported. Therefore all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis are met and no exclusion criteria apply.

This study used a monetary incentive delay task probing neural responses to monetary reward anticipation and receipt in cocaine-dependent versus control participants. It is not a cue-reactivity study presenting visual drug-related or natural reward-related cues, and it does not include within-participant contrasts of drug cues > control stimuli or natural reward-related cues > control stimuli. While the paper reports whole-brain task activation results and provides coordinates and participant details (meeting I5, I6, and I7), it fails to meet core inclusion criteria I3 and I4 for this meta-analysis (visual cue-reactivity to drug or natural rewards). Therefore the study must be excluded.

This is an fMRI cue-reactivity study of alcohol vs control but uses gustatory (taste) cues delivered via a gustometer rather than visual stimuli. It therefore does not meet inclusion criterion I3 (requires visual stimuli). Although it meets other requirements (within-subject alcohol>control contrast, whole-brain voxelwise analyses, reporting of participant demographics and scanner/processing details), failure to use visual stimulus modality excludes it from the meta-analysis focused on visual cue-reactivity.

Although this is an fMRI cue-reactivity study using visual smoking cues with a within-subject smoking>neutral contrast and reports participant demographics and MRI details (meets I3, I4, I7), the study’s analyses are ROI-based using a priori 4mm spheres and subsequent regression models. The manuscript does not report a group-level whole-brain (voxelwise) task-evoked statistical map or voxelwise group activation results. Therefore it matches exclusion criterion E3 (ROI-only reporting with no whole-brain results) and fails to meet I5, so it must be excluded from the meta-analysis.

This is an fMRI study of visual nicotine (smoking) cue reactivity with within-participant smoking>neutral contrasts and whole-brain random-effects results, and reports scanner field strength and processing software. However, the provided full text does not explicitly report participant age information (only that 21 women were studied). Inclusion criterion I7 requires relevant information regarding participant age and sex; age is not reported in the provided material. Because not all inclusion criteria are met, the study must be excluded from the meta-analysis.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual natural reward cues (food images) with healthy adult participants. The study reports within-subject contrasts comparing high-caloric > low-caloric food cues (and interactions with bitter vs. water conditions). Both exploratory whole-brain voxel-wise analyses and ROI analyses are reported, with group-level statistical maps summarized in text/tables/figures and coordinates (supplementary material referenced). Relevant metadata (participant age/sex, cue type: food, 3T scanner field strength, and processing software SPM12) are provided. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only contrasts, or non-empirical) apply.
