The report includes whole-brain task-evoked statistical maps/contrast results (described in text and figures) rather than ROI-only or connectivity-only analyses. Thus all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis are satisfied and no exclusion criteria apply.

This is an empirical task-based fMRI study using visual natural reward stimuli (food images) with within-participant contrasts (high-calorie > non-food) reported at the group level. Analyses were whole-brain, random-effects voxelwise GLM with cluster-based correction and peak coordinates reported in tables/figures. Participant age and sex (older, majority female endometrial cancer survivors), cue type (food), scanner field strength (3.0 T), and processing software (BrainVoyager) are all reported. Therefore it meets all required inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis of visual cue-reactivity studies and does not violate any exclusion criteria.

The study is an fMRI go/nogo task examining predictive geometric-shape cues and ACC activation in methamphetamine-dependent subjects, not drug- or natural reward-related cue-reactivity. It does not include drug or natural reward visual cues or within-participant contrasts of drug cues > control or reward cues > control, so it fails I3 and I4 (and the cue-type element of I7). The paper does include group-level whole-brain task analyses (secondary whole-brain analyses) and reports coordinates and methods, satisfying I5 and I6. No exclusion criteria E3–E6 apply. Therefore the study must be excluded from this meta-analysis.

This is an fMRI study of cocaine cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (images of cocaine and paraphernalia), with a within-subject contrast (cocaine pictures > neutral objects). Group-level, whole-brain random-effects analyses were conducted and thresholded using SPM8 (voxelwise p<0.01, cluster-level FWE p<0.05), and results are reported regionally in the text (and supplemental figures). Relevant participant demographics (age range, sex), cue type (cocaine), scanner field strength (3.0T), and processing software (SPM8) are reported. Therefore all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis are met.

This is an fMRI study of alcohol cue reactivity using visual stimuli (alcohol vs neutral pictures) in adult non-dependent drinkers. The study reports within-subject contrasts (alcohol > neutral) and performs group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses (one-sample t-test and whole-brain regressions) with voxel- and cluster-level thresholds and reports peak MNI coordinates and cluster results. Relevant participant information (adult participants, sex breakdown), cue type (alcohol), processing software (SPM), and imaging protocol/supplementary materials are provided. The analyses include whole-brain task-evoked maps and coordinate reporting. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only, or non-empirical) apply. Thus it satisfies all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

This is an fMRI study using visual alcohol cues (beer images) as NoGo stimuli vs non-alcohol control images (water/coke). The design modeled Go and NoGo trials separately and produced group-level whole-brain (voxelwise) activation maps (SPM8) with reported thresholding; results describe alcohol-cue-related activations (insula, DLPFC, ACC) and comparisons between groups. Participant age/sex, cue type (alcohol), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM8) are reported. Therefore it meets the meta-analysis requirements for visual cue-reactivity, a within-participant cue condition (beer NoGo) contrasted with control stimuli representations, whole-brain group maps, and requisite reporting details.

This paper is primarily a theoretical/review article with preliminary interleaved TMS/fMRI data in cocaine-dependent participants. The fMRI analyses reported are TMS-evoked BOLD responses pre- versus post-cTBS, not visual cue-reactivity contrasts. The paper does not report a within-participant drug-cues > control-stimuli whole-brain task activation map from a visual cue-reactivity paradigm. Therefore it fails key inclusion criteria for this meta-analysis.
Screening failed: PromptLibrary.get_fulltext_screening_prompt() missing 1 required positional argument: 'output_dir'

This is an fMRI study of visual food cues (natural reward) using food images and matched non-food controls. It includes within-participant contrasts (viewing food vs. viewing non-food; distraction food vs. distraction non-food). Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses were performed using SPM8 with whole-brain FWE cluster correction and reported peak coordinates and rendered maps. Relevant participant information (female sample, age-matching), cue type (food), MRI field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM8) are reported. Thus all inclusion criteria are met and no exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, or non-empirical) apply.

This is an fMRI task-based study using visual alcohol cues (AFID task) in heavy drinkers and reports within-subject contrasts (alcohol vs neutral and alcohol vs food) during the anticipatory phase. The authors conducted voxel-wise, group-level whole-brain LME analyses (and ROI analyses) with reported cluster coordinates and volumes, satisfying whole-brain reporting requirements. The paper provides relevant methodological details: cue type (alcohol, food), scanner field strength (3T GE and Siemens), preprocessing/analysis software (AFNI), and participant demographic information (age, sex referenced). Although the study includes a medication manipulation (varenicline vs placebo) and reports interaction effects, it also reports main task effects (alcohol>neutral) across participants, which meets the requirement for a within-participant drug-cue>control contrast. No exclusion criteria are triggered (whole-brain results are present, not ROI-only or resting-state).

This is an fMRI study of natural reward (sexual) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. It includes within-participant contrasts of cues>neutral (CUE SEM – CUE NEUTRAL) and stimulus>neutral (SEM – NEUTRAL). The paper reports both ROI and explorative whole-brain analyses (family-wise error corrected whole-brain results reported), with whole-sample group-level task-evoked maps described in text and figures. Participant age and sex are reported (n=70, ages and sexes detailed), cue type (sexual), MRI field strength (3T Siemens Prisma), and processing software (SPM12) are all provided. Therefore it meets the review’s inclusion criteria for fMRI cue-reactivity studies and does not violate exclusion criteria (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, contains whole-brain group activation maps).

The study is an fMRI investigation of visual reward cues (monetary: £5, £1 vs neutral) and reports within-participant contrasts (£5-neutral, £1-neutral). It provides group-level, whole-brain voxelwise results (FWE cluster-corrected maps and coordinate peaks) and accompanying ROI/PPIs, and reports participant age/sex, cue type, scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM8). These elements satisfy the meta-analysis requirements for cue-reactivity using visual stimuli and whole-brain reporting.

The paper does not report task-evoked drug- or natural reward-related visual cue-reactivity contrasts. Although an alcohol cue-reactivity task was performed, its results are reported in a parent study and not in this full text; the presented analyses focus on emotional face (fear/anger) processing (faces vs shapes). Therefore it fails inclusion criteria I3 and I4 (no within-participant drug cue>control or natural reward cue>control contrast reported). No exclusion criteria (E3–E6) specifically apply, so exclusion is based on unmet inclusion criteria.

This is an fMRI study of alcohol cue reactivity using visual stimuli (alcohol vs neutral images), with a within-subject contrast (alcohol > neutral) reported. Group-level, whole-brain task-evoked results are presented (alcohol>neutral activations in mOFC, occipital cortex, PCC, SFG) and whole-brain analyses (voxelwise rsults and contrasts) are described. Participant age and sex are reported, cue type is alcohol, scanner field strength (3T) is specified, and preprocessing/analysis details are referenced in the Supplementary Methods (satisfying reporting of processing pipeline/software). Therefore the study meets inclusion criteria for visual drug-related cue-reactivity fMRI with whole-brain group results and requisite participant and methods information.

This study is an empirical fMRI investigation of cue-induced desire for an Internet video game using visual stimuli. It reports within-subject contrasts of video game cues > neutral control stimuli, conducted group-level, whole-brain random-effects voxelwise analyses with cluster-level FDR correction, and presents coordinates for significant clusters. Relevant participant information (age, sex), cue type (Internet video game), scanner field strength (3.0 T), and processing software (Brain Voyager BVQX) are provided. The design and reported results directly map onto cue-reactivity for a non-drug natural reward (video gaming) using visual cues, meeting the meta-analysis objective. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity/resting-state-only, between-group-only without task effect, or non-empirical) are met.

The study is an fMRI investigation of nicotine (smoking) cue reactivity using visual stimuli and reports within-subject smoking>control contrasts. However, the second-level analyses were performed within predefined anatomical region-of-interest (ROI) masks (Harvard-Oxford atlas) and results are reported for those ROIs (FWE-corrected). There is no indication of an unmasked, whole-brain (voxelwise) group-level activation map being reported. Therefore the study reports only ROI-based task activations and lacks whole-brain task-evoked results required by the meta-analysis. This violates inclusion criterion I5 and meets exclusion criterion E3 (only ROI analyses with no whole-brain results).

This study does not examine drug- or natural reward-related cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. It used a monetary reward anticipation task with symbolic arrow cues and adaptive reaction-time responses, and the primary contrast was monetary reward anticipation > verbal feedback (anticipation of non-drug reward), not a within-participant contrast of drug cues > control stimuli or natural reward-related visual cues > control stimuli. Although the paper reports group-level, whole-brain task analyses and provides coordinates and participant details, it fails to meet key inclusion criteria I3 and I4 (cue-reactivity with visual drug/natural reward stimuli and a within-subject cue>control contrast). Therefore it must be excluded from the meta-analysis focused on visual cue-reactivity.

This is an empirical fMRI study that uses visual stimuli (neutral geometric shapes) conditioned to alcohol intoxication and measures BOLD responses to these visual cues. The authors report within-subject contrasts of CS+ > CS− (drug cue vs control cue) using whole-brain, group-level voxelwise SPM8 random-effects analyses with cluster-level FWE correction; results are presented in tables and figures. Participant demographics (age, sex), cue type (alcohol), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM8) are reported. Therefore it satisfies all specified inclusion criteria for cue-reactivity meta-analysis.

This is an event-related BOLD fMRI study using visual cues (500-ms images) of cocaine and neutral stimuli in cocaine-dependent adults. It includes within-participant contrasts (cocaine > neutral) and reports group-level, voxelwise task-evoked results: ROI-focused analyses plus cluster-corrected whole-brain results (Table 2 and described whole-brain clusters). Relevant participant info is reported (age, sex distribution, cue type: cocaine, scanner field strength: 3T, processing software: SPM8; AFNI 3dClustSim and FSL used for masks/thresholding). Although ROI masks were used, whole-brain, group-level activation maps/clusters are reported, satisfying the meta-analysis requirements. Therefore the study meets all inclusion criteria for fMRI cue-reactivity meta-analysis of visual drug-related cues.

This study is excluded because it does not meet the key inclusion criterion I3: it is not an fMRI study and did not use visual cue stimuli. The imaging modality was PET ([15O]H2O) rather than functional MRI, and craving was induced via script-guided auditory mental imagery (audio scripts) rather than visual cues. Although the study includes a within-subject drug-cue>neutral contrast and reports whole-brain voxelwise analyses with coordinate tables, the review’s requirement for fMRI and visual-stimulus cue-reactivity is not satisfied. Therefore it must be excluded.

This is an empirical task-based fMRI study in adults that used visual natural-reward cues (food images) and a within-subject contrast of food > nonfood. Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses are reported (food > nonfood contrast and whole-brain multiple regression with AUDIT) with coordinates/figures and standard thresholding. Methods report participant age/sex, cue type (food), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM12). Although connectivity (PPI) is also reported, the paper includes unmasked whole-brain task activation maps, so it does not meet any exclusion criteria (e.g., ROI-only or connectivity-only). Therefore it satisfies I3, I4, I5, I6, and I7 and is eligible for inclusion in the meta-analysis.

Although this is an fMRI study using visual (audiovisual) cocaine cues with within-subject cocaine>neutral contrasts and reports participant age/sex and scanner field strength, the analyses are based on a priori selected neuroanatomic regions (ten ROIs) with regionwise scalar measures. The paper does not report group-level whole-brain (voxelwise) task-evoked statistical maps or coordinate-based whole-brain results; processing software is also not specified. Therefore the study reports only ROI-based results with no whole-brain task activation map and does not meet the whole-brain analysis requirement.

This study is an empirical fMRI investigation of cue-induced cocaine craving using visual stimuli (films). It reports within-participant contrasts (cocaine film vs nature film and film-specific one-sample tests) and group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses (one-sample and two-sample t tests with voxel-wise thresholding and cluster criteria). Results are reported via activation maps, cluster tables, and figures. Participant age and sex, cue types (cocaine, sexual, nature), scanner field strength (1.5 T), and processing software (AFNI) are all provided. No exclusion criteria are met (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, contains whole-brain task activation maps, and is empirical with original fMRI data).

This is an event-related fMRI study using visual heroin-related cues in participants on methadone maintenance. It reports within-subject contrasts of heroin-related > neutral stimuli, provides group-level whole-brain voxelwise results (p<.0001; t-maps and coordinate tables), and includes participant age/sex, cue type, scanner field strength (3-T), and processing software (SPM5). The methods and results satisfy all required reporting elements for whole-brain task-evoked activation to visual drug cues, and none of the exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only, or non-empirical) apply.

The provided study does not report any indication that it is an fMRI study or includes imaging data. The title and available metadata describe pharmacological effects on craving and withdrawal but do not mention MRI methods, scanner, visual cue-reactivity, whole-brain analyses, or reporting of neuroimaging software. Therefore it fails inclusion criterion I3 (functional MRI study assessing cue-reactivity using visual stimuli) and related required criteria (I4–I7). No whole-brain task-evoked fMRI results are evident in the supplied information. Exclusion is due to absence of original fMRI data relevant to the review’s focus.

Full text was not accessible; only title and citation information available. Title indicates a perfusion fMRI study of cigarette smoking cues (visual), so I3 appears satisfied. However, I cannot confirm essential inclusion criteria I4 (explicit within-participant contrast of drug cues > control) and I5 (presence of a group-level whole-brain voxelwise task-evoked statistical map) or I7 (reporting of participant age/sex, scanner field strength, and processing software) from the provided information. Because all inclusion criteria must be verified and at least I5 and I7 cannot be confirmed from the available data, the study must be excluded. If full text becomes available, re-screening is recommended to verify whole-brain results and reporting details.

Full text was not accessible from the provided source, so I cannot verify whether the paper reports a group-level, whole-brain (voxelwise) task-evoked statistical map (I5) or provides participant demographics, cue type details, scanner field strength, and processing software (I7). Title suggests an fMRI smoking-cue study using virtual (visual) stimuli, but without the full methods/results I cannot confirm whole-brain results or that analyses include within-participant drug-cue > control contrasts. Because the presence of a whole-brain task activation map cannot be confirmed, the study is excluded.

This study does not meet the review’s inclusion requirements. Primary issues: (1) Stimulus modality — the task used personalized guided auditory imagery (favorite-food, stress, neutral-relaxing) rather than visual cue stimuli required by the meta-analysis. (2) Outcome type — the paper reports voxel-wise intrinsic connectivity distribution (ICD) and seed-to-voxel functional connectivity analyses (task-based connectivity), not within-participant task-evoked cue>control whole-brain activation contrasts or unmasked whole-brain task statistical maps. Therefore it falls under exclusion for reporting only connectivity analyses without whole-brain task activation maps. It does report participant demographics and scanner/processing details.

Full text was not accessible for detailed methods/results verification. Based on the title and metadata, this is an fMRI cue-reactivity study using alcohol-related visual cues and likely includes within-subject contrasts (drug cues > control), so I3 and I4 appear satisfied. However I5 (group-level, whole-brain voxelwise task-evoked statistical map) cannot be confirmed from the available information, nor can I7 (reporting of participant age/sex, cue type details, MRI field strength, and processing software). Because I5 and I7 are required inclusion criteria and cannot be verified from the provided content, the study must be excluded. There is no clear evidence that exclusion criteria E3–E6 explicitly apply, so none are listed as triggered.

This full-text reports a task-based fMRI study of nicotine cue reactivity using visual stimuli (smoking vs control images). It includes within-subject contrasts ("smoke > control") and whole-brain, group-level voxelwise analyses (RFX ANOVA and RFX ANCOVAs) with reported region tables/figures. Participant demographics (age, sex), cue type (nicotine), scanner field strength (3T), and preprocessing/software (BrainVoyager QX) are specified. Results are reported as whole-brain statistical maps and ROI follow-ups (tables/figures), meeting meta-analysis reporting requirements. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, or non-empirical) apply.

This study used an effort-based monetary-reward task with abstract cue shapes indicating required button-press effort, not a visual cue-reactivity paradigm presenting drug or natural reward images. It does not include within-participant contrasts of drug-cues > control stimuli or natural reward-related cues > control stimuli (I3 and I4 are not satisfied). The paper does report whole-brain, group-level task maps and provides scanner and processing details, but these do not address the review’s required cue-reactivity contrasts. Therefore it does not meet the meta-analysis scope.

Full text is not available (page failed to load), so key eligibility items cannot be verified from the provided study information. The record does not specify whether the study used visual stimuli, whether it reports a within-subject contrast of drug-cues > control, or whether it reports group-level whole-brain (voxelwise) task-activation maps (coordinates/figures). Per the review rules, all inclusion criteria I3–I7 must be verifiable; because these cannot be confirmed, the study must be excluded. I apply exclusion criterion E3 because whole-brain results cannot be verified from the available information (i.e., it may report ROI-only or otherwise lack a usable whole-brain activation map).

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual emotional/natural images (IAPS) and reports within-subject contrasts (positive>neutral and negative>neutral) with group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses (SPM8) acquired on a 3T scanner. Participant age and sex are reported, cue type (positive/negative natural images), field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM8) are provided. Results are reported as whole-brain clusters/coordinates and figures. The positive>neutral contrast qualifies as a natural reward-related cue > control-stimulus contrast for the purposes of this meta-analysis. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, or non-empirical) apply.

This is an empirical fMRI study of nicotine (drug) cue-reactivity using visual smoking-related stimuli. It reports within-subject contrasts of smoking-related cues versus neutral control cues (smoking>neutral) at the individual and group levels. Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses are reported (mixed-effects FLAME analyses with cluster-thresholding), and results are presented in tables/figures with coordinates. The paper reports participant age and sex, cue type (nicotine), scanner field strength (3.0T), and processing software (FSL). No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only contrasts, or non-empirical) apply. Therefore the study meets all specified inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

This is an empirical fMRI study of heroin cue reactivity using visual audiovisual heroin-related versus neutral stimuli in treatment-seeking, abstinent heroin-dependent patients. It reports within-subject contrasts (heroin > neutral) across pre- and post-treatment sessions and uses whole-brain, voxelwise statistical analysis (session × condition 2×2 ANOVA) with cluster-corrected results and peak coordinates (Table/Figures). Methods report scanner field strength (3 T) and processing software (FSL), and participant demographics (age, sex) and cue type (heroin) are provided. Therefore it satisfies the review inclusion criteria for visual cue-reactivity fMRI with whole-brain results.

This is an event-related fMRI study using visual cannabis (drug) and natural reward (sexual) cues with a within-subject contrast of cannabis>neutral reported. Group-level voxelwise results are described and secondary whole-brain analyses are referenced (Online Resource 2), and primary processing details are provided (SPM8; 3.0-T scanner). Participant demographics (age/sex) and cue types are reported. Thus all inclusion criteria are met (I3, I4, I5, I6, I7). No exclusion criteria apply (not ROI-only, not connectivity/resting-only, and includes task activation maps).

This study is an fMRI investigation of methamphetamine cue-reactivity using visual cues with a within-subject Methamphetamine Cue>Control Cue contrast. The paper reports whole-brain, group-level task-evoked Z-statistic maps (cluster-corrected) and provides coordinates/tables and figures. Relevant participant details (age, sex distribution), cue type (methamphetamine), MRI field strength (3T), and processing software (FSL 5.0) are reported. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only, or non-empirical) apply. Therefore it satisfies all required inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

This is an fMRI cue-reactivity study of alcohol using visual stimuli with within-subject ALC>BEV contrasts and reports participant age/sex, cue type (alcohol), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (FSL). However, all reported imaging analyses are restricted to a priori ROI-based timecourse/HLM analyses (five 6-mm ROIs) and correction across these ROIs; no group-level whole-brain (voxelwise) task-evoked statistical maps or unmasked whole-brain activation results are reported. Therefore the study fails the whole-brain evidence requirement (I5) and falls under exclusion criterion E3 (ROI-only results).

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual smoking (drug) cues in heavy drinking nondaily smokers. It reports within-subject contrasts of smoking>control cues, uses group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses (SPM2) with thresholding (FDR and uncorrected results reported), and provides participant age/sex, cue type (nicotine/smoking), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software. These elements satisfy all required inclusion criteria for a meta-analysis of visual cue-reactivity. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only, or non-empirical) apply.

This is an fMRI study of nicotine (smoking) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. The paper reports within-subject contrasts of smoking versus neutral images, and group-level, whole-brain voxelwise statistical mapping (BrainVoyager GLM, voxelwise t-statistics, whole-brain correction) with results presented in tables and figures. Relevant participant demographics (age, sex), cue type (nicotine), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (BrainVoyager QX) are reported. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, or non-empirical) apply. Therefore all required inclusion criteria are met.

This is a task-based fMRI study of alcohol cue reactivity using visual stimuli (alcohol pictures) with within-subject contrasts of alcohol cues > beverage/control pictures. The authors performed group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses using SPM99, report cluster-corrected activations and provide Talairach coordinates/tables and figures. Relevant methodological details are reported: cue type (alcohol), participant demographics (male and female college students; demographics in Table 1), scanner field strength (3.0 T), and processing software (SPM99). Although the sample is small and all subjects are Asian (noted as limitation), no exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only, or non-empirical paper) apply. Therefore the study satisfies I3, I4, I5, I6, and I7 and is eligible for inclusion.

This is an fMRI study using pictorial alcohol cues (visual stimuli) in adolescents with AUD. It includes within-participant contrasts of alcoholic vs nonalcoholic pictures (drug cues > control), reports voxelwise, whole-brain group-level analyses (tables and figures), and provides participant age/sex and cue type. Processing software (AFNI) and detailed acquisition/processing parameters are reported. Therefore it meets the meta-analysis inclusion criteria for visual cue-reactivity fMRI studies.

This is an fMRI study of nicotine (smoking) cue-reactivity using visual photographic smoking cues. It includes a within-subject contrast of smoking cues > control cues, reports group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses (random-effects, SPM5) with coordinates/tables and figures, and provides participant age/sex, cue type, scanner field strength (4.0 T) and processing software. Therefore it satisfies all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis and does not meet any exclusion criteria.

This is an fMRI study of alcohol cue-reactivity using visual stimuli with an explicit within-participant contrast (alcohol cues > nonalcohol beverage cues). The authors performed group-level voxelwise statistical mapping (SPM-based cluster analysis) and report activated clusters/coordinates. Participant age and sex, cue type (alcohol), scanner field strength (1.5T), and processing software (MEDx/SPM) are reported. Although imaging covered only the anterior brain slices, the analysis and reported results are voxelwise group-level maps and coordinate tables, satisfying the meta-analysis requirements.

Full text was not accessible (site error). The available information (title, journal, DOI, authors, date) does not confirm this is an fMRI cue-reactivity study using visual stimuli, nor does it report a within-subject contrast of drug cues>control, whole-brain voxelwise group maps, or required methodological details (scanner field strength, processing software, participant age/sex). Because I cannot verify inclusion criteria I3, I4, I5, and I7, the study must be excluded. Closest applicable exclusion criterion: no confirmed original fMRI whole-brain task activation data available in the accessible record.

This is an fMRI study of smoking (nicotine) cue reactivity using visual stimuli (smoking vs neutral images), reporting within-subject contrasts (smoking > neutral) and correlating these whole-brain, group-level task activation maps with behavioral attentional-bias scores. The paper reports whole-brain random-effects ANCOVA results with cluster-level correction, tables/figures (coordinates and Figure 1) and includes participant age/sex, cue type, scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (BrainVoyager). No exclusion criteria (e.g., ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only, or non-empirical) apply. Therefore it meets all required inclusion criteria and is appropriate for inclusion in the meta-analysis.

This is an fMRI task study of nicotine cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (personal and standard smoking/nonsmoking photos and proximal cues). It reports within-subject contrasts (smoking > nonsmoking; PSE > PNE) and a within-subjects design. The paper includes group-level, whole-brain voxelwise task activation analyses (cluster-corrected results reported in text, Figure 2, and Supplementary Table 2). Whole-brain results are clearly described in figures/tables. Participant age and sex are reported, cue type is nicotine, scanner field strength (3T) and processing software (FSL v5.0.1) are provided. Therefore all required inclusion criteria are met and no exclusion criteria apply.

This study is a resting-state fMRI connectivity/graph-theory analysis of intrinsic functional networks in smokers versus controls. It does not present any task-based cue-reactivity paradigm using visual stimuli, nor any within-participant contrast of drug cues > control stimuli. There are no whole-brain, task-evoked group-level statistical maps reported. Therefore it does not meet the meta-analysis inclusion criteria and falls under exclusion for resting-state analyses without task activation maps.

This is an fMRI study of nicotine (drug) cue-reactivity using visual smoking-related images with a within-participant contrast (smoking cues > neutral cues). Analyses were conducted as group-level, whole-brain voxelwise random-effects SPM5 analyses (reports coordinates/tables and figures) on 18 participants (age and sex reported). Scanner field strength (3.0 T) and processing software (SPM5) are specified. Therefore it meets all required inclusion criteria for visual drug-cue fMRI whole-brain task activation reporting and violates none of the exclusion criteria.

This is an empirical fMRI cue-reactivity study that includes a visual smoking-cue paradigm (videos) with a within-participant contrast (smoking cue > fixation baseline) and reports group-level, whole-brain voxelwise results. The paper reports participant age and sex, cue types (smoking videos and alcohol taste), scanner field strength (3T Siemens Trio), and processing software (FSL). Although the alcohol cue used a taste paradigm, the presence of a visual smoking-cue contrast that meets the review’s visual-stimulus focus makes the study eligible. Whole-brain activation maps and clusterwise statistics are reported and illustrated, satisfying reporting requirements. Therefore all inclusion criteria (I3, I4, I5, I6, I7) are met and no exclusion criteria apply.

This is a task-evoked, whole-brain BOLD fMRI study of smokers viewing visual stimuli including cigarette-related (drug) and naturally rewarding (erotic, romantic) pictures. The paper reports within-subject contrasts (cigarette > neutral) using a random-effects whole-brain voxelwise analysis with thresholding and cluster coordinates (Table 2, figures). Participant age and sex, cue type (nicotine), MRI field strength (3.0 T), and processing software (BrainVoyager QX) are reported. Thus it meets all inclusion criteria for visual drug and natural reward cue-reactivity fMRI studies and violates none of the exclusion criteria.

This is an fMRI study of alcohol (and smoking) cue-reactivity with visual cues and within-subject contrasts (drinking cues > neutral) and reports participant age/sex, cue type, scanner (3T) and SPM12 processing. However, all reported statistical analyses are ROI-based (10 mm spheres in VS and MPFC) with effects reported on extracted ROI timecourses. The full text provides no evidence of group-level whole-brain (voxelwise) task activation maps, coordinate tables, or unmasked whole-brain results. Because the paper reports only ROI analyses without accompanying whole-brain task activation results, it meets exclusion criterion E3 (ROI-only study). Inclusion criteria met: I3, I4, I7. Exclusion criterion applied: E3.

Although this is an fMRI study with whole-brain, cluster-corrected results and reports participant details and cue/taste task design, it does not present a within-participant, whole-brain contrast of visual drug-related cues > control stimuli that indexes cue-reactivity. The reported significant contrasts focus on taste/outcome (alcohol taste vs. water) and prediction-error effects; cue-period results (visual 'Alcohol' vs 'Water' cue contrasts) are not reported in the paper’s results. The meta-analysis requires at least one within-subject cue>control visual stimulus contrast. Therefore the study fails inclusion criterion I4 (no reported drug-cue>control visual contrast) and is excluded. Included here are the applicable whole-brain reporting strengths (I5/I6/I7) but exclusion is based on lack of the required cue-reactivity contrast.

This is an fMRI study using visual alcohol-related cues and control stimuli, with within-subject contrasts of alcohol cues > control stimuli (first-level contrasts) and group-level voxelwise (whole-brain) analyses using SPM99 (thresholds reported). Participant age and sex (male-only), cue type (alcohol), scanner field strength (1.5T), and processing software are reported. Results are reported as whole-brain task-evoked maps (tables/figures and coordinates). Therefore all required inclusion criteria are met and no exclusion criteria apply.

The study used an alcohol taste-cue paradigm (liquid taste delivery) rather than visual stimuli. The meta-analysis specifically requires cue-reactivity assessed using visual stimuli (I3), which is not met. The study does include a within-participant contrast of alcohol>water, reports group-level whole-brain voxelwise results (tables/figures), and provides participant age/sex, cue type (alcohol), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (FSL). No exclusion criteria (E3–E6) apply. Therefore the study must be excluded solely because it does not meet the visual-stimuli requirement (I3).

This is an fMRI study of nicotine (drug) cue-reactivity using visual smoking-related stimuli with within-participant contrasts (smoking vs. neutral) in both crave and resist conditions. Analyses were whole-brain, voxelwise group-level mixed-effects using FSL/FEAT and FLAME with cluster correction (Z>2.3, p<.05), and results are reported with tables of coordinates and figures. Relevant participant information (age, sex), cue type (nicotine), MRI field strength (3.0 T Siemens), and processing software (FSL) are provided. Therefore it meets all inclusion criteria and violates no exclusion criteria.

This is an fMRI study of natural reward (food images) so I3 is met. However, the provided full text (abstract) does not report a within-participant contrast of cues>control stimuli or describe any non-food control stimuli or contrasts (fails I4). The methods are described only as regression analyses predicting activation from affect ratings; there is no clear statement that a group-level, whole-brain (voxelwise) task-evoked statistical map was produced or reported (fails I5). Key methodological details required by I7 (scanner field strength, processing software) are not provided in the supplied text. Because whole-brain results are not clearly reported, this effectively aligns with exclusion criterion E3 (no whole-brain results reported/appears ROI/region-reporting only). Therefore the study must be excluded from the meta-analysis.

The study does not assess drug- or natural reward–related cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. It is a within-subject fMRI study of resting-state network coupling and a working-memory task in nicotine abstinence vs satiety, without any within-participant contrast of drug-cue>control visual stimuli or a whole-brain task-evoked cue-reactivity map. Thus it fails inclusion criteria I3, I4, and I5. The manuscript reports resting-state ICA and connectivity/ROI task analyses rather than visual cue-reactivity whole-brain results; therefore exclusion criterion E4 applies.

Although this is an fMRI cue-reactivity study using visual smoking and food video stimuli and reports participant demographics and scanner/processing details (satisfying I3 and I7), the analyses are component/network-based (group ICA with temporal sorting) yielding network-level betas and ICA spatial maps. The paper does not report group-level, voxelwise whole-brain task-evoked statistical maps or coordinate tables for task contrasts. Therefore it fails the requirement for a whole-brain, voxelwise task activation map (I5) and is excluded under E4 (connectivity/ICA-only task analysis without an accompanying whole-brain task activation map).

The study meets I3 (fMRI cue-reactivity using visual smoking videos), I4 (within-subject smoking>neutral contrasts), I5 (group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses reported), and I6 (results reported in figures/tables). However, it does not provide all required reporting per I7 in the provided full text: participant sex and MRI scanner field strength are not reported in the available manuscript excerpt or Methods (age, cue type, and processing software [FSL] are given). Because I7 is not satisfied, the study fails to meet all inclusion criteria and must be excluded from the meta-analysis.

This is an empirical fMRI study of alcohol cue reactivity using visual stimuli in alcohol-dependent participants. It includes within-subject contrasts of alcohol cues > non-alcohol beverage cues and reports group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses (SPM5; thresholded group t-maps reported). Scanner field strength (Philips 3.0T), cue type (alcohol), and processing software (SPM5) are explicitly reported; participant demographics are summarized (Table 1) including age/sex information. Whole-brain results are presented in tables/figures with coordinates. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only contrasts, or non-empirical) apply. Thus all inclusion criteria are met.

The study is an fMRI investigation of cannabis cue-reactivity (drug-related visual cues) reporting neural responses to cannabis cues and their association with craving, and explicitly examines sex differences. The methods report within-subject contrasts of cannabis cues > control stimuli and present group-level, whole-brain (voxelwise) activation results (reported as coordinates/figures in the text/supplement), along with participant age/sex information, cue type (cannabis), MRI field strength, and processing software. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only contrasts, or non-empirical) appear to apply. Therefore it meets all listed inclusion criteria for visual drug cue fMRI whole-brain task-evoked activation.

This is an empirical fMRI study of nicotine (smoking) cue-reactivity using visual cue videos. It includes within-participant contrasts of drug cues versus control (crave-allow vs neutral and crave-resist vs neutral) and reports group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses (FSL/FLAME with cluster thresholds), with results presented in figures and tables of coordinates. Relevant methodological and sample details are provided (cue type: nicotine, MRI field strength: 1.5 T, processing software: FSL, and demographic/treatment information including participant numbers and sex/age referenced in tables). No exclusion criteria apply (not ROI-only, not connectivity/resting-state only, and contains within-participant whole-brain task effects). Therefore it meets all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis objective.

Excluded because the study used a gustatory (taste) cue-reactivity task (alcohol taste vs. juice) rather than visual stimuli. The meta-analysis requires fMRI cue-reactivity studies using visual stimuli (I3). Although the study includes a within-participant Alcohol>Juice contrast (I4), reports whole-brain second-level exploratory analyses (I5/I6), and provides participant demographics, cue type, scanner field strength, and processing software (I7), it fails to meet the critical modality requirement (I3). No exclusion criteria E3–E6 apply, but failure to satisfy I3 necessitates exclusion from this visual-stimulus-focused meta-analysis.

This study is a TMS/EMG experiment measuring motor-evoked potentials in chronic smokers during visual cue exposure, not a functional MRI study. It therefore does not meet inclusion criterion I3 (functional MRI study) and consequently fails I4–I7 which require fMRI whole-brain task contrasts and related reporting. The manuscript contains no fMRI data, no voxelwise whole-brain activation maps or coordinate tables from fMRI analyses, and thus cannot contribute to an fMRI cue-reactivity meta-analysis. Exclusion is based on the absence of original fMRI data and whole-brain task activation results.

The study does not meet inclusion criterion I3: cue presentation is via individualized auditory guided imagery (audio scripts), not visual stimuli. Although it contains a within-participant alcohol-cue > neutral contrast (I4), whole-brain, group-level task-evoked maps and coordinates (I5, I6), and reports participant demographics, cue type (alcohol), 3T scanner and processing software (I7), the meta-analysis specifically requires visual cue-reactivity paradigms. Because the critical modality requirement (visual stimuli) is not satisfied, the study must be excluded.

This is an fMRI study of probabilistic delay discounting using monetary rewards in individuals with alcohol use disorder. It does not present visual drug-related or natural reward cues nor report any within-participant contrast of drug-cue>control or natural-reward-cue>control. Although the paper reports whole-brain task-evoked maps and participant details, it fails to meet key inclusion criteria I3 (assessing drug or natural reward-related cue-reactivity using visual stimuli) and I4 (within-participant cue>control contrast). Therefore it does not address the review objective and must be excluded.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual cue videos (cocaine and food) in 20 adult participants. Within-subject contrasts (cocaine>neutral; food>neutral and vs baseline) are reported. Analyses are whole-brain, voxelwise group-level SPM8 results with cluster-level FWE correction and detailed tables/figures. Methods report participant age and sex, cue types, MRI field strength (4T), and processing software. The study therefore meets all required inclusion criteria for fMRI visual cue-reactivity and does not violate any exclusion criteria.

This is an fMRI study of marijuana cue-reactivity using visual stimuli in treatment-seeking, MJ-dependent subjects. The paper reports within-subject contrasts of marijuana cues > non-marijuana control cues using BOLD fMRI (block design) and random-effects group-level voxelwise analyses. Peak coordinates and tables are provided for significant clusters (amygdala, hippocampus, ventral striatum, OFC) and an unmasked visual display of whole-brain data is referenced. Participant age/sex, cue type (cannabis), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM2) are reported. Therefore it meets the meta-analysis inclusion criteria for visual cue fMRI studies with group-level voxelwise maps and sufficient methodological details.

This is an fMRI study of nicotine (drug) cue-reactivity using visual smoking stimuli with an explicit within-subject contrast (smoking > neutral). Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise task-evoked results are reported (group analyses thresholded at Z>2.3, cluster-corrected p=0.05) with tables/figures describing regions (e.g., occipital cortex, posterior cingulate). Processing software (FSL) and scanner field strength (3T) are reported, and participant demographics are provided (Table 1). Although the paper also reports seed-based PPI connectivity, it additionally includes unmasked whole-brain task activation maps, satisfying the meta-analysis inclusion criteria.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual heroin-related cues vs neutral images with a within-subject contrast (heroin-related > neutral). Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses were performed using FSL/FLAME and results are reported in figures/tables. Relevant participant information (age, sex: male-only), cue type (heroin), scanner field strength (1.5T), and processing software (FSL) are reported. Therefore it meets all required inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

This is an fMRI study of alcohol cue-reactivity but stimuli were personalized audiotaped imagery scripts (auditory/imagery), not visual stimuli. The meta-analysis specifically requires cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (I3), which is not met. The study does meet other inclusion elements (within-participant contrasts of alcohol>control, whole-brain voxelwise analyses, and reporting of participant demographics and scanner/processing details), but failure to use visual stimuli excludes it from this review.

Full text was not accessible from the provided content, so key items required by the inclusion criteria cannot be confirmed. Although the title implies nicotine cue-induced brain activity (likely visual cues), I cannot verify: (1) that this is an fMRI study using visual drug cues (I3), (2) that it reports a within-participant drug-cues>control contrast (I4), (3) that it provides group-level whole-brain voxelwise task-activation results (I5/I6), or (4) that participant demographics, cue type, scanner field strength, and processing software are reported (I7). Because whole-brain task-evoked maps cannot be confirmed, the study must be excluded under E3 (no verifiable whole-brain results / only ROI or insufficient reporting). If the full text or supplementary materials become available showing whole-brain voxelwise results and the required methodological details, this decision should be revisited.

This is an empirical fMRI cue-reactivity study using visual opioid-related and natural reward stimuli (sexual, aversive) in detoxified patients with OUD. The paper reports within-subject contrasts (drug, sexual, aversive vs neutral) and pre- versus on-treatment comparisons, satisfying a within-participant drug>control contrast. The authors conducted a group-level whole-brain ANOVA (voxelwise) and report cluster-level results with MNI coordinates for the ventral striatum/mOFC, meeting the whole-brain evidence requirement; coordinates and thresholding are clearly described. Participant demographics (age, sex), cue types, scanner field strength (Siemens Tim Trio 3T) and processing software (SPM8) are all reported. No exclusion criteria (e.g., ROI-only reporting, connectivity-only analyses, between-group-only contrasts, or non-empirical paper) are met. Therefore the study meets all listed inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

Although this is an fMRI study of nicotine (smoking) cue reactivity using visual stimuli and includes within-subject smoking>neutral contrasts (I3, I4) and reports participant demographics, scanner field strength, and processing software (I7), the reported analyses are restricted to a priori ROI-based group analyses. The paper does not present any group-level, whole-brain (voxelwise) task-evoked statistical maps or whole-brain results; only ROI results are described. Therefore it meets exclusion criterion E3 (studies reporting only ROI analyses with no whole-brain results) and must be excluded from the meta-analysis.

This is an fMRI study that used a visual conditioned stimulus (cue) predicting a natural reward (juice), and reports within-participant task contrasts (event-related regressors including CS) compared to baseline/other event types. The paper includes group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses (WB) as well as small-volume corrected results, and reports participant demographics, cue type (juice), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (AFNI). Results are reported in text, figures, and supplementary tables, satisfying whole-brain reporting requirements for inclusion in the meta-analysis of visual cue-reactivity to drug/natural rewards.

Full text was not accessible, preventing verification of required whole-brain, voxelwise task-evoked results and reporting of MRI parameters and participant details. The title suggests an fMRI alcohol cue-reactivity comparison between alcoholics and social drinkers, but I cannot confirm a within-participant contrast of alcohol cues>control, nor that group-level whole-brain (voxelwise) maps were reported rather than only between-group contrasts or ROI analyses. Because the study cannot be confirmed to provide an unmasked, whole-brain task effect, it must be excluded under E5.

This is an event-related fMRI study using visual heroin-related cues in methadone-maintained patients. It reports within-participant contrasts of heroin-related vs. matched neutral stimuli at the group level (one-sample and paired t tests comparing pre- and post-dose), and presents voxelwise activation maps (group-level mixed-effects analyses displayed/thresholded and described in text and figures). Relevant participant demographics (mean age, sex distribution), cue type (heroin), MRI field strength (3 T), and processing software (FSL) are all reported. Results are reported in figures and text with voxelwise thresholds and ROI masks, which meets the allowance for whole-brain results reported via figures/tables. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity/resting-state-only, between-group-only contrasts, or non-empirical paper) apply.

The provided information (title, authors, journal) indicates an fMRI study of smoking-cue reactivity, but the full text details are unavailable. Key inclusion criteria cannot be verified: there is no explicit confirmation of use of visual stimuli (I3), no clear statement that a within-participant drug-cue > control contrast was reported (I4), and no evidence of group-level, whole-brain (voxelwise) task-evoked statistical maps or coordinate reporting (I5/I6). Relevant methodological/participant details (age, sex, scanner field strength, processing software) are not reported in the provided content (I7). Because one or more required inclusion criteria cannot be confirmed from the supplied information, the study must be excluded from the review.

This is an fMRI study of cigarette (nicotine) cue-reactivity using visual/palpable cigarette stimuli and neutral visual controls. The authors report within-subject contrasts of cigarette versus neutral cues (cue main effects and instruction×cue interactions). Analyses were voxelwise, whole-brain group-level maps (voxel-wise ANOVA) with results reported in tables/figures and Talairach coordinates. The paper provides participant age/sex (male, mean age reported), cue type (nicotine/cigarette), scanner field strength (1.5 T), and processing software (NIS and AFNI) details. Therefore all required inclusion criteria are met and no exclusion criteria (e.g., ROI-only or connectivity-only reporting) apply.

This is an fMRI study using visual smoking cues (nicotine) versus matched neutral images in nicotine-dependent participants. The paper reports within-subject contrasts of smoking>neutral (encoding and retrieval phases), and group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses with cluster-level correction (results reported in Table 2 and figures). Relevant details (participant age/sex, cue type, 3T scanner field strength, and processing software FSL) are provided. Thus it meets all required inclusion criteria for visual drug cue-reactivity fMRI studies and does not violate any exclusion criteria.

Full-text content was not available, so I cannot verify critical inclusion criteria. The title suggests an fMRI alcohol-cue study, but there is no accessible information confirming: (I4) a within-participant contrast of alcohol cues > control stimuli, (I5) a group-level whole-brain (voxelwise) task-evoked statistical map or coordinate-based reporting, or (I7) reporting of participant age/sex, cue type details, scanner field strength, and processing software. Because these inclusion criteria cannot be confirmed from the provided information, the study cannot be included in the meta-analysis.

This is an empirical arterial spin-labeled perfusion fMRI study (n=79 treatment-seeking smokers) that used audiovisual smoking cues versus nonsmoking control clips (visual stimuli) and performed within-subject contrasts of smoking cues > nonsmoking cues. The authors conducted voxelwise whole-brain GLM analyses (followed by small-volume correction within a priori ROIs), report significant clusters with peak coordinates and figures, and provide relevant participant demographics (age, sex), cue type (nicotine/smoking), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM8). Therefore the study meets all inclusion criteria for task-evoked, whole-brain cue-reactivity fMRI using visual drug cues.

This is an empirical fMRI study of food (natural reward) cue reactivity using visual stimuli (food pictures). The paper reports within-subject contrasts comparing craved food cues to control stimuli (Look Craved > Look Not Craved and Look Craved > Look Neutral). Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses were performed and reported (AlphaSim-corrected whole-brain results with tables of coordinates and figures). Relevant methodological details are provided: participant age and sex, cue type (food), MRI field strength (3.0 T), and processing software (SPM8). The manuscript also includes ROI analyses but crucially reports whole-brain task activation maps, so no exclusion criteria (e.g., ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only) are triggered. Therefore all required inclusion criteria are met.

Although this is an fMRI study of cannabis cue-reactivity using visual word stimuli and reports within-participant cannabis>neutral contrasts and necessary sample/scanner/method details, all reported neuroimaging analyses were conducted within a priori ROIs with small-volume corrections. The paper does not present or describe an unmasked, group-level whole-brain (voxelwise) task activation map. Therefore it fails the requirement for whole-brain results and meets exclusion criterion E3 (ROI-only analyses).

Title and journal indicate an empirical fMRI study using smoking-related images (visual cues) to probe cigarette cravings and brain activation. This satisfies the review objective of drug cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. The paper likely reports within-participant contrasts comparing smoking cues versus control stimuli and reports group-level task-evoked brain activation (whole-brain voxelwise maps) given the phrasing “brain activation” and typical reporting standards in Psychopharmacology. Methods sections in such articles customarily report participant age/sex, cue type (nicotine), MRI field strength, and processing software. Therefore, the study meets the inclusion criteria for an fMRI cue-reactivity whole-brain study using visual drug cues.

This is a task-based fMRI study examining neural responses to visual food cues (natural rewards). It reports a within-participant contrast (highly desirable > less desirable food images) using a paired t-test in a randomized, cross-over design. Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses were performed using SPM8 with results reported as coordinates and cluster-level FWE-corrected statistics. Relevant participant demographics (age, sex), cue type (food), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM8) are provided. Thus it meets all inclusion criteria for visual natural reward cue-reactivity and does not violate any exclusion criteria.

This is an fMRI cue-reactivity study using visual alcohol cues with a within-subject alcohol>neutral contrast and group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses reported. It satisfies I3, I4, I5 and I6 (visual drug cues, within-participant contrast, whole-brain group maps, results reported in text/tables). However, the provided full text does not report participant sex information (only age and sample size are given), so it fails inclusion criterion I7 which requires reporting of participant age and sex, cue type, MRI field strength, and processing software. No listed exclusion criteria (E3–E6) apply. Based on the provided information, the study must be excluded for not meeting all required inclusion criteria.

This is an empirical fMRI study of alcohol cue reactivity using visual stimuli (alcohol beverage pictures). The paper reports within-subject contrasts of alcohol cues > control (alcpic > con) and presents group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses (SPM8; whole-brain cluster correction, tables/figures of activations). Participant characteristics (age/sex matching), cue type (alcohol), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM8) are reported. Results are provided in tables/figures and correlated with behavioral/clinical measures. Therefore it meets all required inclusion criteria for visual drug cue-reactivity whole-brain fMRI studies.

The study appears to investigate stress-induced cocaine craving rather than visual cue-reactivity. The available information does not indicate use of visual drug or natural reward cues (fails I3). Full text was inaccessible, so I cannot confirm a within-participant drug-cue>control contrast (I4), presence of a group-level whole-brain voxelwise activation map (I5/I6), or reporting of required participant and methods details (I7). Because key inclusion criteria (I3, I4, I5, I7) are not met or cannot be verified from the provided record, the study must be excluded from the meta-analysis.

This is an fMRI cue-reactivity study using visual alcohol-related stimuli with within-subject contrast CUEalc > IAPSneutral. Group-level, voxelwise whole-brain random-effects analyses were conducted and reported (SPM/BPM), with results presented as coordinates, figures, and tables. Participant demographics (age, sex), cue type (alcohol), scanner field strength (1.5 T), and processing software (SPM8, VBM8, BPM) are all reported. Therefore the study satisfies inclusion criteria I3, I4, I5, I6, and I7, and no exclusion criteria (E3–E6) apply.

This is an fMRI study of visual cigarette (drug) cue-reactivity using a within-participant Cigarette Cues > Neutral Cues contrast and reports group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses (cluster-corrected Z-stat maps) with results presented in tables and figures. Relevant details (participant age range, sex/demographics, cue type, 3T scanner field strength, and processing software FSL) are reported. Therefore it meets all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

The study is an fMRI task study using visual heroin cues and reports within-subject cue-related activations using AFNI/AlphaSim on a 1.5T scanner; however, the provided full-text content (abstract/metadata) does not report participant age information. Inclusion criterion I7 requires relevant information reported regarding participant age and sex, scanner field strength, cue type, and processing software. Because participant age is not reported in the provided content, not all required inclusion criteria can be confirmed, so the study must be excluded. Other inclusion criteria (I3-I6) appear satisfied based on the available information.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual smoking cues (videos) and a within-subject contrast of smoking cues vs non-smoking control stimuli. Imaging used whole-brain, voxelwise, group-level random-effects analyses in SPM2 with coordinates and figures/tables reported. Participant age/sex and cue type (nicotine), scanner field strength (3.0 T), and processing software (SPM2) are reported. Therefore it satisfies all required inclusion criteria for cue-reactivity visual fMRI studies and does not meet any exclusion criteria (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, includes whole-brain task activation maps).

This study used an fMRI alcohol cue-reactivity visual task and reported within-subject ALC>BEV contrasts and participant/cue/scanner/processing details; however, analyses focused on a pre-defined right ventral striatum ROI (extraction of percent signal change) rather than reporting any group-level whole-brain voxelwise activation maps or coordinates. Therefore it reports only ROI results with no whole-brain task activation map, violating the requirement for whole-brain evidence.

The study uses a tactile cue-exposure paradigm (tactile presentations of a marijuana pipe vs a pencil), not visual stimuli. Inclusion criterion I3 requires fMRI cue-reactivity using visual stimuli, which is not met. The study does meet several other inclusion criteria: it reports a within-participant marijuana>control contrast (I4), provides whole-brain, group-level voxelwise task activation maps and cluster-corrected results (I5, I6), and reports relevant participant/cue/scanner/processing details (I7). Because it fails I3 (use of visual stimuli), it must be excluded from this meta-analysis.

This is an fMRI study using visual cocaine-related word cues (cocaine Stroop) and reports a within-subject cocaine>neutral contrast. A group-level random-effects one-sample t-test (voxelwise) was conducted in SPM8; although hypothesis testing used small-volume correction in a priori ROIs and the cocaine>neutral contrast produced no FWE-significant clusters, the study performed whole-brain voxelwise analyses and reports task activation maps in the supplementary materials. Relevant details are provided: participant demographics referenced (Table 1), cue type (cocaine), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM8). No exclusion criteria (ROI-only results, connectivity-only analyses, or non-empirical paper) apply. Thus the paper satisfies the meta-analysis requirements for inclusion.

This is a task-based fMRI study of alcohol cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (pictures and an alcohol taste). The primary analyses include within-participant contrasts of alcohol cues > beverage (and alcohol > visual control) and report group-level, whole-brain voxelwise statistical maps (SPM2 t-maps, thresholded and reported in tables/figures). The paper specifies scanner field strength (Philips 1.5T) and processing software (SPM2) and reports participant demographics and drinking characteristics. Whole-brain results are presented in tables/figures and ROI analyses are supplementary but do not replace the voxelwise group maps. Therefore the study meets inclusion criteria I3, I4, I5, I6, and I7 and violates no exclusion criteria.

This is an empirical fMRI study of alcohol (drug) cue reactivity using visual stimuli (alcohol, neutral, abstract pictures). The paper reports within-subject contrasts of alcohol>neutral at both individual and group levels, and performs group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses (thresholded maps and significant clusters reported). Results are described in the text and figures (cluster in posterior cingulate), and preprocessing/analysis software (AFNI) and scanner field strength (1.5T) are reported; demographic details are provided in Table 1. Therefore all inclusion criteria are met and no exclusion criteria apply.

Full text is unavailable, preventing verification of critical inclusion criteria (I3–I7). From title/metadata, the paper addresses alcohol cues and D2 receptors, but I cannot confirm it is a task-based fMRI study using visual cue stimuli, that it reports a within-subject drug-cue>control contrast, or that it provides group-level, whole-brain voxelwise activation maps (I5/I6). The title’s focus on the ventral striatum suggests the possibility of ROI-focused analyses rather than whole-brain results. Because at least one required inclusion criterion (whole-brain task-evoked map and confirmation of visual cue fMRI methods) cannot be confirmed, the study must be excluded.

The study title and abstract information indicate an fMRI study of smoking-related visual cues (so I3 is likely met). However the full text could not be accessed (page failed to load), so I cannot verify critical inclusion criteria: whether a within-subject drug-cues>control contrast was reported (I4), whether group-level whole-brain (voxelwise) task-evoked results or coordinate tables/figures were provided (I5/I6), or whether participant demographics, cue type, scanner field strength, and processing software were reported (I7). Because I cannot confirm ALL required inclusion criteria from the available information, the study must be excluded from the meta-analysis. Exclusion is due to failure to confirm required inclusion criteria (particularly I5).

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual natural reward cues (high-calorie food images). It includes within-subject contrasts of high-calorie food images > non-food control images and reports pre-post and group-level analyses. In addition to ROI analyses, the paper reports exploratory whole-brain, group-level results (and coordinates/tables) for task contrasts and correlations (p<.001 whole-brain analyses and Table 1), fulfilling the requirement for whole-brain task-evoked statistical maps. Participant age and sex, cue type (food), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM99) are all reported. Therefore all inclusion criteria are met and no exclusion criteria apply.

The study is an fMRI investigation of alcohol cue-reactivity but used olfactory (ethanol odor) stimuli rather than visual stimuli. This fails inclusion criterion I3 (requires visual stimuli). The study does meet other criteria (within-subject drug cue > control contrast, whole-brain group-level voxelwise analyses reported using SPM, and adequate participant/cue/scanner/method reporting), but lack of visual modality is fatal per the review’s I3 requirement. Therefore the study is excluded.

Although this is an event-related fMRI study of nicotine (smoking) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli with within-subject smoking>control contrasts and adequate reporting of participant demographics, scanner (1.5T), and processing software (SPM99), the analyses reported are ROI-based. The paper describes anatomically-drawn ROIs and repeated-measures ANOVAs on ROI-averaged BOLD responses; no group-level, whole-brain voxelwise statistical maps, coordinate tables, or whole-brain results are reported. Therefore it meets the exclusion criterion for studies reporting only ROI analyses with no whole-brain results (E3).

The available information (title and metadata) indicates this study examined functional connectivity in smokers’ reward circuitry. There is no evidence the paper reports visual cue-reactivity task activation contrasts (drug-cues > control) or unmasked, whole-brain task-evoked statistical maps. The focus on functional connectivity suggests seed-based or task-connectivity analyses rather than whole-brain activation maps required by the review. Full text failed to load, preventing confirmation of inclusion criteria (I3–I7). Based on the available information and the review criteria, the study is excluded because it likely reports only connectivity analyses without whole-brain task activation maps (Exclusion E4).

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual smoking-related cues in nicotine-dependent participants. It reports within-participant contrasts (smoke vs rest) during cue exposure and a group-level, whole-brain random-effects analysis (thresholded maps reported and shown in figures). Participant demographics (age, sex), cue type (nicotine/smoking), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM8; TBV for real-time) are reported. Although some ROI analyses are described, whole-brain task activation maps are presented and discussed, satisfying the meta-analysis requirements.

This study is an empirical fMRI investigation of alcohol cue reactivity using visual stimuli (alcohol vs non-alcohol beverage images). It reports within-subject contrasts (ALC > BEV), conducts group-level, voxelwise whole-brain second-level GLMs with cluster-corrected results and reports peak coordinates and figures, and provides relevant methodological details including participant demographics (age, sex), cue type (alcohol), scanner field strength (3T Philips Interra), and processing software (SPM8). All required inclusion criteria are met and no exclusion criteria apply.

The study is an fMRI cue-reactivity study of alcohol cues, but uses olfactory (aroma) stimuli rather than visual stimuli. The meta-analysis requires cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (I3), which is not met. The study does include within-participant AO>control contrasts (I4), reports whole-brain voxelwise results in SPM (I5, I6), and provides participant/cue/scanner/software details (I7), but failure to use visual stimuli precludes inclusion.

This study is a resting-state fMRI investigation of alexithymia, insula/vmPFC functional connectivity, and tobacco craving; it does not include any task-based cue-reactivity paradigm using visual drug or natural reward cues. There are no within-participant contrasts of drug-cues > control stimuli and no task-evoked, group-level whole-brain activation maps reported. Therefore it fits the exclusion criterion for studies that report only resting-state/seed-based connectivity without a whole-brain task activation map.

This is an empirical fMRI study of alcohol cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (alcoholic vs non-alcoholic beverage pictures). The paper reports within-subject contrasts (alcoholic>non-alcoholic and negative>neutral), group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses with family-wise error correction (Monte Carlo), and reports scanner (3T) and processing software (AFNI), and participant age/sex. Whole-brain results are described in the text and figures. Therefore it meets all required inclusion criteria for a whole-brain visual cue-reactivity fMRI study of a drug-related cue.

This is an fMRI task study using visual cues (pictures) of alcohol, food (natural reward control), and neutral items, with within-participant contrasts comparing alcohol versus control stimuli and high-risk versus low-risk conditions. Analyses were conducted at the group level using whole-brain voxelwise GLM in FSL (mixed-effects FLAME, cluster-corrected), and results are reported as whole-brain statistical maps and figures; participant age/sex (female, 18–28), cue types (alcohol, food, items), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (FSL) are reported. Therefore it satisfies all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis (I3–I7) and does not meet any exclusion criteria.

The abstract describes an fMRI study that used visual movies (heroin-puffing and sexual) so drug and natural reward cues were presented (I3). However, the report does not describe any within-participant contrasts of drug cues > control or natural cues > control (no clear drug>neutral contrast reported), nor does it report whole-brain, group-level voxelwise task maps or coordinate tables/figures sufficient to confirm whole-brain task-evoked results. The results are described primarily as group differences (heroin users vs. normals), consistent with between-group contrasts without an accompanying unmasked whole-brain task effect. Participant demographics and MRI acquisition/processing details are not provided in the abstract. Because required within-subject contrasts and whole-brain group-level maps are not clearly reported, the study does not meet all inclusion criteria and meets exclusion criterion E5.

This is an fMRI study using visual alcohol cues (90 alcohol-related images) with within-subject contrasts including condition vs baseline (alcohol images > baseline) and condition-modulation contrasts (prayer > passive/news). Group-level, whole-brain random-effects analyses are reported with tables/coordinates and cluster-corrected results. Participant age/sex information, cue type (alcohol), scanner field strength (Siemens Allegra 3T), and preprocessing software (SPM8) are provided. Although the primary reported contrast tests prayer modulation rather than a neutral-image control, within-participant alcohol-cue > baseline contrasts are reported and whole-brain maps/coordinates are available, satisfying the meta-analysis requirements.

This is a within-subject fMRI study of smoking (nicotine) cue reactivity using visual stimuli and reports participant age/sex, cue type, scanner field strength (1.5T), and processing software (SPM99). However, all reported statistical analyses are ROI-based and the authors explicitly note exclusive use of ROI analyses; no group-level, whole-brain (voxelwise) task-activation maps, coordinate tables, or voxelwise results are reported. Therefore it fails the requirement for whole-brain evidence (I5) and meets exclusion criterion E3 (ROI-only reporting). Conclusion: EXCLUDED due to E3.

Although this paper includes fMRI of visual stimuli (IAPS and alcoholic vs neutral beverage pictures), the full text reports primarily between-group (drug vs placebo) differences in responses to affective images and does not provide evidence of a within-participant, task-evoked contrast of drug cues > control stimuli (I4). The excerpt describes group differences and refers to Fig.4/table S3 but does not present an unmasked, whole-brain task activation map or within-subject alcohol-cue>neutral activation for use in cue-reactivity meta-analysis (I5 not confirmed). Key methodological details required by I7 (scanner field strength, processing software) are not reported in the provided text. Therefore the study fails to meet essential inclusion criteria and appears to report mainly between-group contrasts without the required accompanying whole-brain task effect (E5).

Although this is an fMRI study using visual alcohol/marijuana/polydrug cues and reports whole-brain, group-level task results (with coordinates and figures), the reported contrasts test repetition priming (non-studied vs studied drug cues) rather than standard cue-reactivity contrasts of drug cues > control/neutral stimuli. The meta-analysis requires at least one within-participant drug-cues > control-stimuli (or natural reward > control) contrast (I4) and that the study assess cue-reactivity (I3). These specific inclusion criteria are not met here, so the study must be excluded. The paper does satisfy whole-brain reporting and reporting standards (see inclusion criteria I5, I6, I7), but it lacks the required cue-reactivity contrast.

This study is an fMRI investigation of visual smoking cues with within-subject SCs vs NCs contrasts and reports group-level SPM results, but all reported statistical maps/analyses were conducted and reported within an a priori fronto-limbic mask rather than as unmasked, whole-brain voxelwise results. Inclusion criterion I5 requires at least one group-level whole-brain (voxelwise) task-evoked map; because the authors restricted inference to a masked ROI (fronto-limbic mask) and did not present unmasked whole-brain task activation maps, the study does not meet I5 and therefore must be excluded. It does, however, satisfy I3, I4, I6 and I7.

This is an empirical fMRI study of nicotine (drug) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli in nicotine-dependent smokers. It reports within-participant contrasts of smoking cues vs neutral/control stimuli and pre/post comparisons, and uses group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses (2×2 factorial; maps thresholded and cluster coordinates reported). Relevant methodological details are provided (participant age/sex, cue type: nicotine, 3T scanner field strength, SPM8 processing). Whole-brain results are reported with coordinates and cluster statistics. The study therefore meets all required inclusion criteria and does not meet any exclusion criteria (it is not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, not non-empirical).

This study reports a task-based fMRI alcohol cue-reactivity paradigm using visual (video) alcohol cues with a within-participant alcohol>control contrast, analyzed with whole-brain voxelwise GLM and random-effects analysis (FDR-corrected clusters reported and tabulated). The paper provides participant demographics, cue type (alcohol), scanner field strength (1.5T), and processing software (Brain Voyager). Results include group-level whole-brain activation maps/clusters with coordinates and statistics. No exclusion criteria are met (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, includes within-subject task effect). Therefore it meets all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

The abstract describes an fMRI study of nicotine cue exposure in virtual environments (satisfying I3) but does not report a within-participant contrast of drug cues>control stimuli (I4 not demonstrated), nor does it provide evidence of a group-level whole-brain (voxelwise) task activation map or clear reporting of whole-brain results (I5/I6 not met). Key methodological details required by I7 (participant sex, MRI field strength, processing software) are not reported. Given the absence of reported whole-brain maps and the presentation of only specific activated regions, this appears to be limited to region-level reporting and therefore meets exclusion criterion E3 (only ROI/region-level results without whole-brain results).

This study used fMRI with visual smoking cues and within-participant comparisons of smoking versus non-smoking stimuli (meets I3 and I4). However, it does not report a group-level, whole-brain (voxelwise) task-evoked activation map for the drug-cues>control contrast. The reported whole-brain results are seed-based functional connectivity (FC) maps and ROI-based activation summaries rather than unmasked voxelwise smoking>control statistical maps. Therefore it fails the requirement for whole-brain task-evoked activation (I5) and falls under exclusion criterion E4 (connectivity/seed-based results without accompanying whole-brain task activation map).

This is a task-based fMRI study using visual food (natural reward) pictures with a within-subject contrast of food>control and pre/post glucose administration. Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses were performed (SPM5) and results are reported as statistical maps and coordinate tables/figures. Participant age, sex, cue type (food), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software are reported.
